# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**"List the six standard parameters used in the designation of a grinding wheel."**

**Model Answer (3 marks – 1 mark per paired element with brief description):**
1. **Abrasive type** – e.g., A (Aluminum Oxide), C (SiC), B (CBN), D (Diamond).
2. **Grit size** – numerical value from 8 (coarse) to 600 (very fine).
3. **Grade (Hardness of bond)** – letter from A (soft) to Z (hard).
4. **Structure** – number from 1 (dense) to 8 or more (open).
5. **Bond type** – e.g., V (Vitrified), B (Resinoid), R (Rubber), S (Silicate), M (Metal).
6. **Manufacturer's code** – free-form alphanumeric identifier by the maker.

> **Mark Distribution:** Listing = 2 marks; Brief purpose of one parameter = 1 mark.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3]**
**"Why is a 'soft' grinding wheel recommended for grinding a hard workpiece? Explain with the principle."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The grade of a grinding wheel refers to the **strength of the bond** holding the abrasive grains, not the hardness of the abrasive itself.
- When grinding a **hard workpiece**, the abrasive grains experience **low wear and high retention**. If a hard-grade wheel is used, the dulled grains will not break off, leading to **glazing** of the wheel surface, excessive heat, and burn on the workpiece.
- A **soft-grade wheel** ensures that the dulled grains **dislodge quickly**, exposing fresh sharp grains. This is the principle of **self-sharpening** of grinding wheels.
- This is the **inverse rule**: *Soft wheel for hard material; Hard wheel for soft material.*

> **Mark Distribution:** Principle explained = 2 marks; Practical example = 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks – Choice Option 1)

> **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Classify grinding machines in detail, with neat sketches showing the kinematics of **(i)** Surface Grinder with horizontal spindle and reciprocating table, and **(ii)** Centerless Grinder (through-feed type). Discuss the relative merits of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **six standard parameters** used in grinding wheel designation with a suitable example. Discuss the **types of bonds** used in grinding wheels and their typical applications. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution – Part (a) – 7 marks**

**(i) Surface Grinder – Horizontal Spindle, Reciprocating Table:**

- **Construction:** The grinding wheel spindle is mounted horizontally. The workpiece is clamped on a magnetic chuck fixed to the **reciprocating worktable** that moves back and forth linearly under the wheel.
- **Kinematics:**
  - **Primary motion:** Rotation of the grinding wheel (peripheral speed 20–30 m/s).
  - **Secondary motion (work feed):** Reciprocation of the table along the longitudinal axis.
  - **Tertiary motion (cross-feed):** The wheel head moves transversely across the table after each stroke.
  - **Vertical feed (depth of cut):** The wheel is lowered vertically into the workpiece.
- **Merits:** High surface accuracy (flatness within 5 µm), good surface finish (Ra 0.4–0.8 µm), ideal for tool rooms, magnetic chuck simplifies clamping of ferromagnetic parts.
- **Limitations:** Limited to flat surfaces; lower productivity than rotating table type for mass production.

**(ii) Centerless Grinder – Through-Feed Type:**

- **Construction:** Has a **regulating wheel** (rubber-bonded, slow speed, controls rotation and feed of the workpiece) and a **grinding wheel** (high speed). The workpiece rests on a **work rest blade** between the two wheels. No centers or chucks are used.
- **Kinematics:**
  - **Grinding wheel:** Rotates at high speed (30 m/s) – performs cutting.
  - **Regulating wheel:** Rotates at slower speed with axis tilted by 0.5°–3° to provide longitudinal feed.
  - The tilt angle of the regulating wheel determines the feed rate.
- **Merits:** High productivity (continuous through-feed), no centering error, suitable for slender parts, low operator skill needed.
- **Limitations:** Not suitable for workpieces with shoulders, heads, or non-cylindrical profiles; requires a pre-round workpiece.

> **Mark Split (a):** Surface grinder explanation = 3 marks; Centerless explanation = 3 marks; Diagram mention = 1 mark.

---

### **Model Solution – Part (b) – 7 marks**

**The six standard parameters of grinding wheel designation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example used: A 60 L 5 V 23 (standard KTU-asked example)**
>
> 1. **Abrasive type – 'A':** Aluminum Oxide – general-purpose abrasive for carbon steels and alloy steels.
> 2. **Grit size – '60':** Medium grain, suitable for general-purpose grinding.
> 3. **Grade – 'L':** Medium-hard bond; releases grain at moderate rate; suitable for medium-hard workpiece.
> 4. **Structure – '5':** Slightly open structure; allows chip clearance.
> 5. **Bond – 'V':** Vitrified bond – rigid, strong, heat-resistant, and porous. Most widely used bond in industry.
> 6. **Maker's code – '23':** Manufacturer-specific identifier (e.g., Grindwell Norton 23).

**Types of Bonds (with applications):**

| **Bond** | **Material** | **Properties** | **Application** |
|----------|--------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Vitrified (V)** | Silicate / Clay | Rigid, strong, porous, heat-resistant | General-purpose; cylindrical, surface, tool grinding |
| **Resinoid / Bakelite (B)** | Phenolic resin | Tough, flexible, fast cutting | Rough grinding, snagging, cut-off wheels |
| **Rubber (R)** | Vulcanized rubber | Very flexible, elastic | Thin cutoff wheels, control wheels in centerless grinders |
| **Silicate (S)** | Sodium silicate | Brittle, easy dressing | Disc grinders, edge grinding |
| **Shellac (E)** | Natural shellac | Elastic, smooth finish | Camshaft, roll finishing |
| **Metal (M)** | Bronze, electrolytic copper | Strong, expensive | Diamond and CBN wheels (sintered metal bond holds super-abrasives) |

> **Mark Split (b):** Six parameters explained = 3 marks; Bond types table = 3 marks; Application example = 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks – Choice Option 2)

> **Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Classify **grinding wheels based on the abrasive type** and explain why different abrasives are used for different workpiece materials. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Differentiate between **plain cylindrical grinder, universal cylindrical grinder, and centerless grinder**. What are the operational and economic advantages of a centerless grinder? **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution – Part (a) – 7 marks**

**Classification of grinding wheels based on abrasive type:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four primary abrasive types (must be memorized):**

1. **Aluminum Oxide (Al₂O₃) – Code 'A':**
   - **Toughness:** Very tough (high fracture resistance).
   - **Hardness:** High (Mohs ~9).
   - **Application:** Most widely used abrasive; for **carbon steel, alloy steel, tool steel, wrought iron**.
   - **Variants:** Pink Al₂O₃, White Al₂O₃ (friable for hardened tool steel), Brown Al₂O₃ (tough for soft steel).
   - **Reason for use:** Toughness prevents grain fracture under high forces generated when cutting tough ferrous alloys.

2. **Silicon Carbide (SiC) – Code 'C':**
   - **Hardness:** Higher than Al₂O₃ (Mohs ~9.5) but **brittle** (low fracture toughness).
   - **Application:** **Cast iron, non-ferrous metals (aluminum, brass, bronze), carbide, stone, ceramics, glass, rubber**.
   - **Reason for use:** Hard abrasive easily shatters the brittle chip in cast iron and chips of ceramics; toughness is not required.

3. **Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN) – Code 'B':**
   - **Hardness:** Second only to diamond (Mohs ~9.5+).
   - **Thermal stability:** Excellent (retains hardness up to 1200 °C, much better than diamond which burns at 700 °C).
   - **Application:** **Hardened tool steels (HRC 50+), high-speed steels, die steels, nickel-based superalloys**.
   - **Reason for use:** Ferritic workpiece materials chemically react with diamond at high temperature; CBN is chemically inert and thermally stable, making it ideal for hardened steel.

4. **Diamond – Code 'D':**
   - **Hardness:** Hardest known material (Mohs 10).
   - **Application:** **Tungsten carbide, ceramics, glass, stone, concrete, cemented carbides**.
   - **Reason for use:** The high hardness of diamond is required to abrade the very hard carbides and ceramics.
   - **Limitation:** Reacts with iron (carbon affinity) at high temperature – **not used on steel workpieces**.

**Why different abrasives for different workpieces?**
- **Hardness compatibility:** Abrasive must be significantly harder than workpiece (rule of thumb: abrasive hardness ≥ 1.5 × workpiece hardness).
- **Chemical inertness:** Abrasive must not chemically react with workpiece at grinding temperature.
- **Toughness match:** Tough abrasive for tough work; hard-brittle abrasive for brittle work.
- **Thermal stability:** Required for high-temperature grinding of aerospace alloys.

> **Mark Split (a):** Four abrasive types explained = 4 marks; Reasons for selection = 3 marks.

---

### **Model Solution – Part (b) – 7 marks**

**Comparative Table – Cylindrical Grinder Variants:**

| **Feature** | **Plain Cylindrical Grinder** | **Universal Cylindrical Grinder** | **Centerless Grinder** |
|-------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Workpiece holding** | Between centers (chuck + tailstock) | Between centers, swiveling headstock | On work rest blade (no centers) |
| **Surface types** | Straight cylindrical, taper (limited) | Cylindrical, taper, internal, complex | Straight cylindrical only |
| **Headstock swivel** | Fixed | Swiveling (up to ~90°) | Not applicable |
| **Internal grinding** | Not possible | Possible with attachment | Not possible |
| **Setup time** | Moderate | Higher (calibration needed) | Low (no centering) |
| **Production rate** | Medium | Low to medium | High (continuous through-feed possible) |
| **Workpiece size limit** | Limited by distance between centers | Same as plain | Limited by regulating wheel diameter and infeed length |
| **Centering error** | Yes (depends on center holes) | Yes | No (no centers used) |
| **Typical application** | Shafts, journals, pins, small batch | Tool room, taper grinding, repair shops | High-volume pins, rollers, bushings, piston pins |

**Operational and Economic Advantages of Centerless Grinder:**

1. **No centering error:** Eliminates the need for center holes, avoiding alignment errors and improving roundness accuracy.
2. **Higher productivity:** Through-feed centerless grinding can feed continuously, achieving very high production rates.
3. **Faster loading/unloading:** No need to set up between centers, reducing non-productive time.
4. **No driver required:** The regulating wheel provides rotation, eliminating the need for a workpiece driver.
5. **Lower operator skill required:** Simpler loading → reduced labor cost.
6. **Suitable for slender parts:** Parts that would deflect between centers can be ground centerlessly.
7. **Lower per-piece cost in mass production:** Although the machine is costlier, the per-piece cost is lower at high volumes.

> **Mark Split (b):** Comparison table = 4 marks; Centerless advantages = 3 marks.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Carefully – 2024 KTU Evaluation):**
> 1. **"Grade" confusion:** Students often confuse **wheel grade (bond strength)** with **abrasive hardness**. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if a student writes *"soft wheel = soft abrasive"*. The grade refers to the **bond**, not the abrasive.
> 2. **Inversion rule misapplication:** A common error is writing *"hard wheel for hard material"*. The correct rule is **"soft wheel for hard material"** – the dull grain must shed off easily to expose fresh sharp grain.
> 3. **Diamond on steel:** Do **not** recommend diamond wheels for steel workpieces. Diamond chemically reacts with iron at high temperatures (graphitization) and wears out rapidly. **Use CBN for steel.**
> 4. **Skipping bond code in designation:** Many students write 5 parameters instead of **6** (they forget the manufacturer's code).
> 5. **Confusing "structure" with "grade":** Structure is the **spacing of grains (1–8)**, grade is the **bond strength (A–Z)**.
> 6. **In sketches, missing arrows** showing the direction of work rotation, table travel, and wheel rotation will result in **1 mark deduction**.
> 7. **Units in MRR problems:** Always report MRR in **mm³/min** (or cm³/min) with explicit units. Bare numbers = 0.5 mark deduction.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist – Classification (Module 3, PCPET403):**

- **Two main classifications to master:** (1) Grinding Machines, (2) Grinding Wheels.
- **Grinding Machines – by geometry:** Surface, Cylindrical, Internal, Centerless, Tool & Cutter, Bench, Pedestal, Portable.
- **Surface Grinder sub-types:** Horizontal/Vertical spindle × Reciprocating/Rotating table = 4 main types + Duplex.
- **Centerless sub-types:** Through-feed, In-feed (Plunge), End-feed.
- **Grinding Wheel Designation – 6 parameters (Mnemonic: A-G-G-S-B-M):**
  * **Abrasive** (A, C, B, D)
  * **Grit size** (8–600, coarse to fine)
  * **Grade** (A–Z, soft to hard bond)
  * **Structure** (1–8, dense to open)
  * **Bond** (V, B, R, S, E, M)
  * **Manufacturer's code**
- **Abrasive Selection Rule:**
  * Aluminum Oxide → Steel
  * Silicon Carbide → Cast Iron, Carbide, Non-ferrous
  * CBN → Hardened Tool Steel
  * Diamond → Carbide, Ceramics, Glass
- **Wheel Grade Rule (Inverse Rule):** Soft wheel for hard material; Hard wheel for soft material.
- **Most common bond:** **Vitrified (V)** – used in 70%+ of industrial grinding wheels.
- **Vitrified bond properties:** Rigid, strong, porous, heat-resistant.
- **Resinoid bond use:** Cut-off wheels, snagging, high-stock-removal operations.
- **Diamond cannot grind steel** (carbon affinity at high temperature → graphitization).
- **Spindle speed formula (must memorize):** $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ (rpm).
- **MRR formula for cylindrical grinding:** $\text{MRR} = \pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot f_r \cdot N_w$ (mm³/min).
- **Surface speed range:** 20–30 m/s for conventional grinding; 30–60 m/s for high-speed grinding; up to 120 m/s for ultra-high-speed grinding (modern CBN).
- **Self-sharpening principle:** The grade must be soft enough that dulled grains shed off to expose sharp grains.
- **Common KTU essay question:** "Classify grinding machines with diagrams" or "Explain the six standard parameters of grinding wheel designation."
- **Common KTU 3-mark question:** "Why is CBN preferred over diamond for grinding steel?" (Answer: CBN has higher thermal stability and is chemically inert to iron; diamond reacts with iron above 700 °C to form iron carbide and graphitize, causing rapid wear.)
- **Universal cylindrical grinder specialty:** Swiveling headstock allows taper grinding and internal grinding with attachment.
- **Magnetic chuck:** Used on surface grinders to clamp ferromagnetic workpieces quickly.
- **Regulating wheel:** A rubber-bonded slow wheel in centerless grinder that controls rotation and longitudinal feed of the workpiece.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specification of Shaper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Specification of Shaper # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Specification of a Shaper** is the standardized, manufacturer-declared set of technical parameters (dimensions, capacities, power ratings, and performance limits) that completely characterize the **size, capacity, and operational envelope** of a shaper machine tool. These specifications enable a manufacturing engineer to **select the appropriate shaper** for a given workpiece geometry and to **estimate machining time, productivity, and cost** before actual production. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPET403, Module 3 — *Reciprocating Machine Tools*), the term "Specification" is formally defined as a **declarative technical data sheet** of the machine following the conventional order prescribed by **IS 2063 / B.I.S. standards for machine tool specifications**. The eight conventional specifications, in the **standard declaration order**, are: | Sl. No. | Specification Parameter | Typical Range (Workshop Shaper) | |:-------:|:------------------------|:-------------------------------:| | 1 | Maximum length of stroke of the ram (L) | 175 – 900 mm | | 2 | Size of the table (Length × Width) | 300×225 – 1500×1000 mm | | 3 | Maximum cross-slide travel (horizontal) | 200 – 750 mm | | 4 | Maximum vertical slide travel | 150 – 600 mm | | 5 | Range of cutting speeds (strokes/min) | 8 – 150 strokes/min | | 6 | Power of the main drive motor | 1.5 – 11 kW (2 – 15 HP) | | 7 | Floor space required (L × W) | 1500×1200 – 4500×3000 mm | | 8 | Net weight of the machine | 800 – 6000 kg | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Passport of a Machine" Imagine you are buying a **refrigerator**. You would never say *"give me a fridge"* — you ask for *"a 250-litre, 5-star, frost-free, double-door refrigerator"*. These numbers and attributes are its **specifications** — they describe its capacity, capability, and limits. A shaper is **no different**. When a workshop owner says *"I need a 450 mm stroke shaper"*, they are specifying: - **What is the longest flat surface I can cut in a single pass?** → Stroke length = **450 mm** - **How wide a workpiece can I accommodate?** → Cross-slide travel = say **400 mm** - **How fast will it cut?** → Number of strokes/min - **Will my factory floor hold it?** → Floor space > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight (2024 Scheme):** > In the KTU examination, students are expected to write specifications **strictly in the conventional order** (Stroke → Table → Cross-slide → Vertical-slide → Speeds → Motor → Floor space → Weight). Marks are awarded for **sequence** as well as content. Writing them out of order may cost **partial credit** in descriptive answers. ## 1.3 Why Specifications Matter in Manufacturing - **Machine Selection:** Match the machine capacity to the largest workpiece. - **Process Planning:** Compute cycle time, cost estimation, and capacity planning. - **Comparison & Vendor Evaluation:** Two shapers from different manufacturers can be objectively compared only through specifications. - **Safety:** Operating beyond rated specifications leads to **tool breakage, machine failure, and operator injury**. --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Explanation of Each Specification ### (1) Maximum Length of Stroke of the Ram (L) This is the **longest distance** the ram (the reciprocating arm carrying the tool) can travel in one complete cycle. It determines the **maximum length of the flat surface** that can be machined in a single pass. - *Symbol:* $L$ (in mm) - *Typical:* $L = 175$ mm (small workshop) to $L = 900$ mm (heavy-duty). - *Effect on selection:* The workpiece length $L_w$ must be **less than $L$** to be machined on the shaper. ### (2) Size of the Table (Length × Width) The table is the **work-holding platform** mounted on the cross-slide. Its dimensions dictate the **maximum size of the workpiece** that can be physically clamped. - *Symbol:* $L_t \times W_t$ (in mm × mm) - *Typical:* $300 \times 225$ mm (mini-shaper) to $1500 \times 1000$ mm (industrial). ### (3) Maximum Cross-Slide Travel The cross-slide moves the table **perpendicular to the ram stroke direction** (in horizontal cross-feed). This travel determines the **maximum width of cut** achievable. - *Symbol:* $C_t$ (in mm) - *Typical:* $200$ to $750$ mm. ### (4) Maximum Vertical Slide Travel The vertical slide (saddle) raises or lowers the table vertically, allowing the cutting tool to engage workpieces of varying heights and enabling **vertical surface generation**. - *Symbol:* $V_t$ (in mm) - *Typical:* $150$ to $600$ mm. ### (5) Range of Cutting Speeds (Strokes per Minute) Expressed as **strokes per minute (spm)** or **number of double strokes per minute**, this is the variable speed range provided by the gearbox to suit different tool materials, workpiece materials, and depth of cut. - *Symbol:* $N$ (strokes/min) - *Typical range:* $8$ to $150$ spm in discrete steps. > [!TIP] > The cutting speed in shaping is **not** linear m/min as in turning — it is expressed as **strokes per minute**, because the ram reciprocates and the cutting action happens only on the forward (cutting) stroke. ### (6) Power of the Main Drive Motor This is the **rated power** of the electric motor that drives the bull gear → pinion → crank → ram mechanism. - *Symbol:* $P$ (in kW or HP) - *Typical:* $1.5$ kW (2 HP) to $11$ kW (15 HP). - $1 \text{ HP} = 0.746 \text{ kW}$. ### (7) Floor Space Required The total **footprint** of the machine including operator access area — important for **plant layout** and **workshop design**. - *Symbol:* $L_f \times W_f$ (mm × mm) ### (8) Net Weight of the Machine The total weight of the machine tool, indicating its **robustness, rigidity, and structural class**. - *Typical:* $800$ to $6000$ kg. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examination Cheat Sheet — Shaper Specifications & Calculations** | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | |:-:|:---------|:------:|:--------|:----:| | 1 | Length of stroke | $L$ | Given in specifications | mm | | 2 | Strokes per minute | $N$ | Given in specifications | spm | | 3 | Cutting speed | $V_c$ | $V_c = \dfrac{2 \cdot L \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | | 4 | Cutting speed (alternate, cutting stroke only) | $V_c$ | $V_c = \dfrac{L \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | | 5 | Feed per stroke | $f$ | Given / selected from chart | mm/stroke | | 6 | Depth of cut | $d$ | Given / selected | mm | | 7 | Feed rate (table feed) | $f_r$ | $f_r = f \times N$ | mm/min | | 8 | Material Removal Rate | $\text{MRR}$ | $\text{MRR} = V_c \cdot f \cdot d = L \cdot N \cdot f \cdot d$ | mm³/min | | 9 | Number of strokes for length $L_w$ | $n_s$ | $n_s = \dfrac{L_w}{f}$ | strokes | | 10 | Machining time | $T_m$ | $T_m = \dfrac{L_w + l_1 + l_2}{f \cdot N}$ | min | | 11 | Length of cut on return | $L_{ret}$ | $L_{ret} = L$ (same as forward) | mm | | 12 | Motor power check | $P_m$ | $P_m = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \times \text{Specific Energy}}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}$ | kW | Where: - $l_1$ = **approach length** (length before tool engages workpiece) ≈ $5$–$15$ mm - $l_2$ = **overtravel length** (length after tool disengages) ≈ $5$–$15$ mm - $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency of shaper (typically $0.7$ – $0.85$) - $\text{Specific Energy}$ for mild steel ≈ $2.0$ W·s/mm³ ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In actual industrial practice (factories like HMT, BFW, ACE), the specification sheet is used to: 1. **Generate quotations** — customers compare two shapers using these specifications. 2. **Capacity planning** — whether a $500$ mm stroke shaper can handle a $480$ mm long gear housing. 3. **Energy audit** — motor power rating helps estimate electricity consumption. 4. **Predictive maintenance** — knowing rated RPM and feed helps schedule bearing/gearbox service. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Cutting Speed Formula ### Derivation 1: Cutting Speed (Considering Cutting Stroke Only) **Step 1 — Define the kinematics:** The ram of the shaper reciprocates with a stroke length $L$ (in mm). It completes $N$ complete double-strokes per minute. A "double stroke" = 1 forward (cutting) + 1 return (idle). **Step 2 — Distance covered in cutting stroke per minute:** In each cutting stroke, the ram covers a distance equal to $L$ mm. Since there are $N$ strokes per minute, the total cutting distance covered per minute is: $$ \text{Cutting distance per minute} = L \times N \quad \text{(in mm/min)} $$ **Step 3 — Convert mm/min to m/min:** To convert mm to metres, divide by $1000$: $$ V_c = \frac{L \times N}{1000} \quad \text{m/min} $$ **Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:** This represents the **linear speed at which the cutting edge engages the workpiece**, analogous to the surface speed in a turning operation. --- ### Derivation 2: Cutting Speed (Considering Total Stroke — Forward + Return) **Step 1 — Total distance per double stroke:** In one complete cycle (forward + return), the ram travels: $$ \text{Distance per cycle} = L + L = 2L \quad \text{mm} $$ **Step 2 — Multiply by number of cycles per minute:** $$ \text{Total distance per minute} = 2L \times N \quad \text{mm/min} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to m/min:** $$ V_c = \frac{2LN}{1000} \quad \text{m/min} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Convention Note:** For examination purposes, the formula $V_c = \dfrac{2LN}{1000}$ is the **most widely accepted** in Indian university textbooks (Bhandari, Pandya & Shah, Ghosh & Mallik). This is because it represents the **actual kinematic speed of the ram** considering both strokes. The cutting-edge engagement velocity uses $V_c = \dfrac{LN}{1000}$ if explicitly mentioned. --- ### Derivation 3: Material Removal Rate (MRR) **Step 1 — Volume removed per stroke:** In one cutting stroke, the tool removes a chip of: - Width = $f$ (feed per stroke) - Length = $L$ (length of cut) - Thickness = $d$ (depth of cut) $$ V_{\text{per stroke}} = L \times f \times d \quad \text{mm}^3/\text{stroke} $$ **Step 2 — Multiply by strokes per minute:** $$ \text{MRR} = L \times f \times d \times N \quad \text{mm}^3/\text{min} $$ **Step 3 — Express via cutting speed:** Since $V_c = \dfrac{LN}{1000}$ m/min $= LN$ mm/min, $$ \text{MRR} = V_c \times f \times d \quad \text{mm}^3/\text{min} $$ --- ### Derivation 4: Machining Time **Step 1 — Determine total length of travel:** The workpiece length is $L_w$. The tool must travel the full workpiece length **plus an approach distance $l_1$** (to accelerate and engage) **plus an overtravel distance $l_2$** (to decelerate and disengage safely): $$ L_{\text{total}} = L_w + l_1 + l_2 \quad \text{mm} $$ **Step 2 — Determine number of strokes required:** Since each stroke advances the tool by $f$ mm (in the feed direction), $$ n_s = \frac{L_w + l_1 + l_2}{f} \quad \text{strokes} $$ **Step 3 — Time per stroke:** Each stroke (forward + return) takes $\dfrac{1}{N}$ minutes. **Step 4 — Total machining time:** $$ T_m = \frac{L_w + l_1 + l_2}{f \times N} \quad \text{minutes} $$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style Problem) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example (14-Mark Style):** > A horizontal shaper has the following specifications: Maximum stroke length = $350$ mm, Range of cutting speeds = $10$ to $60$ strokes/min, Feed range = $0.3$ to $1.2$ mm/stroke. A workpiece of length $280$ mm is to be machined with a depth of cut of $3$ mm. The recommended cutting speed for the workpiece material is $15$ m/min. The approach and overtravel are $10$ mm each. Calculate: (i) Cutting speed in strokes/min, (ii) MRR, (iii) Machining time. **Given:** - $L = 350$ mm (max stroke; selected ≥ $L_w + l_1 + l_2 = 280 + 10 + 10 = 300$ mm) ✓ - $V_c = 15$ m/min (recommended) - $f = 0.8$ mm/stroke (assumed mid-range) - $d = 3$ mm - $L_w = 280$ mm, $l_1 = l_2 = 10$ mm ### Part (i) — Calculate $N$ (strokes/min) Using $V_c = \dfrac{2LN}{1000}$: $$ 15 = \frac{2 \times 350 \times N}{1000} $$ $$ 15 = \frac{700 \times N}{1000} = 0.7 N $$ $$ N = \frac{15}{0.7} = 21.43 \approx 22 \text{ strokes/min} $$ Since $22$ falls within the range $10$–$60$ spm ✓, the setting is feasible. ### Part (ii) — Calculate MRR $$ \text{MRR} = L \times f \times d \times N $$ $$ \text{MRR} = 350 \times 0.8 \times 3 \times 22 = 18480 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} $$ Or equivalently: $$ \text{MRR} = 18.48 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 18.48 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} $$ ### Part (iii) — Calculate Machining Time $$ T_m = \frac{L_w + l_1 + l_2}{f \times N} $$ $$ T_m = \frac{280 + 10 + 10}{0.8 \times 22} = \frac{300}{17.6} = 17.05 \text{ minutes} $$ ### Final Answer Summary | Quantity | Value | |:---------|:-----:| | Strokes per minute ($N$) | $22$ spm | | MRR | $18{,}480$ mm³/min | | Machining time ($T_m$) | $17.05$ min | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Shaper with Specification Annotations The shaper consists of **eight major subsystems**, each contributing to one or more specifications. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DRIVE[DRIVE AND POWER SUBSYSTEM] M[Main Motor
Power kW] GB[Gearbox
Speed Range] BG[Bull Gear and Pinion
Strokes per Minute N] end subgraph RAM[RAM AND STROKE SUBSYSTEM] CR[Crank and Slotted Arm
Quick Return Mechanism] RA[Ram
Maximum Stroke Length L] TH[Tool Head and Clapper Box
Tool Inclination] end subgraph TABLE[WORK HOLDING AND FEED SUBSYSTEM] VB[Vertical Slide
Vertical Travel Vt] CS[Cross Slide
Cross Travel Ct] TB[Table
Size Lt times Wt] WP[Workpiece
Length Lw] end subgraph BASE[STRUCTURAL SUBSYSTEM] CL[Column] BS[Base
Floor Space Lf times Wf] WT[Total Weight of Machine] end M --> GB --> BG --> CR --> RA --> TH RA --> WP TH --> WP TB --> WP CS --> TB VB --> CS CL --> VB BS --> CL BS --> WT style DRIVE fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style RAM fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style TABLE fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px style BASE fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Specification-to-Subsystem Mapping Matrix | # | Specification | Subsystem Responsible | Functional Impact | |:-:|:--------------|:----------------------|:------------------| | 1 | Max stroke length $L$ | Ram + Crank mechanism | Determines max surface length machinable | | 2 | Table size $L_t \times W_t$ | Table (work-holding) | Determines max workpiece footprint | | 3 | Max cross-slide travel | Cross-slide | Determines max width of cut | | 4 | Max vertical-slide travel | Vertical slide | Determines height accommodation | | 5 | Stroke range (spm) | Gearbox + Bull gear | Determines cutting speed versatility | | 6 | Motor power | Main drive motor | Determines max MRR achievable | | 7 | Floor space | Base + Column | Plant layout consideration | | 8 | Net weight | Entire structure | Indicates rigidity class | ## 4.3 Stroke Cycle Timing Diagram The shaper uses a **Quick Return Mechanism** — the cutting stroke is slower, and the return stroke is faster (typically **2:1 ratio**). ```mermaid gantt title Shaper Stroke Cycle (Quick Return Mechanism) dateFormat ss axisFormat %S section Cutting Stroke (Slow) Forward Ram Motion :a1, 00, 1800ms section Return Stroke (Fast) Return Ram Motion :a2, after a1, 900ms ``` In a typical cycle of $1/N$ minutes: - Cutting stroke time : Return stroke time ≈ $2:1$ - Example for $N = 20$ spm: total cycle $= 3$ s; cutting $= 2$ s; return $= 1$ s. --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define the term "specification of a machine tool." List any **six** main specifications of a shaper in their conventional order. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition:** Specification of a machine tool is a standardized, manufacturer-declared set of technical parameters — including dimensions, capacities, power ratings, and operational limits — that completely characterize the machine's **size, capacity, and performance envelope**. **Six Specifications of a Shaper (in conventional order):** 1. Maximum length of stroke of the ram (mm) 2. Size of the table — Length × Width (mm × mm) 3. Maximum cross-slide travel (mm) 4. Maximum vertical slide travel (mm) 5. Range of cutting speeds / strokes per minute (spm) 6. Power of the main motor (kW) *(Additional: Floor space required, Net weight)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` Distinguish between the **cutting speed** in a shaper and the cutting speed in a turning operation. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Shaper | Turning | |:-------|:-------|:--------| | Nature of motion | **Reciprocating** (linear forward-backward) | **Continuous rotary** motion | | Definition of cutting speed | Linear speed of tool during cutting stroke | Surface speed of workpiece at tool tip | | Typical formula | $V_c = \dfrac{2LN}{1000}$ m/min | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ m/min | | Expresses speed in | **Strokes per minute (spm)** | **RPM** of workpiece | | Cutting action | Only on forward stroke (return is idle) | Continuous 360° engagement | | Quick return mechanism | **Required** (to save time) | Not applicable | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **constructional features and working principle** of a standard horizontal shaper. Also state the **eight main specifications** of the shaper. **[7 marks]** **Model Answer:** A horizontal shaper consists of the following main parts: 1. **Base** — Heavy cast iron bed that supports the entire machine and absorbs vibrations. 2. **Column** — Vertical pillar on the base that houses the drive mechanism and supports the ram. 3. **Ram** — The reciprocating arm that carries the tool head and moves horizontally. 4. **Tool Head** — Mounted on the ram's front face; holds the single-point cutting tool and can be swiveled for taper cutting. 5. **Cross-Slide** — Mounted on the column; provides cross-feed (perpendicular to ram motion). 6. **Vertical Slide (Saddle)** — Provides vertical feed to the table. 7. **Table** — Top work-holding surface, mounted on the vertical slide; can be tilted for angular cuts. 8. **Drive Mechanism** — Motor → Gearbox → Bull gear & Pinion → Crank & Slotted Arm → Ram (with Whitworth quick return). **Working:** The motor rotates the bull gear via the gearbox. The pinion on the bull gear drives the crank. The crank is connected to the ram through a slotted lever (Whitworth quick return), converting rotary motion into reciprocating linear motion of the ram. During the cutting stroke, the tool moves forward slowly cutting the workpiece. On the return stroke, the tool lifts (via clapper box) and returns quickly. **Eight Specifications:** 1. Max stroke length — 450 mm 2. Table size — 900 × 700 mm 3. Max cross-slide travel — 600 mm 4. Max vertical-slide travel — 400 mm 5. Range of strokes — 10 to 90 spm 6. Main motor power — 5.5 kW (7.5 HP) 7. Floor space — 2700 × 1800 mm 8. Net weight — 3200 kg --- **(b)** A shaper has a maximum stroke of $300$ mm and operates at $25$ strokes/min. The workpiece length is $200$ mm, feed is $0.6$ mm/stroke, and depth of cut is $4$ mm. The approach and overtravel are $10$ mm each. Calculate the **(i)** cutting speed, **(ii)** feed rate, **(iii)** MRR, and **(iv)** machining time. **[7 marks]** **Model Answer:** **Given:** $L = 300$ mm, $N = 25$ spm, $L_w = 200$ mm, $f = 0.6$ mm/stroke, $d = 4$ mm, $l_1 = l_2 = 10$ mm. **(i) Cutting Speed:** $$ V_c = \frac{2 L N}{1000} = \frac{2 \times 300 \times 25}{1000} = \frac{15000}{1000} = 15 \text{ m/min} $$ **(ii) Feed Rate:** $$ f_r = f \times N = 0.6 \times 25 = 15 \text{ mm/min} $$ **(iii) Material Removal Rate:** $$ \text{MRR} = L \times f \times d \times N = 300 \times 0.6 \times 4 \times 25 = 18{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} $$ **(iv) Machining Time:** $$ T_m = \frac{L_w + l_1 + l_2}{f \times N} = \frac{200 + 10 + 10}{0.6 \times 25} = \frac{220}{15} = 14.67 \text{ minutes} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **significance of each specification** of a shaper. How does each specification influence the **selection of a shaper** for a given manufacturing task? **[7 marks]** **Model Answer:** | Specification | Significance | Selection Influence | |:--------------|:-------------|:--------------------| | **1. Max stroke length** | Determines longest surface machinable in one setup | Choose $L \geq L_w + l_1 + l_2$ | | **2. Table size** | Limits workpiece footprint | Workpiece dims must fit on table | | **3. Cross-slide travel** | Allows cuts across full width of workpiece | Determines max width machinable | | **4. Vertical-slide travel** | Allows accommodation of tall workpieces | Must be ≥ workpiece height | | **5. Strokes per minute range** | Provides flexibility for different materials | Must include the recommended spm | | **6. Motor power** | Determines maximum MRR / depth of cut | Must be adequate for workpiece material | | **7. Floor space** | Determines workshop layout | Must fit available area | | **8. Net weight** | Indicates rigidity (heavier = more rigid) | Heavy workpieces need heavy machines | The selection logic is: **Workpiece dimensions** (length, width, height) → Match with stroke, table size, cross-slide, vertical-slide → **Material** → Match with cutting speed range and motor power → **Workshop** → Match with floor space and supply. --- **(b)** During a shaping operation, the cutting speed is $20$ m/min, the stroke length is $250$ mm, the feed is $0.4$ mm/stroke, and the depth of cut is $5$ mm. If the workpiece is $180$ mm long (approach $10$ mm, overtravel $10$ mm), determine: **(i)** the strokes per minute, **(ii)** the cutting time, **(iii)** the volumetric MRR. **[7 marks]** **Model Answer:** **Given:** $V_c = 20$ m/min, $L = 250$ mm, $f = 0.4$ mm/stroke, $d = 5$ mm, $L_w = 180$ mm, $l_1 = l_2 = 10$ mm. **(i) Strokes per minute:** Using $V_c = \dfrac{2 L N}{1000}$: $$ 20 = \frac{2 \times 250 \times N}{1000} = \frac{500 N}{1000} = 0.5 N $$ $$ N = \frac{20}{0.5} = 40 \text{ spm} $$ **(ii) Cutting Time:** $$ T_m = \frac{L_w + l_1 + l_2}{f \times N} = \frac{180 + 10 + 10}{0.4 \times 40} = \frac{200}{16} = 12.5 \text{ minutes} $$ **(iii) Volumetric MRR:** $$ \text{MRR} = L \times f \times d \times N = 250 \times 0.4 \times 5 \times 40 = 20{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 20 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Wrong formula for cutting speed** — Students often write $V_c = LN/1000$ when the question expects the full-stroke version $V_c = 2LN/1000$. **Read the question carefully**: if it says "cutting speed of the ram", use $2LN/1000$. > 2. **Forgetting the approach and overtravel** in machining time calculation — This causes a 5–10% error, which examiners **specifically deduct marks for**. > 3. **Stroke length must exceed $L_w + l_1 + l_2$** — Students often pick a stroke shorter than the workpiece length, which is physically impossible. **Always check feasibility** before solving. > 4. **Unit mismatches** — Mixing mm with m in MRR or cutting speed is a frequent error. Maintain consistency: use **mm for lengths**, **convert to m only in final answer for $V_c$**. > 5. **Specifications out of order** — In a 7-mark descriptive question on "list specifications", examiners award marks **per correct specification + 1 mark for correct sequence**. Random order = lose the sequence mark. > 6. **Confusing feed (mm/stroke) with feed rate (mm/min)** — These are different quantities with different units. Don't interchange them. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Specification of Shaper (PCPET403, Module 3)** - **Eight standard specifications** (in order): Stroke → Table size → Cross-slide travel → Vertical-slide travel → Stroke range → Motor power → Floor space → Net weight. - **Primary specification** = Maximum stroke length $L$ (mm); **Performance specification** = Stroke range in strokes/min. - **Cutting speed formula** (most common in KTU): $V_c = \dfrac{2 L N}{1000}$ m/min. - **MRR formula:** $\text{MRR} = L \times f \times d \times N$ mm³/min. - **Machining time formula:** $T_m = \dfrac{L_w + l_1 + l_2}{f \times N}$ minutes — **always include approach + overtravel**. - **Feasibility check:** $L \geq L_w + l_1 + l_2$ (stroke must be long enough for the cut). - **Feasibility check:** $N$ calculated from $V_c$ must lie **within the shaper's specified stroke range**. - **Quick Return Mechanism** is what makes shaper efficient — return stroke is ~$2\times$ faster than cutting stroke. - **Cutting happens only on forward stroke** — return stroke is idle (tool lifts via clapper box). - **Units conversion:** $1 \text{ HP} = 0.746 \text{ kW}$; $1 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}$. - **Default approach + overtravel** if not given in problem: assume $l_1 = l_2 = 10$ mm each (unless otherwise stated). - **KTU marking tip:** Always present specifications in **conventional order** for full marks. - **Industrial relevance:** Used for small-batch production, prototyping, tool-room jobs, and machining of large flat surfaces on heavy workpieces. - **Comparison:** Shaper (small workpiece, single-point tool, reciprocating) vs. Planer (large workpiece, multiple tools, reciprocating table) vs. Slotter (vertical reciprocating ram for internal slots).

topics.title: Shaper Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shaper Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Shaper

A **Shaper** is a reciprocating-type machine tool used primarily for producing flat surfaces, grooves, slots, and keyways on a workpiece. The linear reciprocating motion of the ram (which carries the cutting tool) is the defining kinematic characteristic, distinguishing it from rotating-tool machines like milling machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPET403 Module 3):** *A shaper is a machine tool that removes metal by the relative reciprocating linear motion between the tool (held on the ram) and the workpiece. The quick-return mechanism converts uniform rotary motion of the driving motor into a non-uniform reciprocating motion of the ram.*

## 1.2 What is a Shaper Mechanism?

A **Shaper Mechanism** is the kinematic linkage that converts the **uniform rotary motion** of an electric motor (via a bull gear or pulley) into the **non-uniform reciprocating linear motion** of the ram. The non-uniformity is intentional: the **return stroke** (idle stroke) must be faster than the **cutting stroke** to reduce unproductive time, thereby increasing productivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Return Concept:** Because the cutting tool only cuts during the forward stroke and idles during the return stroke, the time spent on the return stroke is "wasted" time. By making the return stroke faster (shorter time for the same stroke length), we increase the proportion of productive cutting time. This is the entire purpose of a quick-return mechanism.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "See-Saw with a Kick"

Imagine a child on a see-saw (the slotted lever) and an adult walking in a circle pushing the see-saw (the crank). The adult walks at a **constant pace** in a circle. The child goes up and down, but interestingly, the upward swing (corresponding to the return stroke) happens *faster* than the downward swing (cutting stroke) — even though the adult walks at a constant speed. This is because the geometry of the circular push, combined with the lever's pivot point, naturally produces a non-uniform rocking motion. **This is exactly how a Crank-and-Slotted-Lever mechanism works!**

## 1.4 Classification of Shaper Mechanisms

Shapers are primarily classified by the type of mechanism used to drive the ram:

| Sl. No. | Mechanism Type | Driving Source | Typical Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Crank and Slotted Lever (Bull Gear)** | Mechanical | Most common; standard shapers |
| 2 | **Whitworth Quick-Return** | Mechanical | Heavy-duty shapers; large strokes |
| 3 | **Hydraulic Shaper** | Hydraulic cylinder | Modern precision shapers |
| 4 | **Gear-Driven (Rack and Pinion)** | Mechanical | Smaller strokes, lighter duty |
| 5 | **Cam-Driven Shaper** | Mechanical (cam) | Special-purpose shaping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Design Metric:** For KTU numerical problems, the most commonly tested mechanisms are the **Crank-and-Slotted-Lever** and **Whitworth Quick-Return** types. The Hydraulic Shaper is increasingly common in industry due to its infinitely variable stroke length and constant cutting force capability.

## 1.5 Kinematic Pair Classification (Grübler's Equation Preview)

All reciprocating mechanisms used in shapers are **four-bar kinematic chains** (or inversions thereof) comprising:
- **Revolute pairs (R)** — at fixed pivots
- **Prismatic pairs (P)** — at sliding joints (ram-tool, pin-in-slot)

The Crank-and-Slotted-Lever, for instance, is an inversion of the **Slider-Crank Chain**, with the slider replaced by a sliding pin in a curved slot.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Crank-and-Slotted-Lever (Bull-Gear) Mechanism

### 2.1.1 Construction and Working

The Crank-and-Slotted-Lever Mechanism consists of four kinematic links:

1. **Frame (Link 1):** The fixed structure, which fixes the crank center **O₁** and the slotted-lever pivot **O₂**.
2. **Crank (Link 2):** Rotates uniformly about **O₁** with angular velocity **ω₂**. The crank pin **P** is at radius **R** from **O₁**.
3. **Slotted Lever (Link 3):** Oscillates about the fixed pivot **O₂**. Contains a straight slot of length greater than **2R**.
4. **Ram (Link 4):** The reciprocating member carrying the tool, constrained to move along the **line of stroke**.

The crank pin **P** slides freely within the slot. As the crank rotates:
- During the cutting stroke, the slotted lever oscillates such that the ram advances through the workpiece.
- During the return stroke, the lever oscillates back, retracting the ram rapidly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Return Mechanism Principle:** Because the crank rotates *uniformly* but the slotted lever oscillates *non-uniformly*, the angular velocity of the slotted lever (and therefore the linear velocity of the ram) varies throughout the cycle. The lever moves faster during the return stroke, hence the name "quick-return."

### 2.1.2 Geometry and the Concept of Offset

Let:
- **R** = crank radius (length of crank arm)
- **L** = distance from crank center **O₁** to slotted-lever pivot **O₂** (i.e., the line-of-centers)
- **e** = perpendicular offset of pivot **O₂** from the **line of stroke**

The slot is always oriented along the slotted-lever direction. At the two **extreme positions of the ram**, the crank pin **P** is at the point where the crank circle is *tangent* to a circle centered at **O₂**. Geometrically, this occurs when **O₁P ⊥ O₂P**.

Defining the angle **θ** such that:

$$\sin\theta \;=\; \frac{e}{L}$$

The cutting stroke and return stroke crank angles are:

$$\alpha \;=\; 180^\circ + 2\theta \quad \text{(Cutting Angle)}$$

$$\beta \;=\; 180^\circ - 2\theta \quad \text{(Return Angle)}$$

### 2.1.3 Quick Return Ratio (QRR)

The **Quick Return Ratio (QRR)** is defined as the ratio of time taken for the cutting stroke to the time taken for the return stroke. Since the crank rotates uniformly:

$$\boxed{\text{QRR} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{cut}}}{T_{\text{ret}}} \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{\beta} \;=\; \frac{180^\circ + 2\theta}{180^\circ - 2\theta}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** If QRR = 1.5, the cutting stroke takes 1.5× as long as the return stroke for the same stroke length, meaning the cutting speed is 1.5× slower than the return speed. Typical industrial shapers have QRR in the range **1.5 to 2.0**.

### 2.1.4 Stroke Length

The stroke length **S** of the ram is the distance traveled between the two extreme ram positions:

$$\boxed{S \;=\; 2R \cdot \frac{\ell}{L} \cdot \cos\theta}$$

Where **ℓ** is the length of the slotted lever from pivot **O₂** to the ram attachment point. For the simplified case where **ℓ = L** and the ram end coincides with **O₂'s projection** on the line of stroke, the stroke length reduces to:

$$S \;=\; 2R \cos\theta$$

### 2.1.5 Velocity Analysis of the Ram

The crank pin **P** has velocity:

$$v_P \;=\; R\,\omega_2 \quad \text{(tangential to crank circle)}$$

This velocity vector can be resolved into two components:
- **Component along the slot** → equals the sliding velocity of the pin within the slot
- **Component perpendicular to the slot** → drives the angular oscillation of the lever

The angular velocity of the slotted lever:

$$\omega_3 \;=\; \frac{R\,\omega_2\,\cos\phi}{\overline{O_2P}}$$

where **φ** is the instantaneous angle between **O₁P** and the slot direction.

The ram velocity (along the line of stroke):

$$v_{\text{ram}} \;=\; \omega_3 \cdot \ell \cdot \cos\gamma$$

where **γ** is the instantaneous angle of the slotted lever with respect to the line of stroke.

## 2.2 The Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism

### 2.2.1 Construction

The Whitworth mechanism is a more complex inversion that uses **two cranks and a connecting rod with a slider**:

1. **Driving crank (small):** Rotates uniformly about fixed pivot **A**.
2. **Slotted driven crank (large):** Oscillates about fixed pivot **B**.
3. **Connecting rod with slider:** Links the two cranks; a pin on the driving crank slides within a slot machined in the larger driven crank.
4. **Ram:** Connected to the driven crank via an additional connecting rod.

This mechanism is effectively a **two-crank inversion of the slider-crank chain**, providing a longer and more uniform cutting stroke compared to the simple crank-and-slotted-lever.

### 2.2.2 Geometry and Quick Return Ratio

Let:
- **r** = radius of the driving (small) crank
- **R** = radius of the driven (large) crank
- **d** = center distance between pivots **A** and **B**

The crank pin traverses an arc of the small crank circle. The driven crank oscillates through an angle governed by the geometry. The cutting and return angles are:

$$\alpha \;=\; 180^\circ - 2\phi$$

$$\beta \;=\; 180^\circ + 2\phi$$

$$\text{QRR} \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{\beta} \;=\; \frac{180^\circ - 2\phi}{180^\circ + 2\phi}$$

where **φ** is the half-angle of the cutting stroke arc and depends on the ratio **r/d** and **R/d**.

## 2.3 Hydraulic Shaper Mechanism

In modern shapers, a **hydraulic cylinder-piston assembly** replaces the mechanical quick-return mechanism:

- A **hydraulic pump** delivers oil under pressure.
- The directional control valve reverses the oil flow at the end of each stroke.
- The piston drives the ram; the ram's stroke length and velocity profile are **infinitely variable**.
- The cutting speed is nearly **constant** throughout the stroke (unlike mechanical mechanisms where velocity varies sinusoidally).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydraulic Shaper Advantage:** Constant cutting velocity → uniform chip thickness → superior surface finish. Mechanical shapers have inherent velocity fluctuations due to the sinusoidal nature of crank-driven motion.

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations for Shaper Mechanisms.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\text{QRR} = \dfrac{T_c}{T_r} = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}$ | Quick Return Ratio (ratio of times/angles) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\alpha + \beta = 360^\circ$ | Sum of cutting and return angles (one revolution) | Degrees |
| 3 | $\alpha = 180^\circ + 2\theta$ | Cutting angle (Crank & Slotted Lever, offset) | Degrees |
| 4 | $\beta = 180^\circ - 2\theta$ | Return angle (Crank & Slotted Lever, offset) | Degrees |
| 5 | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{e}{L}$ | Half-angle offset relation | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{QRR} = \dfrac{180^\circ + 2\theta}{180^\circ - 2\theta}$ | QRR for offset Crank & Slotted Lever | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $S = 2R\cos\theta$ | Stroke length (simplified) | mm |
| 8 | $S = 2R \cdot \dfrac{\ell}{L}\cos\theta$ | Stroke length (generalized) | mm |
| 9 | $N_c = N_{\text{rpm}}$ | Cutting strokes per minute (= crank rpm) | strokes/min |
| 10 | $T_m = \dfrac{L_w}{f \cdot N_c}$ | Machining time (basic) | minutes |
| 11 | $T_m = \dfrac{L_w + L_a}{f \cdot N_c}$ | Machining time (with approach + overtravel) | minutes |
| 12 | $v_{\text{avg,cut}} = \dfrac{S \cdot N_c}{60}$ | Average cutting velocity | mm/s |
| 13 | $v_{\text{avg,ret}} = \dfrac{S \cdot N_c \cdot \text{QRR}}{60}$ | Average return velocity | mm/s |
| 14 | $\omega_3 = \dfrac{R\,\omega_2\,\cos\phi}{\overline{O_2P}}$ | Slotted lever angular velocity | rad/s |
| 15 | $v_P = R\,\omega_2$ | Crank pin linear velocity | mm/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - **R** = crank radius; **L** = distance O₁O₂; **e** = offset
> - **ℓ** = slotted-lever length; **S** = stroke length
> - **L_w** = workpiece length; **L_a** = approach + overtravel allowance
> - **f** = feed per stroke (mm/stroke); **N_c** = cutting strokes per minute
> - **α** = cutting crank angle; **β** = return crank angle

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Quick-Return Mechanisms

- **Shapers, Planers, Slotters** — primary reciprocating machine tools
- **Power Hammers & Presses** — slow downstroke, fast upstroke
- **Sewing Machines** — needle bar uses a modified quick-return
- **Automatic Screw Machines** — tool feed and retraction timing

The underlying engineering principle — **non-uniform speed conversion** — is foundational to mechanical design and is regularly tested in KTU GATE-equivalent problems.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quick Return Ratio for the Offset Crank-and-Slotted-Lever

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Geometry

Let us place the coordinate system so that:
- The crank center **O₁** is at the origin.
- The line of stroke is the **x-axis**.
- The slotted-lever fixed pivot **O₂** is at position **(a, e)**, where:
  - **a** = horizontal distance from O₁ to O₂'s projection
  - **e** = perpendicular offset of O₂ from the line of stroke (x-axis)
- The center-to-center distance is $L = \sqrt{a^2 + e^2}$.

The crank pin **P** at crank angle **θ** (measured from positive x-axis) is at:

$$P(\theta) \;=\; \big(R\cos\theta,\; R\sin\theta\big)$$

### 3.1.2 Condition for Extreme Ram Positions

The ram reaches its **extreme positions** when its velocity is zero, which geometrically corresponds to the crank pin being at the point of tangency — i.e., when **O₁P ⊥ O₂P** (the crank arm is perpendicular to the slotted-lever).

The vector **O₁P** is $(R\cos\theta,\,R\sin\theta)$, and the vector **O₂P** is $(R\cos\theta - a,\, R\sin\theta - e)$. Setting their dot product to zero:

$$\big(R\cos\theta\big)\big(R\cos\theta - a\big) + \big(R\sin\theta\big)\big(R\sin\theta - e\big) \;=\; 0$$

Expanding and simplifying:

$$R^2\cos^2\theta - aR\cos\theta + R^2\sin^2\theta - eR\sin\theta \;=\; 0$$

$$R^2\big(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta\big) - R\big(a\cos\theta + e\sin\theta\big) \;=\; 0$$

$$R^2 \;=\; R\big(a\cos\theta + e\sin\theta\big)$$

Dividing through by R (assuming $R \neq 0$):

$$R \;=\; a\cos\theta + e\sin\theta$$

### 3.1.3 Solving for the Extreme Angles

Using the harmonic identity $a\cos\theta + e\sin\theta = \sqrt{a^2 + e^2}\cos(\theta - \phi)$, where $\tan\phi = e/a$:

$$R \;=\; L\cos(\theta - \phi) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \cos(\theta - \phi) \;=\; \frac{R}{L}$$

$$\theta - \phi \;=\; \pm\arccos\!\left(\frac{R}{L}\right) \;=\; \pm\,\psi$$

where we define the **half-angle of the extreme arc** as:

$$\psi \;=\; \arccos\!\left(\frac{R}{L}\right)$$

The two extreme crank angles are therefore:

$$\theta_1 \;=\; \phi + \psi \qquad \theta_2 \;=\; \phi - \psi$$

### 3.1.4 Computing the Cutting and Return Arcs

The two extreme positions divide the crank's full $360^\circ$ revolution into two arcs:
- **Short arc** between $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ passing through the **near-side** of O₂ → angular span = $2\psi$
- **Long arc** between $\theta_2$ and $\theta_1$ passing through the **far-side** of O₂ → angular span = $360^\circ - 2\psi$

For the **return stroke to be faster** (quick-return effect), the return must correspond to the *short* arc, so:

$$\beta \;=\; 2\psi \;=\; 2\arccos\!\left(\frac{R}{L}\right)$$

$$\alpha \;=\; 360^\circ - 2\psi \;=\; 360^\circ - 2\arccos\!\left(\frac{R}{L}\right)$$

### 3.1.5 Expressing in Terms of the Offset Angle

Recognizing that $\sin\theta_{\text{offset}} = e/L$ and noting the equivalence with $\cos\psi = R/L$, we substitute $\psi = 90^\circ - \theta_{\text{offset}}$ when the geometry demands, and arrive at the **standard KTU form**:

$$\boxed{\alpha \;=\; 180^\circ + 2\theta \qquad \beta \;=\; 180^\circ - 2\theta}$$

where $\sin\theta = e/L$.

### 3.1.6 Quick Return Ratio

By definition:

$$\text{QRR} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{cut}}}{T_{\text{ret}}} \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{\beta} \;=\; \frac{180^\circ + 2\theta}{180^\circ - 2\theta}$$

$$\boxed{\text{QRR} \;=\; \frac{180^\circ + 2\theta}{180^\circ - 2\theta}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for the offset crank-and-slotted-lever quick-return ratio.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Machining Time and Average Cutting Velocity

### 3.2.1 Setup

Let:
- $L_w$ = workpiece length (mm)
- $L_a$ = approach + overtravel allowance (mm)
- $S = L_w + L_a$ = effective stroke length (mm)
- $f$ = feed per stroke (mm/stroke)
- $N$ = crank rpm (also = cutting strokes per minute for one cycle per revolution)

### 3.2.2 Number of Strokes Required

Each cutting stroke advances the tool laterally by **f**. To cover the full workpiece length $L_w$:

$$n \;=\; \frac{L_w}{f} \quad \text{(cutting strokes needed)}$$

### 3.2.3 Time per Cutting Stroke

The crank makes $N$ revolutions per minute, each containing one cutting stroke and one return stroke. The cutting stroke occupies a fraction $\alpha/360^\circ$ of one revolution. Time per cutting stroke:

$$T_{\text{c-st}} \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{360^\circ} \cdot \frac{60}{N} \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{6N} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

### 3.2.4 Total Machining Time

$$T_m \;=\; n \cdot T_{\text{c-st}} \;=\; \frac{L_w}{f} \cdot \frac{\alpha}{6N} \;=\; \frac{L_w \cdot \alpha}{6Nf} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

If we express in minutes:

$$\boxed{T_m \;=\; \frac{L_w}{f \cdot N_c} \quad \text{(minutes)}}$$

where $N_c$ is the number of cutting strokes per minute. For a single-cycle-per-revolution crank:

$$N_c \;=\; N \cdot \frac{\beta}{360^\circ} \cdot \frac{\text{cycles}}{\text{cycle}}$$

Wait — this requires care. **Cutting strokes per minute = N** (since one cutting stroke occurs per revolution). So:

$$T_m \;=\; \frac{L_w}{f \cdot N} \quad \text{(minutes)}$$

### 3.2.5 Average Cutting Velocity

The average cutting velocity is the stroke length divided by the cutting-stroke time:

$$v_{\text{avg,cut}} \;=\; \frac{S}{T_{\text{c-st}}} \;=\; \frac{S \cdot 6N}{\alpha} \quad \text{(mm/s)}$$

Converting to mm/min:

$$\boxed{v_{\text{avg,cut}} \;=\; \frac{60 \cdot S \cdot N}{\alpha} \quad \text{(mm/min)}}$$

Similarly for the return stroke:

$$\boxed{v_{\text{avg,ret}} \;=\; \frac{60 \cdot S \cdot N}{\beta} \quad \text{(mm/min)}}$$

Since $\alpha > \beta$ (cutting takes longer), $v_{\text{ret}} > v_{\text{cut}}$ — confirming the **quick-return effect**.

### 3.2.6 Verification with Numerical Example

Let $L_w = 200$ mm, $f = 0.5$ mm/stroke, $N = 30$ rpm, $\alpha = 200^\circ$, $\beta = 160^\circ$.

- Cutting strokes/min = 30
- Machining time: $T_m = \dfrac{200}{0.5 \times 30} = \dfrac{200}{15} = 13.33$ minutes ✓
- QRR = $200/160 = 1.25$ ✓
- Time per cutting stroke = $\dfrac{200}{360} \times \dfrac{60}{30} = 1.11$ s
- Time per return stroke = $\dfrac{160}{360} \times \dfrac{60}{30} = 0.89$ s ✓ (return is faster)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Velocity of the Ram (Instantaneous)

Using the relative velocity method:

$$\vec{v}_P \;=\; \vec{v}_{O_2} + \vec{v}_{P/O_2}$$

Since $O_2$ is fixed: $\vec{v}_{O_2} = 0$, and $v_P = R\omega_2$ (perpendicular to $O_1P$).

The relative velocity $\vec{v}_{P/O_2}$ has two components:
1. **Tangential to slot** (causes lever rotation about $O_2$): magnitude = $\omega_3 \cdot \overline{O_2P}$
2. **Along the slot** (sliding velocity of pin): magnitude = $v_s$

Resolving $v_P$ along and perpendicular to the slot:

$$R\omega_2 \cos\phi \;=\; \omega_3 \cdot \overline{O_2P}$$

Where $\phi$ is the instantaneous angle between $O_1P$ and the slot direction. Solving:

$$\omega_3 \;=\; \frac{R\omega_2 \cos\phi}{\overline{O_2P}}$$

The ram velocity (along the line of stroke):

$$v_{\text{ram}} \;=\; \omega_3 \cdot \ell \cdot \cos\gamma$$

Where $\gamma$ is the instantaneous angle of the slotted lever with respect to the line of stroke.

$$\boxed{v_{\text{ram}} \;=\; \frac{R \cdot \omega_2 \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \ell \cdot \cos\gamma}{\overline{O_2P}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ram velocity is **maximum at mid-stroke** (when the slotted lever is perpendicular to the line of stroke) and **zero at the extremes**. This sinusoidal velocity profile is one of the limitations of mechanical shapers, motivating the shift toward hydraulic drives.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing α and β:** Always verify which angle (cutting or return) is larger. The cutting stroke is *always* the longer angular sweep.
> 2. **Units mismatch:** Crank angles are typically in degrees but trigonometric functions require radians — convert when using $e/L$ relations in calculus-based problems.
> 3. **Stroke length vs. workpiece length:** The stroke length $S$ must exceed the workpiece length $L_w$ by the approach + overtravel allowance $L_a$.
> 4. **Sign convention for offset angle:** $\theta$ is measured from the perpendicular dropped from $O_2$ to the line of stroke. Drawing a clear diagram is worth 1–2 marks by itself.
> 5. **In Whitworth problems:** Don't forget that the cutting angle and return angle definitions may be reversed compared to the crank-and-slotted-lever; always read the problem statement carefully.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Quick Return Ratio. Why is it always greater than unity in a reciprocating machine tool?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Quick Return Ratio (QRR)** of a reciprocating machine tool is defined as the ratio of the time taken for the cutting (forward) stroke to the time taken for the return (idle) stroke:

$$\text{QRR} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{cut}}}{T_{\text{ret}}} \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{\beta}$$

Since the crank (or driving member) rotates at *uniform* angular velocity, but the same angular displacement produces a *shorter* time when covering a *smaller* angular sweep, the return stroke must occupy a smaller angle (β < 180°) and the cutting stroke a larger angle (α > 180°). Therefore:

$$\alpha > \beta \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{QRR} = \frac{\alpha}{\beta} > 1$$

Physically, this means the return stroke is *faster* than the cutting stroke, reducing unproductive idle time and increasing machine productivity. Typical QRR values for industrial shapers range from **1.5 to 2.0**.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any three differences between a Crank-and-Slotted-Lever quick-return mechanism and a Whitworth quick-return mechanism.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Crank-and-Slotted-Lever | Whitworth Quick-Return |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Uses a single crank and a slotted lever with a sliding pin | Uses two cranks (small driving + large driven) coupled by a pin-slot arrangement |
| 2 | The driving member is a crank; the driven member is a slotted lever | Both driving and driven members are cranks (the driven has a slot) |
| 3 | Slot is in the oscillating lever | Slot is in the driven (large) crank |
| 4 | Suitable for moderate stroke lengths and lighter-duty shapers | Suitable for longer strokes and heavy-duty shapers |
| 5 | Mechanical simplicity, lower cost | More complex, higher cost, but provides more uniform cutting velocity |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**In a crank-and-slotted-lever quick-return mechanism used in a shaper, the crank and slotted lever are 250 mm and 350 mm long respectively. The slotted lever is vibrated about a pivot 100 mm away from the line of stroke. Find the angle of cutting and return strokes, and the quick return ratio. If the crank rotates at 30 rpm, determine the average cutting and return velocities for a stroke length of 150 mm.**

#### Part (a) — Compute angles and QRR [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Crank radius $R = 250$ mm
- Distance $O_1O_2 = L = 350$ mm
- Offset $e = 100$ mm
- Stroke length $S = 150$ mm
- Crank rpm $N = 30$ rpm

**Step 1:** Compute the half-offset angle $\theta$:

$$\sin\theta \;=\; \frac{e}{L} \;=\; \frac{100}{350} \;=\; 0.2857$$

$$\theta \;=\; \arcsin(0.2857) \;=\; 16.60^\circ$$

**Step 2:** Compute cutting and return angles:

$$\alpha \;=\; 180^\circ + 2\theta \;=\; 180^\circ + 2(16.60^\circ) \;=\; 213.20^\circ$$

$$\beta \;=\; 180^\circ - 2\theta \;=\; 180^\circ - 2(16.60^\circ) \;=\; 146.80^\circ$$

**Step 3:** Compute QRR:

$$\text{QRR} \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{\beta} \;=\; \frac{213.20^\circ}{146.80^\circ} \;=\; 1.452$$

#### Part (b) — Average velocities and time [7 Marks]

**Step 4:** Cutting strokes per minute = crank rpm = $N = 30$ strokes/min.

**Step 5:** Average cutting velocity:

$$v_{\text{avg,cut}} \;=\; \frac{60 \cdot S \cdot N}{\alpha} \;=\; \frac{60 \times 150 \times 30}{213.20}$$

$$v_{\text{avg,cut}} \;=\; \frac{270{,}000}{213.20} \;=\; 1266.4 \text{ mm/min} \;\approx\; 1.266 \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 6:** Average return velocity:

$$v_{\text{avg,ret}} \;=\; \frac{60 \cdot S \cdot N}{\beta} \;=\; \frac{60 \times 150 \times 30}{146.80}$$

$$v_{\text{avg,ret}} \;=\; \frac{270{,}000}{146.80} \;=\; 1839.0 \text{ mm/min} \;\approx\; 1.839 \text{ m/min}$$

**Verification:** $v_{\text{ret}} / v_{\text{cut}} = 1839.0/1266.4 = 1.452$ = QRR ✓

---

### Question B (Choice 2): **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a Whitworth quick-return mechanism as used in a shaping machine. Derive the expression for the quick return ratio. A Whitworth mechanism has a crank radius of 50 mm and a distance between the centers of the two cranks of 200 mm. Find the ratio of the time of cutting to the time of return.**

#### Part (a) — Construction and working of Whitworth mechanism [7 Marks]

**Construction:**

The Whitworth quick-return mechanism consists of the following components:

1. **Driving Crank ($O_1A$):** A small crank rotating uniformly about its fixed pivot $O_1$ with crank radius $r$.
2. **Driven Crank (Slotted Lever $O_2B$):** A larger crank oscillating about its fixed pivot $O_2$, with a radial slot of length greater than $2r$.
3. **Connecting Pin:** The crank pin $A$ of the driving crank slides within the slot of the driven crank.
4. **Connecting Rod:** Links the end $B$ of the driven crank to the ram.
5. **Ram:** Reciprocates linearly along the line of stroke, carrying the cutting tool.

**Working:**

As the driving crank $O_1A$ rotates uniformly, the pin $A$ describes a circle. The pin engages the slot of the driven crank, forcing it to oscillate. The angular velocity of the driven crank is non-uniform because of the geometric constraint. The driven crank swings through a wider angle during one half of the driving crank's revolution and a narrower angle during the other half. The connecting rod converts this oscillation into linear reciprocation of the ram.

**Quick-Return Action:**

Since the driving crank rotates at *uniform* angular velocity, the wider angular sweep of the driven crank takes *longer* (cutting stroke), while the narrower sweep takes *shorter time* (return stroke). This asymmetry gives the desired quick-return effect.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of QRR [7 Marks]

Let:
- $O_1$ = center of driving crank; $O_2$ = center of driven crank
- $r$ = radius of driving crank
- $L = O_1O_2$ = center distance
- $\theta$ = angle made by the line $O_1A$ with line $O_1O_2$

When the driving crank pin $A$ is at the **inner dead-center** position (closest to $O_2$), the angle of the driven crank with respect to the line of stroke is at one extreme. As the driving crank rotates through angle $\alpha$ (cutting stroke arc), the driven crank swings through its full cutting arc.

The half-angle subtended at the inner dead-center is:

$$\sin\phi \;=\; \frac{r}{L}$$

The total cutting and return angles of the **driven crank** are:

$$\alpha_c \;=\; 180^\circ - 2\phi \qquad \beta_r \;=\; 180^\circ + 2\phi$$

Therefore, the quick-return ratio (driven crank basis):

$$\boxed{\text{QRR} \;=\; \frac{180^\circ - 2\phi}{180^\circ + 2\phi}}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $r = 50$ mm, $L = 200$ mm.

$$\sin\phi \;=\; \frac{50}{200} \;=\; 0.25 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \phi \;=\; \arcsin(0.25) \;=\; 14.48^\circ$$

$$\text{QRR} \;=\; \frac{180^\circ - 2(14.48^\circ)}{180^\circ + 2(14.48^\circ)} \;=\; \frac{180 - 28.96}{180 + 28.96} \;=\; \frac{151.04}{208.96}$$

$$\boxed{\text{QRR} \;=\; 0.7229}$$

This value being **less than 1** indicates the convention that the *cutting* angle is the *narrower* one for this particular Whitworth configuration — students should verify the convention from the problem statement. *(In alternative conventions where the return is the narrower arc, QRR = 1/0.7229 = 1.383.)*

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shaper Mechanisms**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ A **shaper** is a reciprocating machine tool; the **ram** carries the cutting tool.
- ☐ The driving member (bull gear) rotates **uniformly**; the ram moves **non-uniformly**.
- ☐ The **cutting stroke** is slow (productive); the **return stroke** is fast (idle).
- ☐ **Quick Return Ratio (QRR)** = $T_{\text{cut}}/T_{\text{ret}} = \alpha/\beta$, always $\geq 1$ for crank-and-slotted-lever.
- ☐ $\alpha + \beta = 360^\circ$ (one full crank revolution).

### Key Formulas (must memorize)
- ☐ Offset angle relation: $\sin\theta = e/L$
- ☐ $\alpha = 180^\circ + 2\theta$, $\beta = 180^\circ - 2\theta$
- ☐ QRR $= (180^\circ + 2\theta)/(180^\circ - 2\theta)$
- ☐ Stroke length $S = 2R\cos\theta$ (simplified)
- ☐ Average cutting velocity $v_{\text{cut}} = 60 \cdot S \cdot N / \alpha$
- ☐ Machining time $T_m = L_w / (f \cdot N_c)$

### Mechanism-Specific Facts
- ☐ **Crank-and-Slotted-Lever:** uses 1 crank + 1 slotted lever; common for standard shapers.
- ☐ **Whitworth:** uses 2 cranks (driving small + driven large with slot); for heavy duty.
- ☐ **Hydraulic:** infinitely variable stroke, constant cutting velocity, modern preference.
- ☐ The slotted lever pivot is typically **offset** from the line of stroke to create the QRR asymmetry.

### Velocity Profile Insight
- ☐ Ram velocity = 0 at extremes, maximum at mid-stroke.
- ☐ Mechanical shapers → sinusoidal velocity profile → variable chip thickness.
- ☐ Hydraulic shapers → near-constant velocity → uniform chip thickness.

### Examiner's Hot Buttons
- ☐ Always **draw a labeled diagram** — earns 1–2 marks automatically.
- ☐ **State the assumptions** (uniform crank rotation, no friction, etc.).
- ☐ Verify $\alpha + \beta = 360°$ as a consistency check.
- ☐ Convert units carefully (mm ↔ m, min ↔ s, deg ↔ rad).
- ☐ In numerical answers, retain **3–4 significant figures**; KTU expects clean intermediate steps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Shaper. Cutting Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hydraulic Shaper. Cutting Speed # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Hydraulic Shaper — A Formal Definition A **Hydraulic Shaper** is a reciprocating type machine tool that uses a **hydraulic actuation system** to drive the ram (which carries the single-point cutting tool) in linear forward and return strokes, generating a flat machined surface by progressive chip removal. Unlike its mechanical counterpart (crank-and slotted-lemon or Whitworth quick-return mechanism), the hydraulic shaper uses **pressurized hydraulic oil flowing through a directional control valve (DCV) and a hydraulic cylinder** to produce the cutting stroke and the return stroke. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** > Hydraulic shapers fall under reciprocating machine tools and are tested extensively on (a) constructional layout, (b) hydraulic circuit operation, (c) cutting speed formula derivations, and (d) numerical problems involving stroke length, number of strokes/min, and MRR. | Parameter | Typical Industrial Range | |------------|------------------------| | **Ram stroke length** | 300 mm – 900 mm | | **Number of strokes per minute** | 10 – 90 strokes/min | | **Cutting speed range** | **5 m/min to 25 m/min** | | **Hydraulic pressure** | 35 bar – 70 bar | | **Motor power** | 2.2 kW – 7.5 kW | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Automatic Window Wiper" Imagine an **automated windshield wiper on a heavy truck**: - The wiper blade (this is your **cutting tool**) sweeps across the glass (the **workpiece**) in one slow, deliberate motion to wipe water away. - Then it snaps back quickly to its starting position (the **return stroke**). - The slow, careful motion is the **cutting stroke**, and the fast snap-back is the **return stroke** — exactly the cycle a hydraulic shaper performs. - The speed, length, and force of this wiping motion are controlled by an electronic/electrical circuit, just as a hydraulic shaper controls its stroke through **flow control valves (FCV) and pressure control valves (PCV)** in a hydraulic circuit. The crucial difference: the wiper's slow speed protects the rubber; in a shaper, the slow speed **protects the cutting edge from overheating and gives a clean chip**. ## 1.3 Why Hydraulic? The Key Engineering Advantage > [!IMPORTANT] > The defining feature of a hydraulic shaper is its ability to **infinitely vary the cutting speed within the stroke** and provide a **smooth, shock-free reversal of the ram** at the end of each stroke. Mechanical shapers using Whitworth mechanisms cannot achieve this. | Feature | Mechanical Shaper | Hydraulic Shaper | |---------|-------------------|------------------| | Drive | Crank / slotted link | Hydraulic cylinder | | Speed control | Discrete (gear change) | **Continuous (FCV)** | | Reversal impact | Heavy jerk | **Smooth cushioning** | | Constant cutting speed over stroke | No (sinusoidal) | **Yes (via meter-out circuit)** | | Quick return achievable | Yes (mechanism) | **Yes (independent flow control)** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Principal Parts of a Hydraulic Shaper — Structural Breakdown The hydraulic shaper is built around six major sub-assemblies. Each is engineered to deliver reciprocating linear motion with high positional accuracy. 1. **Base & Column** — A heavily ribbed cast-iron structure that absorbs vibration. The column supports the ram-guide ways and houses the hydraulic power pack. 2. **Cross-rail / Worktable** — Mounted on the column, it carries the workpiece. It can be raised/lowered vertically (Y-axis) and traversed longitudinally. 3. **Ram** — A heavy cast slide that reciprocates on accurately scraped guideways. It carries the tool head at its forward end. 4. **Tool Head / Apron** — Swivels on the ram face to permit cutting at an angle (up to ±30°). Holds the single-point cutting tool in a clapper box. 5. **Hydraulic Cylinder & Piston Assembly** — Fitted at the rear of the ram. Double-acting cylinder with piston rod rigidly coupled to the ram. 6. **Hydraulic Power Unit (HPU)** — Contains electric motor, gear/vane pump, reservoir, relief valve, DCV, flow control valves, and pressure gauges. ## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The hydraulic circuit operates on a four-phase cycle per stroke. Here is the exact operational sequence: - **Phase 1 — Forward (Cutting) Stroke Initiation:** The solenoid-operated 4/3 DCV shifts to its left position. Pressurized oil from the pump enters the **cap-end (blank end)** of the hydraulic cylinder, pushing the piston forward. The ram advances and the tool cuts a chip. - **Phase 2 — Metering the Cutting Speed:** A **meter-out flow control valve** on the rod-end line restricts the oil leaving the cylinder. This throttles the discharge rate, thereby throttling the forward velocity. Cutting speed is set by this FCV. - **Phase 3 — End-of-Stroke Reversal:** As the ram reaches the end of its stroke, it actuates a **limit switch / mechanical tripper** which energizes the DCV solenoid. The DCV shifts, reversing oil flow direction. - **Phase 4 — Return Stroke:** Oil now enters the **rod-end** of the cylinder. The piston retracts rapidly. The return speed is set independently by a **second FCV** on the cap-end line, allowing quick return (typically 2× to 3× the cutting speed). - **Cycle Repeats:** DCV automatically shifts back at the start of the stroke, completing one full reciprocating cycle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Meter-out" and not "Meter-in"?** > Meter-out circuits provide **superior speed control** under varying load conditions because the back-pressure created opposes load-induced speed fluctuations. In a shaper, where cutting load varies during the stroke (entry → steady cutting → exit), meter-out gives a nearly constant cutting speed. ## 2.3 Cutting Speed — The Governing Equation in Shaping The **cutting speed (V)** in a shaper is **not constant during the stroke**. It varies because the tool is reciprocating with simple harmonic motion characteristics under hydraulic drive. Therefore, we define two speeds: - **Mean Cutting Speed, $V_m$** — Average velocity during the cutting stroke (used for time estimation). - **Maximum Cutting Speed, $V_{max}$** — Peak velocity at mid-stroke (used for power and force calculations). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Mean cutting speed | $V_m = \dfrac{2 \cdot L \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | L = stroke length (mm), N = strokes/min | | 2 | Maximum cutting speed (simple harmonic) | $V_{max} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot L \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | Assumes SHM profile | | 3 | Ratio $V_{max} / V_m$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.571$ | — | Dimensionless, constant | | 4 | Return ratio (R) | $R = \dfrac{V_r}{V_m}$ | — | Typically 2 to 3 | | 5 | Time per cutting stroke | $T_c = \dfrac{60}{N} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \frac{1}{R}}$ | s | Reciprocating cycle | | 6 | Time per return stroke | $T_r = \dfrac{60}{N} \cdot \dfrac{R}{1 + R}$ | s | — | | 7 | Time per complete cycle | $T = \dfrac{60}{N}$ | s | One forward + one return | | 8 | Feed per stroke | $f$ | mm/stroke | Cross-feed of tool/worktable | | 9 | Feed rate (mm/min) | $f_r = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | — | | 10 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = V_m \cdot d \cdot f$ | mm³/min | $d$ = depth of cut (mm) | | 11 | Hydraulic mean velocity | $Q = A_p \cdot V_m$ | mm³/s | $A_p$ = piston area | | 12 | Pump flow rate required | $Q_p = \dfrac{V_m \cdot A_p \cdot 60}{10^6 \cdot \eta_v}$ | LPM | $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency | > [!NOTE] > In KTU exams, the **most frequently tested formula** is $V_m = \dfrac{2 L N}{1000}$ m/min.
