```mermaid
    style G fill:#e1f0ff
    style H fill:#f0f0f0
```

### 4.1.6 Working Sequence of Double Planer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Table 1 Moves Forward - Cutting] --> A2[Tool Cuts Top Surface]
    A1 --> A3[Tool Cuts Side Surface Simultaneously]

    B1[Table 2 Returns - Idle] --> B2[Returns to Start Position]

    A2 --> C[Stroke Completion]
    A3 --> C
    B2 --> C

    C --> D[Reverse Direction]
    D --> A4[Table 1 Returns - Idle]
    D --> B3[Table 2 Moves Forward - Cutting]

    style A1 fill:#e1ffe1
    style A2 fill:#ffe1e1
    style A3 fill:#ffe1e1
    style B1 fill:#f0f0f0
    style B2 fill:#f0f0f0
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#e1f5ff
```

### 4.1.7 Textual Description of Physical Layout (Shaper)

> [!NOTE]
> **Layout of a Standard Horizontal Shaper (Top View):**
> - **Bed** — long horizontal base, rests on floor
> - **Column** — vertical support at one end
> - **Ram** — horizontal reciprocating member carrying tool head
> - **Cross-slide & Table** — supports workpiece, moves perpendicular to ram
> - **Drive Unit** — motor + gear box at base
> - **Quick-return mechanism** — inside column

### 4.1.8 Textual Description of Physical Layout (Double Planer — Side View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Layout of a Double Planer (Side View):**
> - **Two horizontal tables** move on **parallel guide ways** on the bed
> - Tables move in **opposite directions** (one forward = cutting, one backward = return)
> - **Central cross-rail** spans across both tables, carrying tool heads
> - **Cross-rail can be raised/lowered** to accommodate different workpiece heights
> - **Tool heads** are mounted on the cross-rail and can feed in **vertical** and **horizontal** directions
> - The arrangement is **symmetric** — operator can stand on either side
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

Define the term **"depth of cut"** in a shaping operation. How is it different from **"feed"** in a shaping machine?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Depth of Cut (d):** It is the perpendicular thickness of metal removed from the workpiece surface in a single cutting pass of the shaper tool. It is measured in mm and is analogous to the uncut chip thickness in turning.

**Feed (f):** In a shaper, feed is the **lateral distance** the tool (or workpiece) advances **per stroke**, i.e., the incremental movement perpendicular to the cutting direction. It is measured in **mm/stroke (mm/ds)**.

**Key Difference:**

| Parameter | Depth of Cut | Feed |
|-----------|--------------|------|
| Direction | Perpendicular to machined surface | Perpendicular to cutting direction |
| Affects | Chip thickness | Width of cut |
| Typical value | 1–6 mm | 0.1–1.5 mm/stroke |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

List **any three** operations that can be performed on a shaper. State the **principal difference** between a shaper and a planer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three Shaper Operations:**
1. Horizontal shaping of flat surfaces
2. Vertical shaping (slotting) using a slotter attachment
3. Angular shaping for inclined surfaces
4. Keyway cutting on shafts
5. Irregular / form shaping using formed tools

**Principal Difference (1 mark):**

| Shaper | Planer |
|--------|--------|
| Tool reciprocates, workpiece is stationary | Workpiece reciprocates, tool is stationary |
| Used for **small to medium** workpieces | Used for **large and heavy** workpieces |
| Cutting force on tool pushes it forward | Cutting force is reacted by the heavy table |

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** A horizontal shaper is used to machine a flat surface on a cast iron workpiece. The **stroke length is 250 mm**, the **approach allowance is 20 mm**, the **over-run allowance is 15 mm**, and the **cutting speed is 18 m/min**. Determine:
- (i) the **number of double strokes per minute** of the ram, and
- (ii) the **machining time per stroke**.
- (iii) If a **total depth of 4 mm** is to be removed in **2 passes**, calculate the **total machining time** and the **MRR per minute**. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **working of a crank and slotted lever quick-return mechanism** used in a shaper. List **any four** shaper operations. **[(7 marks)]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Stroke length $L = 250$ mm
- Approach $l_a = 20$ mm
- Over-run $l_o = 15$ mm
- Cutting speed $V_c = 18$ m/min
- Total depth = 4 mm in 2 passes → depth per pass $d = 2$ mm
- Feed $f$ — assume 0.5 mm/ds (typical)

**Step 1 — Adjusted stroke length**

$$L' = L + l_a + l_o = 250 + 20 + 15 = 285 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Number of double strokes per minute (i)**

$$N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{2 \, L'} = \frac{18 \times 1000}{2 \times 285} = \frac{18000}{570}$$

$$\boxed{N = 31.58 \approx 32 \text{ ds/min}} \quad \textbf{[1.5 marks]}$$

**Step 3 — Machining time per stroke (ii)**

$$t_m = \frac{L'}{V_c \times 1000} = \frac{285}{18 \times 1000} = \frac{285}{18000}$$

$$\boxed{t_m = 0.01583 \text{ min/ds} = 0.95 \text{ s/ds}} \quad \textbf{[1.5 marks]}$$

**Step 4 — Total machining time (iii)**

$$T_{total} = n \times t_m = 2 \times 0.01583 = 0.0317 \text{ min} = 1.9 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} = 0.0317 \text{ min}} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 5 — MRR per minute**

$$MRR = L' \times d \times f \times N = 285 \times 2 \times 0.5 \times 32$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 9120 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 marks**

**Crank and Slotted Lever Quick-Return Mechanism:**

**Principle:** A rotating bull gear carries a crank pin that slides inside a slot in the lever. The lever oscillates about a fixed fulcrum, and the slider end of the lever connects to the ram through a connecting rod. As the bull gear rotates uniformly, the ram reciprocates with non-uniform velocity — slow during the cutting stroke and fast during the return stroke.

**Sequence of Operation:**
1. The bull gear rotates at uniform angular speed $\omega$.
2. The crank pin at radius $R$ describes a circle.
3. The slotted lever pivots about fulcrum $O$.
4. The slot in the lever is horizontal at the slider position; the crank pin slides along the slot.
5. When the crank pin is on the **front half** of its rotation, the lever is slowly displaced → **cutting stroke** (longer time, lower velocity).
6. When the crank pin is on the **rear half**, the lever returns quickly → **return stroke** (shorter time, higher velocity).

**Quick-return ratio** $Q = \dfrac{\alpha}{360 - \alpha}$ where $\alpha$ is the crank angle for cutting.

**Four Shaper Operations:**
1. Horizontal shaping of flat surfaces
2. Vertical shaping (slotting)
3. Angular shaping for inclined surfaces
4. Keyway cutting

**Sketch Description (text-based):**

```
Bull Gear (P) rotates → Crank Pin (A) at radius R
                       ↓
            Slotted Lever pivots at O
                       ↓
        Slider (S) moves horizontally → connects to Ram
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** A double-housing planer is used to machine a **workpiece of length 1500 mm**. The cutting speed is **20 m/min** and the table makes **8 double strokes per minute**. Calculate:
- (i) the **actual cutting speed** in m/min
- (ii) the **time per cutting stroke**
- (iii) the **total time** to remove a **10 mm depth of cut** in **5 passes**.
- (iv) the **MRR per minute**, assuming feed = **2 mm/stroke**. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** Explain the **principal parts of a double planer** with a neat diagram. State **any three advantages** of a double planer over a single planer. **[(7 marks)]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Workpiece length (stroke length) $L = 1500$ mm
- Assumed cutting speed $V_c = 20$ m/min
- $N = 8$ ds/min (actual)
- Total depth = 10 mm, 5 passes → $d = 2$ mm
- Feed $f = 2$ mm/ds

**Step 1 — Actual cutting speed (i)**

$$V_{c,actual} = \frac{2 \, L \, N}{1000} = \frac{2 \times 1500 \times 8}{1000} = \frac{24000}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{V_c = 24 \text{ m/min}} \quad \textbf{[1.5 marks]}$$

> **Note:** Since calculated $V_c \neq$ given 20 m/min, the actual $V_c$ is the calculated 24 m/min.

**Step 2 — Time per cutting stroke (ii)**

$$t_m = \frac{L}{V_c \times 1000} = \frac{1500}{24 \times 1000} = \frac{1500}{24000}$$

$$\boxed{t_m = 0.0625 \text{ min/ds} = 3.75 \text{ s/ds}} \quad \textbf{[1.5 marks]}$$

**Step 3 — Total time (iii)**

$$T_{total} = n \times t_m = 5 \times 0.0625 = 0.3125 \text{ min} = 18.75 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} = 18.75 \text{ seconds}} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 4 — MRR per minute (iv)**

$$MRR = L \times d \times f \times N = 1500 \times 2 \times 2 \times 8$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 48000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 marks**

**Principal Parts of a Double Planer:**

1. **Bed** — Heavy, ribbed cast iron base. Provides foundation and carries the table guide ways. It must be stress-relieved to prevent distortion.

2. **Two Work Tables** — Independent tables, each carries one large workpiece. The two tables reciprocate **in opposite directions** (one cuts while the other returns) — this is the key feature of a double planer.

3. **Cross Rail** — Central horizontal beam spanning across both tables. It is stationary and carries the tool heads. It can be **raised or lowered** to accommodate varying workpiece heights.

4. **Tool Heads** — Mounted on the cross rail. Each tool head has its own **vertical and horizontal feed** mechanism. Multiple tool heads can machine **top and side surfaces simultaneously**.

5. **Drive Mechanism** — Reverses the table motion at the end of each stroke. Older machines use **bull-gear with rack and pinion**; modern machines use **hydraulic drives** for smoother reversal and quick return.

6. **Feed Mechanism** — Provides incremental cross-feed and vertical-feed to the tool. Operates during the **return stroke** (idle stroke).

7. **Control Panel** — Houses electrical controls, starter, reversing switches, and stroke adjustment knobs.

**Three Advantages of Double Planer over Single Planer:**

1. **Higher Productivity** — Two workpieces are machined simultaneously, nearly doubling the output per unit time.
2. **Better Power Utilisation** — The motor is in continuous use (one table always cutting), so there are no idle return periods for the drive.
3. **Reduced Floor Space per Workpiece** — Two workstations in one machine footprint.
4. **Better Cutting Conditions** — While one table is in cutting stroke, the other is in quick return — the combined cycle is smoother.
5. **Lower Operator Idle Time** — Operator can load/unload on one table while cutting occurs on the other.

**Diagram (textual block description):**

```
        ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
        │   CROSS-RAIL (Carries Tool Heads)   │
        │    [Tool 1]        [Tool 2]         │
        └─────────────────────────────────────┘
        ┌──────────┐          ┌──────────┐
        │ TABLE 1  │          │ TABLE 2  │
        │  (Left)  │←-- Bed --│  (Right) │
        │  moves   │  with    │  moves   │
        │ forward  │  guide   │ backward │
        └──────────┘  ways    └──────────┘
```

---

### 5.1.2 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Examiner's Notes):**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $L'$ (adjusted stroke)** — using $L$ instead of $L + l_a + l_o$ will lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Unit confusion** — mixing m/min with mm. Always remember: $1 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}$. The division by 1000 in formulas is **not optional**.
> 3. **Confusing "double stroke" with "single stroke"** — A double stroke = 1 cutting + 1 return. The cutting speed formula has a factor of **2** for this reason.
> 4. **In planer problems**, the workpiece length **is** the stroke length — do not add approach/over-run separately unless stated.
> 5. **MRR formula error** — students often forget to multiply by $N$ (strokes/min) when asked for "MRR per minute." MRR per stroke = $L' \times d \times f$; MRR per minute = $L' \times d \times f \times N$.
> 6. **Sketching the quick-return mechanism** — always label the **bull gear, crank pin, slotted lever, fulcrum, slider, and ram**. Unlabelled sketches fetch zero marks in the diagram portion.
> 7. **In shaper vs planer comparison** — do not write vague answers. State specifically: *"In shaper, the tool reciprocates while the workpiece is stationary; in planer, the workpiece reciprocates while the tool is held stationary on a cross-rail."*
> 8. **Guide-way shaping** — remember to mention **scraping** as a final step for precision, not just shaping.

---

### 5.1.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 (Shaper & Planer)**

- [x] **Depth of cut (d)** is the perpendicular thickness of material removed per pass.
- [x] **Feed (f) in shaper/planer** is **per stroke** (mm/ds), not per minute.
- [x] **Cutting speed in shaper:** $V_c = \dfrac{2 L' N}{1000}$ m/min, where $L' = L + l_a + l_o$.
- [x] **Cutting speed in planer:** $V_c = \dfrac{2 L N}{1000}$ m/min.
- [x] **Machining time per stroke:** $t_m = \dfrac{L'}{V_c \times 1000}$ min (return stroke excluded).
- [x] **Quick-return ratio:** $V_{return} > V_{cutting}$, ratio typically 0.4–0.5.
- [x] **Crank and slotted lever** is the most common shaper quick-return mechanism.
- [x] **Whitworth quick-return** uses a different kinematic arrangement (spherical crank).
- [x] **Shaper:** tool moves, work stationary. **Planer:** work moves, tool stationary.
- [x] **Shaper** for small/medium work; **Planer** for large/heavy work.
- [x] **Double planer** has two tables moving in opposite directions — one always cutting.
- [x] **Shaper operations** include horizontal, vertical, angular, keyway, and form shaping.
- [x] **Guide ways** are classified as **flat-flat, V-flat, V-V, inverted-V**.
- [x] **Guide-way defects:** twist, bell-mouth, barrel-shape, concave, convex.
- [x] **Scraping** is the final manual finishing step for precision guide ways.
- [x] **MRR (shaper/planer)** = $L \times d \times f \times N$ mm³/min.
- [x] **Units conversion:** $1 \text{ m/min} = 1000 \text{ mm/min} = \dfrac{1000}{60}$ mm/s.
- [x] **Number of passes** = total depth ÷ depth per pass.
- [x] **Planer cutting force** is reacted by the heavy table, allowing deep cuts (up to 25 mm).
- [x] **Shaper feed mechanism** is automatic and operates during the return stroke.

> **Final Tip:** For KTU exams, always write the **given data, formula, substitution, and final answer in a box**. Examiners reward systematic presentation generously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planing of Guide Gibs, Slotter, Principal Parts of Slotter, Working of Slotter, Slotting of Keyways
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Planing of Guide Gibs, Slotter, Principal Parts of Slotter, Working of Slotter, Slotting of Keyways

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Planing of Guide Gibs

A **Guide Gib** is a precision-fitted metal strip (usually cast iron or hardened steel) attached to the sliding members of machine tools (lathes, milling machines, planers, etc.) to ensure accurate linear motion, eliminate play/vibration, and bear lateral cutting forces. The gib is adjustable via screws to take up wear over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *Planing of guide gibs* is a finishing operation carried out on a planer (or shaper) to produce flat, parallel, and square reference surfaces on the gibs that guide the slides of machine tool spindles, carriages, and tables. The gib is matched-scraped or machined to its mating slide such that the clearance is uniform across the entire length.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a sliding window in your house. The window moves up and down in a frame, but after years of use, the frame develops a wobble. A gib is like a small, adjustable "shim strip" that you slide into the frame and tighten with screws to remove the wobble. When you take a wobbly gib to a workshop, the machinist clamps it on a planer and shaves off microscopic layers until the contact surface is perfectly flat — like running a plane over a warped piece of wood until it lies straight on a granite table.

**Process Steps:**
1. Clamp the gib securely on the planer table.
2. Set the table stroke length and position using trip dogs.
3. Machine the bearing (sliding) surface in a single light pass.
4. Check for flatness with a surface plate and dial indicator.
5. Reverse the gib and machine the opposite face parallel.
6. Machine the ends square to the sliding face.

**Why planing is preferred for gibs:**
- Long, flat work-pieces require the **long horizontal stroke** of a planer (typically 600 mm – 2000 mm) which a shaper cannot provide.
- Single-pass finishing reduces distortion and gives a better surface finish.

## 1.2 Slotter (Slotting Machine)

A **Slotter** (also called a **vertical shaper** or **slotters**) is a reciprocating-type machine tool in which a **single-point cutting tool moves vertically** (i.e., in a straight-line up-and-down motion), while the work-piece is held on a horizontal table beneath it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU):** *A slotter is a reciprocating machine tool that uses a vertical ram-stroking action, driven by a Whitworth quick-return mechanism, to perform internal and external cutting operations on work-pieces held on a rotary/circular table. It is essentially a vertical-axis shaper.*

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine a hand-held chisel being held vertically and struck repeatedly with a hammer — that is essentially a slotter's action. The "hammer" is the **ram** (a heavy casting that slides up and down on a vertical column), and the "chisel" is the cutting tool mounted at the bottom of the ram. The work-piece sits on a small table below, just like the wood on your workbench. Unlike a drill that spins and bores, the slotter **slaps** metal away in straight lines.

**Distinguishing Feature of a Slotter vs. a Shaper:**
- Shaper → tool moves **horizontally** (in a horizontal plane), work is mounted on a horizontal table.
- Slotter → tool moves **vertically** (in a vertical plane), work is mounted on a horizontal table *below* the ram.

**Typical Applications of a Slotter:**
1. Cutting **internal keyways** in pulleys, gears, and shaft hubs.
2. Producing **internal slots, grooves, and splines**.
3. Machining **square or hexagonal holes**.
4. Surface machining of small flat areas.
5. Die and punch work.

## 1.3 Slotting of Keyways

A **keyway** is a longitudinal slot cut into a shaft or into the bore of a hub to accommodate a **key** (a small rectangular metal piece) that locks the shaft and the rotating element (gear, pulley, coupling) together to transmit torque.

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Slotting of keyways** is the operation of producing internal keyways (slots) in a shaft or in the bore of a hub using a slotter. The work-piece is mounted on the circular table of the slotter, and the reciprocating tool (a special keyway cutting tool) cuts the slot progressively through the full length of the keyway.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of how a key opens a door lock. The key is a small, shaped piece of metal. The "keyway" is the slot inside the lock cylinder that the key slides into. In machines, the shaft and gear are "locked" together by a metal key that fits into matching slots — one in the shaft and one in the gear's bore. The slotter is the tool used to make that internal slot in the gear's hub.

**Why slotter for keyways (not milling)?**
- For **closed keyways** (keyway that does not open to the end of the bore — e.g., in a gear hub), the milling cutter cannot enter. A slotter's vertical tool can plunge into a pre-drilled hole and cut the keyway.
- For **internal keyways on curved/contoured surfaces** (e.g., on a curved shaft), the slotter is more flexible.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Theory: Planing of Guide Gibs

### 2.1.1 Types of Gibs
1. **Flat Gib (Straight Gib)** — A plain rectangular strip. Common on lathe cross-slides and milling machine knees.
2. **Dovetail Gib** — Tapered trapezoidal cross-section. Used where heavy vertical loads exist, e.g., on the ram of a planer.
3. **V-Gib** — V-shaped cross-section for self-aligning motion under load.

### 2.1.2 Work-Holding and Alignment
The gib is clamped to the planer table using **step blocks, clamps, and bolts**. The setup must ensure:
- The bearing surface to be machined is **parallel to the planer table's direction of travel**.
- The ends are set at 90° using a try-square and dial indicator.
- The tool is set with minimum tool overhang to prevent chatter.

### 2.1.3 Tool Used
A **planing tool** (single-point HSS or carbide tool) is used. The tool has a small side-clearance angle (typically **6°–8°**) and a small nose radius to provide good surface finish.

### 2.1.4 Machining Parameters (Standard KTU Values)
- Cutting speed: **15 m/min** (cast iron), **25–30 m/min** (mild steel).
- Feed: **0.8 – 1.5 mm/stroke** (roughing), **0.2 – 0.4 mm/stroke** (finishing).
- Depth of cut: **1 – 3 mm** (roughing), **0.1 – 0.3 mm** (finishing).
- Surface finish obtained: **1.6 – 3.2 µm Ra**.

### 2.1.5 Inspection
- **Flatness** → tested on a surface plate with Prussian blue (scraping test).
- **Parallelism** → dial indicator between the two machined faces.
- **Squareness** → try-square or sine bar.
- **Surface finish** → Surftest (profilometer).

## 2.2 Detailed Theory: Principal Parts of a Slotter

A slotter consists of the following principal parts (each is critical from a KTU exam perspective):

### 2.2.1 Base
- Heavy cast iron bed of the machine.
- Houses the drive mechanism, coolant tank, and provides rigidity.
- Has a **circular table** mounted on top.

### 2.2.2 Column
- Large vertical hollow casting rising from the base.
- Houses the **Whitworth quick-return mechanism** (also called the crank-and slotted-lever mechanism).
- Carries the ram vertically.
- The **ram slides** on accurately machined guideways in the column.

### 2.2.3 Ram
- A heavy rectangular casting that **reciprocates vertically** inside the column.
- Carries the tool head (tool slide) at its lower end.
- The downward stroke is the **cutting stroke**; the upward stroke is the **idle/return stroke**.
- The stroke length is adjustable (typically **50 – 300 mm**).

### 2.2.4 Tool Head (Tool Slide)
- Mounted at the bottom of the ram.
- Allows the tool to be **swivelled** in any direction and fed at an angle.
- Has a clapper box to lift the tool on the return stroke.
- Tool feed can be **vertical, horizontal, or angular**.

### 2.2.5 Circular/Rotary Table
- Mounted on the base, directly below the ram.
- Can be **rotated** manually or automatically.
- Has **T-slots** for clamping the work-piece.
- Provides circular feed and indexing for machining curved surfaces.

### 2.2.6 Feed Mechanism
- Provides automatic cross-feed of the work-piece on the table (linear feed).
- Also rotates the circular table intermittently (rotary feed).
- Driven by a separate gear train and a crank/ratchet mechanism.

### 2.2.7 Drive Motor and Gearbox
- Electric motor drives the Bull Gear (large gear on the crank-shaft).
- Variable speed is achieved by **conical (step) pulleys** or a **multi-speed gear box**.

## 2.3 Detailed Theory: Working of a Slotter

### 2.3.1 Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism
The slotter uses a **Whitworth quick-return mechanism** (also called the *crank-and-slotted-lever* mechanism) to convert rotary motion of the bull gear into reciprocating motion of the ram. The **cutting stroke is slower** (more than 180° of crank rotation) and the **return stroke is faster** (less than 180°).

### 2.3.2 Kinematic Chain
- **Bull gear** (driven by motor through belts) rotates continuously.
- A **crank pin** on the bull gear is connected to a **slotted lever** by a **slide block**.
- The slotted lever is pivoted at a fixed point on the column.
- As the bull gear rotates, the crank pin moves the slide block up and down inside the slot, rocking the slotted lever about its pivot.
- The ram is connected to the slotted lever by a connecting link and reciprocates vertically.

### 2.3.3 Stroke Adjustment
Two adjustments are possible:
1. **Length of stroke** — by moving the crank pin along a **radial slot** in the bull gear. Pin closer to the centre → shorter stroke; pin near the rim → longer stroke.
2. **Position of stroke** — by moving the crank pin along a **circular slot** in the bull gear face, while keeping the radius constant. This shifts the top-dead-centre and bottom-dead-centre positions of the ram.

### 2.3.4 Cutting Action
- During the **downward stroke** of the ram, the tool cuts the metal.
- During the **upward stroke**, the tool is lifted clear of the work by the **clapper box** (so that it does not drag/rub on the freshly machined surface).
- The work-piece is advanced sideways by the **feed mechanism** at the end of each cutting stroke, so the next stroke cuts a new layer.

## 2.4 Detailed Theory: Slotting of Keyways

### 2.4.1 Types of Keyways
1. **Open Keyway** — The keyway extends to the end of the shaft or bore (open at one or both ends). Can be machined with a milling cutter (slitting saw / end mill) on a milling machine.
2. **Closed (Enclosed) Keyway** — The keyway is fully within the bore/shaft; it does not open to the end. **A slotter is essential for this type.**

### 2.4.2 Procedure for Slotting a Closed Keyway
1. **Drill a hole** equal to or slightly larger than the keyway width (this is the relief hole for the tool to enter).
2. Mount the work-piece (e.g., gear hub) on the circular table, centred with a dial indicator.
3. Set the **stroke length** so that the tool moves just below the top of the work-piece and just above the bottom of the keyway.
4. Set the **vertical position** of the ram so the cutting edge enters the pre-drilled hole.
5. Set the **feed rate** (typically 0.1 – 0.5 mm/stroke).
6. Run the machine — the tool plunges into the pre-drilled hole and progressively extends the slot (keyway) along the required length with each stroke.
7. After the slot reaches the desired length, the work-piece is rotated 180° to machine the opposite wall (if the tool cannot machine both walls in one pass).
8. Inspect the keyway with a plug gauge or key gauge.

### 2.4.3 Tool Used
- A **special keyway cutting tool** — long, thin, single-point tool resembling a boring bar.
- Made of **high-speed steel (HSS)**.
- Tool is mounted vertically in the tool holder of the ram.

### 2.4.4 Cutting Parameters
- Cutting speed: **10 – 20 m/min** (for mild steel and cast iron).
- Feed: **0.05 – 0.3 mm/stroke**.
- Stroke length: kept minimal to avoid idle travel.

### 2.4.5 Why a Slotter for Closed Keyways?
- A **milling cutter** (e.g., end mill) cannot enter an enclosed keyway from the side.
- A **broach** is faster but extremely expensive for small batches.
- A **slotter** is the most economical choice for **low-volume production** and **tool-room work**.

---

## KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises all key formulas, ratios, and standard values for Module 3 topics. **Bookmark this for ESE preparation.**

| # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Application |
|---|-----------|----------------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Quick return ratio (Q) | $Q = \dfrac{360 + 2\alpha}{360 - 2\alpha} = \dfrac{T_c}{T_r}$ | ratio | Whitworth mechanism, slotter, shaper |
| 2 | Crank rotation for cutting stroke | $\theta_c = 180° + 2\alpha$ | degrees | Slotter, shaper |
| 3 | Crank rotation for return stroke | $\theta_r = 180° - 2\alpha$ | degrees | Slotter, shaper |
| 4 | Average cutting speed (v) | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | Planer, slotter drive |
| 5 | Cutting time (T) | $T = \dfrac{L + l_c + l_r}{v \cdot f \cdot n}$ | min | Planing time estimate |
| 6 | Material removal rate (MRR) | $MRR = v \cdot f \cdot d$ | mm³/min | All machining |
| 7 | Feed in slotter | $f = 0.05$ to $0.3$ mm/stroke | mm/stroke | Keyway cutting |
| 8 | Length of stroke (slotter) | $L_s = 50$ to $300$ mm | mm | Adjustable |
| 9 | Quick return ratio (typical) | $Q \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ | — | Slotter standard |
| 10 | Cutting speed for planing CI | $v = 15$ m/min | m/min | Cast iron gibs |
| 11 | Cutting speed for planing MS | $v = 25 - 30$ m/min | m/min | Mild steel gibs |
| 12 | Depth of cut (finish) | $d = 0.1 - 0.3$ mm | mm | Finishing pass |
| 13 | Surface finish (planed) | $R_a = 1.6 - 3.2$ µm | µm | After planing |
| 14 | Stroke length (planer typical) | $L_p = 600 - 2000$ mm | mm | Long workpieces |

**Where:**
- $\alpha$ = inclination angle of the slotted lever (Whitworth mechanism)
- $T_c, T_r$ = time for cutting and return stroke
- $D$ = diameter of driving pulley
- $N$ = rpm of motor
- $L$ = length of workpiece
- $l_c$ = cutter approach length
- $l_r$ = cutter overrun length
- $f$ = feed per stroke
- $n$ = number of passes

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Quick-Return Ratio (Q) of the Whitworth Mechanism

The Whitworth quick-return mechanism is the heart of both the **shaper** and the **slotter**. KTU examiners love asking for the derivation of the **quick-return ratio**, so let's derive it in full.

### Setup
Consider the Whitworth mechanism:
- Bull gear (1) rotates with constant angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s.
- Crank pin (A) is at a distance $r$ from the centre of the bull gear.
- Slotted lever (2) oscillates about a fixed pivot O.
- The slide block in the slotted lever is connected to the crank pin A.
- $\alpha$ is the angle of inclination of the slotted lever from the vertical (or from the line OA') when the crank pin is at the inner dead-centre position.

```
        Ram (↑↓)
         │
         │
    ─────O─────  ← Pivot of slotted lever
         ╲α     (slotted lever inclined at angle α to vertical)
          ╲
           ╲
            ● A ← Crank pin (radial position r, rotating)
           ╱
          ╱
   (Bull gear rotating anticlockwise)
```

### Step 1: Define extreme positions of the crank pin
Let the crank pin be at the **topmost position (1)** and **bottommost position (2)** when the slotted lever is inclined at $+\alpha$ and $-\alpha$ respectively from the vertical line passing through the pivot O.

### Step 2: Crank rotation for cutting stroke
As the bull gear rotates, the crank pin moves from position 1 to position 2 (passing through both sides of the vertical). The total angle rotated by the crank during this movement is:

$$\theta_c = 180° + 2\alpha$$

**Explanation:** The crank must rotate through the **entire diameter** of its circular path (180°) **plus** the extra angle $\alpha$ to "clear" the upper dead-centre and **another** $\alpha$ to "reach" the lower dead-centre.

### Step 3: Crank rotation for return stroke
The crank then rotates from position 2 back to position 1:

$$\theta_r = 180° - 2\alpha$$

**Explanation:** The crank rotates through less than a semi-circle because the slot is "nearer" in this direction.

### Step 4: Quick-Return Ratio (Q)
Since the bull gear rotates at **constant angular velocity** $\omega$, the time taken for a stroke is proportional to the angle rotated.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Time for cutting stroke: } & T_c = \dfrac{\theta_c}{\omega} = \dfrac{180° + 2\alpha}{\omega} \\
\text{Time for return stroke: } & T_r = \dfrac{\theta_r}{\omega} = \dfrac{180° - 2\alpha}{\omega}
\end{aligned}$$

The **quick-return ratio** is defined as:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{T_c}{T_r} = \dfrac{\theta_c}{\theta_r} \\
Q &= \dfrac{180° + 2\alpha}{180° - 2\alpha} \\
Q &= \dfrac{360° + 2\alpha}{360° - 2\alpha} \quad \text{(multiplying numerator and denominator by 2)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example
If $\alpha = 30°$, then:

$$Q = \dfrac{180 + 2(30)}{180 - 2(30)} = \dfrac{240}{120} = 2.0$$

So the cutting stroke takes **twice as long** as the return stroke. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Cutting Time for Planing a Guide Gib

Let a guide gib of length $L$ be planed in a single pass. The planer table moves in a straight line with a cutting speed $v$ during the forward stroke and a quick-return speed $v_r$ during the return stroke.

### Step 1: Length of the cutting stroke
The cutting stroke must cover:
- Approach length $l_c$ (tool travels before engaging the work)
- Length of the gib $L$
- Overrun length $l_r$ (tool travels after exiting the work)

$$S_c = L + l_c + l_r$$

### Step 2: Time per stroke

$$\begin{aligned}
T_c &= \dfrac{S_c}{v} \quad \text{(cutting stroke time)} \\
T_r &= \dfrac{S_c}{v_r} \quad \text{(return stroke time)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Total cycle time per pass
Assuming the same distance is traversed in the return stroke (most planers reverse the table over the same length):

$$T = T_c + T_r = \dfrac{S_c}{v} + \dfrac{S_c}{v_r}$$

### Step 4: Number of passes required
If the depth of cut per pass is $d$ and the total depth to be removed is $D$:

$$n = \dfrac{D}{d}$$

### Step 5: Total machining time

$$T_{total} = n \times \left( \dfrac{S_c}{v} + \dfrac{S_c}{v_r} \right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Cutting Time for Slotting a Keyway

For slotting a keyway of length $L_k$ in a hub:
- Tool stroke length: $L_s$
- Feed per stroke: $f$ mm/stroke
- Number of strokes to cover the keyway length:

$$N_{strokes} = \dfrac{L_k}{f}$$

- Time per stroke (cutting) = $T_c$
- Time per stroke (return) = $T_r$

$$T_{total} = N_{strokes} \times (T_c + T_r) = \dfrac{L_k}{f} \times \dfrac{L_s}{v} \times (1 + Q)$$

Where $Q = T_c/T_r$ is the quick-return ratio.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Planer vs. Shaper vs. Slotter

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is **highly favourite** for KTU Part A questions (3-markers). Memorise the differences.

| Feature | Planer | Shaper | Slotter |
|---------|--------|--------|---------|
| Tool motion | **Horizontal** (reciprocating) | **Horizontal** (reciprocating) | **Vertical** (reciprocating) |
| Work-holding | **Table moves**; tool stationary | **Table stationary**; tool moves | **Table stationary** (circular); tool moves vertically |
| Workpiece size | **Large / heavy** (up to 10 tonnes) | **Small / medium** | **Small** (fits on table) |
| Stroke length | **Long** (600 – 2000 mm) | Short (300 – 700 mm) | Medium (50 – 300 mm) |
| Quick-return mechanism | Hydraulic / crank | Whitworth / crank | Whitworth (crank-and-slotted-lever) |
| Typical operation | Flat surfaces on long workpieces | Slots, keyways, flat surfaces | Internal keyways, splines, dies |
| Power | **High** (5 – 25 kW) | Low (1.5 – 5 kW) | Low (1 – 3 kW) |
| Drive | Bull gear + hydraulic | Bull gear + Whitworth | Whitworth + bull gear |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A guide gib of length **500 mm** is to be planed on a planer. The cutting speed during the forward stroke is **20 m/min** and the return stroke is **40 m/min**. The approach and overrun lengths are **30 mm** each. The total depth of cut is **4 mm** and the depth per pass is **1 mm**. Calculate the total machining time.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $L = 500$ mm
- $v_c = 20$ m/min = **333.33 mm/s**
- $v_r = 40$ m/min = **666.67 mm/s**
- $l_c = l_r = 30$ mm
- $D = 4$ mm, $d = 1$ mm → $n = 4$ passes

**Step 1: Length of cutting stroke**

$$S_c = L + l_c + l_r = 500 + 30 + 30 = 560 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Time per forward stroke**

$$T_c = \dfrac{560}{333.33} = 1.68 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Time per return stroke**

$$T_r = \dfrac{560}{666.67} = 0.84 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Time per cycle**

$$T = T_c + T_r = 1.68 + 0.84 = 2.52 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Number of passes**

$$n = \dfrac{4}{1} = 4 \text{ passes}$$

**Step 6: Total machining time**

$$T_{total} = 4 \times 2.52 = 10.08 \text{ seconds} = 0.168 \text{ min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer: $T_{total} \approx 10.08$ seconds.**

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Slotter

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional architecture** of a slotter — i.e., how power flows from the motor through the various sub-systems to perform the slotting action.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Electric Motor] --> B[Conical Step Pulley / Gearbox]
    B --> C[Variable Speed Output Shaft]
    C --> D[Bull Gear with Radial Crank Pin]
    D --> E[Crank Pin Rotation]
    E --> F[Slide Block in Slotted Lever]
    F --> G[Slotted Lever Oscillates about Pivot]
    G --> H[Connecting Link]
    H --> I[Vertical Ram Reciprocates]
    I --> J[Tool Head at Ram End]
    J --> K[Cutting Tool Engages Workpiece]
    L[Feed Gear Train] --> M[Table Feed Drive]
    M --> N[Circular/Rotary Table]
    N --> O[Workpiece Positioned & Indexed]
    P[Clapper Box on Tool Head] --> Q[Lifts Tool on Return Stroke]
    C -.->|Power take-off| L
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows show **power flow** for the cutting motion.
- The dotted arrow shows the **power take-off** to the feed mechanism.
- Note how the **crank-and-slotted-lever (Whitworth)** is the central kinematic element.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Operational Sequence of Slotting a Keyway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Drill relief hole in hub] --> B[Step 2: Mount hub on circular table]
    B --> C[Step 3: Centre hub using dial indicator]
    C --> D[Step 4: Set stroke length on bull gear]
    D --> E[Step 5: Set vertical position of ram]
    E --> F[Step 6: Set feed rate on feed dial]
    F --> G[Step 7: Start machine - tool plunges into relief hole]
    G --> H[Step 8: Tool reciprocates, extends slot per stroke]
    H --> I{Keyway length complete?}
    I -->|No| H
    I -->|Yes| J[Step 9: Stop machine, retract tool]
    J --> K[Step 10: Inspect keyway with plug gauge]
    K --> L{Pass quality check?}
    L -->|No| M[Re-machine or scrap]
    L -->|Yes| N[Operation Complete]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Planer Working Principle (for Gib Planing context)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electric Motor] --> B[Bull Gear Drive]
    B --> C[Crank & Slotted Lever]
    C --> D[Table Reciprocates Horizontally]
    D --> E[Gib clamped on Table moves past Stationary Tool]
    E --> F[Tool Holder with Carbide Insert]
    F --> G[Cuts Flat Surface on Gib]
    H[Coolant Pump] -.->|Flood| G
    I[Feed Mechanism] -.->|Cross-feed| D
    J[Stroke Adjustment] -.->|Changes| C
```

## 4.4 Schematic Sketch: Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[(Bull Gear)] --> B[Crank Pin A on Radial Slot]
    B --> C[Slide Block slides in Lever Slot]
    C --> D[Slotted Lever pivots at fixed point O]
    D --> E[Link connects Lever to Ram]
    E --> F[Ram moves up and down in column]
    F --> G[Tool at Ram End cuts workpiece]
    A -.Rotates at constant ω.-> B
    D -.Oscillates through angle 2α.-> E
```

**Component Legend:**
- **Bull Gear** → the main driving gear mounted on the motor shaft.
- **Crank Pin A** → adjustable pin on the bull-gear face.
- **Slide Block** → small block fitted in the slot of the lever, riding on the crank pin.
- **Slotted Lever** → long lever with a straight slot, pivoted at O.
- **Connecting Link** → rigid link from the end of the slotted lever to the ram.
- **Ram** → vertical reciprocating member carrying the tool.

> [!NOTE]
> The angle $2\alpha$ through which the slotted lever oscillates equals **twice the inclination** of the slot relative to the vertical line at the inner dead-centre. This angle $2\alpha$ is what determines the quick-return ratio.

## 4.5 Tool-Work Geometry: Slotting a Keyway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hub/Gear] --> B[Pre-drilled Relief Hole]
    B --> C[Slotter's Vertical Ram]
    C --> D[Long Thin HSS Keyway Tool]
    D --> E[Tool plunges down into hole]
    E --> F[Cuts one wall of keyway]
    G[Cross-feed of Table] --> H[Tool cuts second wall]
    H --> I[Internal Keyway formed]
    J[Clapper Box] --> K[Lifts tool on return stroke - prevents rubbing]
```
