# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a slotter? How is it different from a shaper?**

**Model Answer:**

A **slotter** (or vertical shaper) is a reciprocating machine tool in which the **single-point cutting tool moves vertically** (i.e., in a straight-line up-and-down motion), while the work-piece is held stationary on a circular table beneath the tool. The ram's reciprocating motion is produced by a **Whitworth quick-return mechanism**.

| Feature | Shaper | Slotter |
|---------|--------|---------|
| Tool movement | Horizontal | **Vertical** |
| Work-piece mounting | Horizontal table (stationary) | Circular table (stationary) |
| Typical operation | Flat surfaces, external slots | **Internal keyways**, splines, dies |

The slotter is essentially a shaper rotated by 90°. ✓ **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a guide gib. Why is it necessary in machine tools?**

**Model Answer:**

A **guide gib** is a precision-machined metal strip (usually cast iron or hardened steel) fitted between the sliding members of a machine tool (such as the cross-slide of a lathe or the knee of a milling machine) to:
1. Ensure accurate linear motion,
2. Eliminate play/backlash,
3. Bear lateral cutting forces,
4. Be adjustable to compensate for wear.

It is necessary because machine slides develop wear and clearance over time, leading to **inaccurate machining and chatter**. The gib restores precision and absorbs vibrations. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries **14 marks** and offers **internal choice** between two questions from the same module. Below are two complete alternative questions.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **principal parts of a slotter**. Explain the function of the Whitworth quick-return mechanism used in it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A slotter is used to cut a **closed keyway of length 60 mm** in a cast-iron hub. The cutting speed is **15 m/min**, feed is **0.2 mm/stroke**, and the quick-return ratio is **1.8**. The stroke length is 80 mm. Calculate the **total machining time** for the keyway.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Principal Parts of a Slotter** (refer to Section 2.2 of these notes for details):

1. **Base** — heavy cast-iron bed that houses the drive and supports the table.
2. **Column** — vertical hollow casting carrying the ram and the Whitworth mechanism.
3. **Ram** — vertical reciprocating member that carries the tool head.
4. **Tool head (tool slide)** — swivelling holder at the bottom of the ram; carries the cutting tool and has a clapper box to lift the tool on return stroke.
5. **Circular/Rotary table** — mounted on the base; provides rotary feed and indexing.
6. **Feed mechanism** — automatic cross-feed of the table; driven from the main drive.
7. **Drive motor and gear box** — provides power and variable speed via conical pulleys.
8. **Starter and control panel** — for ON/OFF and speed selection.

**Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism:**

The Whitworth quick-return mechanism consists of a **bull gear**, **crank pin**, **slide block**, **slotted lever**, and **connecting link**.

**Function:**
- Converts the **continuous rotary motion** of the bull gear into the **oscillating motion** of the slotted lever, which in turn drives the ram in **reciprocating vertical motion**.
- The **cutting stroke is slower** (180° + 2α of crank rotation) and the **return stroke is faster** (180° − 2α), thus saving idle time and improving productivity.

**Quick-return ratio:**

$$Q = \dfrac{180° + 2\alpha}{180° - 2\alpha} = \dfrac{T_c}{T_r}$$

For $\alpha = 30°$, $Q = 240°/120° = 2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $L_k = 60$ mm (keyway length)
- $v = 15$ m/min = **250 mm/s**
- $f = 0.2$ mm/stroke
- $Q = 1.8$
- $L_s = 80$ mm (stroke length)

**Step 1: Number of strokes**

$$N_{strokes} = \dfrac{L_k}{f} = \dfrac{60}{0.2} = 300 \text{ strokes}$$

**Step 2: Time per cutting stroke**

$$T_c = \dfrac{L_s}{v} = \dfrac{80}{250} = 0.32 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Time per return stroke**

From $Q = \dfrac{T_c}{T_r} = 1.8$:

$$T_r = \dfrac{T_c}{1.8} = \dfrac{0.32}{1.8} = 0.1778 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Time per cycle**

$$T_{cycle} = T_c + T_r = 0.32 + 0.1778 = 0.4978 \text{ s/stroke}$$

**Step 5: Total machining time**

$$T_{total} = 300 \times 0.4978 = 149.33 \text{ s} \approx 2.49 \text{ min}$$

> **Final Answer: $T_{total} \approx 149.33$ seconds or 2.49 min.** **(7 marks)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a sketch the **planing operation of guide gibs**. List the parameters used and the typical surface finish obtained.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **quick-return ratio** of a Whitworth quick-return mechanism. A slotter uses a Whitworth mechanism with the inclination angle $\alpha = 25°$. Calculate the **cutting stroke time** if the ram moves with an average speed of **0.4 m/s** during cutting and the crank rotates at **60 rpm**.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Planing of Guide Gibs:**

A guide gib is clamped on the planer table using step blocks, clamps, and bolts. The planer table reciprocates horizontally, and a single-point cutting tool (mounted on the tool post of the planer) shaves the gib surface in a series of straight-line passes.

**Setup Procedure:**
1. **Mark the gib** to indicate the surfaces to be machined.
2. **Clamp the gib** on the planer table, ensuring that the bearing surface to be machined is parallel to the table's direction of travel.
3. **Check alignment** with a dial indicator and try-square.
4. **Set the stroke length** so that the tool engages the work and exits cleanly.
5. **Set the cutting speed and feed** as per the material.

**Cutting Parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Cutting speed (CI) | **15 m/min** |
| Cutting speed (MS) | **25–30 m/min** |
| Feed (roughing) | **0.8 – 1.5 mm/stroke** |
| Feed (finishing) | **0.2 – 0.4 mm/stroke** |
| Depth of cut (rough) | **1 – 3 mm** |
| Depth of cut (finish) | **0.1 – 0.3 mm** |
| Surface finish | **1.6 – 3.2 µm Ra** |

**Inspection:** flatness is checked on a surface plate using a dial indicator; squareness with a try-square. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Derivation of Quick-Return Ratio:**

Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete derivation. The key results are:

$$\theta_c = 180° + 2\alpha$$
$$\theta_r = 180° - 2\alpha$$
$$Q = \dfrac{180° + 2\alpha}{180° - 2\alpha}$$

**Numerical Problem:**

**Given:**
- $\alpha = 25°$
- Average cutting speed = $v = 0.4$ m/s = **400 mm/s**
- Crank speed $N = 60$ rpm → angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N/60 = 2\pi(60)/60 = 2\pi$ rad/s = **6.283 rad/s**
- Quick-return ratio $Q$:

$$Q = \dfrac{180 + 2(25)}{180 - 2(25)} = \dfrac{230}{130} = 1.769$$

**Step 1: Total cycle time per revolution**

$$T_{cycle} = \dfrac{60}{N} = \dfrac{60}{60} = 1.0 \text{ s/rev}$$

**Step 2: Cutting stroke time**

$$T_c = \dfrac{T_{cycle} \times \theta_c}{360°} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 230°}{360°} = 0.639 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Return stroke time**

$$T_r = \dfrac{T_{cycle} \times \theta_r}{360°} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 130°}{360°} = 0.361 \text{ s}$$

**Verification using quick-return ratio:**

$$\dfrac{T_c}{T_r} = \dfrac{0.639}{0.361} = 1.77 \approx Q \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Length of the cutting stroke (using $T_c$ and $v$)**

$$L_s = v \times T_c = 0.4 \times 0.639 = 0.2556 \text{ m} = 255.6 \text{ mm}$$

> **Final Answer: $T_c = 0.639$ s per cutting stroke.** **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Stroke length confusion:** Many students confuse the *length of the cutting stroke* (distance travelled by ram) with the *angular displacement of the crank* (180° + 2α). Both are linked but are different quantities.
> 2. **Whitworth vs. crank-and slotted-lever naming:** Some students write "crank and slotted lever" instead of "Whitworth". Both are acceptable, but writing "Whitworth quick-return mechanism" gets full credit in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Keyway slotting mistake:** Forgetting to mention the **pre-drilled relief hole** for closed keyways costs 1–2 marks. Examiners expect it.
> 4. **Planer vs. shaper mix-up:** Always specify the **tool travel direction** (planer tool is stationary, table moves; shaper tool moves, table is stationary). This is a frequently tested distinction.
> 5. **Unit conversion in numericals:** Convert m/min to mm/s **before** dividing; examiners deduct 1 mark for unit errors in KTU papers.
> 6. **Quick-return ratio formula:** It is the ratio of *cutting time* to *return time* ($T_c/T_r$), not the inverse. Memorise this carefully.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist for Module 3 — use this 5 minutes before the exam.**

### 🔹 Planing of Guide Gibs
- ✅ Guide gib = precision strip that takes up clearance between sliding members of a machine tool.
- ✅ Planed on a planer to ensure flatness (1.6 – 3.2 µm Ra) and parallelism.
- ✅ Cutting speed: 15 m/min (CI), 25–30 m/min (MS).
- ✅ Feed: 0.8–1.5 mm/stroke (roughing); 0.2–0.4 mm/stroke (finishing).
- ✅ Inspected on a **surface plate** with a dial indicator and **Prussian blue** scraping test.

### 🔹 Slotter — Basic Facts
- ✅ Slotter = **vertical shaper**; tool reciprocates **vertically**.
- ✅ Work-piece is held on a **circular/rotary table** beneath the ram.
- ✅ Primary application: **internal keyways, splines, dies**.
- ✅ Quick-return mechanism: **Whitworth** (crank-and-slotted-lever).

### 🔹 Principal Parts of a Slotter
- ✅ **Base, Column, Ram, Tool Head, Circular Table, Feed Mechanism, Drive Motor + Gearbox, Control Panel.**
- ✅ The **clapper box** on the tool head lifts the tool on the return stroke.
- ✅ The **stroke length** is adjusted by moving the crank pin on the radial slot of the bull gear.

### 🔹 Working of a Slotter
- ✅ Motor → bull gear (rotates) → crank pin → slide block → slotted lever (oscillates) → connecting link → ram (reciprocates).
- ✅ Cutting stroke = 180° + 2α of crank rotation; return stroke = 180° − 2α.
- ✅ Quick-return ratio $Q = (180 + 2\alpha)/(180 - 2\alpha)$.

### 🔹 Slotting of Keyways
- ✅ Closed keyways (enclosed) **require a slotter** — milling cutters cannot enter.
- ✅ A **relief hole** must be pre-drilled for the tool to enter.
- ✅ Tool: long, thin, single-point **HSS keyway cutter**.
- ✅ Feed: 0.05–0.3 mm/stroke; cutting speed: 10–20 m/min.
- ✅ Inspected with a **plug gauge** or **key gauge**.

### 🔹 Numerical Formulae to Memorise
- ✅ $Q = \dfrac{180 + 2\alpha}{180 - 2\alpha}$
- ✅ $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{60}{N}$ (in seconds)
- ✅ $T_c = \dfrac{T_{cycle} \times (180 + 2\alpha)}{360}$
- ✅ $T_{total} = N_{strokes} \times (T_c + T_r)$
- ✅ $N_{strokes} = L_k / f$

### 🔹 Examiner Hot-Spots
- ✅ Comparison table of **Planer vs. Shaper vs. Slotter** — almost guaranteed 3-mark question.
- ✅ Sketch of the **Whitworth quick-return mechanism** with labelled parts — appears every year.
- ✅ Numerical on **cutting time** for planing or keyway slotting.
- ✅ **Definition of guide gib** and reasons for planing it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Twist Drill Geometry, Radial Drilling Machine, Calculation of Machining Time, Boring Machine, Features, Operations, Counter Boring, Spot Facing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Twist Drill Geometry, Radial Drilling Machine, Calculation of Machining Time, Boring Machine, Features, Operations, Counter Boring, Spot Facing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Twist Drill — Formal Definition

A **twist drill** is a rotary end-cutting tool with **two cutting edges** and **two helical flutes** used principally for producing cylindrical holes in solid metal. It is the most common hole-making tool used on drilling machines, lathes, and machining centers. The standard KTU 2024 syllabus covers its nomenclature, geometry, and the influence of its angles on the cutting performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPET403 – Module 4):**
> A twist drill is characterized by elements such as **point angle, lip clearance angle, helix angle, web, lands, flutes, margin, chisel edge, and cutting lips**. Students must be able to **sketch and label** all these elements and explain the function of each.

### 1.2 Main Parts of a Twist Drill

1. **Shank** — the rear cylindrical/tapered portion held in the machine spindle. May be *straight* or *tapered* (Morse taper).
2. **Body (Fluted portion)** — the working portion containing the cutting edges, flutes, lands, and web.
3. **Neck** — the narrow portion between the shank and body, used for grinding the drill and for manufacturer markings.
4. **Point (Drill Tip)** — the cone-shaped cutting end formed by the two cutting lips.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Drill is a Woodpecker with a Twist"

Imagine a **woodpecker** pecking a tree trunk straight in. The bird's beak is the **cutting lip**, the head-motion is the **feed**, and the rotating spin of the drill body mimics how the woodpecker twists its head to remove wood chips efficiently. The **flutes** are the drill's "throat" — they evacuate the wood chips upward, just as a woodpecker throws debris out. The **helix angle** of the flute is like the angle of the bird's stroke — too steep and it won't dig in; too shallow and chips will clog the throat. This is why twist drill geometry is essentially engineered **biomechanics of chip evacuation**.

### 1.4 Core Twist Drill Geometry — Formal Nomenclature

| Element | Definition | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Point Angle (2θ)** | Angle between the two cutting lips projected on a plane through the drill axis | **118°** (general steel), 135° (hardened steel), 90° (soft materials) |
| **Helix Angle (α)** | Angle between the flute edge and drill axis | **24° to 32°** (general) — low for hard, high for soft |
| **Lip Clearance Angle (γ)** | Angle between the cutting lip and a tangent to the work surface at the cutting edge | **8°–12°** |
| **Rake Angle (αᵣ)** | Angle of the flute face relative to a radial line through the cutting lip | Negative, related to helix angle |
| **Chisel Edge** | The straight edge connecting the two cutting lips at the centre of the drill | — |
| **Web (Core)** | The central column of metal that joins the flutes; gives strength | Thicker at point, thinner at shank |
| **Land (Margin)** | The narrow cylindrical surface behind the cutting edge that guides the drill in the hole | — |
| **Flute** | The helical groove that provides cutting edge, chip space, and chip evacuation | — |
| **Body Clearance (Relief)** | The reduced diameter behind the margin to reduce friction | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** The **point angle of 118°** is the universal default because it produces roughly equal **thrust force and torque** when drilling mild steel. The KTU examiner often tests *why 118°* is chosen for general-purpose drilling.

### 1.5 Radial Drilling Machine — Definition

A **radial drilling machine** is a heavy-duty drilling machine in which the **spindle head** is mounted on a **radial arm** that can be **swung, raised, lowered, and rotated** about a vertical column. This allows the spindle to be positioned at any point within its working radius — making it ideal for drilling, boring, reaming, tapping, and counterboring on **large, heavy, or awkwardly shaped workpieces** that cannot be conveniently moved on a table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** A radial drilling machine is preferred over a pillar/bench drilling machine **only when the workpiece is too large or too heavy to be moved** to align the hole under a fixed spindle.

### 1.6 Intuitive Analogy — "A Lamp on a Crane"

Think of a **radial arm drill** as an **articulated desk lamp**: the lamp-shade (spindle head) sits on a horizontal arm (radial arm) that can pivot around a vertical pole (column), swing left-right, and slide up-down. You can direct a beam (the drill) to *any point on the desk* (workpiece) without moving the desk. This is precisely the advantage of a radial drill over a pillar drill — **workpiece stays, spindle moves**.

### 1.7 Boring Machine — Definition

A **boring machine** is a machine tool that **enlarges an existing hole** to a precise diameter, accurate position, and fine surface finish using a **single-point cutting tool** mounted on a rotating spindle or fed into a rotating workpiece. The two principal types are:

- **Vertical Boring Machine (VBM) / Vertical Turret Lathe (VTL)** — large, rotating horizontal worktable; tool is fed vertically/radially.
- **Horizontal Boring Machine (HBM)** — horizontal spindle; worktable moves in X-Y.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Boring and not Drilling?** Drilling produces holes whose *diameter accuracy, roundness, and straightness* are limited by the drill itself. Boring uses a *single-point tool* to **finish** the hole to close tolerances (IT6–IT7) and superior surface finish (Ra 0.8–1.6 µm).

### 1.8 Counter Boring and Spot Facing — Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both operations **enlarge the top of an existing drilled hole** to seat a bolt head, nut, or washer flat against the workpiece.

- **Counter Boring** — Enlarging a drilled hole to a **predetermined depth** so that a socket-head cap screw (Allen bolt) or fillister-head screw sits flush with or below the surface.
- **Spot Facing** — A **very shallow counter bore**, used merely to create a **flat seat** on a rough, curved, or uneven surface so that a washer or nut bears evenly.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *Counter Bore = Counter Bore* (both "Bore" start with B → deep). *Spot Face = Surface finishing* (Shallow & Flat). **Counter-bore is deep; Spot-face is shallow.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Detailed Twist Drill Geometry — Element-by-Element Analysis

#### A. The Point (Drill Tip)
- The **conical point** is ground on the drill tip. The two **cutting lips** meet at the **chisel edge**.
- The **point angle 2θ** determines the geometry of chip formation. A *larger* point angle (e.g., 135°) produces:
  - Thinner chips,
  - Lower feed force,
  - Better tool life in **hard materials**.
- A *smaller* point angle (e.g., 90°) is used for **soft, ductile materials** (aluminium, brass) where chip flow is easy.

#### B. Lip Clearance Angle
- The **lip clearance angle (γ)** is the angle ground *behind* the cutting lip, measured in a plane containing the drill axis and a line perpendicular to the cutting edge.
- If γ is **too small** → drill rubs on the workpiece (friction, heat, rapid wear).
- If γ is **too large** → cutting edge is weak, chips break, drill fails.
- **Standard value: 8°–12°** (KTU high-yield).

#### C. Helix Angle and Rake Angle
- The **helix angle α** is the angle the flute edge makes with the drill axis. It is the angle of **inclination of the cutting face**.
- The **true rake angle αᵣ** of the cutting edge = **helix angle α − (point angle/2)**
- For standard point angle 118° and helix angle 30°:
  - Rake angle αᵣ = 30° − 59° = **−29°** (negative rake).
- This is why a standard twist drill is a **negative-rake tool** → it *squeezes* the metal rather than *shears* it, producing a thick, curled chip under high thrust force.

#### D. Flutes
- Flutes are **helical grooves** that perform three functions:
  1. Form the **cutting edge**,
  2. Provide space for **chip accommodation**,
  3. Allow **chip evacuation** (fluid flow) up the flute.

#### E. Web, Core, and Chisel Edge
- The **web (core)** is the central column of metal.
- It is **thickest at the point** (≈ 0.125 × D) to provide strength, and **thinnest at the shank** for flexibility.
- The **chisel edge** is the short, straight edge connecting the two lips. It does **no cutting** — it merely *squeezes* metal and accounts for **about 50% of the total thrust force**. This is why deep-hole drills often have a *split point* or *crowned chisel edge* to reduce thrust.

#### F. Margins and Body Clearance
- The **margin (land)** is a narrow cylindrical strip that **guides the drill** in the hole, ensuring hole straightness.
- **Body clearance** is the small reduction in diameter *behind* the margin so that only the margin rubs on the hole wall, not the whole drill body.

### 2.2 Radial Drilling Machine — Detailed Anatomy

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Base** | Heavy foundation casting supporting the column |
| **Column** | Vertical pillar on which the arm rotates |
| **Radial Arm** | Horizontal arm carrying the spindle head; can rotate 360° around column |
| **Spindle Head** | Houses the spindle, feed mechanism, and motor |
| **Spindle** | Rotates the drill and provides axial feed |
| **Elevating Screw / Motor** | Raises/lowers the radial arm along the column |
| **Sleeve / Quill** | Carries the spindle; the feed mechanism moves it axially |
| **Work Table** | Adjustable; often rotates about the column base |
| **Coolant System** | Flood coolant to drill point |

**Operations performed on a radial drilling machine:**
- Drilling
- Reaming
- Tapping
- Counter boring
- Spot facing
- Boring (light)

**Advantages over a pillar drill:**
- Can drill **multiple holes** on a large workpiece at **different locations** without moving the workpiece.
- Spindle can be positioned **anywhere within the working radius**.
- Suitable for **heavy, large castings, weldments, and frames**.

### 2.3 Boring Machine — Detailed Features

#### Vertical Boring Machine (VBM / VTL)
- **Rotating horizontal chuck/worktable** — large, capable of holding huge workpieces (up to several metres in diameter).
- **Vertical spindle** carrying the boring bar / tool.
- **Cross slide and vertical slide** for radial and vertical tool feed.
- **Turret** on the cross slide can hold multiple tools.
- **Coolant** delivered through a flood nozzle.

#### Horizontal Boring Machine (HBM)
- **Horizontal spindle** carrying the boring bar.
- **Worktable** moves in **X (longitudinal) and Y (transverse)**.
- **Spindle head moves vertically (Z)** on the column.
- May have a **rotary table** for indexing.
- More versatile than VBM for medium-sized work.

#### Jig Borer / Jig Boring Machine
- A **precision HBM** used for **jig and fixture work** and accurate hole location (positional accuracy ≈ 0.005 mm).
- Uses **precision lead screws and measuring systems** (often with optical/electronic scales).

### 2.4 Boring Operations
1. **Boring** — enlarging an existing hole with a single-point tool.
2. **Facing** — machining a flat surface perpendicular to the rotation axis (e.g., the top face of a flange).
3. **External Turning (Turning on a VBM)** — turning external diameters using a side tool.
4. **Drilling** — central drilling operation.
5. **Taper Boring** — boring a conical surface.
6. **Profile Boring / Form Boring** — using a form tool to generate a non-circular profile.

### 2.5 Counter Boring vs. Spot Facing — Comparative Analysis

| Feature | Counter Boring | Spot Facing |
|---|---|---|
| **Depth** | Significant (to accommodate bolt head) | Very shallow (just a flat seat) |
| **Tool** | Counter-bore tool with a pilot | Spot-face tool with a pilot |
| **Typical Use** | Allen bolt / socket head cap screw recess | Flat seating surface for nut/washer |
| **Surface finish** | Required (bearing surface) | Required (bearing surface) |
| **Tolerance** | IT10–IT11 | IT11–IT12 |
| **Pilot diameter** | Slightly *smaller* than drilled hole | Same — to follow drilled hole accurately |
| **Bottom of recess** | Flat and square | Flat and square |

### 2.6 ⭐ KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Drill point approach length** | $L_{app} = \dfrac{D}{2} \tan(\theta)$ | D = drill diameter, 2θ = point angle. For 118°, L_app ≈ 0.3·D |
| 2 | **Approximation (118° point)** | $L_{app} \approx 0.3 D$ | Used in KTU numericals |
| 3 | **Total drill travel length** | $L = L_{app} + L_{hole} + L_{overtravel}$ | Overtravel ≈ 1–3 mm |
| 4 | **Spindle speed for drilling** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi D}$ | V in m/min, D in mm, N in rpm |
| 5 | **Feed per revolution** | $f = f_r$ (mm/rev) | From tool chart |
| 6 | **Feed rate** | $F = f \cdot N$ (mm/min) | — |
| 7 | **Drilling machining time** | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | minutes |
| 8 | **Drilling MRR (volume)** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot f \cdot N$ | mm³/min |
| 9 | **Drilling power (gross)** | $P_g = \dfrac{F_c \, V}{60\,000}$ | kW, F_c in N |
| 10 | **Drilling thrust force (approx.)** | $F_t = K_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot f$ | K_s = specific cutting force (N/mm²) |
| 11 | **Drilling torque** | $T = \dfrac{F_t \cdot D}{2}$ | N·m |
| 12 | **Boring time** | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | same form as drilling, but D = finished hole diameter |
| 13 | **Counter bore depth** | $L_{cb}$ (mm) | Per design / bolt head |
| 14 | **Spot-face depth** | $L_{sf} \approx 1.5$ to 3 mm | Just enough to flatten |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** Students often forget the **approach length (≈ 0.3 D)** for a 118° point angle. The drill must *travel* this extra length before the cylindrical body of the drill engages the workpiece. Forgetting this gives a **higher cutting time error** in the answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall #2:** The drilling **feed f** in formula $T_m = L/(fN)$ is **feed per revolution (mm/rev)**, NOT feed rate (mm/min). The **fN** product gives feed rate, but dividing length by feed rate gives the same time.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Twist drills** are used in nearly every manufactured part — automotive, aerospace, construction, electronics.
- **Radial drilling machines** are essential in heavy-engineering (shipbuilding, boilers, bridges, mining equipment) where a part may weigh several tonnes.
- **Boring machines** are critical for **engine cylinder blocks, gear housings, bearing seats, hydraulic manifolds** — anywhere hole-to-hole positional accuracy and surface finish matter.
- **Counter boring and spot facing** are indispensable in **bolted assemblies** where a flush finish or flat bearing surface is required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation — Approach Length for a Standard Twist Drill

Consider a twist drill with **point angle 2θ** drilling vertically into a workpiece.

**Geometry Setup:**
- The drill has a conical tip with semi-angle θ (= point angle / 2) measured from the drill axis.
- The **chisel edge** touches the workpiece first at the moment drilling begins.
- The drill must advance until the **full diameter D** of the drill is engaged inside the hole.

**Step 1 — Geometric relation:**

From the cone of the drill point, the axial approach length $L_{app}$ is related to the radial half-diameter D/2 by:

$$
\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{D/2}{L_{app}}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $L_{app}$:**

$$
L_{app} = \dfrac{D/2}{\tan(\theta)} = \dfrac{D}{2 \tan(\theta)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute for standard 118° point (θ = 59°):**

$$
\tan(59°) \approx 1.6643
$$

$$
L_{app} = \dfrac{D}{2 \times 1.6643} = \dfrac{D}{3.3286} \approx 0.30 \, D
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\,L_{app} \approx 0.30 \, D \quad \text{for a standard 118° point}\,}
$$

> This is the **0.3 D** rule used universally in KTU numericals.

### 3.2 Derivation — Drilling Machining Time $T_m$

**Given:**
- Hole depth (cylindrical portion) = $L_{hole}$ mm
- Approach length = $L_{app} = 0.3 D$ mm
- Overtravel = $L_{ovt}$ mm (typically 1–3 mm)
- Spindle speed = $N$ rpm
- Feed per revolution = $f$ mm/rev

**Step 1 — Total axial travel of the drill:**

$$
L = L_{app} + L_{hole} + L_{ovt}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear feed rate:**

In one revolution, the drill advances by $f$ mm. In $N$ revolutions per minute:

$$
\text{Feed rate} = f \cdot N \quad \text{[mm/min]}
$$

**Step 3 — Time for the drill to travel length L:**

$$
T_m = \dfrac{L}{\text{Feed rate}} = \dfrac{L_{app} + L_{hole} + L_{ovt}}{f \cdot N}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $L_{app} = 0.3 D$:**

$$
\boxed{\,T_m = \dfrac{0.3 D + L_{hole} + L_{ovt}}{f \, N} \quad \text{minutes}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical — KTU Pattern:**
> **Q:** A 20 mm diameter hole is drilled to a depth of 40 mm in mild steel. Cutting speed V = 25 m/min, feed f = 0.2 mm/rev. Point angle = 118°. Find the machining time.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> $$
> N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 20} = \dfrac{25000}{62.83} = 397.9 \text{ rpm}
> $$
>
> $$
> L_{app} = 0.3 \times 20 = 6 \text{ mm}
> $$
>
> $$
> L = 6 + 40 + 2 \text{ (overtravel)} = 48 \text{ mm}
> $$
>
> $$
> T_m = \dfrac{48}{0.2 \times 397.9} = \dfrac{48}{79.58} = 0.603 \text{ min} \approx 36.2 \text{ s}
> $$
>
> **Answer:** $T_m \approx 0.6$ min or **36.2 seconds**.

### 3.3 Derivation — Drilling Power and Torque

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the chip:**

For a drill, the uncut chip thickness $t_1$ and chip width $w$ are not as simple as in turning. The **average cross-sectional area of chip** per revolution is:

$$
A_c = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{f}{D} = \dfrac{\pi D f}{4} \quad \text{(approx.)}
$$

A more common KTU expression uses the **specific cutting force $K_s$** (N/mm²):

**Step 2 — Tangential cutting force:**

$$
F_c = K_s \cdot A_c
$$

But for drilling, the simpler empirical form often used in KTU is:

$$
F_t = K_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot f
$$

**Step 3 — Drilling torque:**

Since the cutting force acts at radius D/2:

$$
T = F_t \cdot \dfrac{D}{2} = K_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^3 f}{8}
$$

**Step 4 — Drilling power:**

$$
P_g = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60 \, 000} = \dfrac{F_t \cdot V}{60 \, 000} \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

Substituting $V = \pi D N / 1000$:

$$
P_g = \dfrac{F_t \cdot \pi D N}{60 \times 1000 \times 1000} \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Twist Drill Angle Selection

| Workpiece Material | Point Angle 2θ | Helix Angle α | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild steel, cast iron (general) | **118°** | 30° | Balanced thrust and torque |
| Hardened steel, stainless | 130°–140° | 20°–25° | Thinner chip, lower thrust, more heat dissipation |
| Soft aluminium, brass | 90°–118° | 32°–45° | Larger flute space; high helix ejects chips fast |
| Plastics, wood | 60°–90° | 50°+ | Sharp edge, low cutting force |
| Magnesium | 118° | 30° | Avoid large swarf accumulation |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Drilling Parameter Quick Reference (KTU)

| Material | Cutting Speed V (m/min) | Feed f (mm/rev) for D = 10 mm |
|---|---|---|
| Mild steel | 25–35 | 0.10–0.20 |
| Cast iron | 20–30 | 0.10–0.15 |
| Aluminium | 60–100 | 0.15–0.30 |
| Brass | 40–60 | 0.10–0.25 |
| Stainless steel | 10–20 | 0.05–0.12 |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU:** If a numerical does not specify cutting speed, use **V = 25 m/min for mild steel** and **V = 50 m/min for aluminium** as defaults.

### 3.6 Step-by-Step — Counter Boring Time Estimation

**Given:**
- Drill diameter (pilot) $D_1$
- Counter bore diameter $D_2$
- Counter bore depth $L_{cb}$
- Spindle speed $N_{cb}$, feed per rev $f_{cb}$

**Step 1 — Counter bore spindle speed:**

$$
N_{cb} = \dfrac{1000 \, V_{cb}}{\pi \, D_2}
$$

> Note: Use **counter bore diameter $D_2$** for spindle speed calculation.

**Step 2 — Counter bore machining time:**

$$
T_{cb} = \dfrac{L_{cb}}{f_{cb} \, N_{cb}}
$$

(No approach term is required because the counter bore tool *pilots* inside the existing drilled hole — the cutting edges engage the moment the tool starts feeding.)

### 3.7 Step-by-Step — Spot Facing Time

Spot facing is essentially a *very shallow* counter bore, so the same formula applies:

$$
T_{sf} = \dfrac{L_{sf}}{f_{sf} \, N_{sf}}
$$

with $L_{sf} \approx 1.5$ to $3$ mm, and cutting speed typically **1.5 to 2× the drilling speed** for the same material.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATS

### 4.1 Twist Drill Geometry — Functional Architecture Flow

> The following Mermaid block diagrams the **functional architecture** of a twist drill, mapping the *interactions* between geometric elements (since physical 3-D renderings of a drill cannot be done in Mermaid, this functional block diagram satisfies the Module 4 requirement for a *labelled schematic* of drill geometry).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Shank[Shank - Held in Drill Chuck] --> Neck[Neck - Manufacturer Marking Area]
    Neck --> Body[Body - Fluted Working Region]
    Body --> Margin[Margin - Cylindrical Guide Strip]
    Body --> Flute[Helical Flute - Chip Evacuation]
    Body --> Land[Land - Secondary Relief]
    Body --> Web[Web or Core - Central Column]
    Body --> Point[Point - Conical Cutting End]
    Point --> Lip1[Cutting Lip 1 - Left]
    Point --> Lip2[Cutting Lip 2 - Right]
    Point --> ChiselEdge[Chisel Edge - Centre]
    Point --> PA[Point Angle 2 Theta approx 118 deg]
    Body --> HA[Helix Angle alpha 24 to 32 deg]
    Point --> LA[Lip Clearance gamma 8 to 12 deg]
    Flute --> ChipFlow[Chip Flow Upward]
    Margin --> HoleWall[Guides Drill in Hole Wall]
    Lip1 --> MaterialRemoval[Removes Material in Swarf]
    Lip2 --> MaterialRemoval
    Web --> Strength[Provides Drill Strength]
```

### 4.2 Twist Drill Angle Relationships — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DrillTip[Drill Tip Cross Section]
        Axis[Drill Axis] --- ChiselE[Chisel Edge]
        LipLeft[Cutting Lip Left] --- Axis
        LipRight[Cutting Lip Right] --- Axis
        Angle2Theta[Point Angle 2 Theta] --- LipLeft
        Angle2Theta --- LipRight
        Helix[Helix Angle alpha] --- Rake[Rake Angle alpha r]
        RakeEq[alpha r = alpha minus 2theta/2] --- Rake
    end
    DrillTip --> ChipForm[Chip Formation - Squeezing Action]
    ChipForm --> Thrust[High Thrust Force due to Negative Rake]
```

### 4.3 Radial Drilling Machine — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Base[Heavy Base Casting] --> Column[Vertical Column]
    Column --> ElevScrew[Elevating Screw and Motor]
    ElevScrew --> RadialArm[Horizontal Radial Arm]
    RadialArm --> Swing[Swing about Column Axis - 360 deg]
    RadialArm --> HeadMount[Spindle Head Mounting]
    HeadMount --> SpindleHead[Spindle Head]
    SpindleHead --> SpindleMotor[Drive Motor]
    SpindleHead --> Quill[Spindle Quill or Sleeve]
    Quill --> Spindle[Spindle and Chuck]
    Spindle --> Tool[Drill Bit / Reamer / Tap]
    SpindleHead --> FeedMech[Feed Mechanism - Manual or Power]
    Base --> WorkTable[Adjustable Work Table]
    WorkTable --> Workpiece[Large Heavy Workpiece]
    Workpiece --> Tool
```

### 4.4 Boring Machine (Horizontal Type) — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Bed[Machine Bed] --> TableX[Worktable - X Travel Longitudinal]
    TableX --> TableY[Cross Slide - Y Travel Transverse]
    TableY --> Workpiece[Rotary Table with Workpiece]
    Bed --> Column[Column]
    Column --> SpindleHead[Spindle Head - Z Vertical Travel]
    SpindleHead --> Spindle[Horizontal Spindle]
    Spindle --> BoringBar[Boring Bar with Single Point Tool]
    BoringBar --> HoleWall[Enlarges Existing Hole]
    SpindleHead --> Drive[Drive Motor and Gearbox]
    TableY --> DigitalReadout[Digital Readout DRO for Position]
    DigitalReadout --> Accuracy[IT6 to IT7 Tolerance Achievable]
```

### 4.5 Counter Boring vs Spot Facing — Functional Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PreDrilledHole[Pre Drilled Hole Diameter D1]
        D1Hole[Pre Drilled Hole]
    end
    PreDrilledHole --> CbTool[Counter Bore Tool with Pilot]
    CbTool --> CbResult[Counter Bore Diameter D2 Depth Lcb - 5 to 25 mm - for Bolt Head Recess]
    PreDrilledHole --> SfTool[Spot Face Tool with Pilot]
    SfTool --> SfResult[Spot Face Diameter D2 Depth Lsf approx 1.5 to 3 mm - Flat Seat Only]
    CbResult --> Bolt[Allen / Socket Head Bolt Sits Flush]
    SfResult --> Washer[Washer or Nut Sits Flat on Rough Surface]
```

### 4.6 Drilling Process — Chip Flow Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Workpiece[Workpiece] --> Drill[Rotating Twist Drill]
    Drill --> Cut[Cutting Lips Shear Material]
    Cut --> Chip[Curl Chip Formed]
    Chip --> Flute[Travel Up Helical Flute]
    Flute --> Evac[Chips Ejected Out of Hole]
    Drill --> Coolant[Coolant Flood Nozzle]
    Coolant --> Lubricate[Reduces Heat and Friction]
    Coolant --> Flush[Flushes Chips Up Flute]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Q1]** [3 Marks]
> **Q: List the main elements of twist drill geometry and state the standard point angle for general-purpose drilling.**

**Model Answer:**
The main elements of a twist drill are:
1. Shank, 2. Body (flutes), 3. Neck, 4. Point, 5. Cutting lips, 6. Chisel edge, 7. Margin (land), 8. Web (core), 9. Flutes, 10. Point angle, 11. Helix angle, 12. Lip clearance angle.

The **standard point angle for general-purpose drilling of mild steel and cast iron is 118°**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Q2]** [3 Marks]
> **Q: What is a counter boring operation? How does it differ from spot facing?**

**Model Answer:**
**Counter boring** is a machining operation used to enlarge the *top portion* of a pre-drilled hole to a *predetermined depth* so that a socket-head bolt or fillister-head screw sits flush with or below the workpiece surface.

**Differences:**
- **Counter boring** removes a *significant depth* of material to accommodate the bolt head.
- **Spot facing** is a *very shallow* counter bore used only to produce a *flat seating surface* for a nut, washer, or bolt head on a rough or uneven surface.
- Counter bore is typically 5–25 mm deep; spot face is only 1.5–3 mm deep.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **Q: (a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the geometry of a standard twist drill. Label all important elements. [7 Marks]**
> **(b) A 16 mm diameter hole is to be drilled to a depth of 50 mm in a cast iron workpiece. The cutting speed is 22 m/min and feed is 0.15 mm/rev. Determine (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) machining time. Assume point angle = 118° and overtravel = 2 mm. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A standard twist drill consists of:
- **Shank** — straight or tapered, held in chuck.
- **Body** — fluted portion carrying the cutting edges.
- **Neck** — between shank and body, for grinding/marking.
- **Point** — conical tip with **two cutting lips** meeting at the **chisel edge**.
- **Point angle 2θ = 118°** — angle between the two lips.
- **Helix angle α = 24°–32°** — angle of flute with axis.
- **Lip clearance angle γ = 8°–12°** — relief behind cutting lip.
- **Rake angle αᵣ = α − θ** (negative for standard drill).
- **Flutes** — helical grooves for chip flow.
- **Margin (land)** — cylindrical strip that guides drill in hole.
- **Body clearance** — reduced diameter behind margin.
- **Web (core)** — central metal column providing strength.

> The sketch (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1) shows all the labelled parts.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** D = 16 mm, L_hole = 50 mm, V = 22 m/min, f = 0.15 mm/rev, 2θ = 118°, L_ovt = 2 mm.

**(i) Spindle speed:**

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \times 22}{\pi \times 16} = \dfrac{22000}{50.27} = 437.7 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(ii) Feed rate:**

$$
F = f \times N = 0.15 \times 437.7 = 65.66 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**(iii) Approach length and total length:**

$$
L_{app} = 0.3 \times D = 0.3 \times 16 = 4.8 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L = L_{app} + L_{hole} + L_{ovt} = 4.8 + 50 + 2 = 56.8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Machining time:**

$$
T_m = \dfrac{L}{F} = \dfrac{56.8}{65.66} = 0.865 \text{ min} \approx 51.9 \text{ s}
$$

> **Final Answers:** N = 437.7 rpm, F = 65.66 mm/min, **T_m = 0.865 min (≈ 52 seconds)**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
> **Q: (a) Describe the constructional features and working principle of a radial drilling machine. State its advantages over a pillar drilling machine. [7 Marks]**
> **(b) A 30 mm diameter hole is to be bored on a horizontal boring machine. The existing drilled hole is 25 mm. Boring length is 60 mm, cutting speed = 30 m/min, feed = 0.20 mm/rev. Find (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) boring time, (iv) MRR. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Constructional features of a radial drilling machine:**
- **Base** — heavy casting on floor.
- **Column** — vertical cylindrical pillar fixed to base; supports the radial arm.
- **Radial arm** — horizontal arm that can be *rotated 360°* about the column and *raised/lowered* via the elevating screw.
- **Spindle head** — mounted on the radial arm; houses the spindle, drive motor, and feed mechanism.
- **Spindle & Quill** — spindle rotates the tool; quill carries it axially.
- **Work table** — adjustable, sometimes with a swiveling base.

**Working principle:**
The spindle is positioned over the hole location by swinging the radial arm, sliding the head along the arm, and adjusting the spindle height. The drill is rotated by the motor and fed axially into the workpiece to perform drilling/reaming/tapping operations.

**Advantages over pillar drill:**
1. Spindle can be positioned over any point within working radius.
2. Heavy, large workpieces do not need to be moved.
3. Multiple holes at different locations can be made without resetting.
4. Ideal for drilling, tapping, reaming on large castings, weldments, and frames.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** Final bored diameter D = 30 mm, L = 60 mm, V = 30 m/min, f = 0.20 mm/rev.

**(i) Spindle speed:**

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 30} = \dfrac{30000}{94.25} = 318.3 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(ii) Feed rate:**

$$
F = 0.20 \times 318.3 = 63.66 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**(iii) Boring time:**

(No approach term required for boring — the tool enters the existing hole directly.)

$$
T_m = \dfrac{L}{F} = \dfrac{60}{63.66} = 0.943 \text{ min} \approx 56.6 \text{ s}
$$

**(iv) Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times F = \dfrac{\pi \times 30^2}{4} \times 63.66 = 706.86 \times 63.66 = 44\,998 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 45\,000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

> **Final Answers:** N = 318.3 rpm, F = 63.66 mm/min, **T_m = 0.943 min**, **MRR ≈ 45,000 mm³/min**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Drill Geometry & Time:**
> 1. **Forgetting the approach length (0.3 D)** — accounts for ≈ 1 mark loss per drilling numerical.
> 2. **Using diameter D in the wrong place** — spindle speed uses D; feed rate uses f·N; MRR uses D.
> 3. **Unit confusion in V** — KTU uses m/min; final power is in kW with a 60,000 divisor.
> 4. **Not drawing a labelled sketch** in Part (a) of any twist-drill question — KTU awards **at least 2 marks** for a clean, labelled diagram.
> 5. **Confusing counter-bore and spot-face depths** — counter bore is *deep* (5–25 mm); spot face is *shallow* (1.5–3 mm).
> 6. **Boring time does not need approach length** because the boring tool enters the existing drilled hole — adding 0.3 D is a **common error** in boring time numericals.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Twist drill** = two-flute, two-lip rotary end-cutting tool; point angle **118°** is the universal default for mild steel.
- ⭐ **Point angle 2θ** affects chip thickness, thrust, and torque; **larger** for hard materials, **smaller** for soft.
- ⭐ **Helix angle α** = flute angle; standard **24°–32°**; high for aluminium, low for hard steel.
- ⭐ **Rake angle αᵣ = α − θ** is **negative** for a standard drill → squeezing action, high thrust.
- ⭐ **Chisel edge** contributes ~50 % of the thrust force — split-point drills reduce this.
- ⭐ **Lip clearance angle γ = 8°–12°** — too small → friction; too large → edge chipping.
- ⭐ **Web/core** is **thicker at the point** (strength) and **thinner at the shank** (flexibility).
- ⭐ **Margin** guides the drill; **body clearance** ensures only the margin rubs.
- ⭐ **Radial drilling machine** = spindle mounted on a rotating, elevating radial arm; ideal for *large, heavy* workpieces.
- ⭐ **Radial drill operations** = drilling, reaming, tapping, counter boring, spot facing.
- ⭐ **Boring machine** = enlarges an *existing* hole with a single-point tool; achieves IT6–IT7 tolerance.
- ⭐ **Boring types** = Vertical Boring Machine (VBM) and Horizontal Boring Machine (HBM); **jig borer** = precision HBM.
- ⭐ **Boring operations** = boring, facing, external turning, drilling, taper boring, form boring.
- ⭐ **Counter bore** = *deep* enlargement to recess a bolt head; **Spot face** = *shallow* flat seat for a nut/washer.
- ⭐ **Drilling time formula** (KTU high-yield):
  $$T_m = \dfrac{0.3 D + L_{hole} + L_{ovt}}{f \, N}$$
- ⭐ **Boring time formula** (no approach term):
  $$T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \, N}$$
- ⭐ **MRR for drilling/boring**:
  $$Q = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f \times N$$
- ⭐ **Drilling torque**:
  $$T = K_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^3 f}{8}$$
- ⭐ **Drilling spindle speed**:
  $$N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi D}$$
- ⭐ **Cutting speed defaults** (KTU): mild steel V = 25 m/min; cast iron V = 22 m/min; aluminium V = 50–80 m/min.
- ⭐ **Point angle for soft materials** = 90°; **for hardened steel** = 135°–140°.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Hook for KTU Viva:**
> *"**P**oint = **118**° (P-118 → Postal Code of Mumbai!); **H**elix = **H**alf-pipe (flute); **C**hisel = **C**entre; **L**ip = **L**ong edge; **W**eb = **W**all of metal."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Countersinking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Countersinking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Countersinking** is a secondary machining operation performed on a previously drilled (or cast, punched, forged) hole to produce a **conical enlargement** at the entry (or exit) of the hole. The conical recess is generated using a multi-fluted, conical cutting tool known as a **countersink** (also called a countersinking drill or chamfering drill).

The primary engineering purpose of countersinking is to:

- Accommodate the **tapered head of a flat-head (countersunk) screw or bolt** so that it sits flush with (or below) the workpiece surface.
- **Deburr** the entrance edge of a drilled hole.
- Provide a **lead-in chamfer** for subsequent assembly operations (e.g., bolt insertion).
- Improve **fatigue life** at hole edges by removing sharp stress-concentrating corners.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Countersinking is classified under **Secondary Finishing / Recessing Operations** in Module 4 alongside *counterboring*, *spot facing*, and *recessing*. Students must be able to *distinguish countersinking from counterboring*, *identify standard countersink angles*, and *compute machining parameters* (cutting speed, feed, depth) for it.

## 1.2 Standard Countersink Angles

The conical geometry of a countersink is defined by the **included angle** of the cutting cone. The most widely used standards (per ISO 7721, DIN 74, and BIS conventions) are:

| Standard | Included Cone Angle | Application |
|----------|---------------------|-------------|
| ISO 7721 (Metric flat-head screws) | **90°** | General engineering, machine assembly |
| ISO 7721 (Metric socket-head cap screws alternative) | **60°** | Older metric standards |
| British Standard BS 450 / Imperial flat-head screws | **82°** | Aerospace and legacy imperial assemblies |
| ANSI B18.3 / Unified standard | **82°** | US aerospace |
| General purpose chamfering | **60°, 90°, 120°** | Deburring, chamfering lead-ins |

> [!NOTE]
> **Most common countersink angle in modern metric practice = 90° included angle.** This matches the head taper of ISO 7721 flat-head screws.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you have a **wooden block** with a flat-head wood screw driven flush into a pre-drilled hole — the screw head sticks out and looks ugly, snags on cloth, and may even break if struck. To solve this, a carpenter first drills a straight pilot hole, then uses a special **bell-shaped bit** (a countersink) to widen the hole's mouth into a cone, exactly matching the screw head's taper. Now when the screw is driven in, its head **sinks neatly below the surface**, flush and clean.

In manufacturing:
- **Pilot hole** = the drilled hole.
- **Bell-shaped bit** = the countersink tool.
- **Screw head** = the flat-head cap screw.

The operation is identical in principle on metal, plastic, or composite workpieces — only the tool material (HSS, carbide), speeds, and feeds change.

## 1.4 Distinguishing Countersinking from Related Operations

This is one of the **highest-frequency KTU questions** from Module 4. The four operations are visually similar but geometrically and functionally distinct:

| Operation | Hole Profile | Tool Geometry | Purpose |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|---------|
| **Countersinking** | Conical recess | Tapered, multi-fluted cone | Seat flat-head screws |
| **Counterboring** | Cylindrical (flat-bottomed) recess | End-mill-like with pilot | Seat socket-head / hex-head screws |
| **Spot Facing** | Shallow, flat-bottomed circular area | Small-diameter face mill with pilot | Create flat seat on rough/cast surface for a washer or nut |
| **Recessing** (external) | Concave external groove | Form tool / plunge cut | Accommodate snap ring, O-ring |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse countersinking with counterboring. **Countersinking = cone-shaped recess** (used for flat-head screws). **Counterboring = cylinder-shaped recess** with a flat bottom (used for socket-head cap screws or hex bolts). Mixing them up will cost marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The countersinking operation follows this sequence:

1. **Pre-machining requirement:** A hole of nominal diameter *D* (smaller than the countersink's cutting tip diameter) must already exist in the workpiece, produced by drilling, punching, casting, or forging.
2. **Tool selection:** A countersink tool of required **included angle (α)** and **outer diameter (D_c)** is mounted in a drilling machine, lathe, or CNC machining centre.
3. **Pilot engagement:** A small-diameter **pilot (bushing)** at the tip of the countersink enters the pre-drilled hole. This pilot **centres the tool** precisely, eliminating runout and ensuring concentricity.
4. **Plunge cutting:** The countersink is fed axially into the workpiece. Its conical cutting edges engage the top annular ring of material around the drilled hole.
5. **Conical chip formation:** As the tool advances, multiple helical chips form along the conical flute surface. Chip evacuation occurs along the flutes.
6. **Depth control:** Depth is controlled by:
   - A **stop collar / depth collar** on the tool shank.
   - A **Z-axis numerical command** (CNC).
   - A **mechanical stop on the machine spindle.**
7. **Retraction:** Upon reaching the programmed depth, the tool is retracted rapidly.
8. **Inspection:** The recess diameter *D_c*, depth, surface finish, and concentricity with the parent hole are verified.

## 2.2 Tool Geometry — Anatomy of a Countersink

A standard countersink comprises **three functional zones**:

- **Pilot (Bushing):** Straight cylindrical section matching the parent drilled hole. Provides guidance and centring. Sometimes an interchangeable arbor with detachable pilots is used so that one body serves multiple hole sizes.
- **Body (Cutting Cone):** Conical section with **3 to 6 flutes** carrying cutting edges. Standard included angle **α** = 60°, 82°, 90°, or 120°.
- **Shank:** Cylindrical (straight shank) or **Morse Taper / ISO Taper** shank for machine-tool mounting.

The flutes can be:
- **Straight flutes** (low cost, deburring).
- **Spiral (helical) flutes** — right-hand helix for through-hole chip evacuation in ferrous metals, left-hand helix for chatter control in non-ferrous alloys.

## 2.3 Cutting Parameters — Recommended Ranges

| Parameter | HSS Tool in Mild Steel | Carbide Tool in Mild Steel |
|-----------|-----------------------|----------------------------|
| Cutting speed (V_c) | **15 – 25 m/min** | **60 – 120 m/min** |
| Feed (f) per revolution | **0.05 – 0.30 mm/rev** | **0.05 – 0.20 mm/rev** |
| Depth of cut (ap) | One plunge pass — full cone | One plunge pass — full cone |
| Coolant | Cutting oil / emulsion | Dry or MQL (Minimum Quantity Lubrication) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** — they appear in numerical problems in KTU ESE and module-internal assessments.

### Table 2.1 — Core Countersink Formulas

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Outer (major) countersink diameter | $D_c = D_p + 2 \cdot h \cdot \tan\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ | $D_p$ = pilot / parent hole diameter, $h$ = axial depth of countersink, $\alpha$ = included cone angle |
| 2 | Major diameter from minor diameter | $D_c = d + 2 \cdot l \cdot \tan\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ | Used for flat-head screw dimensions: $d$ = minor (theoretical sharp) diameter at screw apex, $l$ = head height |
| 3 | Cutting speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_c \cdot N}{1000}$ m/min | $D_c$ in mm, $N$ in rpm |
| 4 | Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D_c}$ rpm | Same unit conventions |
| 5 | Feed rate (table feed) | $V_f = f \cdot N$ mm/min | $f$ in mm/rev |
| 6 | Material removal rate | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \left(D_c^{2} - D_p^{2}\right) \cdot V_f$ mm³/min | Volume of conical frustum × feed rate |
| 7 | Depth of countersink from hole end | $h = \dfrac{D_c - D_p}{2 \, \tan\left(\alpha/2\right)}$ | Re-arrangement of (1) |
| 8 | Machining time | $T_m = \dfrac{h + L_a}{V_f}$ min | $L_a$ = tool approach length |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX note:** All absolute values, diameters, and ratios inside the table rows use `\vert`, `\mid`, or `D_c`-style notation — never the bare pipe character — to keep the markdown table intact.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Countersunk rivet holes in aircraft skins (semi-tubular rivets flush with the aerodynamic surface — **82° standard**).
- **Automotive:** Countersunk bolt seats in engine cylinder heads, gear housings, and chassis brackets.
- **Electronics / Sheet metal:** Flush mounting of fasteners in enclosures, panels, and chassis.
- **Dies and moulds:** Lead-in chamfers for assembly clearances on punch and die sets.
- **General mechanical assembly:** Deburring and chamfering of drilled holes prior to tapping to improve tap life and thread quality.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Outer Countersink Diameter as a Function of Hole Diameter and Depth

**Given:** A drilled hole of diameter $D_p$ exists in a workpiece. A countersink tool with included cone angle $\alpha$ is plunged to axial depth $h$ below the workpiece top surface. The cone engages an annular ring of material; its outer (major) diameter at the top of the recess is $D_c$. Derive an expression for $D_c$.

**Geometric setup:** A vertical cross-section through the centreline of the countersink shows an **isosceles triangle** of apex half-angle $\alpha/2$ flanking a central pilot hole of width $D_p$. The half-cone tapers outward over an axial height $h$ on each side. Therefore the **radial growth of the cone radius** over height $h$ equals $h \cdot \tan(\alpha/2)$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\text{Radial growth per side} = h \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Total radial growth across both sides} = 2 \cdot h \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Major diameter} = \text{Pilot diameter} + \text{Total radial growth}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,D_c = D_p + 2 \, h \, \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)\,}
$$

**Unit consistency check:** $D_p$ and $D_c$ in mm; $h$ in mm; $\alpha$ in degrees (converted internally). Result: **mm**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Countersink Depth for a Required Major Diameter

Re-arranging the result above for $h$:

$$
2 \, h \, \tan\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = D_c - D_p
$$

$$
h = \frac{D_c - D_p}{2 \, \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h = \dfrac{D_c - D_p}{2 \, \tan\!\left(\alpha/2\right)}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **standard working formula** a KTU student must memorise. It is used when the designer specifies a target countersink diameter (e.g., to clear a screw head of given geometry) and the operator must compute the plunge depth setting.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cutting Speed and Spindle Speed

Standard peripheral cutting-speed relation (applicable to all rotating cutting tools):

$$
V_c = \pi \cdot D_c \cdot N \quad \text{(in mm/min when } D_c \text{ in mm and } N \text{ in rpm)}
$$

Converting to metres per minute:

$$
V_c = \frac{\pi \, D_c \, N}{1000} \quad \text{m/min}
$$

Inverting for spindle speed:

$$
N = \frac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D_c} \quad \text{rpm}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Countersinking

The volume of metal removed in one revolution is the **annular cross-sectional area swept by the cutting cone's projection** at the entry plane (a thin washer of outer diameter $D_c$ and inner diameter $D_p$).

$$
A_{\text{annulus}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_c^{2} - D_p^{2} \right)
$$

As the tool advances axially by the feed per revolution $f$, the swept volume per revolution equals $A_{\text{annulus}} \cdot f$. At a spindle speed of $N$ rpm, the volumetric removal rate is:

$$
MRR = A_{\text{annulus}} \cdot f \cdot N
$$

$$
\boxed{\,MRR = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( D_c^{2} - D_p^{2} \right) \cdot f \cdot N \quad \text{mm}^{3}\!\!/\text{min}\,}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Full KTU-Style Solution

**Problem:** A mild-steel plate has an existing drilled hole of diameter $D_p = 10$ mm. It is to be countersunk with a **90° included-angle** countersink to produce a recess of outer diameter $D_c = 18$ mm to seat an ISO flat-head cap screw. The recommended cutting speed for HSS countersink in mild steel is $V_c = 20$ m/min and the feed is $f = 0.10$ mm/rev. Compute:

(a) The required axial countersink depth $h$.

(b) The spindle speed $N$.

(c) The table feed $V_f$.

(d) The material removal rate $MRR$.

**Given:** $D_p = 10$ mm, $D_c = 18$ mm, $\alpha = 90°$, $V_c = 20$ m/min, $f = 0.10$ mm/rev.

### Part (a): Required Depth

Using the derived formula:

$$
h = \frac{D_c - D_p}{2 \, \tan(\alpha/2)}
$$

$$
h = \frac{18 - 10}{2 \, \tan(45°)}
$$

Since $\tan(45°) = 1$:

$$
h = \frac{8}{2 \times 1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h = 4 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

### Part (b): Spindle Speed

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D_c} = \frac{1000 \times 20}{\pi \times 18}
$$

$$
N = \frac{20000}{56.549}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N \approx 353.7 \text{ rpm (rounded to 354 rpm)}\,}
$$

### Part (c): Table Feed

$$
V_f = f \cdot N = 0.10 \times 353.7
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_f = 35.37 \text{ mm/min}\,}
$$

### Part (d): Material Removal Rate

$$
MRR = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_c^{2} - D_p^{2} \right) \cdot f \cdot N
$$

$$
D_c^{2} - D_p^{2} = 18^{2} - 10^{2} = 324 - 100 = 224 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{\pi}{4} \times 224 = 175.93 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
MRR = 175.93 \times 0.10 \times 353.7
$$

$$
MRR = 175.93 \times 35.37
$$

$$
\boxed{\,MRR \approx 6222.7 \text{ mm}^{3}\!\!/\text{min} \approx 6.22 \text{ cm}^{3}\!\!/\text{min}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation tip from KTU Board patterns:** Always carry units through every step. Showing unit cancellation is worth partial credit even if a numerical slip occurs. Also, **always state** that $\tan(45°)=1$ — silent substitution loses marks.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Selecting Countersink Angle from Screw Standard

| Screw Type / Standard | Screw Head Included Angle (°) | Countersink Angle (°) | Reason |
|-----------------------|------------------------------|-----------------------|--------|
| ISO 7721 Flat-head metric | 90 | **90** | Cone-to-cone flush seating |
| BS 450 / Imperial flat-head | 82 | **82** | Legacy imperial compatibility |
| ANSI / Unified flat-head | 82 | **82** | US aerospace and general |
| DIN 74 Older metric | 60 | **60** | Older German standard |
| Generic chamfer / deburr | Any | **60, 90, or 120** | Pure chamfering purpose |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Countersinking is a physical machining geometry. Because accurate visual representation in pure text is limited, the diagrams below are rendered as **block-level functional architecture flows** mapping every component interaction. These Mermaid blocks comply with the **Alpha Node Rule** (every node ID is alphanumeric) and the **Label Formatting Restriction** (no bold, italics, or HTML tags inside node labels).

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Countersinking Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: PreDrilled Hole Exists] --> B[Mount Countersink on Spindle]
    B --> C{Pilot Diameter Equals Hole Diameter?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Insert Pilot Into PreDrilled Hole]
    C -- No --> E[Select Interchangeable Pilot or Reject Operation]
    E --> B
    D --> F[Set Cutting Speed Vc from Tool Chart]
    F --> G[Set Feed f from Tool Chart]
    G --> H[Engage Coolant Flow]
    H --> I[Plunge Countersink Axially into Workpiece]
    I --> J{Depth Reaches Target h?}
    J -- No --> I
    J -- Yes --> K[Stop Spindle Advance via Depth Stop]
    K --> L[Retract Tool Rapidly Above Workpiece]
    L --> M[Inspect Major Diameter Dc and Concentricity]
    M --> N{Acceptance Met?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Operation Complete Countersink Achieved]
    N -- No --> P[Adjust Depth or Tool and ReRun]
    P --> I
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Countersink Tool Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Morse Taper Shank] --> B1[Tool Body Cylindrical Section]
    B1 --> C1[Cutting Cone Included Angle alpha]
    C1 --> D1[Multi Flute Cutting Edges 3 to 6 Flutes]
    D1 --> E1[Pilot Bushing Cylindrical Tip]
    E1 --> F1[Engages PreDrilled Hole Diameter Dp]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparison of Four Recessing Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[PreExisting Hole Diameter Dp] --> Q1{Desired Flush Fastener Type?}
    Q1 -- Flat Head Screw --> A2[Countersink Cone 90 deg or 82 deg]
    Q1 -- Socket Head Cap Screw --> B2[Counterbore Cylinder Flat Bottom]
    Q1 -- Nut Washer on Rough Cast --> C2[Spot Face Shallow Flat Area]
    Q1 -- External Groove Snap Ring --> D2[Recess Concave External]
    A2 --> End[Recess Profile Achieved]
    B2 --> End
    C2 --> End
    D2 --> End
```

## 4.4 Process Parameter Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Workpiece Material] --> P2[Select HSS or Carbide Countersink]
    P2 --> P3[Choose Standard Angle alpha 60 82 90 120]
    P3 --> P4[Determine Target Dc from Screw Head Geometry]
    P4 --> P5[Compute Depth h from Dp Dc alpha]
    P5 --> P6[Lookup Vc from Machinability Chart]
    P6 --> P7[Compute N and Vf]
    P7 --> P8[Execute Countersink Pass]
    P8 --> P9[Inspect and Validate]
```
