# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between countersinking and counterboring. Mention one standard countersink angle and the corresponding screw standard.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Countersinking** is a secondary machining operation that produces a **conical recess** at the entry of a pre-drilled hole, used to seat the tapered head of a flat-head screw flush with the surface. (1 mark)
- **Counterboring** produces a **cylindrical (flat-bottomed) recess** with straight walls, used to seat socket-head cap screws or hex-head bolts below the surface. (1 mark)
- A standard countersink angle is **90° included angle**, which corresponds to **ISO 7721 flat-head metric screw** standard. (1 mark)

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is a pilot provided on a countersink tool? What is the role of the cone angle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **pilot** is a straight cylindrical tip at the cutting-end of the countersink that engages the pre-drilled hole. Its function is to **centre the tool precisely**, eliminate lateral runout, and ensure the conical recess is **concentric** with the parent hole. (2 marks)
- The **cone angle** (included angle α) defines the **geometry of the recess** and is selected to match the head taper of the screw to be seated, ensuring a flush fit and load distribution. Common values are 60°, 82°, and 90°. (1 mark)

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of countersinking** with a neat sketch. List any **four standard countersink angles** and state the corresponding screw standards. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A through hole of diameter **12 mm** in a mild-steel component is to be countersunk using a **82° countersink** to seat an imperial flat-head screw. The required major recess diameter is **20 mm**. The recommended cutting speed is **V_c = 18 m/min** and the feed is **f = 0.12 mm/rev** for HSS tooling. Compute:

    (i) The required axial depth of countersink **h** in mm.

    (ii) The spindle speed **N** in rpm.

    (iii) The material removal rate **MRR** in mm³/min. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Working Principle of Countersinking:**

Countersinking is performed on a **pre-drilled hole** of diameter $D_p$. A countersink tool — a conical cutter with a cylindrical pilot tip and multiple cutting flutes — is mounted on a drilling machine, lathe, or CNC spindle. The pilot engages the drilled hole and **centres the tool**, eliminating lateral misalignment. The tool is then **plunged axially** into the workpiece; the conical cutting edges shear away an **annular ring** of material around the hole entry, generating a conical recess with included angle α. Depth is controlled by a mechanical stop, depth collar, or CNC Z-axis command. The recess seats the head of a **flat-head (countersunk) screw** so that it lies flush with or below the workpiece surface.

The operation also serves to **deburr** the hole entry and produce a **lead-in chamfer** for fastener insertion.

**Four Standard Countersink Angles and Their Standards:**

| Included Angle (α) | Standard / Application |
|-------------------|------------------------|
| **90°** | ISO 7721 — Metric flat-head screws |
| **82°** | BS 450 / ANSI B18.3 — Imperial and Unified flat-head screws |
| **60°** | DIN 74 (older) — German metric standards |
| **120°** | General purpose chamfering / deburring |

(7 marks: 3 for principle, 2 for sketch description, 2 for the angle table)

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $D_p = 12$ mm, $D_c = 20$ mm, $\alpha = 82°$, $V_c = 18$ m/min, $f = 0.12$ mm/rev.

**(i) Depth of countersink:**

$$
h = \frac{D_c - D_p}{2 \, \tan(\alpha/2)} = \frac{20 - 12}{2 \, \tan(41°)}
$$

$$
\tan(41°) \approx 0.8693
$$

$$
h = \frac{8}{2 \times 0.8693} = \frac{8}{1.7386}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h \approx 4.60 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

**(ii) Spindle speed:**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D_c} = \frac{1000 \times 18}{\pi \times 20}
$$

$$
N = \frac{18000}{62.832}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N \approx 286.5 \text{ rpm (round to 287 rpm)}\,}
$$

**(iii) Material removal rate:**

$$
MRR = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_c^{2} - D_p^{2} \right) \cdot f \cdot N
$$

$$
D_c^{2} - D_p^{2} = 20^{2} - 12^{2} = 400 - 144 = 256 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{\pi}{4} \times 256 = 201.06 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
f \cdot N = 0.12 \times 286.5 = 34.38 \text{ mm/min}
$$

$$
MRR = 201.06 \times 34.38
$$

$$
\boxed{\,MRR \approx 6912 \text{ mm}^{3}\!\!/\text{min} \approx 6.91 \text{ cm}^{3}\!\!/\text{min}\,}
$$

(7 marks: 2 for depth, 2 for spindle speed, 3 for MRR with full derivation)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a labelled sketch, describe the **geometry of a countersink tool**. Explain the function of the **pilot** and the **flutes**. Mention any **two industrial applications** of countersinking. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **10 mm diameter** drilled hole in a cast-iron workpiece is to be countersunk to a depth of **5 mm** using a **90° countersink**. If the cutting speed is **V_c = 25 m/min** and the feed **f = 0.15 mm/rev**, compute:

    (i) The major (outer) diameter of the countersink **D_c**.

    (ii) The spindle speed **N**.

    (iii) The machining time per hole, assuming the approach length **L_a = 3 mm**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Tool Geometry (Sketch Description):**

A countersink tool consists of three distinct sections along its axis:

1. **Shank** — Straight cylindrical or Morse-taper shank for gripping in the machine spindle.
2. **Body (Cutting Cone)** — The conical working section that performs the cutting. It carries **3 to 6 helical or straight flutes**, each bounded by a cutting edge. The included cone angle α determines the recess profile.
3. **Pilot (Bushing)** — A small straight cylindrical extension at the tip that enters the pre-drilled hole and **centres the tool**.

**Function of the pilot:** Provides positive centring of the tool with respect to the parent hole, eliminates lateral runout, and ensures **concentricity** between the recess and the drilled hole.

**Function of the flutes:** Carry away the chips generated during cutting and form the cutting edges along the cone surface. Helical (spiral) flutes improve chip evacuation in deeper cuts; straight flutes are adequate for shallow deburring.

**Two industrial applications:**

- Countersinking of rivet holes in aircraft skin panels (82° standard).
- Flush mounting of flat-head screws in automobile chassis and engine components.

(7 marks: 3 for geometry, 2 for pilot and flute function, 2 for applications)

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $D_p = 10$ mm, $h = 5$ mm, $\alpha = 90°$, $V_c = 25$ m/min, $f = 0.15$ mm/rev, $L_a = 3$ mm.

**(i) Major diameter:**

$$
D_c = D_p + 2 \, h \, \tan(\alpha/2) = 10 + 2 \times 5 \times \tan(45°)
$$

Since $\tan(45°) = 1$:

$$
D_c = 10 + 10 \times 1 = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,D_c = 20 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

**(ii) Spindle speed:**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D_c} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 20}
$$

$$
N = \frac{25000}{62.832}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N \approx 397.9 \text{ rpm (round to 398 rpm)}\,}
$$

**(iii) Machining time:**

First, compute the table feed:

$$
V_f = f \cdot N = 0.15 \times 397.9 = 59.685 \text{ mm/min}
$$

Total axial travel = depth + approach length:

$$
L_{total} = h + L_a = 5 + 3 = 8 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
T_m = \frac{L_{total}}{V_f} = \frac{8}{59.685}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_m \approx 0.134 \text{ min} \approx 8.04 \text{ seconds per hole}\,}
$$

(7 marks: 2 for D_c, 2 for N, 3 for T_m with full derivation)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the depth/major-diameter formula — students often write $h = (D_c - D_p)/\tan(\alpha/2)$ instead of dividing by 2.
> 2. **Mixing up countersink (cone) with counterbore (cylinder)** — loss of 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 3. **Using the pilot diameter $D_p$ instead of $D_c$** when calculating cutting speed $V_c$. The cutting edge is at $D_c$, not $D_p$.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert mm/min to m/min** (factor of 1000) in the cutting-speed equation.
> 5. **Not writing units** — KTU examiners award marks for unit consistency even when numbers are slightly off.
> 6. **Confusing 60° with 82° with 90°** — always pair the angle with the screw standard (ISO 7721 → 90°, BS/ANSI → 82°).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Countersinking = conical recess** to seat a **flat-head screw** flush with the workpiece.
- ✅ Counterbore (cylinder, flat bottom) ≠ Countersink (cone, tapered).
- ✅ Standard angles: **90°** (ISO 7721 metric), **82°** (BS 450 / ANSI imperial), **60°** (DIN 74), **120°** (general chamfer).
- ✅ A **pilot** centres the tool; **flutes** evacuate chips and form cutting edges.
- ✅ Tool material options: **HSS** (Vc ≈ 15–25 m/min), **Carbide** (Vc ≈ 60–120 m/min).
- ✅ Core formula — **major diameter:** $D_c = D_p + 2 h \tan(\alpha/2)$.
- ✅ Core formula — **depth:** $h = (D_c - D_p) / [2 \tan(\alpha/2)]$.
- ✅ Core formula — **spindle speed:** $N = 1000 \, V_c / (\pi \, D_c)$.
- ✅ Core formula — **MRR:** $(\pi/4)(D_c^{2} - D_p^{2}) \cdot f \cdot N$.
- ✅ Machining time = (depth + approach) / table feed.
- ✅ Always carry **units** through every step; **use $D_c$** (not $D_p$) in cutting-speed and MRR formulas.
- ✅ $\tan(45°) = 1$ — a quick check that simplifies 90° countersink problems.
- ✅ Countersinking is a **secondary operation** — a pre-existing drilled (or cast/punched) hole is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trepanning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trepanning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Trepanning** is a specialized material removal machining process in which a **circular groove of controlled width and depth** is cut into a workpiece, leaving an intact central core (slug). The cutting action is performed by a *trepanning tool* — a rotary, annular multi-point cutting tool whose cutting edges are located on the periphery of an annular geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Trepanning is the process of producing a circular slot or cutting out a circular disk from a solid workpiece by means of a single-point or multi-point rotating tool. It is operationally a hybrid between **drilling** and **boring**, and is primarily used when the **diameter of the hole exceeds the practical limit of conventional drilling (typically > 50 mm)**.

Mathematically, the *width of the annular groove* (i.e., the radial engagement of the tool) is given by:

$$w \;=\; \frac{D_{o} \;-\; d_{i}}{2}$$

Where:
- $D_{o}$ = **Outer diameter** of the trepanning cut
- $d_{i}$ = **Inner diameter** (diameter of the central slug retained)
- $w$ = **Width of cut** (radial depth of engagement)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Donut Cutter" Mental Model

Imagine slicing a **ring (donut)** out of a thick slice of bread using a circular cookie-cutter tube. The bread is the *workpiece*, the tube is the *trepanning tool*, and the bread ring that comes out is the *chip* — except the bread ring is connected and continuous, wrapping around a perfectly preserved circular central plug (the *slug*).

A second, even more industrial analogy: think of an **oil pipeline inspection** or **railway wheel machining**, where you cannot easily drill a 200 mm hole (the drill would be massive, slow, and energy-hungry), but you *can* trace a 200 mm circle and only cut a 10 mm wide ring — leaving a 180 mm solid disc that simply drops out. That is trepanning.

## 1.3 Salient Engineering Metrics

- **Cutting Speed (V):** Recommended range **20 – 60 m/min** for steels, **40 – 90 m/min** for aluminium alloys.
- **Feed per Revolution (f):** Typically **0.05 – 0.30 mm/rev** for HSS tools; up to **0.5 mm/rev** for carbide-tipped tools.
- **Power Economy Ratio:** Trepanning consumes only **10 – 25 %** of the power required to drill an equivalent solid hole of the same outer diameter.
- **Material Utilization:** A central slug is *preserved* and can be reused as a blank for smaller components — a major economic advantage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Trepanning Matters in KTU Context:** The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) emphasises *process selection rationale*. Trepanning is the canonical example used by examiners to test whether a student can **justify the choice of a non-conventional hole-making process** based on energy, material economy, and tool life considerations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

Trepanning operation proceeds through the following sequenced logic:

1. **Workpiece Fixturing:** The workpiece (flat plate, cylindrical billet, or forged blank) is rigidly clamped on the machine table. For large workpieces, the *tool rotates* and the *workpiece is stationary*; for small workpieces mounted on a lathe, the *workpiece rotates* and the *tool is fed*.

2. **Tool Engagement Initiation:** A central pilot drill (also called a *lead drill* or *pilot bit*) makes initial contact with the workpiece to ensure concentricity. The pilot diameter is typically equal to $d_{i}$.

3. **Annular Cutting Action:** Multiple peripheral cutting teeth (typically **3 to 12 inserts** of HSS or carbide, brazed/mechanically clamped on the tool body) engage the workpiece simultaneously. Each tooth removes a chip whose cross-section is $a_c = w \times f$.

4. **Axial Feed Progression:** The tool advances axially (or the workpiece feeds, depending on machine kinematics) at feed rate $f$ per revolution, deepening the annular groove.

5. **Slug Separation:** When the depth of cut equals the workpiece thickness, the central slug is *freed* and drops out (or is ejected by an extractor pin built into the tool).

6. **Retraction & Indexing:** The tool retracts; for producing a *full disk* (solid trepanning), the operation is repeated with progressively larger $D_{o}$ until the disk is detached.

## 2.2 Classification of Trepanning Operations

| **Type** | **Description** | **Slug Status** | **Typical Use** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Hollow Trepanning** | Annular cut only; central core retained during cut | Slug ejected at end | Large-diameter holes, ring-shaped blanks |
| **Solid Trepanning** | Multiple concentric cuts progressively enlarge the bore | Slug progressively reduced | Full circular disks, large-diameter boring |
| **Lathe Trepanning** | Workpiece rotates; tool is stationary and fed radially | Slug drops from bore | Thin-walled rings, tube cutting |
| **Vertical Trepanning** | Tool rotates vertically; workpiece is a flat plate | Slug drops downward | Boiler tube sheets, large flange holes |

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Trepanning — Engineering Drivers

- **Power Economy:** Material removed = $\frac{\pi}{4}(D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}) \times L$ vs. drilling = $\frac{\pi}{4} D_{o}^{2} \times L$. Power saving is roughly proportional to $\left(1 - \frac{d_{i}^{2}}{D_{o}^{2}}\right)$.

- **Tool Cost Economy:** A 200 mm drill would cost ₹50,000+ and require a 50 kW spindle. A 200 mm trepanning tool costs ₹15,000–25,000 and runs on a 7.5 kW spindle.

- **Material Yield:** The retained slug is high-quality, unmachined parent material — ideal for downstream forging or smaller components.

- **Tolerance & Surface Finish:** Trepanned holes exhibit superior roundness (±0.025 mm) compared to large drilled holes (±0.1 mm or worse).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Width of cut | $w = \frac{D_{o} - d_{i}}{2}$ | mm | Radial engagement |
| 2 | Mean cutting diameter | $D_{m} = \frac{D_{o} + d_{i}}{2}$ | mm | For cutting speed at mean radius |
| 3 | Cutting speed (mean) | $V = \pi D_{m} N$ | m/min | N in rpm |
| 4 | Spindle speed | $N = \frac{V \times 1000}{\pi D_{m}}$ | rpm | |
| 5 | MRR (annular) | $MRR = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}) \times f \times N$ | mm³/min | f in mm/rev, N in rpm |
| 6 | Uncut chip area (per tooth) | $a_c = w \times f_z$ | mm² | $f_z$ = feed per tooth |
| 7 | Tangential cutting force | $F_c = K_s \times a_c \times Z$ | N | Z = number of effective teeth |
| 8 | Specific cutting force | $K_s$ (material dependent) | N/mm² | Mild steel ≈ 2000; Al ≈ 750 |
| 9 | Cutting power | $P_c = \frac{F_c \times V}{60\,000}$ | kW | V in m/min, F in N |
| 10 | Machining time | $t_m = \frac{L}{f \times N}$ | min | L = depth of cut in mm |
| 11 | Power saved vs. drilling | $\% \text{Saving} = \left(1 - \frac{d_{i}^{2}}{D_{o}^{2}}\right) \times 100$ | % | Direct ratio identity |
| 12 | Slug (core) volume | $V_{slug} = \frac{\pi}{4} d_{i}^{2} \times L$ | mm³ | Recovered material |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Examination Tip:** The cutting speed in trepanning is conventionally taken at the **mean diameter** $D_{m}$ (not $D_{o}$), because the peripheral cutting teeth engage the workpiece across the full radial width $w$, and the arithmetic mean radius best represents the *average* kinematical condition. Examiners frequently award or deduct a full mark on this single point.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power & Nuclear Industry:** Trepanning is used to cut inspection access holes in thick-walled pressure vessels and reactor shielding.
- **Aerospace:** Production of large-diameter bearing races and structural rings from forged billets.
- **Heavy Engineering (Boilers, Heat Exchangers):** Trepanning of tube sheets containing hundreds of holes — a single tube sheet may require 500+ trepanned holes of 50–100 mm diameter.
- **Automotive (Engine Blocks):** Trepanning of cylinder liner bores in cast iron blocks where a drill of that size is impractical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Trepanning

**Statement:** Derive the MRR for an annular (hollow) trepanning operation.

**Given:**
- Outer cut diameter = $D_{o}$
- Inner core diameter = $d_{i}$
- Axial feed per revolution = $f$
- Spindle speed = $N$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area of the Annular Cut:**

The annulus is the area enclosed between the outer circle (diameter $D_{o}$) and the inner circle (diameter $d_{i}$).

$$A_{cs} = A_{outer} - A_{inner} = \frac{\pi}{4}D_{o}^{2} - \frac{\pi}{4}d_{i}^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Axial Advance per Revolution:**

In one revolution, the tool advances axially by distance $f$ (mm/rev).

**Step 3 — Volume Removed per Revolution:**

$$V_{rev} = A_{cs} \times f = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}\right) \times f \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/rev]}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by Spindle Speed N (rev/min) to get MRR (mm³/min):**

$$\boxed{\,MRR = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}\right) \times f \times N \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Limiting Case Check (Drilling):**

If $d_{i} = 0$ (no core; equivalent to a standard drill), the formula collapses to the well-known drilling MRR:

$$MRR_{drill} = \frac{\pi}{4}D^{2} \times f \times N$$

This confirms the **mathematical self-consistency** of the trepanning formula. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Saving Percentage of Trepanning vs. Drilling

**Statement:** Prove that the power saved by trepanning (relative to drilling an equivalent solid hole of diameter $D_{o}$) is exactly equal to the *areal fraction* of the retained core.

**Step 1 — MRR of Solid Drilling of Diameter $D_{o}$:**

$$MRR_{drill} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_{o}^{2} \times f \times N$$

**Step 2 — MRR of Hollow Trepanning (Outer = $D_{o}$, Inner = $d_{i}$):**

$$MRR_{trep} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}\right) \times f \times N$$

**Step 3 — Since Specific Energy $u$ (J/mm³) is approximately constant for a given (material, tool, cutting parameters) set, Power ∝ MRR. Therefore:**

$$\frac{P_{trep}}{P_{drill}} = \frac{MRR_{trep}}{MRR_{drill}} = \frac{\frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}\right)}{\frac{\pi}{4}D_{o}^{2}} = 1 - \frac{d_{i}^{2}}{D_{o}^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Fraction of Power Saved:**

$$\boxed{\,\% \text{Power Saved} = \left(1 - \frac{d_{i}^{2}}{D_{o}^{2}}\right) \times 100\% = \left(\frac{D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}}{D_{o}^{2}}\right) \times 100\%\,}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration:**

Let $D_{o} = 100$ mm and $d_{i} = 80$ mm. Then:

$$\% \text{Saved} = \left(1 - \frac{80^{2}}{100^{2}}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - 0.64\right) \times 100 = 36\,\%$$

**Interpretation:** Trepanning a 100 mm hole with an 80 mm core slug saves **36 % of the cutting power** that would be consumed by solid drilling of the same 100 mm hole. The retained slug is 80 mm in diameter and can be reused.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cutting Power Requirement

**Statement:** Derive the spindle power required for a trepanning operation.

**Step 1 — Width of Cut (radial engagement per side):**

$$w = \frac{D_{o} - d_{i}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Uncut Chip Cross-Section per Tooth (assuming $Z$ effective teeth, each taking equal share of feed):**

The total feed per revolution is $f$. If this is distributed among $Z$ teeth, the feed per tooth is:

$$f_z = \frac{f}{Z}$$

The uncut chip area for one tooth is:

$$a_c = w \times f_z = \frac{D_{o} - d_{i}}{2} \times \frac{f}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Tangential Cutting Force per Tooth (from Merchant's shear-zone theory):**

$$F_{c,\text{tooth}} = K_s \times a_c = K_s \times \frac{D_{o} - d_{i}}{2} \times \frac{f}{Z}$$

where $K_s$ = specific cutting energy in N/mm² (empirical, material-dependent).

**Step 4 — Total Tangential Force on the Tool (all Z teeth contribute):**

$$F_c = Z \times F_{c,\text{tooth}} = K_s \times \frac{D_{o} - d_{i}}{2} \times f$$

**Step 5 — Cutting Velocity at the Mean Diameter:**

$$V = \pi D_{m} N = \pi \left(\frac{D_{o} + d_{i}}{2}\right) N \quad \text{[mm/min]}$$

**Step 6 — Spindle Cutting Power:**

$$P_c = F_c \times V = K_s \times \frac{D_{o} - d_{i}}{2} \times f \times \pi \left(\frac{D_{o} + d_{i}}{2}\right) N$$

$$\boxed{\,P_c = \frac{\pi K_s N f}{4}\left(D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}\right) \quad \text{[N·mm/min]}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to kW (divide by $60 \times 10^6$):**

$$P_c \;[\text{kW}] = \frac{\pi K_s N f \left(D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}\right)}{2.4 \times 10^{8}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Machining Time for Trepanning

**Step 1 — Kinematic Relationship:**

The tool makes $N$ revolutions per minute, advancing $f$ mm axially per revolution. Therefore the *axial feed velocity* is:

$$v_f = f \times N \quad \text{[mm/min]}$$

**Step 2 — Time to Trepan a Depth L:**

$$\boxed{\,t_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} = \frac{L}{v_f} \quad \text{[min]}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This expression is **identical in form** to the drilling time formula — the only structural difference is the *cross-sectional area* of the cut (annular vs. solid), not the *time per se*. KTU examiners frequently pose a *trick question* asking students to contrast the two — the time is the same, but the **energy consumed** differs drastically.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 120 mm diameter hole is to be produced in a 25 mm thick mild-steel plate by trepanning, retaining a 90 mm central slug. The cutting speed is 30 m/min, feed is 0.15 mm/rev, and $K_s = 2000$ N/mm². The tool has 6 effective teeth. Determine: (a) Spindle speed, (b) MRR, (c) Cutting force, (d) Cutting power, (e) Machining time, (f) % power saved vs. drilling.

**Solution:**

(a) **Mean diameter:** $D_{m} = \frac{120 + 90}{2} = 105$ mm

$$N = \frac{30 \times 1000}{\pi \times 105} = 90.94 \approx 91 \text{ rpm}$$

(b) **MRR:**

$$MRR = \frac{\pi}{4}(120^{2} - 90^{2}) \times 0.15 \times 91$$

$$= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6300 \times 0.15 \times 91 = 67\,521 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

(c) **Cutting Force:**

$$F_c = K_s \times \frac{D_{o} - d_{i}}{2} \times f = 2000 \times \frac{120 - 90}{2} \times 0.15 = 2000 \times 15 \times 0.15 = 4500 \text{ N}$$

(d) **Cutting Power:**

$$P_c = \frac{F_c \times V}{60\,000} = \frac{4500 \times 30}{60\,000} = 2.25 \text{ kW}$$

(e) **Machining Time:**

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} = \frac{25}{0.15 \times 91} = 1.83 \text{ min}$$

(f) **% Power Saved vs. Drilling a 120 mm Hole:**

$$\% \text{Saved} = \left(1 - \frac{90^{2}}{120^{2}}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.5625) \times 100 = 43.75\,\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Clamping and Alignment] --> B[Pilot Drill Engagement]
    B --> C[Peripheral Cutting Teeth Contact]
    C --> D[Annular Chip Formation]
    D --> E[Axial Feed Progression]
    E --> F{Depth of Cut equals Plate Thickness}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G[Slug Ejection and Separation]
    G --> H[Tool Retraction]
    H --> I[Quality Inspection of Trepanned Hole]
    I --> J[Slug Reuse or Recycling]
```

## 4.2 Trepanning Tool — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Trepanning Tool Body] --> B[Central Pilot Drill Section]
    A --> C[Peripheral Cutting Tooth Pocket 1]
    A --> D[Peripheral Cutting Tooth Pocket 2]
    A --> E[Peripheral Cutting Tooth Pocket 3]
    A --> F[Peripheral Cutting Tooth Pocket 4]
    A --> G[Slug Ejector Pin]
    A --> H[Tool Shank for Spindle Mounting]
    C --> I[Carbide Insert Indexable Tip]
    D --> J[Carbide Insert Indexable Tip]
    E --> K[Carbide Insert Indexable Tip]
    F --> L[Carbide Insert Indexable Tip]
```

## 4.3 Cutting Kinematics — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Parameters Outer Diameter Inner Diameter Feed Speed] --> B[Compute Mean Diameter]
    B --> C[Compute Spindle Speed]
    C --> D[Compute Uncut Chip Area]
    D --> E[Compute Tangential Force]
    E --> F[Compute Cutting Power]
    F --> G[Compute Machining Time]
    G --> H[Compute Power Saved vs Drilling]
    H --> I[Output Process Sheet]
```

## 4.4 Trepanning vs. Drilling — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T[Trepanning Operation]
        T1[Outer Diameter D Outer] --> T2[Annular Cut Region]
        T3[Inner Diameter D Inner] --> T2
        T2 --> T4[Retained Central Slug]
    end
    subgraph D[Solid Drilling Operation]
        D1[Diameter D Outer] --> D2[Full Cylindrical Volume Removed]
        D2 --> D3[No Slug Retained]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Reading Note:** The diagrams above are functional architecture block diagrams. In an actual engineering drawing, the trepanning tool would be depicted in *section view*, showing the annular cutting edges, the central pilot, the chip flute geometry, and the slug ejector pin. The block diagrams used here serve the pedagogical purpose of clarifying *functional flow* and *process sequence*, which is what KTU 2024 Scheme outcomes emphasise (CO mapping to Bloom's *Analyse* and *Apply* levels).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define trepanning. List any four advantages of trepanning over conventional drilling for producing large-diameter holes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Trepanning is a machining process in which a circular groove or slot of controlled width is cut into a workpiece using a rotating annular cutting tool, producing either a circular disk or an annular ring while leaving an intact central core (slug).

**Four Advantages (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**

1. **Reduced power consumption** — only the annular volume is cut, not the full cylindrical volume; power saving = $\left(1 - \frac{d_{i}^{2}}{D_{o}^{2}}\right) \times 100\%$.

2. **Material economy** — the central slug is retained as a high-quality blank for smaller components, yielding zero chip loss of the core.

3. **Smaller, cheaper tool** — a 200 mm trepanning tool costs a fraction of a 200 mm drill and requires a much smaller spindle (e.g., 7.5 kW vs. 50 kW).

4. **Better tolerance and roundness** — trepanned holes show ±0.025 mm roundness compared to ±0.1 mm or worse for large drilled holes.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between hollow trepanning and solid trepanning. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Hollow Trepanning** | **Solid Trepanning** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Material removed | Only the annular ring | Progressive concentric cuts down to a central disk |
| Slug status | Retained during cut, ejected at end | Progressively reduced in size; final disk recovered |
| Tool type | Annular cutter with internal clearance | Multiple cutters of progressively increasing diameter OR an annular cutter that sweeps radially |
| Application | Boiler tube-sheet holes, large flanges | Production of circular disks (gears blanks, bearing races) from billets |

**Hollow trepanning application (0.5 mark):** Trepanning of tube-sheet holes in heat exchangers (typically 50–100 mm dia., hundreds per sheet).

**Solid trepanning application (0.5 mark):** Production of large-diameter gear blanks from forged billets in heavy engineering.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**A mild-steel plate of thickness 30 mm is to be trepanned to produce a 150 mm outer diameter hole while retaining a 100 mm central slug. The recommended cutting speed is 25 m/min (at the mean diameter), the feed per revolution is 0.20 mm/rev, and the specific cutting force for mild steel is $K_s = 2000$ N/mm². The tool has 8 effective teeth.**

#### **(a) Calculate the spindle speed, MRR, and total cutting force. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mean diameter (1 mark):**

$$D_{m} = \frac{D_{o} + d_{i}}{2} = \frac{150 + 100}{2} = 125 \text{ mm}$$

**Spindle speed (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{V \times 1000}{\pi D_{m}} = \frac{25 \times 1000}{\pi \times 125} = \frac{25\,000}{392.7} \approx 63.66 \text{ rpm}$$

Take $N = 64$ rpm (rounded to nearest integer as is industry convention). **(1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for numerical result)**

**Material Removal Rate (2 marks):**

$$MRR = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}\right) \times f \times N$$

$$MRR = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(150^{2} - 100^{2}\right) \times 0.20 \times 64$$

$$MRR = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 12\,500 \times 0.20 \times 64$$

$$MRR = 0.7854 \times 12\,500 \times 12.8 = 125\,664 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$\boxed{MRR \approx 1.257 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}}$$

**Cutting force (2 marks):**

$$F_c = K_s \times \frac{D_{o} - d_{i}}{2} \times f$$

$$F_c = 2000 \times \frac{150 - 100}{2} \times 0.20 = 2000 \times 25 \times 0.20$$

$$\boxed{F_c = 10\,000 \text{ N} = 10 \text{ kN}}$$

---

#### **(b) Calculate the cutting power in kW, the machining time, and the percentage power saved compared to drilling a solid 150 mm hole. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Cutting power (2.5 marks):**

$$P_c = \frac{F_c \times V}{60\,000} = \frac{10\,000 \times 25}{60\,000}$$

$$\boxed{P_c = 4.167 \text{ kW}}$$

**Machining time (2 marks):**

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} = \frac{30}{0.20 \times 64} = \frac{30}{12.8}$$

$$\boxed{t_m = 2.344 \text{ min} \approx 2 \text{ min } 21 \text{ sec}}$$

**Percentage power saved (2.5 marks):**

$$\% \text{Saved} = \left(1 - \frac{d_{i}^{2}}{D_{o}^{2}}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{100^{2}}{150^{2}}\right) \times 100$$

$$= \left(1 - \frac{10\,000}{22\,500}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.4444) \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{Saved} = 55.56\%}$$

**Interpretation (not for marks, but expected in answers):** Trepanning this 150 mm hole retains the 100 mm slug and consumes only **44.44 % of the power** that solid drilling would require — confirming trepanning as the energetically and economically superior process for large-diameter hole-making.

---

### **Question B-B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**A horizontal lathe is set up to perform a trepanning operation on a 200 mm diameter cylindrical mild-steel workpiece. The trepanning tool has an outer diameter of 120 mm and an inner diameter of 100 mm. The axial feed is 0.25 mm/rev, the workpiece rotational speed is 80 rpm, and $K_s = 1900$ N/mm². The depth of cut (along the axis of the workpiece) is 40 mm.**

#### **(a) Compute the cutting speed, MRR, and cutting force. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Cutting speed at mean diameter (2 marks):**

$$D_{m} = \frac{120 + 100}{2} = 110 \text{ mm}$$

$$V = \pi D_{m} N = \pi \times 110 \times 80 = 27\,646 \text{ mm/min} \approx 27.65 \text{ m/min}$$

**Material Removal Rate (2.5 marks):**

$$MRR = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}\right) \times f \times N$$

$$MRR = \frac{\pi}{4}(120^{2} - 100^{2}) \times 0.25 \times 80$$

$$= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 4400 \times 0.25 \times 80 = 0.7854 \times 4400 \times 20$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 69\,115 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}}$$

**Cutting force (2.5 marks):**

$$F_c = K_s \times \frac{D_{o} - d_{i}}{2} \times f = 1900 \times \frac{120 - 100}{2} \times 0.25$$

$$= 1900 \times 10 \times 0.25$$

$$\boxed{F_c = 4750 \text{ N} = 4.75 \text{ kN}}$$

---

#### **(b) Compute the cutting power, machining time, and the volume of the recovered slug. Comment on the economic significance of slug recovery. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Cutting power (2 marks):**

$$P_c = \frac{F_c \times V}{60\,000} = \frac{4750 \times 27.65}{60\,000}$$

$$\boxed{P_c = 2.189 \text{ kW}}$$

**Machining time (2 marks):**

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} = \frac{40}{0.25 \times 80} = \frac{40}{20}$$

$$\boxed{t_m = 2.0 \text{ min}}$$

**Recovered slug volume (2 marks):**

$$V_{slug} = \frac{\pi}{4} d_{i}^{2} \times L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 100^{2} \times 40$$

$$\boxed{V_{slug} = 314\,159 \text{ mm}^{3} \approx 314.16 \text{ cm}^{3}}$$

**Economic significance (1 mark):**

The recovered slug is a *fully dense, unworked, defect-free* cylinder of mild steel with volume ≈ 314 cm³. At mild-steel density of 7.85 g/cm³, the slug mass is ≈ **2.47 kg**. Assuming a mild-steel scrap value of ₹40/kg and a finished-blank value of ₹90/kg, the slug's *value-retention benefit* is roughly ₹125 per trepanning cycle — a non-trivial economic gain when the operation is performed thousands of times in a boiler or pressure-vessel shop.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Cutting Speed Diameter Confusion:** Students frequently use $D_{o}$ instead of $D_{m}$ when computing cutting speed. This produces a ~10 % error and results in **deduction of 1 full mark**. Always use the *mean diameter* unless the question explicitly states "at the outer diameter."
> 2. **MRR Formula Mix-up:** Some students confuse *per revolution* and *per minute* MRR. The final MRR in KTU problems is **always per minute** (mm³/min). If you write MRR per revolution, you will **lose 1 mark** for not multiplying by $N$.
> 3. **Units in Power Formula:** The conversion factor $60\,000$ (from N·mm/min to kW) is often written as $60$ or $60 \times 10^3$ — both correct, but **the final numerical answer must be in kW**, not W or N·m/s. Unit mismatches cost 0.5 to 1 mark depending on the examiner.
> 4. **Drilling Comparison Slip:** When asked "compare with drilling," students often compare the *time* — but time is **identical** for the same depth and feed. The comparison must be on **power, material economy, and tool cost** — not on time.
> 5. **Slug Recovery Statement:** Failing to mention the *economic value* of slug recovery in long-answer questions costs up to **1 mark** in the "engineering judgment" component of the KTU 2024 rubric.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Definition:** Trepanning = annular (ring-shaped) cutting operation that produces a circular groove and retains a central core (slug). ✔️

- **Primary Use:** Large-diameter hole-making (typically > 50 mm) and circular disk blank production. ✔️

- **Key Geometric Parameters:** $D_{o}$ (outer), $d_{i}$ (inner), $w = \frac{D_{o} - d_{i}}{2}$ (width of cut), $D_{m} = \frac{D_{o} + d_{i}}{2}$ (mean diameter). ✔️

- **MRR Formula (most-tested):** $MRR = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}\right) \times f \times N$. ✔️

- **Cutting Speed Convention:** Computed at the **mean diameter** $D_m$, not at $D_o$. ✔️

- **Cutting Force Formula:** $F_c = K_s \times w \times f = K_s \times \frac{D_{o} - d_{i}}{2} \times f$. ✔️

- **Power Formula:** $P_c \text{ [kW]} = \frac{F_c \times V}{60\,000}$ with $V$ in m/min and $F_c$ in N. ✔️

- **Power Saving vs. Drilling:** $\left(1 - \frac{d_{i}^{2}}{D_{o}^{2}}\right) \times 100\%$. ✔️

- **Machining Time Formula:** Identical to drilling: $t_m = \frac{L}{f \times N}$. ✔️

- **Types:** Hollow trepanning (annular cut, slug retained) and Solid trepanning (full disk produced). ✔️

- **Tool Components:** Pilot drill, peripheral cutting teeth (3–12 inserts), tool body, slug ejector pin. ✔️

- **Key Advantages:** Power economy, material economy (slug reuse), smaller & cheaper tool, better roundness. ✔️

- **Industrial Applications:** Boiler tube sheets, pressure vessels, aerospace bearing races, gear blanks, reactor shielding. ✔️

- **Limiting Case Check:** When $d_i = 0$, all trepanning formulas collapse to standard drilling formulas. ✔️

- **KTU Bloom's Level:** This topic tests **Apply** (formula selection) and **Analyse** (process comparison) levels most heavily. ✔️

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reaming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reaming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Reaming** is a precision, low-material-removal, **sizing and finishing operation** performed on an already drilled (or bored) hole using a multi-point cutting tool called a **Reamer**. The objective is to obtain a hole of:
- Accurate **dimensional tolerance** (typically **IT7 to IT8**),
- Excellent **surface finish** (typically **Ra 0.8 to 0.4 µm**),
- Correct **geometry** (roundness and cylindricity),
- And to correct minor **axial/positional errors** left by the previous operation (drilling/boring).

It is a **secondary cutting process** and is *not* intended for heavy stock removal. Stock removal per side is generally between **0.1 mm and 0.4 mm** depending on the hole diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Reaming is a **finishing operation**, NOT a roughing operation. If the hole is undersized, do not compensate by over-reaming – re-drill or re-bore first.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have drawn a circle freehand on a piece of paper (this is like **drilling** – rough, slightly off-round). Now, you take a stencil of the exact circle size and trace it over your drawing (this is **reaming**). The stencil doesn't cut away much paper (low material removal), but it makes the circle **perfectly round and exactly the right size**. The stencil represents the reamer.

Another analogy: Reaming is to a hole what **fine sandpaper** is to a wooden surface – it is the "polishing" stage that achieves the final dimensions and surface quality.

## 1.3 Standard Reaming Stock Allowance (KTU High-Yield Data)

| Nominal Hole Diameter (mm) | Reaming Allowance per Side (mm) |
|:---:|:---:|
| Up to 12 | 0.10 – 0.20 |
| 12 – 25 | 0.20 – 0.30 |
| 25 – 50 | 0.30 – 0.40 |
| 50 – 80 | 0.40 – 0.50 |
| Above 80 | 0.50 – 0.60 |

> [!NOTE]
> The total stock to be removed by the reamer = 2 × (reaming allowance per side). This equals the **diametral oversize** provided in drilling/boring.

## 1.4 Why Reaming is Preferred over Boring for Small Holes

- **Cost-effective** for small diameter holes (< 25 mm).
- Reaming is **faster** than boring.
- Produces **better surface finish** than single-point boring.
- Produces holes of consistent size (high **repeatability**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reamer Geometry – The Anatomy of a Reamer

A reamer has multiple cutting teeth distributed around its circumference. The key geometric elements are:

### 2.1.1 Nomenclature of a Reamer Tooth

- **Cutting Edge (Flank):** The straight portion of the tooth that does the actual cutting.
- **Rake Face (Top of Tooth):** The face over which the chip slides.
- **Rake Angle (α):** 
  - For **steel workpiece:** α = **+5° to +15°** (positive rake).
  - For **cast iron and bronze:** α = **0° to +7°** (zero or small positive rake).
  - For **hard, abrasive materials:** α = **negative** (down to −10°).
- **Clearance Angle (Primary Clearance, γ):** Generally **5° to 15°**, provided to prevent the tooth flank rubbing on the machined surface.
- **Secondary Clearance / Margin:** A narrow cylindrical land (~0.1–0.3 mm wide) at the back of the tooth that:
  - Provides **support and guidance** to the reamer in the hole,
  - Performs the **sizing action** (sets the final diameter),
  - Prevents the reamer from chattering.
- **Chamfer / Lead Angle (Bevel Lead):** A small conical lead-in at the entering end (1°–2° chamfer) that initiates the cut gradually and prevents sudden loading.
- **Core Diameter:** Diameter of the reamer body at the root of the teeth (smaller than the cutting diameter).
- **Shank:** The cylindrical portion held in the machine spindle or floating holder.
- **Flutes (Gullets):** The helical or straight grooves that provide chip space and allow chip evacuation.

### 2.1.2 Number of Teeth

For a reamer, the number of teeth is chosen such that:
- The chip load is small (good surface finish),
- Sufficient chip space exists,
- Reamer is dynamically balanced.

**Rule of thumb:** For reamers of diameter *D* mm, the number of teeth (Z) is generally:
- For *D* up to 12 mm: **Z = 4 to 6**
- For *D* = 12–25 mm: **Z = 6 to 8**
- For *D* = 25–50 mm: **Z = 8 to 12**
- For *D* > 50 mm: **Z = 10 to 16**

For an **even number of teeth** with **irregular spacing** (e.g., unequal angular pitch), this is preferred for better surface finish (avoids vibration and resonance marks).

## 2.2 Types of Reamers (KTU High-Yield Classification)

### 2.2.1 Based on Method of Operation
- **Hand Reamer:** Has a square end (tang) for a tap wrench. Used for finishing holes by hand. Has a long taper lead.
- **Machine Reamer (Chuck Reamer):** Has a straight cylindrical shank for holding in a drill chuck. Used on drilling machines/lathes.
- **Floating Reamer:** Held in a floating holder that allows self-alignment with the existing hole axis. Used in production lines.
- **Shell Reamer:** The cutting part is a separate shell that mounts on an arbor. Used for large diameter holes (cost effective as cutting part can be replaced).

### 2.2.2 Based on Construction / Geometry
- **Straight Flute Reamer:** Flutes are parallel to the axis. Used for general purpose reaming.
- **Helical Flute Reamer:** Helical flutes for better chip evacuation in soft, gummy materials. Left-hand helix prevents the reamer from being pushed into the hole.
- **Adjustable Reamer:** Diameter can be adjusted slightly by moving the cutters in tapered slots. Used for reaming worn or oversize holes, and for specific non-standard sizes.
- **Expansion Reamer:** Has slots that allow the reamer to expand slightly when the shank is forced in. Used for fine adjustments.
- **Rose Reamer / Starting Reamer:** Has a beveled cutting edge for rough reaming, often used in conjunction with a finishing reamer.
- **Tapered Reamer (Taper Pin Reamer):** Used for finishing tapered holes (e.g., Morse taper).
- **Bridge Reamer:** Has long cutting edges with a steep taper lead. Used to align holes before bolting/riveting in bridges and large structures.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the following formulas as the primary toolkit for reaming numerical problems in KTU exams. The KTU typical question involves finding spindle speed, MRR, machining time, or power.

| Parameter | Formula | Symbol Description |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Cutting Velocity (m/min) | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | D = Reamer diameter (mm); N = Spindle speed (rpm) |
| Spindle Speed (rpm) | $N = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D}$ | — |
| Feed Velocity (mm/min) | $v_f = f \times N$ | f = Feed per revolution (mm/rev) |
| Depth of Cut (mm) | $d = \dfrac{D - d_h}{2}$ | $d_h$ = Pre-drilled/pre-bored hole diameter (mm) |
| Material Removal Rate (mm³/min) | $\mathrm{MRR} = v_f \times d \times \pi D$ | — |
| Equivalent MRR Form | $\mathrm{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d_h^2) \times f \times N$ | (Cross-sectional area × feed velocity) |
| Machining Time (min) | $t_m = \dfrac{L + l}{f \times N}$ | L = Length of hole (mm); l = Approach distance (mm) |
| Approximate Cutting Force (N) | $F_c = K_s \times A_c$ | $K_s$ = Specific cutting force (N/mm²); $A_c$ = Uncut chip area |
| Power Required (kW) | $P = \dfrac{F_c \times v_f}{60 \times 1000}$ | $v_f$ in mm/min |
| Number of Effective Teeth | Z (4 to 16, depending on D) | — |
| Feed per Tooth | $f_z = f / Z$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Cutting Velocity:** Reaming cutting speed is **significantly lower** than drilling. For mild steel, typical V = **8–15 m/min**; for aluminum, V = **15–30 m/min**. Using drilling speeds in reaming will cause **rapid tool wear and poor finish**.

## 2.4 Reaming Feed Recommendations (KTU Practical Data)

| Workpiece Material | Feed (mm/rev) |
|:---|:---:|
| Mild Steel | 0.30 – 0.80 |
| Cast Iron | 0.50 – 1.20 |
| Aluminum / Brass | 0.50 – 1.50 |
| Hard Steel | 0.15 – 0.50 |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive industry:** Cylinder bore finishing, valve guide holes, oil holes.
- **Hydraulic and pneumatic systems:** Precision pipe-port holes, manifold holes.
- **Aerospace:** Bearing seat holes, pin holes in structural assemblies.
- **General machine tool manufacturing:** Bores for shafts, pins, dowels in fixtures.
- **Tool room work:** Sizing of standard holes in jigs and fixtures.
- **Bridge & structural fabrication:** Bridge reamers align mismatched bolt holes between steel sections.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Reaming

Reaming is essentially a *plunge* operation (no cross-feed for through reaming). The MRR formula can be derived from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the Cross-Sectional Area Being Cut

The reamer removes an annular ring of material around the pre-existing hole.

$$
A_c = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d_h^2\right)
$$

**Textual explanation:** The cross-sectional area of material removed is the area of the larger circle (reamer) minus the area of the smaller circle (pre-drilled hole). The factor $\pi/4$ converts diameter-squared to area.

### Step 2: Define the Feed Velocity

In one spindle revolution, the reamer advances axially by an amount equal to the feed per revolution:

$$
v_f = f \times N
$$

**Textual explanation:** If the feed is f mm/rev and the spindle rotates N revolutions per minute, then in one minute, the reamer moves forward by f·N millimetres.

### Step 3: Compute the MRR

MRR is the volume of material removed per unit time. Since the cross-sectional area being cut per revolution is $A_c$ and the reamer moves forward by $f \times N$ mm per minute, the volume removed per minute is:

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = A_c \times v_f
$$

Substituting the expressions for $A_c$ and $v_f$:

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d_h^2\right) \times f \times N
$$

**Final MRR formula in expanded form:**

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{MRR} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d_h^2\right) \cdot f \cdot N \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}}
$$

### Step 4: Equivalent Form in Terms of Depth of Cut

If we use the depth of cut $d = (D - d_h)/2$, then:

$$
A_c = \pi \cdot d \cdot D
$$

(geometry of an annulus with large radius D/2 and small radius $d_h/2$ gives the area as $\pi(R_o^2 - R_i^2) = \pi \cdot \frac{(D+d_h)}{2} \cdot d = \pi D d$ approximately, when $d \ll D$).

Thus the equivalent form:

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = \pi D d \cdot f \cdot N
$$

This is the same as the **drilling MRR** formula. **Textual explanation:** The reason is that reaming is essentially a "drilling" process with a small radial depth of cut on a multi-tooth tool.

## 3.2 Derivation of Machining Time in Reaming

The reamer starts cutting only after traversing an *approach distance* $l$ (typically 3–5 mm) beyond the workpiece surface. It then travels through the actual hole length $L$. After exiting, the reamer continues for a small *overrun* distance $o$ (optional).

Total axial travel of the reamer:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = L + l + o
$$

The feed velocity is $v_f = f \times N$ mm/min.

Machining time:

$$
\boxed{t_m = \frac{L + l + o}{f \cdot N} \quad \text{[min]}}
$$

**Textual explanation:** Since the reamer advances f mm per revolution, in N revolutions per minute, the axial distance covered per minute is $f \cdot N$ mm. The total time is therefore the total distance divided by this velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> The overrun $o$ is generally **2–3 mm** for finishing reaming. For hand reaming, $l$ includes the entire lead-in chamfer length.

## 3.3 Cutting Force and Power Estimation

For a quick analytical estimate (used by KTU problems), the specific cutting energy approach is used.

### Step 1: Uncut Chip Area per Tooth

$$
A_{c,\text{tooth}} = f_z \times d = \frac{f}{Z} \times d
$$

**Textual explanation:** Each tooth removes a thin chip of width equal to the feed-per-tooth $f_z = f/Z$ and radial depth equal to the radial depth of cut $d$.

### Step 2: Cutting Force per Tooth

Using a generalized force formula:

$$
F_{c,\text{tooth}} = K_s \cdot A_{c,\text{tooth}}^{1-m}
$$

where $K_s$ and $m$ are empirical constants. For **simplified KTU analysis**, the *total tangential cutting force* is often given as:

$$
F_c = K_s \cdot A_c
$$

where $A_c$ is the *total uncut chip cross-section* (in mm²).

### Step 3: Power Required

$$
P = \frac{F_c \times V}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{F_c \times \pi D N}{60 \times 10^6} \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

Alternative, using feed velocity:

$$
P = \frac{F_c \times f \times N}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical KTU Assumption:** If specific cutting force is not given, use a **rule of thumb** for reaming: cutting force is **40–60% of the drilling force** for the same hole, because the radial depth of cut is much smaller.

## 3.4 Sample Worked Derivation: Numerical Form

**Problem:** A hole of diameter 30 mm is to be reamed to a finished diameter of 32 mm using a reamer with 8 teeth. The cutting velocity is 18 m/min and the feed is 0.4 mm/rev. The length of the hole is 60 mm and the approach distance is 5 mm. Calculate: (a) Spindle speed, (b) MRR, (c) Machining time.

**Given:**
- D = 32 mm (reamer diameter)
- $d_h$ = 30 mm (pre-drilled hole)
- Z = 8 teeth
- V = 18 m/min
- f = 0.4 mm/rev
- L = 60 mm
- l = 5 mm

### Part (a): Spindle Speed

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi D} = \frac{1000 \times 18}{\pi \times 32}
$$

$$
N = \frac{18000}{100.531} = 179.05 \text{ rpm}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 179 \text{ rpm}}
$$

### Part (b): MRR

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d_h^2\right) \times f \times N
$$

$$
D^2 - d_h^2 = 32^2 - 30^2 = 1024 - 900 = 124 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 124 \times 0.4 \times 179.05
$$

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = 0.7854 \times 124 \times 0.4 \times 179.05
$$

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = 38.96 \times 0.4 \times 179.05
$$

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = 15.58 \times 179.05 = 2790.4 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{MRR} \approx 2790 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

### Part (c): Machining Time

$$
t_m = \frac{L + l}{f \times N} = \frac{60 + 5}{0.4 \times 179.05} = \frac{65}{71.62}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_m \approx 0.908 \text{ min} \approx 54.5 \text{ sec}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Anatomy of a Reamer – Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture of a reamer, showing the structural hierarchy and the role of each component.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[REAMER ASSEMBLY] --> B[Cutting Section]
    A --> C[Body / Shank]
    A --> D[Driving Feature]
    
    B --> B1[Chamfer / Lead]
    B --> B2[Cutting Teeth]
    B --> B3[Flutes / Gullet]
    B --> B4[Margin / Cylindrical Land]
    B --> B5[Heel]
    
    B1 --> B1A[Conical Lead-In: 1 to 2 deg]
    B2 --> B2A[Rake Face: 0 to 15 deg Rake Angle]
    B2 --> B2B[Cutting Edge: Straight or Helical]
    B2 --> B2C[Number Z: 4 to 16 teeth]
    B3 --> B3A[Helix Angle: 0 to 45 deg]
    B3 --> B3B[Chip Evacuation Channel]
    B4 --> B4A[Width: 0.1 to 0.3 mm]
    B4 --> B4B[Sizing and Guidance Function]
    B5 --> B5A[Tapered Heel for Relief]
    
    C --> C1[Taper Shank or Straight Shank]
    D --> D1[Square Tang for Hand Reamer]
    D --> D2[Driven Slot for Machine Reamer]
```

## 4.2 Reaming Process Flow Architecture

The following flow diagram shows the reaming operation chain in a manufacturing context.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Workpiece with Pre-Drilled Hole] --> P2{Is Hole Round and Within Tolerance?}
    P2 -- No --> P3[Re-Bore or Re-Drill]
    P3 --> P4[Reaming]
    P2 -- Yes --> P4[Reaming]
    P4 --> P5[Quality Inspection]
    P5 --> P6{Dimensional OK?}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Finished Hole: IT7 to IT8, Ra 0.8 to 0.4 micron]
    P6 -- No --> P8[Re-Work / Reject]
    
    P4 --> F1[Cutting Speed V: 8 to 30 m per min]
    P4 --> F2[Feed f: 0.15 to 1.5 mm per rev]
    P4 --> F3[Coolant: Cutting Oil or Soluble Oil]
    P4 --> F4[MRR Calculation]
    
    style P4 fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P7 fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Process Parameters Decision Tree

```mermaid

    Start[Start: Select Reaming Parameters] --> Q1{Workpiece Material?}
    Q1 -- Mild Steel --> S1[V = 8 to 15 m/min, f = 0.3 to 0.8 mm/rev]
    Q1 -- Cast Iron --> S2[V = 8 to 15 m/min, f = 0.5 to 1.2 mm/rev]
    Q1 -- Aluminum --> S3[V = 15 to 30 m/min, f = 0.5 to 1.5 mm/rev]
    Q1 -- Hard Steel --> S4[V = 5 to 8 m/min, f = 0.15 to 0.5 mm/rev]
    Q1 -- Brass --> S5[V = 15 to 25 m/min, f = 0.4 to 1.0 mm/rev]
    
    S1 --> Calc[Compute N, MRR, t_m]
    S2 --> Calc
    S3 --> Calc
    S4 --> Calc
    S5 --> Calc
    
    Calc --> End[Outputs for KTU Problem Solving]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper Set A]**

**What is reaming? List any four different types of reamers used in production.**

**Model Answer:**

**Reaming** is a precision finishing operation performed on a previously drilled or bored hole to obtain accurate dimensions, correct geometry, and good surface finish. It uses a multi-point cutting tool called a **reamer**, with stock removal typically limited to **0.1–0.4 mm per side**.

The four types of reamers are:
1. **Hand Reamer** – operated manually, has a square tang.
2. **Machine Reamer** – held in a drill chuck, operated on a machine.
3. **Shell Reamer** – cutting shell mounted on an arbor (for large diameters).
4. **Adjustable Reamer** – diameter can be adjusted to compensate for wear.
5. **Helical Flute Reamer** – used for through holes in soft materials.
6. **Bridge Reamer** – used for aligning bolt holes in structural work.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Why is reaming classified as a finishing operation and not a roughing operation? What happens if excessive stock is given for reaming?**

**Model Answer:**

Reaming is a **finishing operation** because:
1. The stock removal is **very small** (0.1–0.4 mm per side).
2. The objective is to achieve **dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and geometric accuracy**, not bulk material removal.
3. The cutting speeds and feeds are **lower** than drilling, and the tool is **multi-tooth** for a smooth cut.

**If excessive stock is given:**
- The cutting force on each tooth becomes **high**, causing **chattering**.
- **Surface finish deteriorates** (roughness increases, chatter marks appear).
- **Tool life of the reamer reduces drastically** (teeth chip, margin wears).
- The hole may become **oversized, out-of-round**, or have **bell-mouthed** entry.
- Chips may clog the flutes, leading to **workpiece damage** or **reamer breakage**.

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / ESE Module 4 Practice Set]**

**(a) With a neat sketch, describe the geometry of a reamer. Explain the role of rake angle, clearance angle, margin, and chamfer in reaming. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A reamer consists of a **shank**, a **body with multiple cutting teeth**, **flutes**, and a **chamfer/lead** at the entering end.

**Sketch of Reamer (Schematic):**
```
                 |||||||||
                /         \
   ___________/           \___________
  |  ___  ___  ___  ___    |  <-- Reamer Body
  | /   \/   \/   \/   \   |       (Z teeth visible)
  | |***||***||***||***|  |       with flutes
  | \___/\___/\___/\___/   |
  |_________________________|
       |  (Chamfered)  |
       |  (Lead In)    |  
       |   _____________|  <-- Shank (Straight/Taper)
       |  |             |
```

**Roles of the key geometric features:**

1. **Rake Angle (α):** 0° to +15° for steel, slightly negative for very hard materials. It determines the chip flow and the **cutting force required**. A positive rake reduces cutting force and improves surface finish.

2. **Clearance / Primary Clearance Angle (γ):** 5° to 15°. It prevents the **flank of the tooth from rubbing** against the freshly machined surface, reducing friction and built-up edge formation.

3. **Margin (Cylindrical Land):** A narrow land (~0.1–0.3 mm wide) running along the periphery of each tooth. It provides:
   - **Sizing** – ensures the final diameter is held within tolerance.
   - **Support and guidance** – keeps the reamer concentric with the hole axis.
   - Prevents the reamer from **chattering** and wandering.

4. **Chamfer / Lead Angle:** A small conical lead-in (1°–2° chamfer length) at the entering end. It ensures **gradual engagement** of the teeth with the workpiece, avoiding sudden impact loading and ensuring a clean entry without burring.

5. **Number of Teeth (Z):** Generally 4 to 16 depending on reamer diameter. More teeth give better finish and balanced cutting, but less chip space.

6. **Flutes:** Straight or helical grooves for **chip evacuation**. Helical flutes are preferred for through-holes and softer materials.

**(b) A reamer of 25 mm diameter is used to finish a hole previously drilled to 23 mm. The cutting speed is 20 m/min and the feed is 0.3 mm/rev. The length of the hole is 40 mm and the approach is 3 mm. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Material removal rate, (iii) Machining time, (iv) Feed per tooth for an 8-tooth reamer. (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- D = 25 mm
- $d_h$ = 23 mm
- V = 20 m/min
- f = 0.3 mm/rev
- L = 40 mm
- l = 3 mm
- Z = 8

**(i) Spindle Speed:**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi D} = \frac{1000 \times 20}{\pi \times 25} = \frac{20000}{78.54}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 254.65 \text{ rpm}}
$$

**(ii) Material Removal Rate:**

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d_h^2) \times f \times N
$$

$$
D^2 - d_h^2 = 625 - 529 = 96 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 96 \times 0.3 \times 254.65
$$

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = 0.7854 \times 96 \times 0.3 \times 254.65
$$

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = 75.40 \times 0.3 \times 254.65
$$

$$
\mathrm{MRR} = 22.62 \times 254.65 = 5761.0 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{MRR} \approx 5761 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

**(iii) Machining Time:**

$$
t_m = \frac{L + l}{f \times N} = \frac{40 + 3}{0.3 \times 254.65} = \frac{43}{76.40}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_m \approx 0.563 \text{ min} \approx 33.8 \text{ sec}}
$$

**(iv) Feed per Tooth:**

$$
f_z = \frac{f}{Z} = \frac{0.3}{8} = 0.0375 \text{ mm/tooth}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_z = 0.0375 \text{ mm/tooth}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / ESE Module 4 Practice Set]**

**(a) Discuss the different types of reamers based on construction and method of operation. State at least one specific application for each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Based on Method of Operation:**

1. **Hand Reamer:** Operated manually using a tap wrench. Has a square tang at the shank. Used for **field work, maintenance, and tool room finishing** of precision holes where machine reaming is not possible.

2. **Machine (Chuck) Reamer:** Held in a drill chuck. Used on **drilling machines, lathes, and machining centers** for high-volume production.

3. **Floating Reamer:** Held in a floating holder that **self-aligns** with the existing hole axis. Used in **transfer lines and CNC production** for finishing bolt holes and dowel holes where minor alignment errors exist.

4. **Shell Reamer:** Cutting shell is mounted on an arbor. Used for **large diameter holes (> 25 mm)** in heavy-duty production; the cutting part is **replaceable**, making it economical.

**Based on Construction:**

5. **Straight Flute Reamer:** Most common, simple to manufacture. Used for **general purpose reaming** of through and blind holes.

6. **Helical Flute Reamer:** Has helical flutes (left-hand helix typically). Used for **through holes in soft, gummy materials** (aluminum, copper, mild steel) where chip evacuation is critical.

7. **Adjustable Reamer:** The teeth are mounted in tapered slots in the body. By moving the teeth radially, the diameter can be **fine-tuned** to compensate for wear. Used for **non-standard sizes and re-grindable cutters**.

8. **Taper Reamer (Taper Pin Reamer):** Has a tapered cutting section. Used for **finishing Morse taper holes, taper pin holes, and dowel pin holes**.

9. **Bridge Reamer:** Has a long taper lead and is used to **enlarge and align mismatched bolt holes** in steel structures and bridges before riveting or bolting.

10. **Rose Reamer / Roughing Reamer:** Has a heavy chamfer for roughing cuts. Used in **two-stage reaming** (rough + finish) for hard materials.

**(b) Explain the various defects encountered in reaming operations. State the causes and remedies for each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The common defects in reaming are:

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **1. Oversized Hole** | Excessive feed, reamer worn out, reamer misalignment, runout of spindle | Use proper feed, replace or regrind reamer, use floating holder, check spindle runout |
| **2. Undersized Hole** | Insufficient stock allowance, built-up edge, thermal contraction, excessive clearance angle | Provide proper reaming allowance, use coolant, reduce clearance angle, use sharper reamer |
| **3. Poor Surface Finish (Rough)** | Low cutting speed (built-up edge), high feed, worn reamer, inadequate coolant | Increase cutting speed, reduce feed, use sharp reamer, ensure good coolant flow |
| **4. Chatter Marks (Tear Marks)** | Insufficient rigidity, too few teeth, excessive runout, loose holder | Use more rigid setup, reamer with more teeth or irregular pitch, check tool holder |
| **5. Bell-Mouthed Hole** | Reamer misalignment, worn spindle bearings, insufficient guidance at entry | Use floating holder, check spindle alignment, use piloted reamer or bush |
| **6. Tapered Hole** | Reamer misalignment or worn reamer, axial force causing deflection | Use floating holder, replace reamer, use lead-in chamfer properly |
| **7. Reamer Chipping / Tooth Breakage** | Excessive stock, hard inclusions, insufficient coolant, sudden entry shock | Provide correct allowance, use proper coolant, gradual entry, check workpiece material |
| **8. Rapid Tool Wear** | Excessive speed, abrasive workpiece, insufficient coolant | Reduce cutting speed, use coated reamer, improve lubrication |
| **9. Out-of-Round Hole** | Misaligned spindle, worn machine bearings, reamer deflection | Machine maintenance, use floating holder |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse **bell-mouthed** (trumpet-shaped) holes with **tapered** holes. Remember:
> - **Bell-mouthed:** Diameter is *larger at the entry* and reduces along the depth (entry defect).
> - **Tapered:** Diameter *gradually changes linearly* along the entire depth (reamer misalignment).
> - These two defects have **different root causes** and require **different remedies**. Do not mix them up in Part B answers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Quick-Reference Table for Reaming

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|:---|:---|
| Reaming allowance per side | 0.1 – 0.6 mm (based on diameter) |
| Tolerance achievable | IT7 – IT8 |
| Surface finish | Ra 0.8 – 0.4 µm |
| Number of teeth (Z) | 4 – 16 |
| Rake angle (steel) | +5° to +15° |
| Clearance angle | 5° – 15° |
| Margin width | 0.1 – 0.3 mm |
| Cutting speed – Mild steel | 8 – 15 m/min |
| Cutting speed – Cast iron | 8 – 15 m/min |
| Cutting speed – Aluminum | 15 – 30 m/min |
| Feed – Mild steel | 0.3 – 0.8 mm/rev |
| Feed – Cast iron | 0.5 – 1.2 mm/rev |
| Approach distance | 3 – 5 mm |
| Overrun (after cut) | 2 – 3 mm |
| Chamfer angle (lead) | 1° – 2° |
| Coolant required | Yes (cutting oil for steel, soluble oil for cast iron) |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] Reaming is a **finishing operation** with low material removal (0.1–0.4 mm per side).
- [x] It is performed **after drilling or boring** to improve **dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and roundness**.
- [x] **Tolerance:** IT7 to IT8; **Surface finish:** Ra 0.8 to 0.4 µm.
- [x] Reamer has **multiple teeth (4–16)** with **rake, clearance, margin, and chamfer**.
- [x] **Rake angle:** +5° to +15° for steel; zero or slightly positive for cast iron; negative for very hard materials.
- [x] **Clearance angle:** 5° to 15° to prevent flank rubbing.
- [x] **Margin (cylindrical land)** does the **sizing action** and provides guidance.
- [x] **Chamfer/lead** ensures gradual engagement of teeth.
- [x] **Cutting speed** is *lower* than drilling: 8–30 m/min depending on material.
- [x] **Feed per revolution** is *higher* than drilling: 0.3–1.5 mm/rev depending on material.
- [x] **MRR formula:** $\mathrm{MRR} = (\pi/4)(D^2 - d_h^2) \times f \times N$.
- [x] **Machining time:** $t_m = (L + l + o) / (f \times N)$.
- [x] **Number of teeth** increases with reamer diameter (4 for small, 16 for large).
- [x] **Floating reamer holders** are used when hole-to-spindle alignment is imperfect.
- [x] **Shell reamers** are economical for large diameters.
- [x] **Bridge reamers** are used in structural/bridge fabrication for aligning bolt holes.
- [x] **Common defects:** oversize, undersize, bell-mouth, taper, chatter, poor finish.
- [x] **Common remedies:** proper allowance, sharp reamer, correct feed/speed, coolant, floating holder, machine maintenance.
- [x] **Coolant is mandatory** for steel reaming to prevent built-up edge and ensure good finish.
- [x] **Bell-mouth ≠ taper:** entry defect vs. alignment defect – remember the distinction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Tip:** When a numerical problem is given, ALWAYS show your **substitution step explicitly**. The KTU board examiner awards 1 mark for the correct formula, 2 marks for correct substitution, 2 marks for the numerical value, and 2 marks for the unit and conclusion. Don't skip the formula statement!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tapping. Milling: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tapping. Milling: Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tapping — Formal Definition

**Tapping** is a precision machining operation used to produce **internal threads** inside a pre-existing drilled hole (called a *tap drill hole*). It is performed using a multipoint cutting tool called a **Tap**, which has a series of cutting edges shaped exactly to the profile of the desired thread. The tap is rotated and simultaneously fed axially into the workpiece, progressively removing material to form the helical thread profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Tapping**
> Tapping is covered as a thread-generating internal cutting operation. Students must know the types of taps, the kinematics of tapping, and basic force/feed calculations.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a corkscrew being driven into wood — but instead of wood shavings coming out as a long spiral, the wood (or metal) is cut *thread-by-thread* from the inside walls of a pre-drilled hole. A corkscrew is a "pushing" tool, while a tap is a "cutting" tool that forms a permanent internal thread (like the grooves inside a bolt-hole). Each tooth of the tap acts like a tiny turning tool that scrapes away a chip from the wall of the hole.

### Standard Physical Metrics
- **Cutting Speed (V)** for tapping is typically **5 – 20 m/min** (much lower than turning/milling).
- **Tap Pitch (P)** = axial advance per revolution = **lead (l)** for single-start threads.
- **Standard Tap Drill Diameter (D₀)** ≈ **D − P** (rule of thumb for 75% thread engagement).

---

## 1.2 Milling — Formal Definition

**Milling** is a fundamental material-removal process in which a **rotating multipoint cutter** removes material from a stationary or slowly-moving workpiece by advancing the workpiece against the cutter (or vice versa). Each tooth of the milling cutter engages the workpiece for only a fraction of a revolution, producing **discontinuous chips**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of milling like a hand-held rotary rasp (file) being spun very fast and pressed against a piece of wood. As the rasp spins, multiple "teeth" gouge out tiny chips, one after another. In a milling machine, the *workpiece* moves past the spinning multi-tooth cutter — like a loaf of bread moving past a rotating bread slicer with many blades.

### Standard Physical Metrics
- **Cutting Speed (V)** = **20 – 60 m/min** for HSS cutters; **100 – 300 m/min** for carbide.
- **Feed Rate (f_r)** is expressed in **mm/min** (table feed) or **mm/tooth** (chip load).
- **Number of teeth (z)** on a standard cutter: **2 to 16**, depending on cutter type.

> [!NOTE]
> **Milling vs Turning — Key Distinction**
> In **turning**, the tool has one cutting edge and the workpiece rotates. In **milling**, the cutter rotates and the tool has **multiple teeth**, each of which acts as an independent single-point cutter.

---

## 1.3 Why Study Tapping and Milling Together?

Both processes share the concept of **multipoint cutting tools with defined geometry**, but differ in kinematics:

| Feature | Tapping | Milling |
|---|---|---|
| **Cutter** | Tap (with thread-form teeth) | Multi-tooth rotating cutter |
| **Workpiece motion** | Stationary (or rotating for large parts) | Translates linearly under cutter |
| **Operation type** | Internal thread generation | External/internal surface shaping |
| **Feed** | Equal to pitch per revolution | Independent variable (mm/tooth) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive — Tapping

### 2.1.1 Types of Taps (KTU High-Yield)

Based on application and geometry, taps are classified as follows:

**A. Hand Taps (Set of Three Taps)**

These are supplied as a set, each progressively cutting deeper:

1. **Taper Tap (No. 1)** — Chamfered over **7 to 10 threads**; used to start a thread in a difficult hole.
2. **Plug Tap (No. 2)** — Chamfered over **3 to 5 threads**; most common for general through-holes and blind holes (where allowed).
3. **Bottoming Tap (No. 3)** — Chamfered over only **1 to 1.5 threads**; used to cut threads as close as possible to the bottom of a blind hole.

**B. Machine Taps (Spiral-Point / "Bull-Nose" / "Gun" Taps)**
- Designed for high-speed production tapping.
- Have **straight or helical flutes** to direct chips **forward** (spiral-point) or **backward** (spiral-flute).
- Cannot be reversed easily.

**C. Pipe Taps**
- Used for tapered pipe threads (e.g., BSP, NPT).
- The tap itself is tapered, not the thread form.

**D. Collapsible / Chasing Taps**
- Used in automatic screw machines; collapse radially after cutting to release from threaded part.

**E. Thread-Forming Taps (Roll Taps / Fluteless Taps)**
- Do **not** cut chips; they **displace** material plastically to form the thread.
- Higher strength threads, no chip disposal problem.

### 2.1.2 Geometry of a Standard Tap

A tap consists of three regions:
- **Chamfer (Lead) Section**: First 3 to 10 threads are relieved (cutting takes place here).
- **Threaded Body (Full Diameter Section)**: Threads are at full diameter; provides guidance and minor cutting.
- **Shank**: Cylindrical portion held in the tap holder; has a square drive end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In tapping, the **chamfered teeth do the bulk of cutting**. The full-diameter threads mainly act as guides and do *very little* cutting.

### 2.1.3 Mechanics of Tapping

The tap performs two motions:
1. **Rotary motion** about its axis (cutting speed V).
2. **Axial feed motion** exactly equal to the thread pitch (P) per revolution.

Since the feed is **forced to equal the pitch**, the tap has **no free cutting feed** — it is essentially a *constrained* cut. This is why tap breakage is a common problem.

**Cutting Force Components in Tapping:**

$$F_c = \text{Component along cutting edge (torque-generating)}$$

$$F_a = \text{Axial force} = \text{Feed force that the machine must resist}$$

The ratio $F_a / F_c$ is typically about **1.5 to 2.0** for steel, depending on chamfer angle and lubrication.

### 2.1.4 Effect of Tap Geometry on Cutting

- **Hook angle (rake)**: Positive rake reduces cutting force; negative rake increases strength (for hard materials).
- **Relief angle**: Provides clearance behind the cutting edge to prevent rubbing.
- **Chamfer length**: A *longer* chamfer distributes the cut over more teeth, reducing force per tooth and tool wear.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive — Milling: Classification

Milling machines are classified in multiple ways. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **classification based on machine design and table motion**.

### 2.2.1 Classification Based on Spindle Orientation

| Type | Description | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal Milling Machine** | Spindle axis horizontal; cutter mounted on an arbor | Straddle milling, gang milling, plain milling |
| **Vertical Milling Machine** | Spindle axis vertical; cutter mounted directly in spindle nose | End milling, face milling, drilling, boring |

### 2.2.2 Classification Based on Table/knee Configuration

**A. Column-and-Knee Type Milling Machines**

The most common type, having a vertically adjustable knee that supports the table. Subdivided into:

1. **Plain Horizontal Milling Machine**
   - Table has only 3 movements: longitudinal, cross, vertical.
   - Spindle is horizontal.
   - Used for plain and straddle milling using a horizontal arbor-mounted cutter.

2. **Universal Horizontal Milling Machine**
   - Same as plain horizontal, but the **table can be swiveled (tilted) horizontally** through an angle of ±45°.
   - Allows milling of helical gears, spiral grooves, twist drills, etc.

3. **Vertical Milling Machine**
   - Knee-type with vertical spindle.
   - Cutter mounted directly in spindle (no arbor needed).
   - Subtypes: **Turret-mill** (overhanging ram) and **Bed-mill** (fixed spindle).

4. **Tracer-controlled Milling Machine (Duplicator / Diesinker)**
   - Reproduces a 3-D shape traced by a stylus (e.g., die-cavity machining).

**B. Fixed-Bed (Bed-Type) Milling Machines**

- No vertically-adjustable knee.
- Table moves only longitudinally.
- Column is a single rigid casting.
- **Higher rigidity**, suitable for heavy-duty milling.

Subtypes:
1. **Simplex Milling Machine** — Table has 2 movements (longitudinal + cross).
2. **Duplex Milling Machine** — 2 tables or 2 spindles for simultaneous work.
3. **Triplex Milling Machine** — 3 movements on the table.

**C. Special-Purpose Milling Machines**

1. **Rotary Table Milling Machine** — Circular worktable for machining large circular parts.
2. **Drum Milling Machine** — Convex/concave contours produced by a shaped drum cutter.
3. **Profile Milling Machine** — Reproduces a profile using a tracer.
4. **Planer-type Milling Machine** — For very long workpieces.
5. **Planomilling Machine** — Multiple cutters for high material removal.
6. **Duplex Milling Machine** — Two cutters simultaneously work on both sides.
7. **Thread Milling Machine** — Cuts threads using a thread-milling cutter.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Classification Question**
> Examiners frequently ask to "Classify milling machines" or "Differentiate between plain and universal horizontal milling machines" (3 marks). Memorize the sub-types and key differences.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Tapping Formulas

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | D = nominal tap diameter |
| 2 | Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rev/min | |
| 3 | Feed per rev | $f = P$ (pitch) | mm/rev | Forced feed; equals pitch |
| 4 | Feed rate (axial) | $f_r = P \cdot N$ | mm/min | |
| 5 | Torque | $T = F_c \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$ | N·m | $F_c$ = tangential cutting force |
| 6 | Tapping power | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta}$ | kW | $\eta$ = machine efficiency |
| 7 | Tap drill diameter | $D_0 = D - P$ | mm | 75 % thread engagement rule |
| 8 | Specific cutting force | $F_c = K_c \cdot A$ | N | $A$ = cross-sectional area of chip |

### Milling Formulas

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | D = cutter diameter |
| 2 | Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rev/min | |
| 3 | Feed per tooth | $f_z$ | mm/tooth | 0.05 – 0.4 typical |
| 4 | Feed per rev | $f_r = f_z \cdot z$ | mm/rev | z = number of teeth |
| 5 | Table feed rate | $f_m = f_r \cdot N = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | mm/min | **Master feed formula** |
| 6 | MRR (volume) | $Q = f_m \cdot a \cdot d$ | mm³/min | a = depth, d = width |
| 7 | Milling time | $t_m = \dfrac{L + l_a + l_o}{f_m}$ | min | l_a = approach, l_o = overtravel |
| 8 | Power | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta}$ | kW | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - **Tapping** is the *only practical way* to produce internal threads in mass production (e.g., engine blocks, gearboxes). Failure modes include tap breakage, oversize/undersize threads, and rough surface finish.
> - **Milling machines** form the backbone of tool-rooms and mass-production shops. Correct classification helps in choosing the right machine for a given contour, ensuring rigidity and productivity.
