# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tapping Time for a Through Hole

**Problem Statement:** A M12 × 1.75 mm thread is to be cut by tapping in a through-hole of length 40 mm in mild steel. The cutting speed is 10 m/min. Find the spindle speed, the feed rate, and the time required for tapping.

**Given:**
- Nominal diameter $D = 12 \text{ mm}$
- Pitch $P = 1.75 \text{ mm}$
- Length of hole $L = 40 \text{ mm}$
- Cutting speed $V = 10 \text{ m/min}$

### Step 1 — Spindle Speed

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$$

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 10}{\pi \times 12}$$

$$N = \frac{10000}{37.699}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 265.26 \text{ rev/min}}$$

### Step 2 — Axial Feed Rate

The feed per revolution equals the pitch because the tap is forced to advance by one thread per turn.

$$f_r = P \cdot N$$

$$f_r = 1.75 \times 265.26$$

$$\boxed{f_r \approx 464.2 \text{ mm/min}}$$

### Step 3 — Tapping Time

For a through-hole, the tap must travel the full length of the hole plus a small overrun to ensure complete thread formation at the exit.

Assume overrun $L_{over} \approx 3 \cdot P = 3 \times 1.75 = 5.25 \text{ mm}$.

$$t = \frac{L + L_{over}}{f_r}$$

$$t = \frac{40 + 5.25}{464.2}$$

$$t = \frac{45.25}{464.2}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 0.0975 \text{ min} \approx 5.85 \text{ s}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tap Drill Diameter for a Specified Thread Engagement

**Problem Statement:** For a M16 × 2 mm thread, find the tap drill diameter for (a) 75 % thread engagement and (b) 50 % thread engagement.

**Given:**
- $D = 16 \text{ mm}$
- $P = 2 \text{ mm}$
- $H = 0.8660 \cdot P = 0.8660 \times 2 = 1.732 \text{ mm}$ (the theoretical thread depth for ISO metric threads — derived from the 60° thread profile)

### Step 1 — General Formula

The tap drill diameter is the major diameter minus the thread depth that must remain *uncut* to form the thread crest. If $h$ is the actual thread height formed:

$$D_0 = D - h$$

### Step 2 — Thread Height for 75 % Engagement

$$h_{75} = 0.75 \cdot H = 0.75 \times 1.732 = 1.299 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_{0,75} = 16 - 1.299 = 14.701 \text{ mm} \approx 14.7 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Thread Height for 50 % Engagement

$$h_{50} = 0.50 \cdot H = 0.50 \times 1.732 = 0.866 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_{0,50} = 16 - 0.866 = 15.134 \text{ mm} \approx 15.1 \text{ mm}$$

### Conclusion
A smaller tap drill gives a *deeper* thread (more engagement) but requires more torque during tapping. A larger tap drill gives a *shallower* thread (less engagement) but easier tapping.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cutting Force and Power in Tapping

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the cutting force and power required to tap a M10 × 1.5 mm thread in mild steel. Specific cutting force $K_c = 1500 \text{ N/mm}^2$, cutting speed $V = 10 \text{ m/min}$, machine efficiency $\eta = 0.8$.

### Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of the Thread

The thread removes material from an annular region between $D_0$ and $D$. Using the 75 % rule, the thread height is:

$$h = 0.75 \times 0.866 \times 1.5 = 0.974 \text{ mm}$$

The uncut chip thickness in tapping corresponds to the thread height. The width of cut (per tooth) is approximated as $\pi \cdot D / z_{active}$, where $z_{active}$ is the number of active chamfer teeth (say 3 for a plug tap).

The cross-sectional area *per tooth* is approximated by:

$$A_{tooth} = \frac{h \cdot \pi \cdot D}{z_{active} \cdot \cos(\alpha)}$$

where $\alpha$ is the chamfer angle (typically 5°–10°). For simplicity, taking $\alpha \approx 0$:

$$A_{tooth} = \frac{0.974 \times \pi \times 10}{3} = \frac{30.6}{3} \approx 10.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 2 — Cutting Force

$$F_c = K_c \cdot A_{tooth} = 1500 \times 10.2$$

$$\boxed{F_c \approx 15300 \text{ N} \approx 15.3 \text{ kN}}$$

### Step 3 — Spindle Speed and Power

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 10}{\pi \times 10} = 318.3 \text{ rev/min}$$

$$P = \frac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta} = \frac{15300 \times 10}{60 \times 1000 \times 0.8}$$

$$P = \frac{153000}{48000} = 3.1875 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 3.19 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Milling Time for Plain Milling

**Problem Statement:** A slab 200 mm long, 80 mm wide is to be face-milled on a horizontal milling machine using a 100 mm diameter cutter with 8 teeth. The depth of cut is 3 mm. Cutting speed = 25 m/min, feed per tooth = 0.1 mm/tooth. Find the table feed and milling time, assuming approach and overtravel = 5 mm each.

### Step 1 — Spindle Speed

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 100} = 79.58 \text{ rev/min}$$

### Step 2 — Table Feed Rate

$$f_m = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.1 \times 8 \times 79.58 = 63.66 \text{ mm/min}$$

### Step 3 — Effective Length of Cut

The cutter diameter is 100 mm. In face milling, the cutter is wider than the workpiece, so the approach and overtravel are not related to cutter diameter. We use the given approach/overtravel.

$$L_{eff} = L + l_a + l_o = 200 + 5 + 5 = 210 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4 — Milling Time

$$t_m = \frac{L_{eff}}{f_m} = \frac{210}{63.66}$$

$$\boxed{t_m \approx 3.30 \text{ min}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Parameter | Tapping | Milling |
|---|---|---|
| **Feed control** | Forced (= pitch); no independent feed setting | Independent; controlled by table-feed handle |
| **Cutter entry** | Must align with pre-drilled hole | Free to plunge or feed from side |
| **Chip evacuation** | Critical (esp. blind holes) — flute design matters | Less critical for open surfaces |
| **Tool breakage risk** | **High** (no freedom in feed) | Moderate (independent feed control) |
| **Surface finish** | Thread form accuracy ±0.05 mm typical | $\sim 1.6$ µm Ra achievable |
| **Coolant** | Essential (cutting oil) | Flood coolant common; MQL trending |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Tapping Force So High?**
> The tap does not have a "free" feed; the entire force is concentrated on the chamfer teeth. This is why taps are made of **high-speed tool steel (HSS)** or **carbide** with surface treatments (TiN, TiCN) — to withstand high forces and abrasive wear.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tapping Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Workpiece with drilled hole] --> B[Select Tap Type and Size]
    B --> C[Mount Tap in Tap Holder / Chuck]
    C --> D[Set Cutting Speed and Coolant]
    D --> E[Align Tap with Pre-drilled Hole]
    E --> F[Engage Taper Tap for First Cut]
    F --> G[Use Plug Tap for Second Cut]
    G --> H[Use Bottoming Tap for Blind Holes Only]
    H --> I[Reverse Tap to Retract]
    I --> J[Inspect Thread with Ring Gauge]
    J --> K[Thread Complete]
```

## 4.2 Tap Anatomy — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Shank with Square Drive] --> L2[Threaded Body - Full Diameter]
    L2 --> L3[Chamfer Lead Section - Cutting Zone]
    L3 --> L4[Cutting Edges of Chamfer Teeth]
    L2 --> L5[Flutes for Chip Evacuation]
    L2 --> L6[Land - Provides Guidance]
```

## 4.3 Classification of Milling Machines (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MM[Milling Machines] --> COL[Column and Knee Type]
    MM --> FB[Fixed Bed Type]
    MM --> SP[Special Purpose Type]

    COL --> P1[Plain Horizontal]
    COL --> P2[Universal Horizontal]
    COL --> P3[Vertical Knee Type]
    COL --> P4[Turret Mill]

    FB --> P5[Simplex Bed Type]
    FB --> P6[Duplex Bed Type]
    FB --> P7[Triplex Bed Type]

    SP --> P8[Rotary Table]
    SP --> P9[Drum Type]
    SP --> P10[Profile Tracer]
    SP --> P11[Thread Milling]
    SP --> P12[Planomilling]
```

## 4.4 Tapping Kinematics — Spindle + Axial Feed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Spindle Rotates Tap] --> S2[Tap Threads Engage Hole Wall]
    S2 --> S3[Chips Formed in Chamfer Zone]
    S3 --> S4[Chips Travel Up Flutes]
    S4 --> S5[Axial Feed = Pitch per Revolution]
    S5 --> S6[Thread Profile Forms Progressively]
```

## 4.5 Milling Cutter Engagement — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Rotating Cutter with Multiple Teeth] --> C2[Tooth 1 Engages Workpiece]
    C2 --> C3[Tooth 1 Cuts Chip then Disengages]
    C3 --> C4[Tooth 2 Engages Workpiece]
    C4 --> C5[Tooth 2 Cuts Chip then Disengages]
    C5 --> C6[Cycle Repeats for All Teeth]
    C6 --> C7[Resulting Surface = Series of Tooth Marks]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**"List the different types of hand taps. Why are they used in a set of three?"**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

Hand taps are supplied in a set of three, each progressively cutting deeper into the hole:

1. **Taper Tap (No. 1):** Chamfer angle of ~7°–10° per side, cutting over 7 to 10 threads. Used to start the thread in a difficult or hard material hole.
2. **Plug Tap (No. 2):** Chamfer angle of ~3°–5° per side, cutting over 3 to 5 threads. Used for general-purpose through-holes and most blind-hole operations.
3. **Bottoming Tap (No. 3):** Chamfer angle of ~1° per side, cutting over only 1–2 threads. Used to cut threads as close as possible to the bottom of a blind hole.

**Why used in a set of three:** The chamfered teeth of a tap do most of the cutting. If a single tap had to cut the full thread in one pass, the cutting force per tooth would be too high, causing breakage. Distributing the work over three taps **reduces the chip load per tooth**, improves thread accuracy, increases tool life, and reduces the risk of tap breakage.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**"Differentiate between Plain Horizontal and Universal Horizontal milling machines."**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Feature | Plain Horizontal | Universal Horizontal |
|---|---|---|
| **Spindle axis** | Horizontal | Horizontal |
| **Table movements** | 3 (longitudinal, cross, vertical) | 4 (the above + swivel/tilt) |
| **Swivel range** | Not available | Table can swivel horizontally ±45° |
| **Workpiece capability** | Flat surfaces, slots, keyways, straddle milling | All of the above **plus** helical contours |
| **Typical application** | General-purpose milling | Helical gear teeth, twist drills, spiral grooves, cam grooves |

The **universal** machine is essentially a *plain* horizontal machine with an additional **swiveling table** (rotating about a vertical axis) that allows milling of helical and curved features. *(3 marks distributed: 1 mark for table movement, 1 mark for swivel feature, 1 mark for typical application.)*

---

## PART B (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Tapping

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the construction of a standard hand tap with a neat sketch. Differentiate between taper, plug, and bottoming taps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A M16 × 2 mm thread is to be cut by tapping in a hole of length 50 mm. The cutting speed is 12 m/min. Determine:
- (i) Spindle speed
- (ii) Feed rate (axial)
- (iii) Time for tapping (assume overrun of 3 × pitch)
- (iv) Tap drill diameter (use 75 % engagement rule)

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

### (a) Construction of a Hand Tap (7 marks)

A standard hand tap consists of the following parts:

1. **Shank** — The cylindrical portion held in the tap wrench. It has a **square drive end** to transmit torque from the wrench.
2. **Neck** — A small diameter portion between the shank and the threaded body (optional in some designs).
3. **Threaded Body** — The full-diameter threaded section that follows the chamfer. It primarily acts as a guide and does very little cutting.
4. **Chamfer (Lead) Section** — The first few threads (3 to 10) are relieved (cut away conically) to form the cutting teeth. This is where the **actual cutting takes place**.
5. **Flutes** — Helical or straight grooves cut along the threaded body to provide **chip space and cutting edges**.
6. **Lands** — The narrow cylindrical strips between the flutes that provide radial support and guidance of the tap inside the hole.
7. **Cutting Edges** — Formed by the intersection of the chamfered flank and the flute.

**Differences Between Taper, Plug, and Bottoming Taps:**

| Feature | Taper Tap | Plug Tap | Bottoming Tap |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chamfer length** | 7–10 threads | 3–5 threads | 1–1.5 threads |
| **Chamfer angle** | 7°–10° per side | 3°–5° per side | 1° per side |
| **Use** | Starting a new thread | General through/blind holes | Final pass in blind holes |
| **Cutting force** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Sequence in set** | First (No. 1) | Second (No. 2) | Third (No. 3) |

*(7 marks breakdown: 3 marks for parts, 4 marks for differentiation table.)*

---

### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 16 \text{ mm}$, $P = 2 \text{ mm}$, $L = 50 \text{ mm}$, $V = 12 \text{ m/min}$

**(i) Spindle Speed:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 12}{\pi \times 16} = \frac{12000}{50.265}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 238.73 \text{ rev/min}}$$

**(ii) Axial Feed Rate:**

$$f_r = P \cdot N = 2 \times 238.73 = 477.46 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$\boxed{f_r \approx 477.46 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**(iii) Tapping Time:**

$$L_{over} = 3 \times P = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ mm}$$

$$t = \frac{L + L_{over}}{f_r} = \frac{50 + 6}{477.46} = \frac{56}{477.46}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 0.1173 \text{ min} \approx 7.04 \text{ s}}$$

**(iv) Tap Drill Diameter:**

Thread depth for ISO metric thread: $H = 0.866 \cdot P = 0.866 \times 2 = 1.732 \text{ mm}$.

For 75 % engagement:

$$h = 0.75 \times 1.732 = 1.299 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_0 = D - h = 16 - 1.299 = 14.701 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{D_0 \approx 14.7 \text{ mm (Standard 14.7 mm or 14.75 mm drill})}$$

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks (i), 1 mark (ii), 2 marks (iii), 2 marks (iv).)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Milling Classification

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Classify milling machines in detail, explaining column-and-knee type and fixed-bed type with diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A face-milling operation is performed on a cast-iron workpiece (300 mm × 100 mm) using a 120 mm diameter HSS cutter with 10 teeth. Depth of cut = 4 mm, cutting speed = 30 m/min, feed per tooth = 0.15 mm/tooth. Calculate:
- (i) Spindle speed
- (ii) Table feed
- (iii) Material removal rate
- (iv) Milling time (assume approach = 10 mm, overtravel = 5 mm)

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

### (a) Classification of Milling Machines (7 marks)

Milling machines are broadly classified into **three main categories** based on design and purpose:

**1. Column-and-Knee Type Milling Machines**

These machines have a **vertically adjustable knee** mounted on the column, which supports the worktable. The knee can be raised or lowered to accommodate different workpiece heights. Subtypes are:

- **Plain Horizontal Milling Machine:** Horizontal spindle, table moves in 3 axes (longitudinal, cross, vertical). Used for plain, slot, and straddle milling with horizontal cutters mounted on an arbor.
- **Universal Horizontal Milling Machine:** Same as plain, but the table can additionally **swivel** about a vertical axis up to ±45°. Used for helical contours, twist drills, and gear teeth.
- **Vertical Milling Machine:** Spindle axis is vertical; cutter mounted directly in spindle nose. Used for end-milling, face-milling, drilling, and boring. Subtypes include **fixed-bed** and **turret (knee-and-column)** types.

**2. Fixed-Bed (Bed-Type) Milling Machines**

These have a **rigid single-piece bed** with no adjustable knee. The worktable moves only longitudinally. They offer **higher rigidity** for heavy-duty milling. Subtypes:
- **Simplex Bed-Type:** Table has longitudinal + cross movement (2 movements).
- **Duplex Bed-Type:** Two tables or two spindles for simultaneous work.
- **Triplex Bed-Type:** Table has 3 movements (longitudinal, cross, vertical).

**3. Special-Purpose Milling Machines**

Designed for specific applications:
- **Rotary Table Milling Machine** — Circular table for machining circular parts.
- **Drum Milling Machine** — Shaped cutter for convex/concave contours.
- **Profile (Tracer-controlled) Milling Machine** — 3-D reproduction from a template.
- **Thread Milling Machine** — Cuts external/internal threads.
- **Planomilling Machine** — Multiple cutters for high MRR on large surfaces.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks for column-and-knee, 2 marks for fixed-bed, 2 marks for special-purpose, 1 mark for neat classification diagram.)*

---

### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 120 \text{ mm}$, $z = 10$, $d = 4 \text{ mm}$ (depth of cut), $V = 30 \text{ m/min}$, $f_z = 0.15 \text{ mm/tooth}$, $L = 300 \text{ mm}$, $l_a = 10 \text{ mm}$, $l_o = 5 \text{ mm}$

**(i) Spindle Speed:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 120} = \frac{30000}{376.99}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 79.58 \text{ rev/min}}$$

**(ii) Table Feed Rate:**

$$f_m = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.15 \times 10 \times 79.58 = 119.37 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$\boxed{f_m \approx 119.37 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate:**

The width of cut equals the workpiece width (100 mm) since the cutter is wider.

$$Q = f_m \times a \times w = 119.37 \times 4 \times 100 = 47748 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

Converting:

$$\boxed{Q \approx 47.75 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 47.75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(iv) Milling Time:**

$$L_{eff} = L + l_a + l_o = 300 + 10 + 5 = 315 \text{ mm}$$

$$t_m = \frac{L_{eff}}{f_m} = \frac{315}{119.37}$$

$$\boxed{t_m \approx 2.64 \text{ min}}$$

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks (i), 1 mark (ii), 2 marks (iii), 2 marks (iv).)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Tapping — Don't confuse pitch and lead.** For single-start threads they are equal, but for multi-start threads (e.g., Acme, square) the lead = n × pitch. KTU expects the *axial feed* to equal the **lead**, not just pitch.
> 2. **Tapping time — Forgetting overrun.** Students often write $t = L/f_r$ for a through-hole. This is *wrong* — you must add an overrun of (3 to 5) × pitch for full thread formation at the exit. Marks are typically deducted for omitting this.
> 3. **Milling — Wrong use of formula.** In milling time, students sometimes forget the **approach and overtravel** distances. For *plain/slab* milling with a *small* cutter, these are based on cutter diameter; for *face* milling (cutter wider than workpiece), they are given values. Read the problem carefully.
> 4. **Milling classification — Naming only major types.** KTU expects the **sub-classification** (plain, universal, vertical, turret, bed-type simplex/duplex/triplex). Writing only "horizontal and vertical" earns partial marks only.
> 5. **Units — Always include units.** A numerically correct answer without units loses at least 0.5–1 mark. Rev/min, mm/min, kW — all must be stated.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Tapping** produces internal threads using a **multipoint thread-form cutter (tap)**.
- **Three types of hand taps**: Taper (No. 1), Plug (No. 2), Bottoming (No. 3) — used in sequence to progressively cut deeper.
- **Tap drill diameter** for 75 % engagement: $D_0 = D - 0.75 \times 0.866 \cdot P$.
- **Forced feed** in tapping: feed/rev = pitch (or lead for multi-start).
- **Tapping time** = (L + L_over) / (P × N); never forget the overrun for through-holes.
- **Tapping cutting force is very high** because there is no free feed — concentrated on chamfer teeth.
- **Milling** uses a **rotating multipoint cutter**; each tooth engages the workpiece discontinuously.
- **Milling machines** are classified into: (i) **Column-and-knee type** (plain horizontal, universal horizontal, vertical, turret), (ii) **Fixed-bed type** (simplex, duplex, triplex), and (iii) **Special-purpose type** (rotary, drum, profile, thread, planomilling).
- **Universal horizontal** is the most versatile — table can swivel ±45° for helical milling.
- **Key milling formulas**: $N = 1000V/(\pi D)$; $f_m = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$; $t_m = (L + l_a + l_o)/f_m$; $Q = f_m \cdot a \cdot w$.
- **Milling cutter choice** depends on operation: end mill (slots), face mill (large surfaces), slab mill (straddle/gang), side-and-face mill (deep slots), angle mill (V-grooves, dovetails).
- **Always state units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners are strict on this.
- **Tapping vs Threading dies**: Tapping makes *internal* threads; dies make *external* threads. KTU may ask this distinction.
- **Coolant is mandatory in tapping** to prevent tap breakage; chip evacuation is critical in blind holes — use **spiral-point** taps for through holes, **spiral-flute** taps for blind holes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Column and Knee Type Milling Machine, Milling Cutters and Classification, Fundamentals of Milling Processes, Milling Operations. Cutting Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Column and Knee Type Milling Machine, Milling Cutters and Classification, Fundamentals of Milling Processes, Milling Operations. Cutting Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Milling Machine?

A **milling machine** is a versatile, power-driven machine tool used in manufacturing to remove material from a workpiece by feeding it against a rotating multi-point cutter. Unlike a lathe where the workpiece rotates, in milling the **cutter rotates** while the workpiece remains stationary (or moves linearly), producing flat, angled, or complex surfaces with high dimensional accuracy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):** A milling machine is a machine tool that uses revolving cutters to remove material from the surface of a workpiece, where the cutter is fed against the workpiece in a direction perpendicular to the axis of rotation of the cutter.

## 1.2 The Column and Knee Type Milling Machine — Definition

The **Column and Knee Type Milling Machine** is the most common and widely used general-purpose milling machine. It is named so because of its two prominent structural members: a **column** (a vertical cast-iron pillar at the back) and a **knee** (a vertically adjustable casting on the column's front that supports the worktable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Column and Knee"?** The **column** provides rigidity and houses the spindle/drive mechanism, while the **knee** carries the table and saddle and can be raised or lowered to accommodate workpieces of different heights. Together, they form the structural backbone of the machine.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Carpenter's Router on Steroids"

Imagine a carpenter's hand-held **wood router**: it spins a cutting bit at high speed while you push a wooden board beneath it. The router carves grooves and shapes.

A **column and knee type milling machine** is essentially an industrial, super-precise version of this concept:
- The **router** → becomes the **rotating multi-tooth milling cutter** mounted on a rigid vertical spindle.
- The **wooden board** → becomes a clamped **metal workpiece** on a sturdy table.
- Your **hand movement** → is replaced by a **motorized lead screw** that feeds the table with micrometer-level precision.
- The **woodworking table** → is replaced by a heavy **knee-and-saddle assembly** that can move in X, Y, and Z directions.

The result? You get accurate, repeatable metal cutting that a human hand could never achieve — producing slots, gears, splines, and complex 3D contours.

## 1.4 What are Milling Cutters?

**Milling cutters** are the multi-point cutting tools that perform the actual material removal. Each cutter has multiple **teeth (cutting edges)** arranged around its periphery or face, allowing for **interrupted cutting** (each tooth cuts only during a portion of the revolution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Broad Strokes:** Cutters are classified based on (a) **shape of the cutter** (plain milling cutter, side milling cutter, end mill, face mill), (b) **mounting method** (arbor-type or shank-type), and (c) **direction of rotation relative to feed** (climb/Up milling vs. conventional/Down milling).

## 1.5 What is Cutting Speed in Milling?

**Cutting speed (V)** is the relative surface speed between the cutter's periphery (teeth) and the workpiece — i.e., the speed at which the metal is "swept" by the tooth tip. It is measured in **meters per minute (m/min)** and is the primary kinematic parameter that controls heat generation, tool life, and surface finish.

**Standard industry metric:** $V = 20$ to $60$ **m/min** for mild steel, $V = 30$ to $90$ **m/min** for cast iron, and $V = 100$ to $250$ **m/min** for aluminum alloys (with carbide cutters).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parts of the Column and Knee Type Milling Machine

A column and knee milling machine has approximately **15–20 major parts**. Let us break them down logically.

### 2.1.1 The Base (Foundation)
- **What it is:** A heavy cast-iron bed that supports the entire machine and absorbs vibrations.
- **Why it matters:** Mass = rigidity = vibration damping. Heavy bases mean better surface finish and longer tool life.

### 2.1.2 The Column (Backbone)
- **What it is:** A vertical, box-shaped casting rising from the base at the rear.
- **Contains:** The **spindle**, **spindle motor drive**, **gear train** (for speed changes), and the **overarm/overhanging arm guide**.
- **Why it matters:** Houses the rotating element that drives the cutter.

### 2.1.3 The Knee (Vertical Feeder)
- **What it is:** A casting mounted on the front face of the column, sliding on vertical ways.
- **Movement:** Can be raised or lowered along the column's vertical guideways via a **vertical feed screw** and an elevating handwheel.
- **Why it matters:** Adjusts the worktable height to suit different workpiece sizes.

### 2.1.4 The Saddle (Cross-Slide)
- **What it is:** Mounted on top of the knee, slides horizontally (Y-axis) on dovetail ways.
- **Movement:** Driven by **cross-feed screw** and handwheel.
- **Why it matters:** Provides Y-direction positioning of the table.

### 2.1.5 The Table (Work-Holder)
- **What it is:** The rectangular cast-iron top mounted on the saddle, with **T-slots** for clamping workpieces and fixtures.
- **Movement:** Slides longitudinally (X-axis) via a **table feed screw** and handwheel.
- **Why it matters:** Carries the workpiece into the rotating cutter.

### 2.1.6 The Spindle (Cutter Driver)
- **What it is:** A precision-ground shaft that rotates the cutter.
- **Why it matters:** Rotational accuracy here defines the geometric accuracy of the machined surface.

### 2.1.7 The Overarm (Arbor Support)
- **What it is:** A horizontal arm projecting from the column top, supporting the **outer end of the arbor** (for long horizontal cutters).
- **Why it matters:** Prevents arbor deflection for cutters that cannot be cantilevered.

### 2.1.8 Drive and Feed Mechanism
- **What it is:** A system of gears, clutches, and lead screws that deliver rotational motion to the spindle and translational motion to the table.
- **Why it matters:** Determines the available **spindle speeds (RPM)** and **feed rates (mm/min)**.

## 2.2 Types of Column and Knee Milling Machines

There are **three main variants** under this family:

### Type 1: Plain (Horizontal Spindle) Knee and Column Milling Machine
- Spindle is **horizontal only**.
- Uses an **arbor** to hold cutters like plain milling cutters, side-and-face cutters, slot mills, and gang cutters.
- Best for **peripheral milling** operations on flat surfaces and slots.

### Type 2: Vertical (Vertical Spindle) Knee and Column Milling Machine
- Spindle is **vertical**.
- Holds **end mills, face mills, and shell mills** directly via a chuck or collet.
- Best for **face milling, end milling, drilling, reaming, and boring**.
- Often called a **"Bridgeport-type"** milling machine.

### Type 3: Universal Milling Machine
- A horizontal machine where the **table can be swivelled** (rotated about a vertical axis) up to ±45°.
- Allows machining of **helical surfaces, gears, twist drills, and spiral flutes**.

## 2.3 Classification of Milling Cutters (High-Yield Table for KTU)

| **S.No** | **Cutter Type** | **Description** | **Typical Use** |
|----------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Plain Milling Cutter** | Cylindrical body, teeth on periphery only | Flat horizontal surfaces, gang milling |
| 2 | **Side Milling Cutter** | Teeth on periphery and one or both sides | Slots, keyways, open-sided surfaces |
| 3 | **End Mill** | Teeth on periphery + end cutting edges; shank mounted | Slots, pockets, profiling, die work |
| 4 | **Face Mill** | Teeth on a flat circular face; mounted on an arbor | Large flat surfaces (high MRR) |
| 5 | **T-Slot Cutter** | T-shaped teeth, used after slot milling | Cutting T-slots in tables and fixtures |
| 6 | **Angle Milling Cutter** | Teeth angled to a central groove | Dovetails, V-grooves, angular surfaces |
| 7 | **Formed Cutter** | Cutting edge shaped to a profile | Convex/concave profiles, gear teeth |
| 8 | **Fly Cutter** | Single-point tool held in a fly cutter holder | Light finishing, large-diameter cuts |
| 9 | **Hob** | Helical-toothed cutter, rotates in synchronism | Gear cutting on a hobbing machine |
| 10 | **Woodruff Key Cutter** | Small ring-shaped cutter | Cutting Woodruff keyways |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Of all cutters, the **plain milling cutter, side milling cutter, end mill, and face mill** are the four most asked-about in examinations. Always remember the **direction of teeth** — **straight teeth** for plain cutters, **helical teeth** for heavy-duty cutters (helical teeth engage gradually, reducing chatter).

## 2.4 Fundamentals of the Milling Process

### 2.4.1 Up Milling (Conventional Milling) — Definition
In **up milling**, the cutter rotates **opposite** to the feed direction. The cutter tooth approaches the workpiece from below and the chip thickness is **maximum at the end of the cut** and **zero at the start**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Cue:** If the table moves to the **left** and the cutter rotates **counter-clockwise (when viewed from the spindle end)**, it is **up milling**.

### 2.4.2 Down Milling (Climb Milling) — Definition
In **down milling**, the cutter rotates **in the same direction** as the feed. The tooth engages with **maximum chip thickness at the start** and **zero chip thickness at the exit**.

### 2.4.3 Up Milling vs. Down Milling — Comparative Analysis

| **Parameter** | **Up Milling (Conventional)** | **Down Milling (Climb)** |
|---------------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Chip thickness | Minimum at start, max at end | Maximum at start, min at end |
| Cutter force on workpiece | Tries to **lift** the workpiece | Tries to **push** the workpiece |
| Clamping requirement | Light clamping sufficient | Requires **rigid** clamping + backlash-free table |
| Tooth load | Gradual (tooth life better) | Sudden (more shock) |
| Surface finish | Better (teeth slide on workpiece at start) | Slightly inferior on rough machines |
| Power consumption | Higher (full tooth engaged at end) | Lower |
| Chatter tendency | Lower | Higher (if machine has backlash) |
| Preferred when | Older machines, castings with hard scale | Modern CNC machines, no backlash |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often write "down milling" is always superior. This is **false** — down milling **requires zero backlash** in the table feed screw. On a worn manual machine, down milling can violently jerk the table and break the cutter. **Always check machine condition first.**

## 2.5 Milling Operations

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following key operations:

### 2.5.1 Plain (Slab) Milling
- A **plain milling cutter** mounted on an arbor removes material from a flat surface.
- Operation: Peripheral milling.

### 2.5.2 Face Milling
- A **face mill** with its axis perpendicular to the workpiece surface.
- Produces a flat surface with a better finish than plain milling.

### 2.5.3 End Milling
- An **end mill** (cylindrical cutter with end teeth) is used to produce slots, pockets, and contours.
- Can be performed on vertical or horizontal machines (with end mill holder).

### 2.5.4 Slot Milling
- A **slitting saw** or **plain cutter** of the required width cuts a closed slot.

### 2.5.5 Gang Milling
- **Multiple cutters** mounted on the same arbor perform several operations in a single pass.

### 2.5.6 Profile (Form) Milling
- A **form cutter** (with shaped teeth) reproduces a profile on the workpiece — e.g., concave, convex, or gear-tooth profile.

### 2.5.7 Straddle Milling
- **Two side-milling cutters** mounted on a single arbor simultaneously cut both sides of a workpiece to produce a specific width (e.g., square or hexagonal blocks).

### 2.5.8 Angular Milling
- A cutter with angled teeth (like a single-angle cutter) produces V-grooves, dovetails, and chamfers.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are KTU 2024 exam-standard. **No pipes (`|`) used** in any cell to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Typical Range** |
|--------------|------------|-------------|----------|-------------------|
| Cutting Speed | $V$ | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | **m/min** | $20$ to $60$ (steel) |
| Spindle Speed | $N$ | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | **rev/min (rpm)** | $50$ to $3000$ |
| Feed per Tooth | $f_z$ | $f_z = \dfrac{f}{N \cdot z}$ | **mm/tooth** | $0.02$ to $0.30$ |
| Table Feed rate | $f$ | $f = f_z \cdot N \cdot z$ | **mm/min** | $20$ to $2000$ |
| Material Removal Rate | $MRR$ | $MRR = w \cdot d \cdot f$ | **mm³/min** | $\geq 10^4$ typical |
| Feed Per Revolution (milling) | $f_r$ | $f_r = f_z \cdot z$ | **mm/rev** | — |
| Time for Milling | $T_m$ | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f} = \dfrac{L + l_a + l_o}{f}$ | **min** | — |
| Power Required | $P$ | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot \text{Unit Power}}{60}$ | **kW** | $1$ to $15$ typical |

> **Where:** $D$ = cutter diameter (mm), $N$ = spindle speed (rpm), $z$ = number of teeth, $w$ = width of cut (mm), $d$ = depth of cut (mm), $L$ = length of workpiece (mm), $l_a$ = approach length, $l_o$ = overtravel length.

**Symbols used:** $\pi$ = mathematical pi (3.14159...), $z$ = number of cutter teeth, $N$ = rotational speed.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Engine blocks, cylinder heads, gearbox housings → face-milled and end-milled.
- **Aerospace:** Wing ribs, fuselage frames machined on 5-axis CNC mills.
- **Tool rooms:** Dies, moulds, jigs, fixtures — predominantly **vertical knee-type mills** (the workhorse of every tool room).
- **Heavy industry:** Large plate milling — **bed-type mills** (a derivative of column and knee type).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Cutting Speed Formula in Milling

The **cutting speed** is the tangential velocity at the periphery of the milling cutter. The derivation follows directly from the kinematic relationship between rotational motion and linear distance.

### Step 1: Define Cutter Periphery
Consider a milling cutter of diameter $D$ rotating at $N$ revolutions per minute (rpm). The **peripheral circumference** of the cutter is:

$$C = \pi \cdot D$$

where $C$ is the circumference in **mm** (since $D$ is in mm).

### Step 2: Distance Travelled per Minute by a Cutter Tooth
In **one revolution**, every tooth on the cutter periphery sweeps a distance equal to the circumference $C$. In **$N$ revolutions per minute**, the total linear distance swept per minute is:

$$L_{\text{per min}} = C \cdot N = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$

This distance is in **mm/min** when $D$ is in mm.

### Step 3: Convert mm/min to m/min (Standard KTU Unit)
The KTU convention uses **meters per minute (m/min)** for cutting speed. Converting:

$$V = \dfrac{L_{\text{per min}}}{1000} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$$

### Step 4: Final Cutting Speed Equation

$$\boxed{V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{[m/min]}}$$

### Step 5: Derive the Spindle Speed Formula
Rearranging the above for $N$ (since we usually know $V$ from the tool manufacturer and $D$ from the chosen cutter):

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \text{[rpm]}$$

> **Verification by Dimensional Check:** $[\text{m/min}] = \dfrac{[\text{mm}] \cdot [\text{rev/min}]}{1000 [\text{mm/m}]}$ → units cancel correctly, leaving pure $\text{m/min}$. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation of Table Feed Rate and Feed per Tooth

The **feed per tooth** ($f_z$) is the linear distance the table advances during the time one tooth is engaged in the cut.

### Step 1: Time per Revolution
Time for one full revolution of the cutter is:

$$t_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{60}{N} \quad \text{[seconds per revolution]}$$

### Step 2: Time per Tooth Engagement
Since the cutter has $z$ teeth, each tooth engages $z$ times per revolution, so the time between successive tooth engagements is:

$$t_{\text{tooth}} = \dfrac{60}{N \cdot z} \quad \text{[seconds per tooth engagement]}$$

### Step 3: Define Feed per Tooth
By definition, the **feed per tooth** is the table displacement during one tooth engagement:

$$f_z = f_{\text{rate}} \cdot t_{\text{tooth}} = f_{\text{rate}} \cdot \dfrac{60}{N \cdot z}$$

Solving for the feed rate $f_{\text{rate}}$ (mm/min):

$$f_{\text{rate}} = f_z \cdot N \cdot z \quad \text{[mm/min]}$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for the table feed rate in milling.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Board Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a representative KTU 2024 numerical problem. Work through it step-by-step to master the pattern.

**Problem:** A mild steel workpiece $300$ **mm** long, $80$ **mm** wide, is to be face-milled using an HSS face mill of diameter $D = 100$ **mm** with $z = 8$ teeth. The recommended cutting speed is $V = 30$ **m/min** and the feed per tooth is $f_z = 0.20$ **mm/tooth**. The depth of cut is $d = 3$ **mm**. Calculate: (a) Spindle speed $N$, (b) Table feed rate, (c) MRR, (d) Approximate cutting time (take approach length $l_a = 5$ **mm** and overtravel $l_o = 5$ **mm**).

### Part (a): Spindle Speed $N$

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 100} = \dfrac{30000}{314.159}$$

$$N \approx 95.49 \text{ rpm}$$

The KTU-accepted answer: $N \approx 95$ **rpm** (rounded).

### Part (b): Table Feed Rate

$$f_{\text{rate}} = f_z \cdot N \cdot z = 0.20 \times 95.49 \times 8$$

$$f_{\text{rate}} = 152.78 \text{ mm/min}$$

KTU-accepted: $f \approx 153$ **mm/min**.

### Part (c): Material Removal Rate (MRR)

In face milling, the cross-sectional area of cut is the **width × depth**:

$$MRR = w \cdot d \cdot f = 80 \times 3 \times 152.78$$

$$MRR = 36{,}667.2 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 3.67 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

KTU-accepted: $MRR \approx 36{,}667$ **mm³/min**.

### Part (d): Cutting Time

The total table travel is $L + l_a + l_o$ (workpiece + approach + overtravel):

$$L_{\text{total}} = 300 + 5 + 5 = 310 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_m = \dfrac{L_{\text{total}}}{f_{\text{rate}}} = \dfrac{310}{152.78}$$

$$T_m \approx 2.03 \text{ min} \approx 2 \text{ min } 2 \text{ sec}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Exam Pattern Cross-Reference

| **Given** | **Asked in KTU** | **Required Formula** | **Step Count** |
|------------|------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| $V, D$ | $N$ | $N = 1000V/(\pi D)$ | 1 |
| $V, N$ | $D$ | $D = 1000V/(\pi N)$ | 1 |
| $f_z, N, z$ | $f_{\text{rate}}$ | $f = f_z N z$ | 1 |
| $w, d, f$ | $MRR$ | $MRR = w d f$ | 1 |
| $L, f$ | $T_m$ | $T_m = L/f$ (plus approach & overtravel) | 2 |
| $MRR$, unit power | Power $P$ | $P = MRR \cdot \text{Unit Power}/60$ | 1 |

> **Pro tip for KTU 2024:** Always write the **formula first, then substitute the values, then the answer with units**. This earns full marks even if a numerical slip occurs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Column and Knee Type Milling Machine

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **interactions and functional flow** of components. This adheres to the protocol's safeguard rules: alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside quoted labels, no reserved keyword node names.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    baseA[Base: Vibration damping foundation]
    colA[Column: Houses spindle and motor drive]
    kneeA[Knee: Vertical adjustable casting]
    saddA[Saddle: Cross slide Y axis]
    tabA[Table: Workpiece holder with T slots]
    spinA[Spindle: Rotates the cutter]
    armA[Overarm: Supports arbor outer end]
    driveA[Drive System: Gears and clutch assembly]
    feedA[Feed Mechanism: Lead screws and handwheels]
    coolerA[Coolant System: Flood or mist]
    elecA[Electrical Controls: Start stop RPM selector]

    baseA --> colA
    colA --> kneeA
    kneeA --> saddA
    saddA --> tabA
    colA --> spinA
    colA --> armA
    elecA --> driveA
    driveA --> spinA
    driveA --> feedA
    feedA --> kneeA
    feedA --> saddA
    feedA --> tabA
    coolerA --> tabA

    classDef struct fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#0B3D91
    classDef motion fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F57C00,color:#6A3700
    classDef auxil fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,color:#1B5E20

    class baseA,colA,kneeA,saddA,tabA,armA struct
    class spinA,driveA,feedA motion
    class coolerA,elecA auxil
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Milling Cutter Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA[Cutting Speed V in milling]

    rootA --> kin1[Kinematic Block: Spindle and Cutter]
    rootA --> kin2[Kinematic Block: Table Feed System]
    rootA --> kin3[Kinematic Block: Cutter Engagement Zone]

    kin1 --> k1a[Spindle Motor delivers N rpm]
    kin1 --> k1b[Arbor or Chuck holds cutter of D]
    k1a --> k1c[Peripheral Speed = pi times D times N divided by 1000]

    kin2 --> k2a[Feed Drive provides f rate mm per min]
    kin2 --> k2b[Number of teeth z]

    k1c --> k3a[Cutting Speed V in m per min]
    k2a --> k3b[Feed per tooth fz in mm per tooth]
    k2b --> k3c[Feed rate f equals fz times N times z]

    k3a --> outA[Result: V controlled by N and D]
    k3c --> outB[Result: f controlled by fz, N, and z]
    k3a --> powA[Power Block: P equals MRR times Unit Power]
    k3c --> powA
    k3b --> mrA[MRR Block: w times d times f]
    powA --> outC[Final Outputs: V, f, MRR, P, Tm]

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1
    classDef process fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825,color:#5D4037
    classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20

    class rootA,kin1,kin2,kin3 input
    class k1a,k1b,k1c,k2a,k2b,k3a,k3b,k3c,mrA,powA process
    class outA,outB,outC output
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Up Milling vs. Down Milling (Cutter Rotation vs Feed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UpM[UP MILLING - Conventional]
        ufeed[Table feed direction LEFT]
        urot[Cutter rotation COUNTER CLOCKWISE]
        ufeed --> uthick[Chip thickness ZERO at start MAX at end]
        urot --> uthick
        uthick --> ulift[Force direction tries to LIFT workpiece]
    end

    subgraph DownM[DOWN MILLING - Climb]
        dfeed[Table feed direction LEFT]
        drot[Cutter rotation CLOCKWISE]
        dfeed --> dthick[Chip thickness MAX at start ZERO at end]
        drot --> dthick
        dthick --> dpush[Force direction tries to PUSH workpiece]
    end

    classDef upStyle fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#7F0000
    classDef downStyle fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20

    class ufeed,urot,uthick,ulift upStyle
    class dfeed,drot,dthick,dpush downStyle
```

> **Reading the diagrams:** Each Mermaid block is intentionally **block-functional** rather than pictorial, in line with the engine's protocol. They map the **causal and functional relationships** between the kinematic sub-systems of the milling machine.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q1a, Module 4]**

### Q1. Differentiate between up milling and down milling. List any **three** differences.

**Model Answer:**

| **Sl. No** | **Up Milling (Conventional)** | **Down Milling (Climb)** |
|------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Cutter rotation **opposite** to feed direction | Cutter rotation **same** as feed direction |
| 2 | Chip thickness is **zero at start, max at end** of engagement | Chip thickness is **max at start, zero at end** of engagement |
| 3 | Cutter tries to **lift** the workpiece off the table | Cutter tries to **push** the workpiece along the table |
| 4 | Requires **light clamping** | Requires **rigid clamping** + zero backlash |
| 5 | Smoother cut, less tool wear at start | More shock loading, but lower power consumption |
| 6 | Preferred on **conventional machines** | Preferred on **CNC / backlash-free machines** |

**(Any 3 valid differences = 3 marks; tabular format preferred at KTU valuation)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Q1b, Module 4]**

### Q2. List **six** different types of milling cutters and state **one** application of each.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Plain Milling Cutter** — Used for **slab (plain) milling** to produce flat surfaces.
2. **Side Milling Cutter** — Used for **slot milling** and machining open-sided surfaces.
3. **End Mill** — Used for **slot, pocket, and profile milling** on a vertical mill.
4. **Face Mill** — Used for **face milling** large flat surfaces with high MRR.
5. **T-Slot Cutter** — Used to cut **T-slots** in machine tables and fixtures.
6. **Formed Cutter** — Used to machine **contoured profiles** like gear teeth and concave shapes.

**(1 mark for 4 cutters + 1 mark for 2 more + 1 mark for applications → 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Q2, Module 4 — Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **constructional features of a column and knee type milling machine**. List the functions of the **column, knee, saddle, table, and spindle**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A horizontal plain milling cutter of $D = 80$ **mm** diameter with $z = 10$ teeth is used to machine a mild steel workpiece $250$ **mm** long. The recommended cutting speed is $V = 25$ **m/min** and the feed per tooth is $f_z = 0.15$ **mm/tooth**. Depth of cut is $d = 4$ **mm**, width $w = 70$ **mm**. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed $N$, (ii) Table feed rate, (iii) MRR, (iv) Cutting time (take $l_a = 5$ **mm**, $l_o = 5$ **mm**). **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

The column and knee type milling machine consists of the following major parts:

1. **Base** — heavy cast-iron foundation that supports the entire machine and damps vibration.
2. **Column** — vertical box casting at the rear; houses the spindle, drive motor, and gear train. Provides the overarm guide.
3. **Knee** — vertically adjustable casting mounted on the front face of the column; carries the saddle and table. Raised/lowered by the **elevating handwheel** via a vertical lead screw.
4. **Saddle** — sits on the knee, slides horizontally in the **Y-direction** via the cross-feed screw. Carries the table.
5. **Table** — top rectangular casting with **T-slots** for clamping workpieces. Moves longitudinally (**X-direction**) via the table feed screw.
6. **Spindle** — precision shaft that rotates the cutter; driven by the motor through gear trains.
7. **Overarm** — horizontal arm projecting from the column top to support the **outer end of an arbor** for long horizontal cutters.

A simple block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1) should accompany the answer for full marks.

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**(i) Spindle speed:**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 80} = \dfrac{25000}{251.327}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 99.47 \text{ rpm} \approx 99 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(ii) Table feed rate:**

$$f = f_z \cdot N \cdot z = 0.15 \times 99.47 \times 10$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 149.2 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate:**

$$MRR = w \cdot d \cdot f = 70 \times 4 \times 149.2$$

$$\boxed{MRR \approx 41{,}776 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(iv) Cutting time:**

$$L_{\text{total}} = 250 + 5 + 5 = 260 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_m = \dfrac{L_{\text{total}}}{f} = \dfrac{260}{149.2}$$

$$\boxed{T_m \approx 1.74 \text{ min} \approx 1 \text{ min } 45 \text{ sec}}$$

> **Mark split suggestion (KTU standard):** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 1.5 marks, (iv) 1.5 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** Classify milling cutters in detail. Explain the working principle and applications of **face milling, end milling, and gang milling** with neat sketches (block diagrams acceptable). **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between **cutting speed, spindle speed, and cutter diameter** in a milling operation. A $50$ **mm** diameter HSS plain milling cutter with $8$ teeth is used on mild steel. The recommended cutting speed is $V = 30$ **m/min** and feed per tooth $f_z = 0.10$ **mm/tooth**. Calculate the table feed rate. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Classification of milling cutters** (refer to Section 2.3 table). The examiner expects the student to mention:

- **Based on shape:** Plain, side-and-face, end, face, angle, form, T-slot, slitting saw.
- **Based on mounting:** Arbor-type (with bore) vs. Shank-type (with cylindrical or morse taper shank).
- **Based on tooth geometry:** Straight-tooth, helical-tooth, and inserted-tooth cutters.

**Working Principle of:**

1. **Face Milling** — The cutter axis is **perpendicular** to the workpiece surface. The cutting action occurs at the **end (face) of the cutter teeth**, which engage and remove material in a sweeping action. Provides a superior finish and high MRR.

2. **End Milling** — An end mill is a **cylindrical cutter with cutting edges on both the periphery and the end face**. It is mounted on a vertical spindle and performs cutting in both radial and axial directions. Ideal for slots, pockets, and 3D contours.

3. **Gang Milling** — Two or more cutters are **mounted together on a common arbor** to perform multiple machining operations (e.g., roughing + finishing + side cutting) in a single pass. Used for high-volume production.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Derivation** (refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation). Key result:

$$V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad [\text{m/min}]$$

**Substituting numerical values:**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 50} = \dfrac{30000}{157.08} \approx 190.99 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 191 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Table feed rate:**

$$f = f_z \cdot N \cdot z = 0.10 \times 190.99 \times 8$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 152.8 \text{ mm/min}}$$

> **Mark split:** Derivation = 3.5 marks, numerical solution = 3.5 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Always convert $D$ from mm and the answer to m/min. Writing the answer in mm/min is a **0-mark** trigger.
> 2. **Forgetting approach + overtravel** in cutting time formula — examiners deduct **1 full mark**.
> 3. **Confusing $f_z$ (feed per tooth) with $f$ (table feed rate)** — these are **NOT** the same; multiplying $f_z$ by $N \cdot z$ is a must.
> 4. **Drawing a wrong rotation direction** for up vs down milling — even if the answer is textually correct, a wrong sketch costs **1 to 1.5 marks**.
> 5. **Mixing unit power with specific energy** — in milling, use **unit power in W·s/mm³** or **N·m/mm³**; the KTU standard is the former.
> 6. **Not labelling sub-parts (a), (b) clearly** in Part B — leads to evaluator confusion and possible partial marking.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final high-density summary — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Milling = rotating multi-point cutter + linearly fed stationary workpiece.**
- [ ] **Column and knee type** milling machine = most common general-purpose mill; column = rigid spindle housing, knee = vertical adjustable support.
- [ ] **Three variants:** Plain (horizontal spindle), Vertical (vertical spindle), Universal (swivelling table).
- [ ] **Cutter classification** (must-know 6): plain, side, end mill, face mill, T-slot, form cutter.
- [ ] **Plain cutter:** teeth on periphery only. **Face mill:** teeth on bottom face. **End mill:** teeth on both periphery and end.
- [ ] **Up milling** = conventional = chip thickness zero→max, lift force on workpiece.
- [ ] **Down milling** = climb = chip thickness max→zero, push force on workpiece, needs rigid clamping + no backlash.
- [ ] **Cutting speed formula:** $V = \pi D N / 1000$ **[m/min]** — the most important formula of Module 4.
- [ ] **Spindle speed formula:** $N = 1000 V / (\pi D)$ **[rpm]**.
- [ ] **Table feed rate:** $f = f_z \cdot N \cdot z$ **[mm/min]**.
- [ ] **MRR in milling:** $MRR = w \cdot d \cdot f$ **[mm³/min]**.
- [ ] **Cutting time:** $T_m = (L + l_a + l_o)/f$ — **always include approach and overtravel**.
- [ ] **Typical cutting speeds:** mild steel $V = 20$ to $60$ m/min (HSS), aluminum $V = 100$ to $250$ m/min.
- [ ] **Slitting saw** is the cutter of choice for **deep slot cutting**.
- [ ] **Straddle milling** uses **two side-milling cutters on one arbor** to produce a fixed-width slot/block.
- [ ] **Helical teeth** reduce chatter; **carbide cutters** allow 2 to 3× higher cutting speeds than HSS.
- [ ] **Always draw a block diagram** for the column-and-knee mill question in Part B — 1.5 marks reserved.
- [ ] **Final answer rule:** write formula → substitute → numerical value with unit. This is the KTU valuation order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Feed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of FEED

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

In the context of **Metal Cutting and Machine Tools**, **Feed (f)** is formally defined as the **distance travelled by the cutting tool relative to the workpiece per unit event of the cutting action** — which can be either per revolution of the spindle (for turning/drilling) or per tooth of the cutter (for milling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> Feed is the **rate of advancement of the cutting tool against the workpiece**, expressed in **mm/rev**, **mm/tooth**, or **mm/min**, and is one of the **three primary machining parameters** alongside cutting speed and depth of cut.

### The Three Primary Machining Parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Common Units | Controlling Knob |
|-----------|--------|--------------|------------------|
| Cutting Speed | V or vc | m/min | Spindle RPM |
| **Feed** | **f** | **mm/rev** | **Cross-slide / Power feed lever** |
| Depth of Cut | d or ap | mm | Cross-slide handwheel |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are slicing a loaf of bread with a knife:
- **Cutting speed** = how fast you move the knife back and forth.
- **Depth of cut** = how thick a slice you cut.
- **Feed** = how much you push the knife forward between each back-and-forth slice. If you push a lot, slices are thick but rough. If you push a little, slices are thin and smooth, but it takes more time.

In machining, **Feed is the "thickness" of the material removed per cutting event** — it directly controls:
- ✅ How fast you finish the job (production rate)
- ✅ How smooth the surface looks (surface roughness)
- ✅ How hard the tool has to work (cutting forces & power)
- ✅ How long the tool lasts (tool life)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Feed is the **single most influential parameter affecting surface finish and chip thickness** in any machining operation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **1 mm/rev** = the tool advances **1 millimetre for every one full revolution** of the workpiece or cutter.
- For milling cutters, the **feed per tooth (fz)** is typically between **0.05 mm/tooth to 0.5 mm/tooth** for steel.
- For turning, typical **feed per revolution (f)** ranges from **0.1 mm/rev to 0.5 mm/rev** for roughing and **0.05 to 0.2 mm/rev** for finishing.
- The dimensional unit of feed is always a **length** — millimetres (mm) or inches.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Feed — The Three Faces of Feed

Feed can be expressed in **three different ways**, depending on the machining operation. KTU examiners frequently test the ability to **convert between these forms**.

### Type 1: Feed per Revolution (f) — used in Turning & Drilling

$$
f \;=\; \frac{\text{Length travelled by tool per revolution of spindle}}{\text{1 revolution}}
$$

**Units:** mm/rev

### Type 2: Feed per Tooth (fz) — used in Milling

$$
f_z \;=\; \frac{\text{Length travelled by cutter per tooth engagement}}{\text{1 tooth}}
$$

**Units:** mm/tooth

### Type 3: Feed Rate / Table Feed (vf) — used in Production Planning

$$
v_f \;=\; \text{Velocity of tool/worktable relative to workpiece}
$$

**Units:** mm/min

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown — Why Three Types?

The **Why** behind three feed notations:
- Turning and drilling have a **single-point geometry** → one tool, one cut per revolution.
- Milling has **multi-tooth geometry** → multiple cuts per revolution (one per tooth).
- Production engineers want **time-based** quantities → feed rate in mm/min.

## 2.3 🔥 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Exams)

| # | Formula | Description | Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | $v_f = f \times N$ | Feed rate from feed per rev | vf, f, N | mm/min, mm/rev, rpm |
| 2 | $v_f = f_z \times Z \times N$ | Feed rate from feed per tooth | vf, fz, Z, N | mm/min, mm/tooth, rev/min |
| 3 | $f = f_z \times Z$ | Feed per rev from feed per tooth | f, fz, Z | mm/rev, mm/tooth |
| 4 | $v_c = \frac{\pi \times D \times N}{1000}$ | Cutting speed | vc, D, N | m/min, mm, rpm |
| 5 | $N = \frac{1000 \times v_c}{\pi \times D}$ | Spindle speed | N, vc, D | rpm, m/min, mm |
| 6 | $t_m = \frac{L}{v_f} = \frac{L}{f \times N}$ | Machining time | tm, L, f, N | min, mm, mm/rev, rpm |
| 7 | $Z_c = \frac{v_f}{f_z \times N} = Z$ | Number of teeth engaged | Zc, vf, fz, N | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Q = v_f \times a_p \times a_e$ | Material Removal Rate (Turning) | Q, vf, ap, ae | mm³/min |
| 9 | $h_1 = f \times \sin(\kappa_r)$ | Uncut chip thickness (turning) | h1, f, κr | mm |
| 10 | $h_m = \frac{a_p}{\sin(\kappa_r)}$ | Chip width / Chip thickness (turning) | hm, ap, κr | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Memorize Formulas 1, 2, 3, 6, and 8. These five alone cover **>80%** of all feed-based numerical problems in KTU 2024 scheme examinations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAM/CNC Programming:** Every G-code line uses an **F-word** specifying feed rate (e.g., `G01 X50 Y30 F150` means feed = 150 mm/min).
- **Process Planning:** Manufacturing engineers compute **machining time** using feed to estimate cost.
- **Surface Finish Control:** Theoretical surface roughness in turning: $R_a \approx \frac{f^2}{8R}$ — feed **squared** controls roughness.
- **Tool Life Optimisation:** Increasing feed by 20% can reduce tool life by **~30–40%** (Taylor's tool life equation dependency).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Feed Rate Equation (Turning/Drilling)

**Statement to Prove:**

$$
v_f = f \times N
$$

### Step-by-Step Proof:

**Step 1 — Define Feed per Revolution:**

By definition, the tool advances a distance **f** (mm) for every **one full revolution** of the workpiece (turning) or tool (drilling).

**Step 2 — Number of Revolutions per Minute:**

The spindle rotates at **N revolutions per minute (rpm)**.

**Step 3 — Total Distance Travelled per Minute:**

If each revolution produces **f** mm of travel, then in **N** revolutions per minute:

$$
\text{Total distance per minute} = f \times N
$$

**Step 4 — Identify as Feed Rate:**

The "distance per minute" is by definition the **feed rate (vf)** in mm/min:

$$
v_f = f \times N \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Feed Rate Equation (Milling)

**Statement to Prove:**

$$
v_f = f_z \times Z \times N
$$

### Step-by-Step Proof:

**Step 1 — Number of Teeth Engaged:**

A milling cutter has **Z** teeth arranged around its periphery.

**Step 2 — Distance per Tooth:**

Each tooth removes material as it rotates. Each tooth engagement advances the table by **fz** mm (feed per tooth).

**Step 3 — Distance per Revolution:**

In one full revolution, all **Z** teeth engage, so:

$$
\text{Distance per revolution} = f_z \times Z
$$

This quantity is also equal to **f** (feed per revolution):

$$
f = f_z \times Z
$$

**Step 4 — Total Distance per Minute:**

Since the spindle rotates at **N** rpm:

$$
v_f = f \times N = (f_z \times Z) \times N
$$

$$
\boxed{v_f = f_z \times Z \times N} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Machining Time

**Statement to Prove:**

$$
t_m = \frac{L}{v_f} = \frac{L}{f \times N}
$$

### Step-by-Step Proof:

**Step 1 — Define Machining Time:**

Machining time is the **time taken for the tool to traverse the required length L** at the given feed rate.

**Step 2 — Use Basic Kinematics:**

By the fundamental relation: Time = Distance / Speed.

$$
t_m = \frac{\text{Length of cut (L)}}{\text{Feed rate (v}_f\text{)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Feed Rate:**

Substituting $v_f = f \times N$:

$$
t_m = \frac{L}{f \times N}
$$

**Step 4 — For Turning (where L = workpiece length + tool approach + overtravel):**

$$
t_m = \frac{L + L_a + L_o}{f \times N}
$$

where $L_a$ = tool approach length, $L_o$ = tool overtravel length.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A mild steel workpiece of length **400 mm** is turned on a lathe. The cutting speed is **30 m/min**, feed is **0.25 mm/rev**, and the depth of cut is **2 mm**. The workpiece diameter is **60 mm**. Calculate:
1. Spindle speed (N)
2. Feed rate (vf)
3. Machining time (tm)
4. Material removal rate (Q)

**Solution — Step 1: Spindle Speed**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times v_c}{\pi \times D}
$$

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 60}
$$

$$
N = \frac{30000}{188.496}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 159.15 \; \text{rpm}}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Feed Rate**

$$
v_f = f \times N = 0.25 \times 159.15
$$

$$
\boxed{v_f = 39.79 \; \text{mm/min}}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Machining Time**

Assuming L = 400 mm (length of cut):

$$
t_m = \frac{L}{v_f} = \frac{400}{39.79}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_m = 10.05 \; \text{minutes}}
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Material Removal Rate**

$$
Q = v_f \times a_p \times \pi \times D
$$

(Note: For turning, the cross-sectional area is $a_p \times \pi D$ approximately $a_p \times d$, but using instantaneous diameter:)

$$
Q = v_f \times a_p \times d = 39.79 \times 2 \times 60
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 4774.6 \; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Feed Relationships Across Operations

| Operation | Feed Notation | Symbol | Cutting Event | Number of Cutting Edges |
|-----------|---------------|--------|---------------|------------------------|
| Turning | Feed per revolution | f (mm/rev) | 1 rev of workpiece | 1 (single-point tool) |
| Drilling | Feed per revolution | f (mm/rev) | 1 rev of drill | 2 (two cutting edges) |
| Milling (peripheral) | Feed per tooth | fz (mm/tooth) | 1 tooth engagement | Z (multiple) |
| Milling (face) | Feed per tooth | fz (mm/tooth) | 1 insert engagement | Z (multiple) |
| Broaching | Feed per tooth | fz (mm/tooth) | 1 tooth engagement | Z (multiple) |
| Grinding | Feed rate | vf (mm/min) | Continuous | Many abrasive grains |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Feed Parameter Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed in Machining] --> B[Feed per Revolution f]
    A --> C[Feed per Tooth fz]
    A --> D[Feed Rate vf]
    B --> E[Used in Turning and Drilling]
    C --> F[Used in Milling and Broaching]
    D --> G[Used in CNC Programming]
    B --> H[Formula vf equals f times N]
    C --> I[Formula vf equals fz times Z times N]
    D --> J[Formula tm equals L divided by vf]
    H --> K[Output Machining Time tm]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Output Material Removal Rate Q]
    K --> M[Output Surface Roughness Ra]
    L --> N[Cost Estimation]
    M --> N
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Feed Conversion Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Given Spindle Speed N rpm] --> P2[Given Feed per Tooth fz mm per tooth]
    P2 --> P3[Multiply by Z number of teeth]
    P3 --> P4[Feed per Revolution f equals fz times Z]
    P4 --> P5[Multiply f by N]
    P5 --> P6[Feed Rate vf in mm per min]
    P6 --> P7[Machining Time tm equals L divided by vf]
    style P6 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P7 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Effect of Feed on Machining Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F[Feed f] --> F1[Chip Thickness h1]
    F --> F2[Cutting Force Fc]
    F --> F3[Surface Roughness Ra]
    F --> F4[Tool Life T]
    F --> F5[Machining Time tm]
    F1 --> F6[MRR Material Removal Rate]
    F2 --> F6
    F3 --> F7[Quality of Product]
    F4 --> F8[Economics]
    F5 --> F8
    F6 --> F8
    style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F8 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Feed in CNC Context

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CNC[CNC Machine Controller] --> PRG[G Code Program]
    PRG --> F1[F Word Feedrate Command]
    F1 --> F2[mm per min value]
    F2 --> F3[Servo Motor Drive]
    F3 --> F4[Ball Screw Rotation]
    F4 --> F5[Linear Table Movement]
    F5 --> F6[Actual Feed Achieved]
    F6 --> F7[Encoder Feedback]
    F7 --> CNC
    style CNC fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F6 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define feed in machining. Distinguish between feed per revolution and feed per tooth.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Feed (f):** Feed is defined as the distance travelled by the cutting tool relative to the workpiece per unit event of the cutting action (per revolution or per tooth).

**Feed per Revolution (f):** It is the distance the tool advances **per one revolution** of the workpiece (turning) or tool (drilling). Unit: **mm/rev**. It is used in operations with **single-point cutting tools**.

**Feed per Tooth (fz):** It is the distance the tool advances per **engagement of one tooth** of a multi-point cutting tool like a milling cutter. Unit: **mm/tooth**. It is used in milling and broaching.

**Relationship:** $f = f_z \times Z$, where Z = number of teeth.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**State the formula for feed rate in turning and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Formula:**

$$
v_f = f \times N
$$

where:
- $v_f$ = feed rate in mm/min
- $f$ = feed per revolution in mm/rev
- $N$ = spindle speed in rpm

**Significance:**
1. Determines the **production time** (machining time = L / vf).
2. Directly affects **material removal rate (MRR)**.
3. Controls **surface finish quality** — higher feed → rougher surface.
4. Influences **cutting forces and power consumption**.

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> ⚠️ **Common mistakes students make in feed-based problems:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — mixing mm/min with m/min in cutting speed formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1000 conversion factor** in $v_c = \pi D N / 1000$.
> 3. **Confusing feed per revolution with feed rate** — they are NOT the same.
> 4. **Using initial vs final diameter** incorrectly in MRR calculation for turning.
> 5. **Forgetting approach & overtravel lengths** in machining time calculations.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Define feed per revolution and feed per tooth. Derive the relationship between feed rate, feed per tooth, and number of teeth for a milling operation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical workpiece of initial diameter **80 mm** is reduced to **70 mm** by turning on a lathe in a single pass. The cutting speed is **25 m/min**, feed is **0.3 mm/rev**, and length of cut is **500 mm**. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Feed rate, (iii) Machining time, (iv) Material removal rate. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Definition and Derivation (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**

**Feed per revolution (f):** Distance advanced by the tool per one revolution of the workpiece. Unit: mm/rev.

**Feed per Tooth (fz):** Distance advanced per engagement of one tooth of a multi-point cutting tool. Unit: mm/tooth.

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

Given:
- Milling cutter has **Z teeth**.
- Each tooth removes a chip of thickness corresponding to **fz** (mm/tooth).

In **one revolution** of the cutter, all Z teeth engage, so the table advances:

$$
\text{Distance per rev} = f_z \times Z
$$

By definition, this equals feed per revolution **f**:

$$
f = f_z \times Z \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}
$$

Since the spindle rotates at **N** rpm, the table advances per minute:

$$
v_f = f \times N
$$

Substituting Equation 1:

$$
v_f = (f_z \times Z) \times N
$$

$$
\boxed{v_f = f_z \times Z \times N} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}
$$

This is the **feed rate** in mm/min.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Initial diameter, $D_1 = 80$ mm
- Final diameter, $D_2 = 70$ mm
- Cutting speed, $v_c = 25$ m/min
- Feed, $f = 0.3$ mm/rev
- Length of cut, $L = 500$ mm
- Depth of cut, $a_p = (D_1 - D_2)/2 = (80-70)/2 = 5$ mm

---

**(i) Spindle Speed (N):**

Using the average diameter $D = (D_1 + D_2)/2 = 75$ mm:

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times v_c}{\pi \times D}
$$

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 75}
$$

$$
N = \frac{25000}{235.62}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 106.10 \; \text{rpm}}
$$

---

**(ii) Feed Rate (vf):**

$$
v_f = f \times N = 0.3 \times 106.10
$$

$$
\boxed{v_f = 31.83 \; \text{mm/min}}
$$

---

**(iii) Machining Time (tm):**

$$
t_m = \frac{L}{v_f} = \frac{500}{31.83}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_m = 15.71 \; \text{minutes}}
$$

---

**(iv) Material Removal Rate (Q):**

$$
Q = v_f \times a_p \times \pi \times D_{avg}
$$

$$
Q = 31.83 \times 5 \times \pi \times 75
$$

$$
Q = 31.83 \times 5 \times 235.62
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 37497 \; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} = 37.5 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the three types of feed notations used in machining. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A face milling operation is performed on a cast iron workpiece using a **6-tooth** carbide insert cutter of **100 mm** diameter. The cutting speed is **150 m/min**, feed per tooth is **0.15 mm/tooth**, and the length of cut is **200 mm**. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Feed per revolution, (iii) Feed rate, (iv) Machining time, (v) Number of teeth in contact per revolution. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Three Types of Feed Notations (7 Marks)

**Sketch Description (text-based):**

```
Type 1: Feed per Revolution (Turning)
+--------------------+
|    Workpiece       |
|     <----O---->    |  <-- Tool moves f mm per 1 rev
|     (rotating)     |
+--------------------+
f = mm/rev

Type 2: Feed per Tooth (Milling)
    +---+
   / Z1 \  <-- Tooth 1 engages, advances fz
   |  *  |
   \ Z2 /  <-- Tooth 2 engages next, advances fz
    \___/
fz = mm/tooth

Type 3: Feed Rate (Table Feed)
  Tool -->------->------
  (moves at vf mm/min continuously)
vf = mm/min
```

**Explanations (7 Marks breakdown: 2 marks each + 1 mark for diagram):**

1. **Feed per Revolution (f):** Distance travelled by the tool per revolution of spindle. Used in turning, drilling, boring. Unit: mm/rev.

2. **Feed per Tooth (fz):** Distance travelled per tooth engagement. Used in milling, broaching. Unit: mm/tooth.

3. **Feed Rate (vf):** Velocity of tool relative to workpiece. Used in production planning and CNC programming. Unit: mm/min.

**Conversion Relationship:**

$$
v_f = f \times N = f_z \times Z \times N
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Number of teeth, $Z = 6$
- Cutter diameter, $D = 100$ mm
- Cutting speed, $v_c = 150$ m/min
- Feed per tooth, $f_z = 0.15$ mm/tooth
- Length of cut, $L = 200$ mm

---

**(i) Spindle Speed (N):**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times v_c}{\pi \times D} = \frac{1000 \times 150}{\pi \times 100}
$$

$$
N = \frac{150000}{314.16}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 477.46 \; \text{rpm}}
$$

---

**(ii) Feed per Revolution (f):**

$$
f = f_z \times Z = 0.15 \times 6
$$

$$
\boxed{f = 0.9 \; \text{mm/rev}}
$$

---

**(iii) Feed Rate (vf):**

$$
v_f = f \times N = 0.9 \times 477.46
$$

$$
\boxed{v_f = 429.72 \; \text{mm/min}}
$$

---

**(iv) Machining Time (tm):**

$$
t_m = \frac{L}{v_f} = \frac{200}{429.72}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_m = 0.465 \; \text{minutes} = 27.9 \; \text{seconds}}
$$

---

**(v) Number of Teeth in Contact per Revolution:**

For a face mill, the number of teeth simultaneously engaged equals Z (all teeth can be in cut depending on width):

$$
\boxed{Z_c = Z = 6 \; \text{teeth (maximum)}}
$$

(For peripheral milling at width less than cutter diameter, $Z_c < Z$.)

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — FEED**

### Must-Know Definitions
- ✅ **Feed (f):** Tool advance per cutting event (mm/rev or mm/tooth).
- ✅ **Feed Rate (vf):** Tool advance per minute (mm/min).
- ✅ **Feed per Tooth (fz):** Used in multi-tooth cutters.

### Must-Know Formulas (Top 5)
- ✅ $v_f = f \times N$
- ✅ $v_f = f_z \times Z \times N$
- ✅ $f = f_z \times Z$
- ✅ $t_m = \dfrac{L}{v_f}$
- ✅ $N = \dfrac{1000 \times v_c}{\pi \times D}$

### Must-Know Units
- ✅ $v_c$ = m/min (NOT mm/min)
- ✅ $f$ = mm/rev
- ✅ $f_z$ = mm/tooth
- ✅ $v_f$ = mm/min
- ✅ $D$ = mm
- ✅ $N$ = rpm

### Must-Know Relationships
- ✅ Higher feed → **rougher** surface finish.
- ✅ Higher feed → **larger** cutting force.
- ✅ Higher feed → **higher** MRR but **lower** tool life.
- ✅ Higher feed → **shorter** machining time.

### Common KTU Traps
- ⚠️ Convert units consistently before substitution.
- ⚠️ Use **average diameter** for spindle speed in multi-pass turning.
- ⚠️ Remember **1000 conversion** for cutting speed formula.
- ⚠️ Don't confuse feed per tooth with feed per revolution in milling.

### Quick Conversion Trick
> 💡 **From fz to vf (in 1 line):**
> Multiply fz × Z × N → you directly get vf in mm/min!

### Real-World Connect
- CNC machines use **F-word** in G-code for feed rate.
- Aerospace turning of Inconel uses **very low feed** (~0.1 mm/rev) for surface integrity.
- High-feed milling uses **fz up to 1 mm/tooth** with reduced ap for productivity.

---

> **End of Notes — Feed (Module 4, PCPET403)**
