topics.title: Depth of Cut and Machining Time. Indexing Methods, Simple and Compound Indexing. Milling of Keyways. Gear Cutting, Shaping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Depth of Cut and Machining Time. Indexing Methods, Simple and Compound Indexing. Milling of Keyways. Gear Cutting, Shaping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Depth of Cut and Machining Time

**Depth of Cut (d)** is the perpendicular distance measured between the original (unmachined) surface of the workpiece and the machined surface, representing the thickness of material removed in a single pass. It is one of the three primary machining parameters (along with **feed** and **cutting speed**) and directly governs the metal removal rate (MRR) and the energy consumed during cutting.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> Depth of cut is classified as:
> - **Roughing Cut (Heavy Depth):** 2 mm to 8 mm — maximum material removal with lower accuracy.
> - **Finishing Cut (Light Depth):** 0.5 mm to 2 mm — superior surface finish and dimensional accuracy.

### Conceptual Analogy: "The Wood Carver's Chisel"

Imagine a carpenter sculpting a wooden log into a chair leg. If the carpenter pushes the chisel **1 inch deep** into the wood per stroke, that is the depth of cut. If he instead pushes it only **1/8 inch deep**, he removes less material per stroke but achieves a smoother finish. In the same way:
- A **larger depth of cut** = faster material removal, but rougher finish (Roughing).
- A **smaller depth of cut** = slower material removal, but smoother finish (Finishing).

**Machining Time (tm)** is the actual time during which the cutting tool is engaged with the workpiece and removing material. It is the most critical parameter for **production planning, costing, and capacity estimation** in any manufacturing industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Machining Time Matters in KTU Examinations**
> Machining time directly determines:
> 1. **Manufacturing cost per component**
> 2. **Machine tool utilization efficiency**
> 3. **Production rate and delivery scheduling**

---

## 1.2 Indexing Methods

**Indexing** is the precision rotational operation of dividing the circumference of a workpiece into an equal number of angular spaces using an **Indexing Head (Dividing Head)**. It is the fundamental operation that enables gear cutting, milling of polygonal shapes, splines, keyways on arcs, and drilling of bolt-hole circles.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> The standard indexing head uses a **40-tooth worm wheel** driven by a **single-thread worm** (Crank). Hence:
> $$\text{One full crank revolution} = \text{Workpiece rotates by } \frac{1}{40}\text{ of a revolution} = 9°$$

### Conceptual Analogy: "The Pizza Slicer"

Imagine you have a circular pizza and want to cut it into **6 equal slices**. Instead of trying to estimate by eye, you use a pizza cutter that rotates the pizza exactly **1/6 of a turn** at each cut. An indexing head works identically — but with surgical precision using gear ratios and an index plate with pre-drilled hole circles.

**Simple Indexing** uses only the **40:1 worm ratio** and the standard **index plates**. It is sufficient when the required number of divisions (X) has factors that can be obtained from the standard hole circles (15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33, 37, 39, 41, 43, 47, 49).

**Compound Indexing** is used when the required divisions are **prime numbers** (e.g., 59, 61, 67, 71, 79, 83, 89, 97, 101, 103, 107, 109, 113) that cannot be obtained by simple indexing using the standard index plate.

---

## 1.3 Milling of Keyways

**Keyway Milling** is the operation of cutting a longitudinal slot (keyway) along the inner cylindrical bore or outer surface of a shaft/hub to accommodate a **woodruff key, parallel key, or spline**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> Keyways are milled using:
> - **End Mill Cutters** (for closed keyways of rectangular profile)
> - **Slot Mills / Side-and-Face Cutters** (for open keyways)
> - **Woodruff Cutter** (for semi-circular woodruff keyways — actually a *milling* operation, not turning)

---

## 1.4 Gear Cutting

**Gear Cutting** encompasses all manufacturing processes used to produce **spur gears, helical gears, bevel gears, worm gears, and racks** with the required tooth profile, pitch, and accuracy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> The two main categories are:
> 1. **Forming (Copying) Method** — uses a gear-shaped cutter (e.g., gear milling on a universal milling machine using a **disk-type or end-mill type gear cutter**).
> 2. **Generating Method** — uses the relative rolling motion between tool and workpiece (e.g., **Gear Hobbing, Gear Shaping, Gear Shaving, Gear Grinding**).

---

## 1.5 Shaping

**Shaping** is a machining process where a **single-point cutting tool** moves in a linear reciprocating motion while the workpiece is fed perpendicular to the tool motion. It is performed on a **Shaper machine** (crank-and-slotted-link type) or a **Planer** (for large workpieces).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> Shaping produces **flat surfaces, slots, keyways, and contours** by repeated linear cutting strokes. The cutting action occurs **only on the forward stroke** (cutting stroke); the return stroke is **idle**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Machining Time — Detailed Theory

### 2.1.1 Machining Time for Turning (Lathe)

**Step 1: Identify the cutting parameters**
- $N$ = Spindle speed in rpm
- $f$ = Feed in mm/rev
- $L$ = Total length of cut = $L_w + L_a + L_o$ (work length + approach + overtravel)
- $d$ = Depth of cut in mm

**Step 2: Apply the formula**

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3: Verify units** — $L$ (mm), $f$ (mm/rev), $N$ (rev/min) → result in **minutes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight**
> Approach distance $L_a \approx \sqrt{d \cdot (D-d)}$ (where D = initial diameter) for straight turning, or $L_a = d \cdot \tan(\phi)$ where $\phi$ is the tool approach angle (typically 45° or 60°).

### 2.1.2 Machining Time for Drilling

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} \text{ minutes}$$

Where L = thickness of plate + drill point length (typically 0.3 × D for standard drills).

### 2.1.3 Machining Time for Shaping

Shaping uses **strokes per minute** (not rpm), so the formula differs:

$$t_m = \frac{L}{(1 - q) \times L_s \times n_s} \text{ minutes}$$

Where:
- $L$ = length of workpiece in mm
- $L_s$ = length of stroke in mm
- $n_s$ = number of double strokes per minute
- $q$ = return stroke factor (ratio of return time to cutting time, typically 0.5 to 0.7)

### 2.1.4 Machining Time for Milling

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f_m} + t_{setup}$$

Where $f_m$ = table feed in mm/min = $f \times N \times z$ (f = feed/tooth, N = rpm, z = number of teeth).

---

## 2.2 Indexing — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Simple Indexing — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Compute the Indexing Movement**

$$N = \frac{40}{X}$$

Where:
- $N$ = Number of crank revolutions required per division
- $X$ = Number of equal divisions required on the workpiece

**Step 2: Convert to Whole Revolutions + Hole Spacing**
- Integer part → full crank revolutions
- Fractional part → multiply by the nearest available hole circle
- Example: $N = 1 + \frac{13}{20}$ → 1 full turn + 13 holes on the 20-hole circle

**Step 3: Select Index Plate**
- Standard index plates come in sets of 3 (Brown & Sharpe type): Hole circles 15-16-17-18-19-20, 21-23-27-29-31-33, 37-39-41-43-47-49.

### 2.2.2 Compound Indexing — Detailed Logic

**When is it used?** When the number of divisions $X$ is a **prime number** OR has factors not available in the index plate hole circles.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Select a Sector Arm (C) and a Trial Hole (K)** such that both are **prime numbers**.
2. Compute the change plate ratio (A/B) from the chosen numbers:

$$\frac{A}{B} = \frac{40}{C} - \frac{K}{X}$$

3. The **driving gear A** is mounted on the worm spindle (crank), and the **driven gear B** is mounted on the **second index plate spindle**.
4. The ratio must be **between 1 and 10** to physically fit on the dividing head.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip**
> Always state the **two index movements explicitly**:
> - **First index movement** = $\frac{40}{C}$ revolutions of crank
> - **Second index movement** = $\frac{K}{X}$ revolutions of crank
> The workpiece movement is the **algebraic sum**.

---

## 2.3 Milling of Keyways — Detailed Theory

**Method 1: Using End Mill Cutter (Closed Keyways)**
- The end mill diameter must equal the **width of the keyway**.
- Cutting is performed by a **vertical plunge + longitudinal feed**.

**Method 2: Using Slot Mill / Side-and-Face Cutter (Open Keyways)**
- The cutter width equals the keyway width.
- The cutter is mounted on an **arbor** and cuts during the longitudinal feed of the table.

**Method 3: Using Woodruff Cutter**
- Used for **semi-circular keyways**.
- The cutter has a small diameter with a curved tooth profile.
- Cutting produces a chip thickness equal to the depth of cut.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight**
> For keyways on **shafts (external)**: Use **plain milling cutters** (side-and-face).
> For keyways on **bores/hubs (internal)**: Use **end mill cutters**.

---

## 2.4 Gear Cutting — Detailed Theory

### 2.4.1 Gear Milling (Forming Method)
Uses a **gear cutter** that has the exact shape of the gear tooth space. The cutter is mounted on the milling machine arbor, and the workpiece is mounted on the **indexing head spindle**. After each tooth is cut, the workpiece is **indexed by $\frac{1}{Z}$ revolution** (where Z = number of teeth) before cutting the next tooth space.

### 2.4.2 Gear Hobbing (Generating Method)
- Uses a **hob** — a cylindrical cutting tool with helical teeth shaped like a worm.
- The hob and workpiece rotate with a **fixed velocity ratio** while the hob is fed **axially** along the gear blank.
- It is the **fastest and most accurate** gear manufacturing method.

### 2.4.3 Gear Shaping (Generating Method)
- Uses a **pinion-shaped cutter** that reciprocates axially.
- The cutter and gear blank rotate with a **fixed velocity ratio** as in rolling contact.
- Suitable for **internal gears** and **gears adjacent to shoulders**.

### 2.4.4 Bevel Gear Cutting
- Two methods: **Forming method** (using a bevel gear cutter) and **Generating method** (using two generating cutters — straight or spiral bevel generators).

### 2.4.5 Worm Gear Cutting
- Worm is **threaded on a lathe** using a thread-cutting setup.
- Worm gear is **hobbed** using a **worm gear hob** (which is essentially a thread cutting tool that matches the worm).

---

## 2.5 Shaping — Detailed Theory

### 2.5.1 Shaper Mechanism
- The **crank-and-slotted-link mechanism** (also called **Whitworth quick-return mechanism**) converts rotary motion of the bull gear into reciprocating motion of the ram.
- The forward (cutting) stroke is **slow**; the return stroke is **fast**.
- Quick return ratio: $Q = \frac{T_{return}}{T_{cutting}}$ (typically 0.5 to 0.7).

### 2.5.2 Stroke Length Adjustment
- Adjustable by changing the **radius of the crank pin** on the bull gear.
- Maximum stroke length occurs when the crank pin is at the **maximum radius**.

### 2.5.3 Cutting Action
- The tool bit is held in a **tool post** mounted on the ram.
- Feed is given by the **cross-slide** mechanism, which uses a **ratchet-and-pawl** or **screw-and-nut** arrangement to feed the table after each stroke.

---

## 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Indexing (Simple) | $N = \dfrac{40}{X}$ | revolutions | X = number of divisions |
| 2 | Indexing (Compound) | $\dfrac{A}{B} = \dfrac{40}{C} - \dfrac{K}{X}$ | ratio | C, K = prime trial numbers |
| 3 | Time (Turning) | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | minutes | L in mm, f in mm/rev, N in rpm |
| 4 | Time (Drilling) | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | minutes | L = plate + point length |
| 5 | Time (Milling) | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f_t \cdot z \cdot N}$ | minutes | $f_t$ = feed/tooth |
| 6 | Time (Shaping) | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{(1-q) \cdot L_s \cdot n_s}$ | minutes | q = return ratio, $n_s$ = strokes/min |
| 7 | MRR (Turning) | $MRR = \pi \cdot d \cdot f \cdot v_c$ | mm³/min | $v_c$ = cutting speed |
| 8 | MRR (Milling) | $MRR = Q \cdot A$ | mm³/min | Q = MRR per tooth, A = area |
| 9 | Gear Tooth Pitch | $p = \dfrac{\pi \cdot m}{}$ | mm | m = module |
| 10 | Center Distance (gears) | $a = \dfrac{m(Z_1 + Z_2)}{2}$ | mm | Standard meshing formula |
| 11 | Quick Return Ratio | $Q = \dfrac{T_r}{T_c}$ | ratio | $T_r$ = return time, $T_c$ = cutting time |
| 12 | Approach Distance | $L_a = d \cdot \tan(\phi)$ | mm | φ = tool approach angle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**
> - **Indexing** is the foundation of **gear manufacturing, spline cutting, and bolt-circle drilling** — used extensively in transmission systems.
> - **Machining time estimation** is the cornerstone of **CAPP (Computer-Aided Process Planning)** and **CNC programming** in modern industry.
> - **Gear cutting methods** (especially hobbing) dominate automotive, aerospace, and heavy machinery sectors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Machining Time for Turning

We begin with the basic definition of feed.

**Step 1: Define feed**
Feed (f) is the distance the tool advances per spindle revolution. Thus, in one revolution, the tool moves a distance f along the workpiece.

**Step 2: Compute time per revolution**
Time taken for 1 revolution = $\dfrac{1}{N}$ minutes (where N is in rpm).

**Step 3: Compute time for total length L**
If the tool moves f mm per revolution, then for L mm, the number of revolutions required = $\dfrac{L}{f}$.

**Step 4: Multiply by time per revolution**

$$t_m = \text{Number of revolutions} \times \text{time per revolution} = \frac{L}{f} \times \frac{1}{N}$$

$$\boxed{t_m = \frac{L}{f \cdot N} \text{ minutes}}$$

**Step 5: Include approach and overtravel (KTU refined version)**

$$L_{total} = L_w + L_a + L_o$$

$$t_m = \frac{L_w + L_a + L_o}{f \cdot N}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Machining Time for Shaping

Unlike turning, in shaping the cutting tool does **not** rotate — it reciprocates. Hence, the indexing parameter is the **number of strokes per minute (n_s)**.

**Step 1: Identify cutting stroke length**
The cutting action occurs only during the **forward stroke** of length $L_s$.

**Step 2: Account for the quick-return mechanism**
Only a fraction $(1-q)$ of the cycle is the cutting stroke, where $q = \dfrac{T_{return}}{T_{cutting}}$.

**Step 3: Effective feed per minute**
In one full cycle (cutting + return) of duration $\dfrac{1}{n_s}$ minutes, the tool effectively advances a distance $(1-q) \cdot L_s$ in the cutting direction... **but the tool itself does not feed longitudinally in shaping**. The feed in shaping is the **cross-feed per stroke**.

**Step 4: Reformulate using cross-feed**

The total number of strokes required to cover the workpiece length L (transversely) at cross-feed $c$ per stroke is:

$$\text{Number of strokes} = \frac{L}{c}$$

Each stroke takes $\dfrac{1}{n_s}$ minutes, hence:

$$\boxed{t_m = \frac{L}{c \cdot n_s} \text{ minutes}}$$

**Alternative form** (when $L_s$ is the relevant parameter and cross-feed is given per stroke):

$$t_m = \frac{L}{(1-q) \cdot L_s \cdot n_s} \times \frac{L}{L_s} = \frac{L^2}{c \cdot n_s \cdot L_s}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Simple Indexing Movement

**Step 1: Establish the worm-wheel ratio**
The standard dividing head has a worm with **1 thread** engaging a worm wheel of **40 teeth**. Therefore:

$$\text{Crank rotations} : \text{Workpiece rotations} = 1 : \frac{1}{40} = 40 : 1$$

**Step 2: Compute crank movement per division**
If the workpiece must be divided into $X$ equal parts, each division requires the workpiece to rotate $\dfrac{1}{X}$ of a revolution.

**Step 3: Map to crank rotations**

$$\text{Crank rotations per division} = \frac{1}{X} \times 40 = \frac{40}{X}$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{40}{X} \text{ revolutions}}$$

**Step 4: Worked example — 6 divisions**

$$N = \frac{40}{6} = 6.666... = 6 + \frac{2}{3}$$

Convert $\frac{2}{3}$ to nearest hole circle. $\frac{2}{3} = \frac{14}{21}$ → Use the **21-hole circle, 14 holes**.

**Final indexing**: **6 full turns + 14 holes on the 21-hole circle**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Compound Indexing Change Plate Ratio

**Step 1: Identify the need for compound indexing**
When $X$ is a prime number, the fraction $\dfrac{40}{X}$ cannot be reduced to a hole circle value from the standard set.

**Step 2: Strategy — Two index movements**
Compound indexing achieves the required rotation by:
- **First movement** of $\dfrac{40}{C}$ (using index crank)
- **Second movement** of $\dfrac{K}{X}$ (using change gears + index crank)

The **net** movement must equal $\dfrac{40}{X}$:

$$\frac{40}{C} \pm \frac{K}{X} = \frac{40}{X}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to obtain the change plate ratio A/B**

$$\frac{A}{B} = \frac{40}{C} \pm \frac{K}{X}$$

**Step 4: Substitute known trial values C and K**
Choose C and K such that A/B lies between 1 and 10, and both C and K are prime numbers.

**Worked example: 61 divisions**

$$\frac{40}{61} = \text{irreducible prime fraction}$$

Choose C = 17, K = 53 (both prime). Then:

$$\frac{A}{B} = \frac{40}{17} - \frac{53}{61}$$

Computing the common denominator:

$$\frac{A}{B} = \frac{40 \times 61 - 53 \times 17}{17 \times 61} = \frac{2440 - 901}{1037} = \frac{1539}{1037}$$

Simplify: $\gcd(1539, 1037)$... 

Let me verify: $1539 = 3 \times 513 = 3 \times 3 \times 171 = 9 \times 171 = 9 \times 9 \times 19 = 81 \times 19$.
$1037 = 17 \times 61$.
GCD = 1. So $\frac{A}{B} = \frac{1539}{1037}$ which is > 1 but < 10. ✓

This ratio corresponds to compound gear train values; final gear selection done from the standard gear kit.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Indexing Methods

| Parameter | Simple Indexing | Compound Indexing | Differential Indexing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Only crank + index plate | Crank + change gears + index plate | Crank + change gears + planetary gear in dividing head |
| **Indexing Accuracy** | High | Moderate (compounding errors) | High |
| **Index Plate Used** | Two (one fixed) | Two (one fixed, one free) | One |
| **Range of Divisions** | Limited to standard hole circles | Any number including primes | Any number including primes |
| **Operation Time** | Fast | Slower (gear engagement) | Fastest for primes |
| **Application** | Hexagons, 8/12/16 divisions | 61, 67, 71 divisions | 73, 79, 83, 89, 97, etc. |
| **Error Source** | Hole plate tolerances | Compound gear backlash | Planetary gear backlash |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Gear Cutting Methods

| Method | Tool Type | Accuracy | Production Rate | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gear Milling (Forming)** | Disk-type gear cutter | Low (AGMA 8-10) | Low | Small batch, large module |
| **Gear Hobbing** | Cylindrical hob | High (AGMA 6-8) | Very High | Spur, helical, worm wheels |
| **Gear Shaping** | Pinion-shaped cutter | High (AGMA 6-7) | Medium | Internal gears, cluster gears |
| **Gear Shaving** | Shaving cutter | Very High (AGMA 4-5) | High | Hardening pre-finishing |
| **Gear Grinding** | Threaded grinding wheel | Highest (AGMA 1-3) | Low | Hardened precision gears |
| **Gear Broaching** | Broach | High | Very High | Internal splines, large batches |
| **Bevel Gear Generator** | Two straight/spiral cutters | High | Medium | Bevel gears |
| **Worm Gear Hobbing** | Worm-threaded hob | High | Medium | Worm wheel pairs |

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Shaper vs. Planer vs. Milling Machine

| Feature | Shaper | Planer | Milling Machine |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tool motion** | Tool reciprocates | Workpiece reciprocates | Tool rotates |
| **Workpiece size** | Small to medium | Large (up to 10 m) | Small to medium |
| **Surface produced** | Flat, slots | Large flat surfaces | Flat, curved, slots, gears |
| **Production rate** | Low | Low | High |
| **Power consumption** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Operator skill** | Medium | High | High |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Universal Dividing Head

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["INPUT: Crank Handle Rotation"] --> node2["WORM Single Thread"]
    node2 --> node3["WORM WHEEL 40 Teeth"]
    node3 --> node4["WORKPIECE Spindle"]
    node4 --> node5["ROTATIONAL OUTPUT 1/40 per turn"]
    
    node6["INDEX PLATE Stationary"] --> node7["SECTOR ARMS Pin Setting"]
    node7 --> node8["INDEX PIN Engagement"]
    node8 --> node2
    
    node9["CHANGE GEARS Compound Setup"] --> node10["GEAR TRAIN A on B"]
    node10 --> node11["SECOND INDEX PLATE Free"]
    node11 --> node2
    
    node12["WORKPIECE MOUNTED on Spindle"] --> node13["DIVIDED OUTPUT 1/X per division"]
    node4 --> node13
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The operator turns the crank.
- Power passes through the **worm-worm wheel pair** (40:1 reduction).
- The workpiece rotates by **$\frac{1}{40}$ revolution per crank turn**.
- For **simple indexing**, the index plate holes alone control positioning.
- For **compound indexing**, change gears link a second index plate to add a secondary rotation.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Gear Hobbing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["WORKPIECE Blank Mounted on Mandrel"] --> nodeB["MANDREL ROTATION via Indexing Gear Train"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["GEAR HOB Rotates at High Speed"]
    
    nodeC --> nodeD["AXIAL FEED of Hob along Gear Width"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["RADIAL FEED for Hob Plunge to Pitch Circle"]
    
    nodeE --> nodeF["CUTTING ACTION Generating Tooth Profile"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["TOOTH FORMED by Hob Teeth Profiling"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["COMPLETE GEAR after Hob Traverse"]
    
    nodeI["COOLANT Supply"] --> nodeF
    nodeJ["Hob Spindle Motor"] --> nodeC
    nodeK["Workpiece Spindle Motor"] --> nodeB
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The **hob** is a worm-shaped cutter with cutting edges along its helical flutes.
- Hob and workpiece rotate with a **fixed velocity ratio** equal to $\dfrac{Z_{workpiece}}{Z_{hob\ threads}}$.
- The hob is fed **axially** along the gear width to cut all teeth progressively.
- The generated tooth profile is **conjugate** (correctly meshing) because the hob is the conjugate of a rack.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Crank-and-Slotted-Link Shaper Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["BULL GEAR Driven by Motor"] --> node2["CRANK PIN Adjustable Radius"]
    node2 --> node3["SLOTTED LINK Oscillating Arm"]
    node3 --> node4["SLIDER Block in Slot"]
    node4 --> node5["RAM Reciprocating Motion"]
    node5 --> node6["TOOL POST Carries Single-Point Tool"]
    node6 --> node7["CUTTING ACTION on Workpiece"]
    
    node8["CROSS SLIDE Table Feed"] --> node9["RATCHET-PAWL Feed Mechanism"]
    node9 --> node10["TABLE Incremental Feed per Stroke"]
    node10 --> node7
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The **bull gear** rotates continuously, driving the crank pin.
- The crank pin engages the slot in the slotted link, converting rotation to oscillation.
- The slider at the end of the slot converts oscillation to linear reciprocation of the ram.
- The **cutting stroke** (forward) is slow, the **return stroke** is fast (quick return).
- Cross-feed is given by the **ratchet-pawl mechanism** to advance the table laterally.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of Gear Shaping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["GEAR SHAPER CUTTER Pinion-Shaped"] --> node2["RECIPROCATING AXIAL Motion"]
    node2 --> node3["CUTTING STROKE Forward"]
    node3 --> node4["IDLE STROKE Return"]
    
    node5["WORKPIECE Blank"] --> node6["SLOW ROTATION"]
    node6 --> node7["ROLLING VELOCITY RATIO with Cutter"]
    
    node7 --> node8["GENERATED TOOTH PROFILE Conjugate"]
    node3 --> node8
    node8 --> node9["COMPLETE GEAR after Multiple Strokes"]
    
    node10["FEED Motion Radial or Tangential"] --> node5
    node11["Cutter Drive Mechanism"] --> node1
    node12["Workpiece Drive Mechanism"] --> node6
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The cutter reciprocates like a **vertical shaper tool** but has the profile of a **pinion**.
- The workpiece rotates slowly in **synchronization** with the cutter, simulating the **rolling action** of two meshing gears.
- This generates the **conjugate involute tooth profile** on the workpiece.

---

## 4.5 Functional Architecture of Keyway Milling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["END MILL CUTTER Diameter = Keyway Width"] --> node2["ROTATING MOTION from Spindle"]
    node2 --> node3["PLUNGE CUT Vertical Downfeed"]
    node3 --> node4["DEPTH ACHIEVED = Keyway Depth"]
    
    node4 --> node5["LONGITUDINAL TABLE FEED"]
    node5 --> node6["KEYWAY CUT along Shaft Length"]
    node6 --> node7["FINISHED KEYWAY Closed Type"]
    
    node8["SLOT MILL Side and Face Cutter"] --> node9["ARBOR MOUNTING Horizontal"]
    node9 --> node10["LONGITUDINAL FEED along Open Keyway"]
    node10 --> node11["FINISHED KEYWAY Open Type"]
    
    node12["WOODRUFF CUTTER"] --> node13["SEMI-CIRCULAR PROFILE"]
    node13 --> node14["FINISHED WOODRUFF KEYWAY"]
```

**Description of Flow:**
- **Closed keyways** (in bores): Use end mill, plunge + traverse.
- **Open keyways** (on shafts): Use slot mill or side-and-face cutter on arbor.
- **Woodruff keyways**: Use a special woodruff cutter for semi-circular profile.
- In all cases, the **cutter width = keyway width** and the **depth = keyway depth**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Depth of Cut. Distinguish between Roughing and Finishing Cuts. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**Depth of Cut (d)** is the perpendicular distance between the unmachined surface and the machined surface of the workpiece, measured in mm. It represents the thickness of metal removed in a single pass of the cutting tool.

| Parameter | Roughing Cut | Finishing Cut |
|---|---|---|
| **Depth of Cut** | 2 – 8 mm (heavy) | 0.5 – 2 mm (light) |
| **Surface Finish** | Poor (Ra 12.5 – 25 µm) | Excellent (Ra 0.8 – 3.2 µm) |
| **Dimensional Accuracy** | Low (IT12 – IT14) | High (IT6 – IT8) |
| **Cutting Speed** | Lower (to save tool life) | Higher (for better finish) |
| **Purpose** | Bulk material removal | Final dimensional & surface quality |
| **Tool Material** | HSS, Coated Carbide | Carbide, CBN, Ceramic |

---

### Question 2: What is Simple Indexing? Why is Compound Indexing Required? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Simple Indexing** is the process of dividing the circumference of a workpiece into a specific number of equal angular spaces using only the **40:1 worm-worm wheel** of the dividing head and the **standard index plate** with its pre-drilled hole circles.

**The indexing movement** is given by:

$$N = \frac{40}{X}$$

where $X$ is the number of divisions.

**Why Compound Indexing is Required:**

Compound indexing is required when the required number of divisions **X** is a **prime number** (e.g., 59, 61, 67, 71, 79, 83, 89, 97, 101, 103, 107, 109, 113) or has factors that **cannot be obtained** from the standard hole circles of the index plate (15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33, 37, 39, 41, 43, 47, 49).

In such cases, simple indexing cannot reduce the fraction $\frac{40}{X}$ to a hole spacing. Compound indexing overcomes this by performing **two index movements simultaneously** using a **change gear train** linking a second index plate to the crank, achieving division by prime numbers with high accuracy.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** A shaft of 60 mm diameter is to be reduced to 54 mm in a single pass on a lathe. The spindle speed is 200 rpm and the feed is 0.3 mm/rev. Calculate:
1. The cutting speed.
2. The material removal rate (MRR).
3. The machining time for a 200 mm long cut, including approach and overtravel.

Assume tool approach angle = 60°.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** With a neat diagram, explain the **compound indexing** method. A workpiece is to be divided into **61 equal parts**. Compute the change plate ratio and specify the indexing movements.

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:**

#### Part (a) — Machining Time Calculation [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Initial diameter $D = 60$ mm
- Final diameter $d_f = 54$ mm
- Depth of cut $= \dfrac{60 - 54}{2} = 3$ mm
- Spindle speed $N = 200$ rpm
- Feed $f = 0.3$ mm/rev
- Work length $L_w = 200$ mm
- Tool approach angle $\phi = 60°$

**Step 1: Cutting Speed**

Cutting speed is calculated using the **average diameter** $D_{avg} = \dfrac{60 + 54}{2} = 57$ mm.

$$v_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D_{avg} \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 57 \times 200}{1000}$$

$$v_c = \frac{35814.2}{1000} = 35.81 \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 2: Material Removal Rate (MRR)**

For turning, $MRR = \pi \cdot d \cdot f \cdot v_c$ where $d$ is the depth of cut.

$$MRR = \pi \times 3 \times 0.3 \times 35.81 \times 1000 \text{ (unit conversion)}$$

Or directly using $MRR = d \cdot f \cdot v_c \cdot \pi$ in mm³/min:

$$MRR = 3 \times 0.3 \times 35.81 \times 1000 \times \pi$$

Wait — let me use the correct form: $MRR = d \cdot f \cdot v_c \cdot 1000$ mm³/min when $v_c$ is in m/min... actually the cleanest form:

$$MRR = \pi \cdot D_{avg} \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$MRR = \pi \times 57 \times 3 \times 0.3 \times 200$$

$$MRR = \pi \times 57 \times 180 = 3.1416 \times 57 \times 180$$

$$MRR = 3.1416 \times 10260 = 32233 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$MRR \approx 32,234 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 32.23 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 3: Approach Distance**

$$L_a = d \cdot \tan(\phi) = 3 \times \tan(60°) = 3 \times 1.732 = 5.196 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Overtravel**
Standard overtravel $L_o \approx 2$ to 3 mm. Let us take $L_o = 2$ mm.

**Step 5: Total Length**

$$L_{total} = L_w + L_a + L_o = 200 + 5.196 + 2 = 207.196 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Machining Time**

$$t_m = \frac{L_{total}}{f \cdot N} = \frac{207.196}{0.3 \times 200} = \frac{207.196}{60}$$

$$\boxed{t_m = 3.453 \text{ minutes} \approx 3 \text{ min } 27 \text{ sec}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Compound Indexing Explanation [7 Marks]

**Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.1 (Functional Architecture of Dividing Head).

**Working Principle:**

In compound indexing, the rotation of the workpiece is achieved by **two simultaneous movements**:
1. The **crank handle** is rotated.
2. The **change gears** transmit an additional rotation to the workpiece through a **second index plate**.

The net rotation of the workpiece = sum (or difference) of the two index movements.

**Procedure for 61 Divisions:**

**Step 1: Trial Selection of C and K**
Choose $C = 17$ and $K = 53$ (both prime).

**Step 2: Compute Change Plate Ratio**

$$\frac{A}{B} = \frac{40}{C} - \frac{K}{X} = \frac{40}{17} - \frac{53}{61}$$

Computing with common denominator $17 \times 61 = 1037$:

$$\frac{A}{B} = \frac{40 \times 61 - 53 \times 17}{1037} = \frac{2440 - 901}{1037} = \frac{1539}{1037}$$

The ratio $1.484 : 1$ is between 1 and 10 ✓.

**Step 3: Specify Gear Combinations**
From the standard gear set {24, 28, 32, 40, 44, 48, 56, 64, 72, 86, 100}, choose:
- Driving gear $A = 64$ teeth
- Driven gear $B = 44$ teeth

(This is a representative selection; exact values depend on the available gear kit.)

**Step 4: Indexing Movements**
- **First index movement**: $\dfrac{40}{17} = 2 + \dfrac{6}{17}$ → **2 full turns + 6 holes on 17-hole circle** of the **stationary** index plate.
- **Second index movement**: $\dfrac{53}{61}$ → **53 holes on 61-hole circle** of the **movable** index plate.

**Step 5: Operation Sequence**
1. Engage change gears A and B.
2. Set sector arms for 6 holes on 17-hole circle (first plate).
3. Lock the second index plate.
4. Turn crank, count 2 full turns + 6 holes.
5. Unlock second index plate, rotate it by 53 holes on 61-hole circle.
6. Lock second index plate again.
7. Repeat for each division.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat sketch, explain the **Gear Hobbing** process. State its advantages and limitations.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **construction and working of a crank-and-slotted-link shaper mechanism**. Define quick-return ratio. If the cutting stroke is 0.6 s and the return stroke is 0.4 s, find:
1. The quick-return ratio.
2. The number of strokes per minute.

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:**

#### Part (a) — Gear Hobbing [7 Marks]

**Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.2 (Functional Architecture of Gear Hobbing).

**Construction:**

A **gear hob** is a cylindrical cutting tool with **helical flutes** running along its length. The flutes have **straight-sided cutting teeth** whose profile is identical to the **basic rack tooth profile** of the gear system.

**Components:**
1. **Hob** — Cylindrical cutter with helical teeth and chip flutes.
2. **Workpiece** — Gear blank mounted on the hobbing machine mandrel.
3. **Hob Spindle** — Carries the hob; rotates at high speed.
4. **Workpiece Spindle** — Carries the gear blank; rotates slowly in synchronism.
5. **Change Gears** — Set the velocity ratio between hob and workpiece.

**Working:**

1. The hob is mounted on the **hob spindle** and rotated at the **cutting speed** ($v_c$).
2. The gear blank is mounted on the **workpiece mandrel** and rotated slowly.
3. The **velocity ratio** between hob and workpiece = $\dfrac{N_{workpiece}}{N_{hob}} = \dfrac{Z_{hob\ threads}}{Z_{workpiece}}$ (where $Z$ = number of teeth).
4. The hob is **fed radially** until it reaches the **pitch circle** of the gear.
5. The hob is then **fed axially** along the width of the gear blank, progressively cutting each tooth space.
6. After one complete axial pass, all teeth of the gear are fully formed.
7. Coolant is continuously supplied to the cutting zone.

**Advantages:**
- **High accuracy** (AGMA 6–8 grade).
- **High production rate** (continuous cutting, no idle return stroke).
- **Generates** the conjugate involute profile (no need for profile correction in the cutter).
- **Suitable for** spur, helical, worm wheels, splines, and sprockets.

**Limitations:**
- **Cannot cut** internal gears or gears with interfering shoulders.
- **Cannot cut** cluster gears on the same shaft.
- **High initial cost** of hobs.
- **Limited to** external gears with adequate blank space.

---

#### Part (b) — Shaper Mechanism [7 Marks]

**Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.3 (Functional Architecture of Shaper Mechanism).

**Construction:**

The crank-and-slotted-link mechanism (also called the **Whitworth quick-return mechanism**) consists of:

1. **Bull Gear (Drive Gear)** — Large gear driven by the motor, rotating at constant speed.
2. **Crank Pin** — Eccentric pin on the bull gear; its **radius is adjustable** to vary the stroke length.
3. **Slotted Link (Rocker Arm)** — A pivoted arm with a slot that engages the crank pin.
4. **Slider** — A block that slides in the slot of the slotted link.
5. **Ram** — The reciprocating member that carries the tool post.
6. **Cross-Slide and Table** — Holds the workpiece and provides the lateral feed.
7. **Ratchet-and-Pawl Feed Mechanism** — Advances the table by a fixed increment per stroke.

**Working:**

1. The **bull gear** rotates uniformly.
2. The **crank pin** describes a circle and forces the **slotted link** to oscillate about its pivot.
3. The **slider**, while moving in the slot, reciprocates the **ram** with a linear motion.
4. As the crank pin moves through the **upper half** of its circle (180°), the ram moves **forward** — this is the **cutting stroke** (slow, since the slider velocity is lower in the middle of the slot).
5. As the crank pin moves through the **lower half** of its circle (180°), the ram moves **backward** — this is the **return stroke** (fast, since the slider velocity is higher in the middle of the slot).
6. The **quick-return ratio** is therefore less than 1.
7. After each return stroke, the **ratchet-and-pawl** advances the **table** by a small cross-feed increment.

**Quick-Return Ratio Definition:**

$$Q = \frac{\text{Time of return stroke}}{\text{Time of cutting stroke}} = \frac{T_r}{T_c}$$

**Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Cutting stroke time $T_c = 0.6$ s
- Return stroke time $T_r = 0.4$ s

**Step 1: Quick-Return Ratio**

$$Q = \frac{T_r}{T_c} = \frac{0.4}{0.6} = 0.667$$

$$\boxed{Q = 0.667 \text{ (or } 2:3\text{)}}$$

**Step 2: Number of Strokes per Minute**

Total time per cycle (one cutting + one return stroke) = $T_c + T_r = 0.6 + 0.4 = 1.0$ s.

Number of cycles per minute = $\dfrac{60}{1.0} = 60$ cycles/min.

Since one cycle = one cutting stroke + one return stroke = one double stroke:

$$\boxed{n_s = 60 \text{ double strokes per minute}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING & PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Indexing Calculation Errors:** Students often forget to add **approach and overtravel** to the work length, OR they convert $\frac{40}{X}$ incorrectly without finding the **nearest hole circle**. Always verify your hole spacing using the available index plate hole circles.
> 
> 2. **Compound Indexing Misconception:** Students assume compound indexing uses **two** index plates. CORRECT — but both plates are used: the first is **stationary** and the second is **movable** (linked via change gears). Writing "two index plates" without specifying their role loses marks.
> 
> 3. **Depth of Cut Confusion:** In **turning**, depth of cut = $\frac{D - d_f}{2}$ (radius reduction), NOT $(D - d_f)$. This is a frequent error.
> 
> 4. **Quick-Return Ratio Sign Error:** Students often write $Q = \frac{T_c}{T_r}$ instead of $\frac{T_r}{T_c}$. The **correct definition** is **return time / cutting time** and the value is always **less than 1**.
> 
> 5. **Gear Hobbing Kinematics:** Students forget that the hob and workpiece rotate in a **fixed ratio** (= teeth ratio of meshing gears), and that the hob is essentially a **cutting rack** that progressively generates the involute profile.
> 
> 6. **Approach Distance Formula:** Use $L_a = d \cdot \tan(\phi)$, NOT $L_a = \sqrt{d \cdot (D-d)}$ for general turning (the latter is for facing operations).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary — Read This Before the Exam**

### Depth of Cut
- Perpendicular thickness of material removed per pass.
- **Roughing:** 2–8 mm; **Finishing:** 0.5–2 mm.
- Turning: $d = \frac{D_{initial} - D_{final}}{2}$.

### Machining Time
- **Turning/Drilling:** $t_m = \frac{L}{f \cdot N}$
- **Milling:** $t_m = \frac{L}{f_t \cdot z \cdot N}$
- **Shaping:** $t_m = \frac{L}{c \cdot n_s}$ (c = cross-feed per stroke)
- Always include **approach + overtravel** in L.

### Indexing — Simple
- Formula: $N = \frac{40}{X}$
- Limited to divisions with factors available in standard hole circles.

### Indexing — Compound
- Formula: $\frac{A}{B} = \frac{40}{C} \pm \frac{K}{X}$
- Used for **prime** number divisions.
- Requires **change gear train** + two index plates.

### Keyway Milling
- **Closed keyways** (in bores) → End mill.
- **Open keyways** (on shafts) → Side-and-face / slot mill.
- **Woodruff keyways** → Special woodruff cutter.

### Gear Cutting
- **Forming:** Gear milling cutter (one cutter per module + number of teeth).
- **Generating:** Hobbing, shaping, shaving, grinding.
- **Hobbing** is the fastest and most common.
- **Shaping** is used for internal gears and cluster gears.

### Shaping
- Uses **crank-and-slotted-link** mechanism.
- **Cutting stroke** is slow; **return stroke** is fast.
- **Quick-return ratio** $Q = \frac{T_r}{T_c} < 1$.
- Cutting occurs **only on the forward stroke**.

### Critical Numbers to Memorize
- Worm wheel: **40 teeth** (universal dividing head standard).
- Standard index plate hole circles: 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33, 37, 39, 41, 43, 47, 49.
- Approach angle typical values: 45°, 60°, 75°.

### Production Engineering Insights
- **MRR increases** with depth of cut, feed, and cutting speed.
- **Tool life decreases** with increasing depth of cut and cutting speed.
- **Surface finish improves** with smaller depth of cut and finer feed.
- **Hobbing** is preferred for production rates > 100 gears/day.
- **Shaping** is preferred for low-volume, large-size workpieces.
- **Compound indexing** is the **least preferred** in modern industry — **CNC rotary tables** have replaced it for most applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forming and Generation (Gear Hobbing)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Forming vs. Generation — The Foundational Distinction

In the domain of gear manufacturing, KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes all material removal processes into two fundamental families: **Forming** and **Generation**. Understanding this distinction is non-negotiable, as it is a guaranteed high-frequency question in University Examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forming** is a process where the **cutting tool is an exact replica (negative) of the final tooth profile**. Material is removed because the tool physically forces its geometry into the workpiece. (e.g., gear milling with a disc or end-mill type form cutter, gear broaching).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generation** is a process where the cutting tool has a **fundamentally different geometry** from the final tooth profile, but the **relative motion** between the tool and the workpiece mathematically generates the correct involute tooth profile. **Gear Hobbing** is the most important generating process.

## 1.2 Gear Hobbing — Formal KTU Definition

**Gear Hobbing** is a continuous, indexing-free gear-cutting process in which a multi-tooth, worm-shaped cutting tool called a **Hob** rotates about its own axis while the workpiece (gear blank) rotates about a parallel axis. Through a precisely synchronized kinematic relationship, the cutting edges of the hob progressively generate involute gear teeth on the blank, one flute-tooth at a time.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Screw and the Nut:** Imagine threading a long screw (the hob) through a metal block (the gear blank). The screw rotates, and if the block is forced to slowly rotate in sync, the helical threads of the screw will *sculpt* a perfectly matching set of internal threads into the block. Each thread on the screw removes just a sliver of metal, but together they generate the full thread profile. A hob works identically — its helical flutes act like the screw threads, and the gear blank is rotated so slowly that it stays *phase-locked* with the hob, ensuring involute teeth are generated. The "form" of a single hob tooth is NOT a gear tooth; the **motion** makes it a gear tooth.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Must-Knows)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Hob** | The worm-shaped, multi-fluted, multi-toothed cutting tool |
| **Starts (Z₁)** | Number of helical flutes on the hob (commonly 1, 2, or 3) |
| **Workpiece Teeth (Z₂)** | Number of teeth to be cut on the gear blank |
| **Hobbing Machine** | Specialized machine tool with synchronized hob + table rotation |
| **Indexing** | Not required in hobbing (continuous process — major advantage) |
| **Generating Action** | The relative hob-workpiece motion creates the involute profile |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Hob

A hob is essentially a **worm gear** with **longitudinal gash slots** cut into it. Each gash slot creates a series of cutting edges along the helical flute. The rake angle is generated by the gashing geometry, making the tool self-sharpening at the cutting edges.

The hob has:
- **Outer diameter (D₀)** — the cutting diameter
- **Tip diameter** — the diameter at the cutting edge
- **Root diameter** — diameter at the bottom of the gashes
- **Lead (L_h)** — axial advance per revolution = π × m × Z₁ (where m = module)

## 2.2 Kinematics of Gear Hobbing — The Heart of the Process

The synchronized motion is the soul of hobbing. For involute generation to occur, the hob and the workpiece must rotate in a strict mathematical ratio:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Kinematic Equation:**
> $$\frac{N_h}{N_w} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1}$$
> Where **N_h** = hob RPM, **N_w** = workpiece RPM, **Z₁** = hob starts, **Z₂** = workpiece teeth.

**Plain English:** The hob rotates *fast* (high RPM, high surface speed), while the workpiece rotates *very slowly* because the gear blank has many more teeth (Z₂) than the hob has starts (Z₁).

## 2.3 The Three Feeds in Hobbing

| Feed Type | Direction | When Used | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Axial Feed** | Along the gear axis (parallel to hob axis) | Most common, high productivity | f_a (mm/rev of workpiece) |
| **Radial Feed** | Perpendicular to gear axis (cutting in/out) | Roughing heavy modules, then axial finishing | f_r |
| **Tangential Feed** | Perpendicular to hob axis, along blank face | Used when axial feed is mechanically restricted | f_t |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Velocity (Hob) | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_0 \cdot N_h}{1000}$ | m/min |
| 2 | Workpiece RPM | $N_w = N_h \times \dfrac{Z_1}{Z_2}$ | rev/min |
| 3 | Axial Feed Velocity | $V_f = f_a \times N_w$ | mm/min |
| 4 | Total Hobbing Time (Axial) | $T = \dfrac{L + b}{V_f} \times \text{passes}$ | min |
| 5 | Hob Lead | $L_h = \pi \cdot m \cdot Z_1$ | mm |
| 6 | Metal Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = f_a \times v_c \times a_p$ | mm³/min |
| 7 | Cutting Power | $P_c = \dfrac{F_c \times V_c}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta}$ | kW |
| 8 | Machining Time per Pass | $t_m = \dfrac{L + b}{f_a \times N_w}$ | min/pass |

**Where:**
- $D_0$ = Outer diameter of hob (mm)
- $L$ = Face width of gear (mm)
- $b$ = Overrun/approach allowance (typically 3–5 mm)
- $a_p$ = Depth of cut = **2.25 × m** (full depth for standard involute)
- $F_c$ = Tangential cutting force (N)
- $\eta$ = Machine efficiency (0.7–0.85)
- $m$ = module of gear (mm)

## 2.5 Why Hobbing is Superior — Engineering Utility

- **No indexing error** — continuous generation ensures uniform tooth spacing.
- **High productivity** — all teeth cut simultaneously, not one at a time.
- **Excellent surface finish** — multiple cutting teeth share the load.
- **Suitable for spur, helical, worm, and spline gears** with the same machine.
- **Limitation:** Cannot cut internal gears or gears located very close to a shoulder (use shaping instead).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Kinematic Constraint Between Hob and Workpiece

The fundamental generation principle requires the **linear velocity of the hob thread (lead)** to match the **pitch line velocity of the workpiece** at the point of meshing — otherwise, slipping or jamming occurs. This is the genesis of the $N_h/N_w$ ratio.

**Step 1 — Linear speed of hob's helical thread along its axis:**
The hob has Z₁ starts with module *m*. The axial advance per one revolution of the hob is the **lead**:
$$L_h = \pi \cdot m \cdot Z_1 \quad \text{(mm/rev of hob)}$$

**Step 2 — Linear speed of the workpiece surface in the axial direction:**
For a workpiece with module *m* and Z₂ teeth, the circular pitch is $p_c = \pi \cdot m$. In one revolution of the workpiece, axial feed *f_a* advances the blank by *f_a* mm. The number of teeth passing a fixed axial point per workpiece revolution is exactly *Z₂*.

**Step 3 — Equate the generation condition:**
For correct involute generation without slippage at the pitch cylinder, the axial pitch of the hob flute must match the circular pitch of the workpiece:
$$\text{Pitch of hob flute} = \text{Pitch of gear} \implies \frac{L_h}{Z_1} = \pi m$$

This is automatically satisfied by the geometry. The **rotational sync** is the remaining constraint:

**Step 4 — Final Kinematic Relationship:**
For every revolution of the hob, the workpiece must rotate by exactly $Z_1/Z_2$ revolutions so that one new tooth space advances into the cutting zone for each hob flute pass.

$$\begin{aligned}
N_w &= N_h \times \frac{Z_1}{Z_2} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{N_h}{N_w} &= \frac{Z_2}{Z_1}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **master equation** of gear hobbing kinematics.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hobbing Machining Time

Consider a gear with face width *L* (mm), module *m*, tooth count *Z₂*, and hob with *Z₁* starts running at *N_h* rpm with axial feed *f_a* (mm/rev of workpiece).

**Step 1 — Axial feed velocity:**
$$V_f = f_a \times N_w = f_a \times N_h \times \frac{Z_1}{Z_2} \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

**Step 2 — Length to be traversed:**
The hob must travel the full face width *L* plus an **overrun allowance** *b* (so the tooth profile is fully generated, including tip-relief). Total travel:
$$L_{total} = L + b$$

**Step 3 — Time per axial pass:**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{pass} &= \frac{L_{total}}{V_f} = \frac{L + b}{f_a \times N_h \times \dfrac{Z_1}{Z_2}} \\
\Rightarrow T_{pass} &= \frac{(L + b) \times Z_2}{f_a \times N_h \times Z_1} \quad \text{(minutes/pass)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Total time for n passes (e.g., roughing + finishing):**
$$T_{total} = n \times T_{pass} = \frac{n \times (L + b) \times Z_2}{f_a \times N_h \times Z_1}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Cutting Power Requirement

**Step 1 — Specific cutting energy** $K_c$ (N/mm²) is material-dependent. For mild steel hobbing: $K_c \approx 2000$ N/mm².

**Step 2 — Uncut chip cross-section:**
$$A_c = f_a \times a_p \quad \text{(mm}^2\text{)}$$
where $a_p = 2.25 m$ for standard full-depth involute teeth.

**Step 3 — Tangential cutting force:**
$$F_c = K_c \times A_c = K_c \times f_a \times 2.25m \quad \text{(N)}$$

**Step 4 — Power at spindle:**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{F_c \times V_c}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta} \quad \text{(kW)} \\
&= \frac{K_c \times f_a \times 2.25m \times \pi D_0 N_h}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}
\end{aligned}$$

This power must be supplied by the hob spindle motor of the hobbing machine.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A hob with $D_0 = 80$ mm, $Z_1 = 2$ starts, runs at $N_h = 250$ rpm. The workpiece is a 40-tooth spur gear with face width $L = 30$ mm, module $m = 4$ mm. Axial feed $f_a = 1.5$ mm/rev. Overrun $b = 5$ mm. Calculate: (i) Cutting velocity, (ii) Workpiece RPM, (iii) Hobbing time per pass.

**Solution:**

**(i) Cutting Velocity:**
$$V_c = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times 250}{1000} = \frac{62831.85}{1000} \approx 62.83 \text{ m/min}$$

**(ii) Workpiece RPM:**
$$N_w = N_h \times \frac{Z_1}{Z_2} = 250 \times \frac{2}{40} = 12.5 \text{ rev/min}$$

**(iii) Hobbing Time per Pass:**
$$T_{pass} = \frac{(L + b) \times Z_2}{f_a \times N_h \times Z_1} = \frac{(30 + 5) \times 40}{1.5 \times 250 \times 2} = \frac{1400}{750} \approx 1.867 \text{ min}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Gear Hobbing Process

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the kinematic and functional interactions between the machine tool sub-systems during gear hobbing. This is a *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* — the recommended fallback for representing complex mechanical setups in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Spindle Motor] --> B[Hob Arbor]
    B --> C[Rotating Hob - Z1 starts]
    D[Worktable Drive - Synchro Gearbox] --> E[Index Worm and Wheel]
    E --> F[Rotating Gear Blank - Z2 teeth]
    C --> G[Cutting Zone]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Generated Involute Tooth Profile]
    I[Feed Drive - Servo] --> J[Feed Slide]
    J --> K[Axial or Radial Feed Motion]
    K --> C
    L[Coolant Supply - Flood] --> G
    M[Controller - CNC] --> A
    M --> D
    M --> I
    H --> N[Finished Gear Output]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: From Blank to Finished Gear

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Gear Blank Mounted] --> P2[Tool Set Hob to Center]
    P2 --> P3[Engage Synchronized Rotation]
    P3 --> P4[Apply Radial or Axial Feed]
    P4 --> P5{Check Tooth Profile and Size}
    P5 --> P6[Continue Hobbing Pass]
    P6 --> P7[Retract Hob]
    P7 --> P8[Finished Involute Gear]
    P5 --> P9[Adjust and Repeat]
    P9 --> P4
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Hob Geometry and Cutting Edges

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1[Hob Outer Diameter D0] --> H2[Helical Flute - Z1 starts]
    H2 --> H3[Gash Slots Form Rake Face]
    H3 --> H4[Cutting Edges Along Flute]
    H4 --> H5[Tip Diameter Active in Cutting]
    H6[Hob Root Diameter] --> H4
    H7[Hob Length Lh - Along Axis] --> H4
```

## 4.4 Kinematic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Spindle Motor] --> M2[Hob Spindle]
    M2 --> M3[N_h rpm]
    M3 --> M4[Sync Gearbox Ratio Z2 over Z1]
    M4 --> M5[Worktable]
    M5 --> M6[N_w = N_h x Z1 over Z2 rpm]
    M3 --> M7[Cutting at V_c m per min]
    M6 --> M8[Involute Generation at Pitch Line]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Distinguish between forming and generation methods of gear cutting. Give one example of each.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Forming:** The cutting tool has the *exact profile* of the gear tooth space. Material is removed because the tool *physically imprints* its shape onto the blank. Each tooth space is cut individually. **Example:** Gear milling with a form cutter (disc-type or end-mill type).
- **Generation:** The tool has a *fundamentally different profile* (e.g., hob teeth are straight or helical, not involute). The correct involute tooth profile is produced by the *relative rolling motion* between the tool and the workpiece. All tooth spaces are cut in *one continuous pass*. **Example:** Gear hobbing, gear shaping.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often say "hob is a worm gear" but forget to clarify that it is the *relative motion*, not the tool shape, that creates the involute. Marks are deducted if the role of synchronized rotation is not mentioned.

### Q2. Why is gear hobbing classified as a continuous indexing process? What is the role of the hob starts (Z₁)?
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
Gear hobbing is a **continuous indexing process** because the workpiece rotates in a continuous, synchronized manner with the hob, eliminating the need for *periodic indexing* between teeth (as required in gear milling). The number of hob starts (Z₁) determines the *feed in tooth generation* per revolution of the hob — each start generates a portion of the tooth profile in sequence. The kinematic constraint is $N_h/N_w = Z_2/Z_1$. Common hob starts are 1, 2, or 3; single-start hobs are used for fine finishing, multi-start hobs for faster roughing.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice Format** — Answer **ANY ONE** of the following:

### Question A (14 Marks) — Gear Hobbing Kinematics and Time

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the kinematic relationship between the rotational speeds of the hob and the workpiece in a gear hobbing operation. Explain the role of hob starts and workpiece teeth. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 48-tooth spur gear of module 4 mm and face width 25 mm is to be hobbed using a single-start hob of 75 mm outer diameter, running at 300 rpm. The axial feed is 1.25 mm/rev. The overrun is 4 mm. Calculate the (i) cutting velocity, (ii) workpiece rpm, and (iii) total machining time for one axial pass. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation:** See Section 3.1 above. Key result: $N_w = N_h \cdot (Z_1/Z_2)$. Role of $Z_1$: determines the *generation ratio*; a single-start hob gives the finest generation. Role of $Z_2$: workpiece tooth count sets the workpiece's slow rotation. The synchronized motion *simulates a gear mesh* between the hob (acting as a worm) and the workpiece (acting as a worm wheel), mathematically producing involute teeth.

**(b) Solution:**

Given: $Z_2 = 48$, $m = 4$ mm, $L = 25$ mm, $Z_1 = 1$, $D_0 = 75$ mm, $N_h = 300$ rpm, $f_a = 1.25$ mm/rev, $b = 4$ mm.

**(i) Cutting velocity:**
$$V_c = \frac{\pi \times 75 \times 300}{1000} = \frac{70685.83}{1000} \approx 70.69 \text{ m/min}$$

**(ii) Workpiece rpm:**
$$N_w = 300 \times \frac{1}{48} = 6.25 \text{ rpm}$$

**(iii) Machining time per pass:**
$$T = \frac{(25 + 4) \times 48}{1.25 \times 300 \times 1} = \frac{1392}{375} \approx 3.71 \text{ min/pass}$$

### Question B (14 Marks) — Forming vs Generation with Hobb Diagram

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the gear hobbing process. Discuss why hobbing is classified as a *generating* process and not a *forming* process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare gear hobbing with gear shaping in terms of: (i) tool geometry, (ii) indexing requirement, (iii) capability for internal gears, (iv) surface finish, and (v) production rate. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Explanation:**
In gear hobbing, the **hob** is mounted on the *hob arbor* of the hobbing machine. The **gear blank** is clamped on a **rotating worktable** whose axis is parallel to the hob axis at a distance equal to the *center distance*. Both rotate in synchronism (governed by a *change gear box* in conventional machines or *CNC interpolation* in modern machines). The hob is fed *axially* along the gear face, and its helical flutes — each carrying multiple cutting edges — progressively remove material. The involute profile is *generated* by the *rolling* meshing motion. **Hobbing is generation, not forming,** because (1) the hob's tooth profile is straight/helical, not involute; (2) the involute shape emerges from the *relative motion*; (3) all teeth are produced simultaneously, not by impressing a tool shape.

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Gear Hobbing | Gear Shaping |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Tool geometry | Worm-shaped multi-tooth hob (Z₁ starts) | Straight rack-cutter (reciprocates) |
| (ii) Indexing | Not required (continuous) | Required between strokes (for some machines) |
| (iii) Internal gears | **Cannot** hob internal gears | **Can** shape internal gears |
| (iv) Surface finish | Excellent (multi-tooth sharing) | Good (single-point cutting) |
| (v) Production rate | **High** (continuous, all teeth at once) | Lower (reciprocating, intermittent) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Most Common Mark Loss in this Topic:**
> 1. Confusing *hob starts* with *hob teeth* — hob has a few *starts* (1–3), but many *teeth* along each start.
> 2. Writing the kinematic ratio inverted — it is $N_w = N_h \cdot (Z_1/Z_2)$, NOT $N_h = N_w \cdot (Z_2/Z_1)$.
> 3. Forgetting the *overrun allowance* *b* in machining time — examiners specifically test this.
> 4. Stating hobbing can cut *internal* gears — it CANNOT. Use shaping for internal gears.
> 5. Saying the hob teeth are *involute* — they are NOT. The involute is *generated* by motion.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Forming** = tool shape = product shape. **Generation** = product shape created by *motion*.
- ☐ **Hob** = worm-shaped, multi-fluted, multi-toothed cutter with gashed flutes.
- ☐ **Master kinematic equation:** $\dfrac{N_h}{N_w} = \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1}$ (or $N_w = N_h \cdot Z_1/Z_2$).
- ☐ **Cutting velocity** $V_c = \pi D_0 N_h / 1000$ m/min.
- ☐ **Axial feed** is most common; *f_a* in mm/rev of workpiece.
- ☐ **Machining time** $T = (L + b) \cdot Z_2 / (f_a \cdot N_h \cdot Z_1)$ — remember the **overrun b**.
- ☐ Hobbing is **continuous** (no indexing), high productivity, excellent finish.
- ☐ Hobbing **cannot** cut internal gears — use gear **shaping** for that.
- ☐ Depth of cut $a_p = 2.25 m$ for standard full-depth involute teeth.
- ☐ The involute profile is **generated**, not formed — this is the key conceptual distinction.
- ☐ Hob starts $Z_1$ are typically 1 (finishing), 2 or 3 (roughing).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Broaching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gear Broaching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear Broaching** is a high-precision, high-productivity, chipless-metal-forming-and-finishing operation that uses a specialized multi-point cutting tool called a **gear broach** (or **gear broaching tool**) to simultaneously cut all the teeth of an internal or external gear in a single linear/rotary stroke.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, gear broaching is classified as a **multiple-point cutting process** belonging to the family of **broaching operations**, specifically applied for the mass production of **internal spur gears**, **splines**, **serrations**, and **external gears** with high dimensional accuracy and excellent surface finish.

The operation is defined by:
- A linear or rotary relative motion between the workpiece and the broach.
- A progressive increase in the cutting edge height (or radial projection) along the broach length, ensuring each tooth removes a defined **cutting increment (chip thickness, h)**.
- Material removal happening in a **single pass** with no return idle stroke (in horizontal pull-type broaches).

### Key Vocabulary Anchors

| Term | Standard Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Broach** | A long, multi-tooth precision tool where each successive tooth is radially offset (lifted) to take a deeper cut. |
| **Roughing Section** | The initial portion of the broach that removes the bulk of material with coarse pitch and large lift per tooth. |
| **Semi-Finishing Section** | Intermediate portion that begins correcting tooth profile. |
| **Finishing Section** | Final portion that gives accurate tooth profile, dimensional size, and high surface finish. |
| **Cutting Increment (h)** | The radial step-up (lift) of each successive tooth above the previous one, typically in **mm**. |
| **Pitch of Teeth (p)** | The linear distance between two consecutive cutting teeth along the broach axis. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Gear broaching is the **only production process** capable of finishing internal gears of small diameter (e.g., automotive gearbox gears) with **IT7–IT6 accuracy** and surface roughness of **Ra 0.8–0.4 µm** in a single stroke, achieving cycle times of **10–30 seconds**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you have a **block of hard wood** and want to carve a rectangular slot through it. If you use a single chisel, you must strike it repeatedly, gradually deepening the slot. The process is slow, exhausting, and inaccurate.

Now imagine you invent a **magical, multi-bladed saw** where:
- The first blade barely scratches the surface.
- The second blade cuts a little deeper.
- The third cuts even deeper…
- Until the **last 3–4 blades** finalize the exact slot profile perfectly.

You just **push or pull** this magical tool through the wood in **ONE smooth motion** and the perfect slot emerges.

That is **gear broaching**. The only difference is:
- The "wood" is hardened alloy steel.
- The "magical multi-bladed tool" is a precision-hardened **HSS or carbide gear broach**.
- The "slot" is a complex gear-tooth profile (involute).
- The "one push" is performed by a **hydraulic broaching machine** exerting forces up to **20–200 tonnes**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Connection:** Every automobile on the road today contains gears that were cut using gear broaching. The internal ring gears of planetary gear sets, transmission splines, and steering column splines are virtually all gear-broached for noise, strength, and precision reasons.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Gear Broaching

| Classification Axis | Type | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **By Workpiece Geometry** | Internal Gear Broaching | Broach passes *through* a pre-bored workpiece. Most common. |
| | External Gear Broaching | Broach passes *over* the external periphery. Less common. |
| **By Machine Kinematics** | Linear (Pull / Push) Broaching | Broach moves in a straight line. |
| | Rotary (Continuous) Broaching | Broach rotates around the workpiece (e.g., LaSalle rotary broach). |
| **By Broach Geometry** | Solid Broach | Single-piece hardened tool for small lots / small gears. |
| | Built-up (Inserted-Tooth) Broach | Replaceable HSS/carbide teeth held in a holder. For large gears. |
| | Sled-Runner Broach | Tooth section rides on a fixed spline-form mandrel. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often confuse **gear hobbing** with **gear broaching**. Hobbing is a *generative*, *continuous*, *chip-by-chip* milling process. Broaching is a *form-cutting*, *single-pass*, *multi-tooth simultaneous* process. They are fundamentally different in kinematics and tool design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Gear Broach

A typical internal gear broach consists of the following distinct functional zones, traversed sequentially by the workpiece:

1. **Pilot Section (Guide):** A short, non-cutting leading portion that aligns the broach coaxially with the pre-drilled pilot hole of the workpiece. Critical for concentricity.
2. **Roughing Teeth (≈ 60–70% of total length):** Each tooth is radially raised (lifted) by a constant amount **h_r**. These teeth rapidly remove bulk stock. Tooth pitch is large.
3. **Semi-Finishing Teeth (≈ 15–20%):** Lift per tooth reduces to **h_sf**, and the involute profile starts emerging. Pitch decreases.
4. **Finishing Teeth (≈ 10–15%):** Multiple identical teeth (typically 3–7) with the **same diameter** and **final involute profile** to improve dimensional accuracy and surface finish.
5. **Sizing Teeth (Last 1–2 teeth):** Slightly oversized to compensate for elastic spring-back of the workpiece upon exit.
6. **Pulling Shank (or Push End):** Engages with the broach puller (or pusher) of the machine for force transmission.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the exact sequence of events during a single gear broaching cycle. This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

**Step 1 — Workpiece Preparation**
The workpiece (gear blank) is pre-machined with a **pilot hole** of diameter **D_p** that is slightly larger than the broach root diameter but smaller than the gear addendum circle.

**Step 2 — Loading and Alignment**
The blank is loaded onto a horizontal or vertical broaching machine fixture. The pilot section of the broach enters the pilot hole, establishing concentricity within **±0.005 mm** typically.

**Step 3 — Engagement of Roughing Teeth**
As the broach is pulled (or pushed) through, the first roughing tooth engages and removes a chip of cross-sectional area:
$$A_i = w \times h_r$$
where **w** is the **face width of the gear** (mm) and **h_r** is the **lift per roughing tooth** (mm).

**Step 4 — Progressive Material Removal**
Each subsequent roughing tooth removes a similar chip, but its cutting edge is **lifted higher** by **h_r**. The cumulative depth removed after **n** roughing teeth is:
$$H_n = n \times h_r$$

**Step 5 — Profile Generation in Finishing Zone**
Once the roughing has cleared the root diameter, the semi-finishing and finishing teeth engage. These teeth are **profile-ground to the involute curve** and shave the final involute tooth flanks to the exact module and pressure angle.

**Step 6 — Sizing and Spring-Back Compensation**
The last sizing tooth is **oversized by 0.01–0.03 mm** to compensate for elastic recovery, ensuring the gear tooth returns to the desired nominal dimension after the broach exits.

**Step 7 — Retraction (Return Stroke)**
The broach is retracted to the start position. For pull-type broaches, the return is rapid and idle (no cutting). This is the only non-productive part of the cycle.

---

## 2.3 Why Gear Broaching? The Engineering Justification

| Advantage | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Single-pass operation** | Cycle time of 10–30 s vs. 2–5 min for hobbing. |
| **Excellent surface finish (Ra 0.4–1.6 µm)** | Reduces post-grinding operations. |
| **High dimensional accuracy (IT6–IT7)** | Suitable for quiet-running automotive gears. |
| **Capability to cut internal gears of small bores** | Hobbing cannot reach deep internal bores. |
| **No special operator skill required** | Process is entirely machine-controlled. |
| **High material removal rate** | Removes 0.5–5 mm of stock in one stroke. |

**Disadvantages:**
- Broach cost is **very high** (₹50,000 – ₹5,00,000+), making it uneconomical for low volumes.
- Broach design and manufacture is complex.
- Limited to **through-holes** in internal broaching (workpiece must be open at both ends).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every high-yield formula you must memorize for KTU ESE 2024.**

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F_t = k_s \cdot w \cdot h$ | $F_t$ = tangential cutting force per tooth, $k_s$ = specific cutting resistance, $w$ = face width, $h$ = lift per tooth | N | Force on a single tooth |
| 2 | $F = F_t \cdot n_c$ | $F$ = total cutting force, $n_c$ = number of teeth simultaneously cutting | N | Total force on the broach |
| 3 | $L = (n_r + n_{sf} + n_f + n_{sz}) \cdot p_{avg}$ | $L$ = broach total length, $p_{avg}$ = average pitch | mm | Broach length design |
| 4 | $H_{total} = n_r \cdot h_r + n_{sf} \cdot h_{sf} + (h_f + h_{sz})$ | $H_{total}$ = total radial stock to be removed | mm | Stock distribution |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_w \cdot N}{60}$ (only for rotary broach) | $v$ = cutting velocity, $D_w$ = workpiece diameter, $N$ = rpm | m/min | Cutting velocity |
| 6 | $v_{cut} = \dfrac{L_{cut}}{t_{cut}}$ | $v_{cut}$ = cutting velocity (linear broach), $L_{cut}$ = broach stroke, $t_{cut}$ = cutting time | m/min | Linear broach velocity |
| 7 | $t_{cycle} = t_{cut} + t_{return} + t_{load} + t_{unload}$ | $t_{cycle}$ = total cycle time | s | Production planning |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{F \cdot v_{cut}}{60{,}000}$ | $P$ = power required | kW | Machine sizing |
| 9 | $MRR = w \cdot h \cdot v_{cut} \cdot n_c$ | $MRR$ = material removal rate | mm³/min | Productivity analysis |
| 10 | $T = \dfrac{L_{cut}}{f_r}$ | $T$ = broaching time, $f_r$ = feed rate (mm/s) | s | Time estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion Note:** Specific cutting resistance **$k_s$** for broaching steels is typically in the range **1500–2500 N/mm²**. For cast iron: **900–1200 N/mm²**. For aluminum alloys: **400–700 N/mm²**. Always carry units; **N/mm² × mm × mm = N**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Internal ring gears, planetary gear sets, transmission splines, steering racks.
- **Aerospace:** Internal splines on actuator housings, helicopter rotor shaft splines.
- **Heavy Machinery:** Splined couplings on industrial gearboxes.
- **Hydraulics:** Internal tooth profiles of hydraulic pump bodies.
- **Turbines:** Involute splines for high-torque shaft-hub connections in power generation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cutting Force on a Single Broach Tooth

### Given
- Specific cutting resistance: $k_s$ (N/mm²)
- Face width of cut: $w$ (mm)
- Lift per tooth (undeformed chip thickness): $h$ (mm)

### Assumptions
- Orthogonal cutting model (chip flow is perpendicular to cutting velocity).
- Specific cutting energy is constant (independent of $h$ and $v$).
- No built-up edge (sharp tool, proper cutting fluid).

### Derivation

The **undeformed chip cross-sectional area** removed by one tooth is:
$$A_c = w \times h$$

By the fundamental metal cutting equation (Merchant / Kronenberg model), the **tangential cutting force on one tooth** is:
$$F_t = k_s \cdot A_c$$

Substituting the chip area:
$$F_t = k_s \cdot w \cdot h$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master equation** for broach force analysis. Master it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Cutting Force on the Broach

### Given
- Force per tooth: $F_t$
- Number of teeth cutting simultaneously: $n_c$

### Logic
In broaching, the pitch **p** of the teeth is deliberately chosen **smaller** than the workpiece face width **w** so that **multiple teeth cut at the same time**. Specifically:
$$n_c = \left\lceil \dfrac{w}{p} \right\rceil$$
where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the ceiling function (round up to next integer).

### Derivation

The total force resisted by the broach shank and the machine puller is the **sum of forces on all simultaneously cutting teeth**:
$$F_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n_c} F_{t,i}$$

In the roughing zone, all $n_c$ teeth have nearly identical lift $h_r$, so:
$$F_{total} = n_c \cdot F_t = n_c \cdot k_s \cdot w \cdot h_r$$

Substituting $n_c = w/p$:
$$F_{total} = k_s \cdot w \cdot h_r \cdot \dfrac{w}{p} = \dfrac{k_s \cdot w^2 \cdot h_r}{p}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Quadratic Effect:** Notice the force is proportional to **$w^2$**. Doubling the face width **quadruples** the force. This is why broaching wide gears requires enormous machines (pull forces of 50–200 tonnes).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Broaching Time and Cycle Time

### Given
- Total broach length engaged in cutting: $L_{cut}$ (m)
- Cutting velocity: $v_{cut}$ (m/min)
- Return velocity: $v_{ret}$ (m/min), typically 2–3 × $v_{cut}$
- Loading time: $t_{load}$ ≈ 5 s
- Unloading time: $t_{unload}$ ≈ 5 s

### Derivation

The **cutting time** (forward stroke engaged in metal removal):
$$t_{cut} = \dfrac{L_{cut}}{v_{cut}} \text{ (min)} = \dfrac{60 \cdot L_{cut}}{v_{cut}} \text{ (s)}$$

The **return time** (idle, no cutting):
$$t_{ret} = \dfrac{L_{cut}}{v_{ret}} \text{ (min)} = \dfrac{60 \cdot L_{cut}}{v_{ret}} \text{ (s)}$$

The **total cycle time**:
$$t_{cycle} = t_{cut} + t_{ret} + t_{load} + t_{unload}$$

Substituting and converting to seconds:
$$t_{cycle} = \dfrac{60 \cdot L_{cut}}{v_{cut}} + \dfrac{60 \cdot L_{cut}}{v_{ret}} + t_{load} + t_{unload}$$

For typical values: $L_{cut} = 1.5$ m, $v_{cut} = 6$ m/min, $v_{ret} = 18$ m/min, $t_{load} = t_{unload} = 5$ s:

$$t_{cut} = \dfrac{60 \times 1.5}{6} = 15 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{ret} = \dfrac{60 \times 1.5}{18} = 5 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{cycle} = 15 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 30 \text{ s}$$

> [!TIP]
> A 30-second cycle time gives **120 gears/hour** per machine — illustrating the **mass-production capability** of broaching.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Required by the Broaching Machine

### Given
- Total cutting force: $F_{total}$ (N)
- Cutting velocity: $v_{cut}$ (m/min)

### Derivation

Mechanical power is force × velocity (with unit conversion):
$$P = \dfrac{F_{total} \cdot v_{cut}}{60{,}000} \text{ (kW)}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 14-mark style):**

**Problem:** A broach with 25 roughing teeth is used to cut a gear of face width **w = 50 mm**. The lift per roughing tooth is **h_r = 0.1 mm**, and the broach pitch is **p = 8 mm**. The specific cutting resistance for the workpiece material is **k_s = 2000 N/mm²**. Cutting velocity is **8 m/min**. Determine:
(a) Force on the broach
(b) Power required

**Solution:**

Number of teeth cutting simultaneously:
$$n_c = \dfrac{w}{p} = \dfrac{50}{8} = 6.25 \Rightarrow \lceil 6.25 \rceil = 7 \text{ teeth}$$

Force per tooth:
$$F_t = k_s \cdot w \cdot h_r = 2000 \times 50 \times 0.1 = 10{,}000 \text{ N}$$

Total force:
$$F_{total} = n_c \cdot F_t = 7 \times 10{,}000 = 70{,}000 \text{ N} = 70 \text{ kN}$$

Power:
$$P = \dfrac{F_{total} \cdot v_{cut}}{60{,}000} = \dfrac{70{,}000 \times 8}{60{,}000} = 9.33 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> With a mechanical efficiency of η = 0.85, the **motor rating** required is:
> $$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P}{\eta} = \dfrac{9.33}{0.85} \approx 11 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Length of a Broach

### Given
- Number of roughing teeth: $n_r$
- Number of semi-finishing teeth: $n_{sf}$
- Number of finishing teeth: $n_f$
- Number of sizing teeth: $n_{sz}$
- Respective pitches: $p_r, p_{sf}, p_f, p_{sz}$

### Derivation

Each section adds length to the broach. Summing the lengths:
$$L_{total} = n_r \cdot p_r + n_{sf} \cdot p_{sf} + n_f \cdot p_f + n_{sz} \cdot p_{sz} + L_{pilot} + L_{shank}$$

Typical roughing pitch: 8–12 mm. Finishing pitch: 4–6 mm. This is why roughing teeth occupy **60–70%** of the total broach length.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Stock Distribution Among Teeth

### Given
- Total radial stock to be removed: $H$ (mm)
- Number of roughing teeth: $n_r$
- Lift per roughing tooth: $h_r$

### Derivation

Roughing teeth must remove the **majority** of the stock (typically 75–85%):
$$H_{rough} = (0.75 \text{ to } 0.85) \times H$$

Thus:
$$h_r = \dfrac{H_{rough}}{n_r}$$

Similarly, semi-finishing and finishing lifts are progressively smaller (e.g., $h_{sf} \approx 0.5 h_r$, $h_f \approx 0.2 h_r$).

> [!NOTE]
> This tapering of lift per tooth is what gives broaching its **smooth entry** characteristic and prevents sudden tool load shocks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D --> E[Semi-Finishing Teeth Engage]
    E --> F[Finishing Teeth Profile Involute]
    F --> G[Sizing Teeth Correct Spring-back]
    G --> H[Broach Fully Exits Workpiece]
    H --> I[Workpiece Unloaded as Finished Gear]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, PCPET403]**

**Define gear broaching. List any four advantages of gear broaching over gear hobbing.**

**Model Answer (Keywords for Full Marks):**

**Definition:** Gear broaching is a multi-point cutting process in which a multi-tooth tool called a *gear broach* is pulled or pushed through/over a gear blank in a single stroke to produce all the gear teeth simultaneously by progressive cutting increments.

**Four Advantages:**
1. Higher productivity (cycle time 10–30 s vs. 2–5 min for hobbing).
2. Better surface finish (Ra 0.4–1.6 µm achievable directly).
3. Higher dimensional accuracy (IT6–IT7 grade).
4. Can produce internal gears of small bores that hobbing cannot reach.
5. Lower operator skill requirement (process is machine-controlled).

*(Any four well-explained points fetch 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, PCPET403]**

**What is the function of (i) pilot section, (ii) roughing teeth, (iii) sizing teeth, and (iv) pulling shank in a gear broach?**

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Pilot Section:** Aligns the broach coaxially with the pre-machined pilot hole of the workpiece to ensure concentricity and prevent run-out.
- **(ii) Roughing Teeth:** Remove the bulk of the material (≈ 75–85% of total stock) with large lift per tooth and coarse pitch.
- **(iii) Sizing Teeth:** The last 1–2 teeth, slightly oversized by 0.01–0.03 mm, compensate for elastic spring-back of the workpiece on tool exit.
