- **(iv) Pulling Shank:** The rear end of the broach that engages with the hydraulic puller of the broaching machine for force transmission.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, PCPET403]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the constructional features of an internal gear broach. Describe the function of each section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A broach has 30 roughing teeth used to cut a gear of face width **45 mm**. The lift per roughing tooth is **0.12 mm**, and the broach pitch is **9 mm**. The specific cutting resistance is **k_s = 2200 N/mm²**. If the cutting velocity is **6 m/min**, determine:
  (i) The number of teeth cutting simultaneously.
  (ii) The total cutting force on the broach.
  (iii) The power required by the broaching machine, assuming mechanical efficiency η = 0.80. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a)

A gear broach consists of the following sections from front to back:

1. **Pilot Section (Leading Guide):** A short, non-cutting cylindrical extension that enters the pre-drilled pilot hole of the blank. It establishes **concentricity** and prevents the broach from drifting.

2. **Roughing Section:** Contains ≈ 60–70% of the total teeth. Each tooth is lifted radially by a constant increment **h_r** (0.05–0.15 mm). These teeth perform bulk material removal. Tooth pitch is large (8–12 mm) to ensure adequate chip clearance and tool strength.

3. **Semi-Finishing Section:** The pitch is reduced (5–7 mm) and lift per tooth decreases (≈ 0.5 h_r). The involute profile begins to emerge; the cross-section of the chip becomes crescent-shaped rather than rectangular.

4. **Finishing Section:** Contains 3–7 identical teeth ground to the **exact involute profile** of the gear. The lift is essentially zero (same diameter) to give an exact size and superior surface finish.

5. **Sizing Section:** The last 1–2 teeth are intentionally **oversized by 0.01–0.03 mm** to account for **elastic spring-back** of the workpiece when the broach exits.

6. **Pulling Shank:** The trailing end that engages with the broach puller of the machine to transmit the high axial pull force.

> Each section contributes 1 mark in the answer; 1 mark for the sketch.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Given:**
- $n_r = 30$ (roughing teeth)
- $w = 45$ mm
- $h_r = 0.12$ mm
- $p = 9$ mm
- $k_s = 2200$ N/mm²
- $v_{cut} = 6$ m/min
- $\eta = 0.80$

**(i) Number of teeth cutting simultaneously:**

$$n_c = \left\lceil \dfrac{w}{p} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{45}{9} \right\rceil = \lceil 5 \rceil = 5 \text{ teeth}$$

**(ii) Total cutting force:**

Force per tooth:
$$F_t = k_s \cdot w \cdot h_r = 2200 \times 45 \times 0.12$$

$$F_t = 2200 \times 5.4 = 11{,}880 \text{ N}$$

Total force:
$$F_{total} = n_c \cdot F_t = 5 \times 11{,}880$$

$$F_{total} = 59{,}400 \text{ N} = 59.4 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) Power required:**

$$P = \dfrac{F_{total} \cdot v_{cut}}{60{,}000} = \dfrac{59{,}400 \times 6}{60{,}000}$$

$$P = \dfrac{356{,}400}{60{,}000} = 5.94 \text{ kW}$$

With mechanical efficiency $\eta = 0.80$:

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P}{\eta} = \dfrac{5.94}{0.80} = 7.425 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer:** $n_c = 5$, $F_{total} = 59.4$ kN, $P_{motor} \approx 7.43$ kW.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, PCPET403]**

**(a)** Compare gear broaching with gear hobbing on the basis of: (i) process principle, (ii) tool geometry, (iii) productivity, (iv) surface finish, (v) suitable workpiece type, and (vi) cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a broaching operation on a steel gear, the following data is recorded:
- Broach has **20 roughing teeth** with **lift h_r = 0.08 mm** per tooth.
- Face width **w = 40 mm**, pitch **p = 10 mm**.
- Specific cutting resistance **k_s = 2000 N/mm²**.
- Broach stroke length **L = 1200 mm**.
- Cutting velocity **v_cut = 8 m/min**, return velocity **v_ret = 24 m/min**.
- Loading and unloading time: **6 s each**.

Calculate:
  (i) Number of teeth cutting simultaneously.
  (ii) Total cutting force.
  (iii) Cutting time and total cycle time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a)

| Parameter | Gear Broaching | Gear Hobbing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Process principle** | Form-cutting; all teeth cut simultaneously in a single linear/rotary stroke. | Generating; tooth flanks are progressively generated by a rotating helical cutter. |
| **(ii) Tool geometry** | Long, multi-tooth bar with progressively lifted teeth. | Short, worm-shaped (helical) cutter with gashed flutes. |
| **(iii) Productivity** | Very high (10–30 s/cycle). | Moderate to high (2–5 min/cycle). |
| **(iv) Surface finish** | Excellent (Ra 0.4–1.6 µm). | Good (Ra 1.6–3.2 µm); may need shaving/grinding. |
| **(v) Suitable workpiece type** | Internal gears and external gears; especially small-bore internal gears. | External gears predominantly; large-bore internal gears only. |
| **(vi) Cost** | High tooling cost; economical only for mass production. | Moderate tooling cost; flexible for batch production. |

*(1 mark per row × 6 rows = 6 marks; 1 mark for presentation/conclusion.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Given:**
- $n_r = 20$, $h_r = 0.08$ mm, $w = 40$ mm, $p = 10$ mm
- $k_s = 2000$ N/mm²
- $L_{cut} = 1200$ mm = 1.2 m
- $v_{cut} = 8$ m/min, $v_{ret} = 24$ m/min
- $t_{load} = t_{unload} = 6$ s

**(i) Number of teeth cutting simultaneously:**

$$n_c = \left\lceil \dfrac{w}{p} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{40}{10} \right\rceil = 4 \text{ teeth}$$

**(ii) Total cutting force:**

Force per tooth:
$$F_t = k_s \cdot w \cdot h_r = 2000 \times 40 \times 0.08 = 6400 \text{ N}$$

Total force:
$$F_{total} = n_c \cdot F_t = 4 \times 6400 = 25{,}600 \text{ N} = 25.6 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) Cutting time and total cycle time:**

Cutting time:
$$t_{cut} = \dfrac{60 \times L_{cut}}{v_{cut}} = \dfrac{60 \times 1.2}{8} = 9 \text{ s}$$

Return time:
$$t_{ret} = \dfrac{60 \times L_{cut}}{v_{ret}} = \dfrac{60 \times 1.2}{24} = 3 \text{ s}$$

Total cycle time:
$$t_{cycle} = t_{cut} + t_{ret} + t_{load} + t_{unload} = 9 + 3 + 6 + 6 = 24 \text{ s}$$

**Final Answer:** $n_c = 4$, $F_{total} = 25.6$ kN, $t_{cut} = 9$ s, $t_{cycle} = 24$ s.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **1. Forgetting the Ceiling Function (–1 to –2 marks):** When the face width $w$ is **not an exact multiple** of the pitch $p$ (e.g., $w = 45$ mm, $p = 8$ mm → $n_c = 5.625$), you **must round UP** to the next integer, i.e., 6 teeth, NOT 5. A 5.625 result means 5 full teeth and a partial 6th — the partial tooth still cuts and contributes force. Using 5 instead of 6 underestimates the force by ~17%.
> 
> **2. Unit Mismatch (–1 mark):** $v_{cut}$ is in m/min. To get power in **kW**, you **must** divide by 60,000 (not 60, not 600). $P = Fv / 60{,}000$ when $F$ is in N and $v$ is in m/min.
> 
> **3. Confusing Pull Force with Cutting Force (–2 marks):** The machine's puller exerts a force equal to $F_{total}$, but this is **NOT** the force on the workpiece. The question always asks for the broach force, which equals the sum of all per-tooth forces.
> 
> **4. Skipping the Sketch (–1 to –2 marks):** In 7-mark theory questions, **a labelled diagram is mandatory**. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if no sketch is provided, even if the text description is perfect.
> 
> **5. Omitting Sizing Teeth (–1 mark):** Many students list only 4 sections of a broach and forget the **sizing teeth**. Always mention all 5 cutting sections plus the pilot and shank.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 1 hour before the exam!)**

- ☐ Gear broaching = **multi-point, single-pass, form-cutting** process using a multi-tooth progressive tool.
- ☐ Broach sections in order: **Pilot → Roughing → Semi-Finishing → Finishing → Sizing → Pulling Shank**.
- ☐ Roughing teeth have **largest lift** (0.05–0.15 mm) and **largest pitch** (8–12 mm); they remove **75–85%** of the stock.
- ☐ Finishing teeth have **same diameter** (zero lift) and are **involute-ground** to the final gear profile.
- ☐ Sizing teeth are **oversized by 0.01–0.03 mm** to compensate for **elastic spring-back**.
- ☐ Master equation: $F_t = k_s \cdot w \cdot h$ → $F_{total} = n_c \cdot F_t$ → $P = F_{total} \cdot v / 60{,}000$ (kW).
- ☐ Always use the **ceiling function** for $n_c = \lceil w/p \rceil$, never round down.
- ☐ Specific cutting resistance $k_s$: **1500–2500 N/mm²** for steel; **900–1200 N/mm²** for cast iron; **400–700 N/mm²** for aluminum.
- ☐ Cutting velocity $v_{cut}$: typically **3–12 m/min** for steel gears (lower for hard alloys).
- ☐ Total cycle time = cutting time + return time + load + unload. The **return stroke is idle** and non-productive.
- ☐ Gear broaching is **uneconomical for low volumes** due to **high broach cost** (₹50K–₹5L+).
- ☐ **Key advantage** over hobbing: ability to finish **small-bore internal gears** with **IT6–IT7 accuracy** in a single pass.
- ☐ **Key disadvantage:** limited to **through-hole internal workpieces**; the workpiece must be open at both ends.
- ☐ **Forces scale as $w^2$** — doubling the face width **quadruples** the pull force.
- ☐ Broach length = sum of (number of teeth × pitch) for each section + pilot + shank.
- ☐ Power-unit trap: $P$(kW) = $F$(N) × $v$(m/min) ÷ **60,000** — memorize this number.
- ☐ Rotary (continuous) broaching uses $v = \pi D_w N / 60$ for cutting velocity; linear broaching uses $v = L_{cut} / t_{cut}$.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Gear Broaching**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Shaving. Abrasive Processes: Grinding Wheel, Designation and Selection, Types of Grinding Operations, Honing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Gear Shaving. Abrasive Processes: Grinding Wheel, Designation and Selection, Types of Grinding Operations, Honing ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Gear Shaving > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Gear shaving is a free-cutting, chip-removal, surface-finishing process used to improve the accuracy and surface finish of hardened or unhardened spur and helical gears by traversing a precision-shaped shaving cutter across the gear tooth flanks at a crossed-axis arrangement.* **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine you have just printed a photograph on rough paper, and you run a smooth stone over it gently to remove the rough edges and make the surface perfect — that is exactly what gear shaving does to gear teeth. The shaving cutter is like a "precision rasp" that has serrations (cutting edges) on its tooth flanks, and as it meshes with the workpiece gear at a slight crossed angle, these serrations shave off microscopic slivers of metal, correcting tooth profile errors, lead errors, and pitch errors left behind by hobbing or shaping. **Key Characteristics of Gear Shaving:** - **Crossed-axis arrangement** (typically 3°–15°) between cutter and workpiece - Performs **only finishing** — not a primary stock-removal process - Achieves **AGMA Quality 6 to 8** from a Quality 8–10 pre-shaved gear - Typical **stock removal:** **0.05 mm to 0.30 mm** per flank - **Surface finish achieved:** **0.4 µm to 0.8 µm Ra** --- ### 1.2 Abrasive Processes — The General Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *An abrasive process is a material-removal operation in which the cutting action is performed by hundreds/thousands of sharp, hard, geometrically uncontrolled abrasive particles that remove tiny chips of material through ploughing, cutting, and rubbing actions.* **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of abrasive processes as "swarms of microscopic chisels." A grinding wheel contains millions of tiny cutting points (alumina or silicon carbide grains) bonded together. When the wheel touches the workpiece, each grain acts like a miniature single-point tool, but because there are so many, the result is a very fine, controlled cut. **Three Core Components of any Abrasive System:** 1. **Abrasive Grains** — the actual cutting tools (e.g., Aluminum Oxide, Silicon Carbide, CBN, Diamond) 2. **Bond** — holds grains together (e.g., vitrified, resinoid, metal, rubber) 3. **Workpiece Material** — the metal being shaped --- ### 1.3 Grinding Wheel > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *A grinding wheel is a multi-tooth, self-sharpening cutting tool composed of abrasive grains held together by a bond, with interspersed pores for chip storage and coolant flow.* **Key Parameters (the four pillars):** - **Abrasive type** - **Grain size** - **Grade (hardness)** - **Structure** --- ### 1.4 Honing > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Honing is a low-speed, low-pressure abrasive process used to improve the dimensional accuracy, geometric form, and surface finish of internal cylindrical surfaces (primarily bores) using a set of bonded abrasive sticks that expand radially while rotating and reciprocating simultaneously.* **Conceptual Analogy:** Picture a barbershop where a vibrating, rotating emery-board block is gently scrubbed inside a cylinder — that is honing. The combination of rotation (circular motion) + reciprocation (up-and-down motion) creates a **cross-hatch pattern** (typically 30°–45° angle) on the bore wall, which is the visual fingerprint of a properly honed surface. **Key Features:** - Operates at **low speeds** (typically 30–150 m/min peripheral) - Uses **low pressures** (0.2–1.5 MPa) - Achieves **surface finish of 0.05 µm to 0.4 µm Ra** - Corrects **out-of-roundness, taper, and bore-barrel** errors - Typical **stock removal:** **0.005 mm to 0.05 mm** radius ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Gear Shaving — Operational Principles **Why crossed-axis?** When the cutter axis and workpiece axis are crossed at a small angle (typically 5°–10°), the relative sliding velocity along the tooth flank creates a shearing action. The serrated cutting edges of the shaving cutter then peel off micro-chips. **Two Main Types of Gear Shaving:** | Type | Relative Motion | Application | |------|----------------|-------------| | **Rotary Shaving** | Workpiece rotates continuously, cutter rotates in mesh | High-volume spur & helical gears | | **Reciprocating Shaving** | Workpiece reciprocates axially under the cutter | Wider face widths, large modules | | **Underpass Shaving** | Cutter passes under the gear (special configuration) | Cluster gears, shaft-mounted gears | **Process Parameters:** - **Crossed angle (Σ):** 3°–15° (optimum ≈ 10°) - **Radial infeed:** 0.025–0.20 mm - **Number of passes:** 1–6 - **Cutting velocity:** 60–180 m/min - **Workpiece speed:** 100–400 rpm - **Number of teeth in mesh:** Always > 2 (for stability) --- ### 2.2 Grinding Wheel — Designation & Selection (HIGH-YIELD) **The Standard Marking System (per IS 1521 / FEPA / ISO 525):** A grinding wheel is specified by a **6-position standard marking**: | Position | Symbol | Meaning | |----------|--------|---------| | **1** | A / 32A / 54C / etc. | **Abrasive type** & manufacturer prefix | | **2** | 36 / 60 / 100 | **Grain size** (mesh number) | | **3** | H / J / K | **Grade** (soft → hard) | | **4** | 5 / 6 / 8 | **Structure** (open → dense) | | **5** | V / R / B / M | **Bond type** | | **6** | m/s or rpm | **Maximum operating speed** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Example:** *51 A 36 L 5 V 23* → Aluminium Oxide (A), Grain size 36 (medium), Grade L (medium-hard), Structure 5 (medium), Vitrified bond (V), 23 m/s max speed. **Detailed Breakdown:** **A. Abrasive Type (Position 1):** - **Aluminium Oxide (A / Al₂O₃):** for **ferrous metals** (steel, cast iron) - **Silicon Carbide (C / SiC):** for **non-ferrous, hard, brittle** (carbide, ceramics, aluminium) - **Cubic Boron Nitride (B / CBN):** for **hardened steels (>50 HRC)** - **Diamond (D):** for **cemented carbides, glass, stone** **B. Grain Size (Position 2):** - Coarse: 8–24 (rough grinding, high MRR) - Medium: 30–60 (general purpose) - Fine: 80–220 (finishing, form grinding) - Very fine: 240+ (lapping, polishing) **C. Grade / Hardness (Position 3):** Soft: A, B, C, D, E, F, G Medium: H, I, J, K Hard: L, M, N, O, P, Q, R, S, T Very Hard: U, V, W, X, Y, Z > [!NOTE] > **Selection rule:** Hard workpiece → soft wheel (so worn abrasive grains are released); soft workpiece → hard wheel (to resist dull grain embedment). **D. Structure (Position 4):** - Indicates **spacing of grains**: dense (1) → open (15) - **Open structure:** high chip space, cool cutting, rough finish - **Dense structure:** fine finish, form retention, low chip clearance **E. Bond (Position 5):** - **V – Vitrified (most common, 70% market):** rigid, porous, heat-resistant - **B – Resinoid (phenolic):** tough, used for rough grinding, cut-off wheels - **R – Rubber:** thin wheels, snag grinding - **E – Shellac:** fine finish, bearing races - **M – Metal:** superabrasive (CBN, Diamond) bonding **F. Operating Speed (Position 6):** - Common values: 23, 33, 35, 45, 60, 80 m/s --- ### 2.3 Types of Grinding Operations **1. Surface Grinding** - Wheel rotates + table reciprocates (or rotates for rotary surface grinder) - Produces flat surfaces - Three types: **reciprocating-table, rotary-table, vertical-spindle** **2. Cylindrical Grinding** - External cylindrical surfaces - **Between-centers** and **centerless** configurations - Used for shafts, pins, rollers **3. Internal (Cylindrical) Grinding** - Inside bores using small wheels - Wheel speed limited by bore diameter **4. Centerless Grinding** - No workholding centers; uses regulating wheel + work rest blade - **Through-feed, In-feed (plunge), End-feed** **5. Tool and Cutter Grinding** - Sharpens milling cutters, drills, reamers - Universal tool and cutter grinder **6. Form Grinding** - Wheel shaped to inverse profile of desired workpiece - Profile grinding of gears, threads, dies **7. Thread Grinding** - Produces precision threads - Single-ribbed or multi-ribbed wheels **8. Creep-Feed Grinding** - Very low table feed (1–10 mm/min), high depth of cut - High MRR, used for creep-feed blades, slot grinding --- ### 2.4 Honing — Process Mechanics **Why Honing is Unique:** The **combination of two motions** (rotation + reciprocation) is what distinguishes honing from boring or internal grinding. | Motion | Typical Magnitude | Purpose | |--------|------------------|---------| | **Rotation** | 30–150 m/min | Circular path | | **Reciprocation** | 5–30 m/min | Generates cross-hatch pattern | | **Expansion (feed)** | 0.005–0.1 mm/radial | Stone pressure & stock removal | **Cross-hatch Angle (α):** The angle of the cross-hatch is governed by the ratio of reciprocating to rotational velocity: $$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{v_{\text{rec}}}{v_{\text{rot}}}\right)$$ **Typical α = 30°–45°** — this angle is the diagnostic fingerprint of the honed surface. **Types of Honing:** - **Horizontal honing** (most common, for cylinder bores) - **Vertical honing** (for large bores, hydraulic cylinders) - **Single-stroke honing** (one pass, used for thin parts) - **Multi-spindle honing** (mass production) --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Z_w = \dfrac{v_w \cdot a \cdot b}{60}$ | MRR in surface grinding (mm³/s) | mm³/s | | 2 | $G = \dfrac{V_w}{V_s}$ | **Grinding Ratio** (volume of metal removed / wheel wear) | – | | 3 | $G' = \dfrac{v_w \cdot a}{v_s \cdot \Delta r}$ | Specific grinding ratio | mm²/mm³ | | 4 | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v_s}{60}$ | Grinding power (W) | W | | 5 | $u = \dfrac{P}{Z_w}$ | **Specific energy** (J/mm³) | J/mm³ | | 6 | $S_c = \dfrac{l_t}{f_t} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{D}}$ | Surface roughness in cylindrical grinding (µm) | µm | | 7 | $MRR = \pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot f_r$ | Cylindrical grinding MRR | mm³/s | | 8 | $v_s = \pi D n$ | Wheel surface speed (m/s) | m/s | | 9 | $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{N \cdot n_w \cdot \cos\beta}$ | Shaving cycle time | s | | 10 | $H_R = \dfrac{\Delta r \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L}{1000}$ | Honing material removal rate (mm³/s) | mm³/s | | 11 | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{v_{\text{rec}}}{v_{\text{rot}}}\right)$ | Cross-hatch angle in honing | degrees | | 12 | $F_n = P_h \cdot A_h$ | Honing normal force (N) | N | | 13 | $S_p = \dfrac{C \cdot v_s^{0.5}}{n_w^{0.5} \cdot f_a^{0.25}}$ | Peak-to-valley roughness in grinding | µm | > [!NOTE] > **Where:** $v_w$ = work speed, $a$ = depth of cut, $b$ = width, $V_w$ = work material removed, $V_s$ = wheel wear volume, $F_c$ = tangential cutting force, $D$ = wheel diameter, $d$ = workpiece diameter, $l_t$ = nose radius, $f_t$ = feed/rev, $P_h$ = honing pressure, $A_h$ = honing stick contact area, $\beta$ = helix angle, $C$ = constant. --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive industry:** Gear shaving produces transmission gears with low NVH (noise-vibration-harshness); honing produces hydraulic cylinder bores with perfect sealing surface. - **Aerospace:** CBN grinding of turbine blade roots; honing of landing-gear bores. - **Hydraulics:** Honing of piston-cylinder assemblies for leak-proof operation. - **Power transmission:** Ground gears in EV reducers require shaving to achieve **AGMA Q6** for quiet operation. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Material Removal Rate in Surface Grinding **Given:** - Workpiece speed $v_w$ in mm/min - Depth of cut $a$ in mm - Width of grinding $b$ in mm (equal to wheel width or less) **Step 1: Volume swept per unit length** As the worktable moves by one unit length ($dx$), the volume removed is: $$dV = a \cdot b \cdot dx$$ **Step 2: Express in terms of work velocity** Since $v_w = dx/dt$, we have $dx = v_w \cdot dt$, hence: $$dV = a \cdot b \cdot v_w \cdot dt$$ **Step 3: Differentiate with respect to time** $$Z_w = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = a \cdot b \cdot v_w$$ **Step 4: Convert to mm³/s (since $v_w$ is in mm/min)** $$Z_w = \dfrac{a \cdot b \cdot v_w}{60} \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]$$ > This is the standard **MRR for surface grinding** under plain down-grinding or up-grinding. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Grinding Ratio ($G$) **Definition:** $$G = \dfrac{\text{Volume of metal removed from workpiece}}{\text{Volume of wheel wear}}$$ **Step 1: Workpiece volume removed** $$V_w = a \cdot b \cdot L_w$$ where $L_w$ is the total length traversed on the workpiece. **Step 2: Wheel wear volume** For a straight wheel of width $b$, the radial wear $\Delta r$ translates to volume: $$V_s = \pi \cdot b \cdot D \cdot \Delta r$$ (geometrically, a thin ring of thickness $\Delta r$ on the wheel periphery) **Step 3: Form the ratio** $$G = \dfrac{a \cdot b \cdot L_w}{\pi \cdot b \cdot D \cdot \Delta r} = \dfrac{a \cdot L_w}{\pi \cdot D \cdot \Delta r}$$ **Higher G = better wheel life (more material removed per unit wheel wear).** --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Specific Energy in Grinding **Step 1: Power required for grinding** $$P = F_c \cdot v_s \quad [\text{W}]$$ where $F_c$ is the tangential force in N, $v_s$ in m/s. **Step 2: Specific energy definition** $$u = \dfrac{\text{Energy per unit time}}{\text{Material removed per unit time}} = \dfrac{P}{Z_w}$$ **Step 3: Substitute** $$u = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v_s \cdot 60}{a \cdot b \cdot v_w} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]$$ > **Typical values of $u$ in grinding:** 10–100 J/mm³ (much higher than turning's 2–5 J/mm³ due to ploughing + rubbing). --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Cross-Hatch Angle in Honing **Given:** - $v_{\text{rec}}$ = reciprocating velocity (m/min) - $v_{\text{rot}}$ = rotational velocity (m/min) **Step 1: Geometric interpretation** In one complete rotation, the stone travels a circumferential distance $2\pi r$. The reciprocating motion moves the stone linearly by $2L$ (up + down) in that same time. The trace on the bore wall forms a helix-like path with helix angle $\alpha$. **Step 2: Relate angles** $$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{\text{axial displacement per rev}}{\text{circumferential distance per rev}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{rec}}}{v_{\text{rot}}}$$ **Step 3: Solve for cross-hatch angle** $$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{v_{\text{rec}}}{v_{\text{rot}}}\right)$$ **Step 4: Numerical example** If $v_{\text{rot}} = 100$ m/min and $v_{\text{rec}} = 60$ m/min: $$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.6) \approx 30.96°$$ > **Practical significance:** Cross-hatch angle is critical for **oil retention** in cylinder bores. Too small (<20°) → poor lubrication; too large (>60°) → poor sealing, oil leakage. --- ### 3.5 Derivation: Honing Material Removal Rate **Given:** - Bore diameter $d$ (mm) - Hone length $L$ (mm) - Radial expansion per unit time $\Delta r$ (mm/min) **Step 1: Volume removed per unit time** The wall is being reduced radially by $\Delta r$, so the material removed is essentially a thin cylindrical shell of thickness $\Delta r$ and length $L$: $$V_{\text{hone}} = \pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot \Delta r$$ **Step 2: Convert to mm³/s** $$V_{\text{hone}} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot \Delta r}{60} \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]$$ --- ### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Exam Style **Problem:** A surface grinding operation is performed on a mild steel workpiece using a wheel of diameter 250 mm rotating at 1500 rpm. Workpiece speed is 12 m/min, depth of cut is 0.02 mm, and wheel width is 50 mm. Find: (a) Wheel surface speed (b) Material removal rate (c) Specific energy if tangential force is 80 N (d) If radial wheel wear is 0.005 mm after grinding 1000 mm of workpiece length, find the grinding ratio. **Solution:** **(a) Wheel surface speed:** $$v_s = \pi D n = \pi \times 0.25 \times \dfrac{1500}{60} = 19.63 \text{ m/s}$$ **(b) MRR:** $$Z_w = \dfrac{a \cdot b \cdot v_w}{60} = \dfrac{0.02 \times 50 \times 12000}{60} = 200 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$ **(c) Specific energy:** $$P = F_c \cdot v_s = 80 \times 19.63 = 1571 \text{ W}$$ $$u = \dfrac{P}{Z_w} = \dfrac{1571}{200} = 7.85 \text{ J/mm}^3$$ **(d) Grinding ratio:** $$G = \dfrac{a \cdot L_w}{\pi \cdot D \cdot \Delta r} = \dfrac{0.02 \times 1000}{\pi \times 250 \times 0.005} = 5.09$$ > This grinding ratio is **low**, indicating aggressive wheel wear — selection of harder grade recommended. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Gear Shaving — Block Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD WPG["WORKPIECE GEAR"]:::wp SCRT["SHAVING CUTTER
SERRATED FLANKS"]:::cut ANG["CROSSED AXIS ANGLE 3 to 15 DEG"]:::angle RLY["RELATIVE SLIDING
ALONG TOOTH FLANKS"]:::slide RIN["RADIAL INFEED 0.025 TO 0.2 MM"]:::feed RTR["ROTARY OR RECIPROCATING
RELATIVE MOTION"]:::rot CHP["MICRO CHIPS
0.05 TO 0.3 MM FLANK"]:::chip FIN["FINISHED GEAR
AGMA Q6 TO Q8
RA 0.4 TO 0.8 UM"]:::fin CLG["COOLANT NOZZLE"]:::cool WPG <--> SCRT SCRT --- ANG ANG --> RLY RLY --> CHP RIN --> RLY RTR --> RTR RTR --> SCRT WPG --> FIN CLG --> WPG classDef wp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef cut fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef angle fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef slide fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef feed fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef rot fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef chip fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef fin fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef cool fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` --- ### 4.2 Grinding Wheel — Standard Marking (6-Position Designation) ```mermaid graph LR P1["POS 1
ABRASIVE TYPE
A ALUMINA OXIDE
C SILICON CARBIDE
B CBN
D DIAMOND"]:::p1 P2["POS 2
GRAIN SIZE
8 TO 24 COARSE
30 TO 60 MEDIUM
80 TO 220 FINE"]:::p2 P3["POS 3
GRADE HARDNESS
A TO G SOFT
H TO K MEDIUM
L TO Z HARD"]:::p3 P4["POS 4
STRUCTURE
1 DENSE
5 MEDIUM
10 TO 15 OPEN"]:::p4 P5["POS 5
BOND TYPE
V VITRIFIED
B RESINOID
R RUBBER
M METAL"]:::p5 P6["POS 6
MAX SPEED
23 / 33 / 45 / 60 / 80 M PER S"]:::p6 EX["EXAMPLE
51 A 36 L 5 V 23"]:::ex P1 --> EX P2 --> EX P3 --> EX P4 --> EX P5 --> EX P6 --> EX classDef p1 fill:#FF7F50,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef p2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef p3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef p4 fill:#9ACD32,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef p5 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef p6 fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef ex fill:#FF1493,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` --- ### 4.3 Types of Grinding Operations — Hierarchical Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD GR["GRINDING OPERATIONS"]:::root SG["SURFACE GRINDING"]:::sg CG["CYLINDRICAL GRINDING"]:::cg IG["INTERNAL GRINDING"]:::ig CLG["CENTERLESS GRINDING"]:::clg TCG["TOOL AND CUTTER GRINDING"]:::tcg FG["FORM GRINDING"]:::fg THG["THREAD GRINDING"]:::thg CFG["CREEP FEED GRINDING"]:::cfg GR --> SG GR --> CG GR --> IG GR --> CLG GR --> TCG GR --> FG GR --> THG GR --> CFG SG --- SG1["RECIPROCATING TABLE"] SG --- SG2["ROTARY TABLE"] SG --- SG3["VERTICAL SPINDLE"] CG --- CG1["BETWEEN CENTERS"] CG --- CG2["CENTERLESS EXTERNAL"] CG --- CG3["UNIVERSAL"] CLG --- CLG1["THROUGH FEED"] CLG --- CLG2["IN FEED PLUNGE"] CLG --- CLG3["END FEED"] classDef root fill:#000000,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef sg fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef cg fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef ig fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef clg fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef tcg fill:#9ACD32,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef fg fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef thg fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef cfg fill:#FF1493,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` --- ### 4.4 Honing — Process Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD HTOOL["HONING TOOL
EXPANDABLE MANDREL"]:::tool STK["ABRASIVE STICKS
SIC OR ALUMINA OR CBN"]:::stk SP1["ROTATION 30 TO 150 M PER MIN"]:::sp SP2["RECIPROCATION 5 TO 30 M PER MIN"]:::sp SP3["RADIAL EXPANSION
0.005 TO 0.1 MM"]:::sp BR["CYLINDER BORE
WORKPIECE"]:::br SR["REMOVED LAYER
0.005 TO 0.05 MM"]:::sr CH["CROSS HATCH PATTERN
30 TO 45 DEG"]:::ch FN["FINISHED BORE
RA 0.05 TO 0.4 UM
ROUNDNESS LESS THAN 2 UM"]:::fn HTOOL --> STK STK --> SP1 STK --> SP2 STK --> SP3 SP1 --> BR SP2 --> BR SP3 --> BR BR --> SR SR --> CH CH --> FN classDef tool fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef stk fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef sp fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef br fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef sr fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef ch fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef fn fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain the standard marking system of a grinding wheel. How is the grade of a grinding wheel selected for a given application?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A grinding wheel is designated by a 6-position standard marking system (per IS 1521/ISO 525): **(1)** Abrasive type (A/C/B/D), **(2)** Grain size (8 to 220), **(3)** Grade (A–Z), **(4)** Structure (1–15), **(5)** Bond type (V/B/R/E/M), **(6)** Maximum operating speed (m/s). **Selection of grade (1 mark):** Hard workpiece material (e.g., hardened steel, carbide) → soft-grade wheel (A to H), so that dull grains are released easily; soft workpiece (e.g., aluminium, brass) → hard-grade wheel (M to Z), to resist grain pull-out and embedment. **Example:** *51 A 36 L 5 V 23* designates aluminium oxide, grain 36, grade L (medium-hard), structure 5, vitrified bond, 23 m/s. --- #### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define honing. With a neat sketch, explain the principle of honing and the formation of the cross-hatch pattern.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Honing (1 mark):** It is a low-speed, low-pressure abrasive process for improving dimensional accuracy, geometry, and surface finish of internal cylindrical surfaces using bonded abrasive sticks that rotate and reciprocate simultaneously inside the bore. **Principle (1 mark):** The honing tool rotates while reciprocating axially. Abrasive stones contact the bore wall under light pressure (0.2–1.5 MPa), removing a few micrometres of stock. **Cross-hatch formation (1 mark):** The combination of rotation and reciprocation produces a characteristic cross-hatch (lay) pattern on the bore wall, with cross-hatch angle $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(v_{\text{rec}}/v_{\text{rot}})$, typically 30°–45°. This pattern is essential for oil retention in cylinder bores. --- ### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **Answer any one of the following:** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **gear shaving process**. Discuss the **principle of crossed-axis shaving**, the **types of shaving cutters**, and the **process parameters** that affect the quality of the shaved gear. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain in detail the **types of grinding operations** with neat sketches. Compare **surface grinding, cylindrical grinding, and centerless grinding** based on their applications, accuracy, and production rate. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Solution (a) — Gear Shaving (7 marks):** **1. Introduction (1 mark):** Gear shaving is a free-cutting finishing process used to correct tooth profile, lead, and pitch errors of spur and helical gears after hobbing or shaping. Typical AGMA quality improvement: from Q10 to Q6–Q8. **2. Principle of Crossed-Axis Shaving (2 marks):** The shaving cutter and workpiece gear are mounted with their axes crossed at an angle **Σ = 3°–15°** (typically 5°–10°). This crossed-axis arrangement creates a **relative sliding velocity** between the cutter serrations and the workpiece tooth flank. The cutter has a serrated/grooved surface that acts as a multiplicity of miniature cutting edges. As the gears rotate in mesh, these serrations shave off microscopic slivers from the workpiece tooth flanks, correcting geometric inaccuracies. **3. Types of Shaving Cutters (2 marks):** - **Rotary Shaving Cutter:** Disc-shaped with serrated teeth; used in conventional rotary shaving machines. - **Rack-type Shaving Cutter:** Linear rack with serrated flanks; used for large-diameter gears. - **Internal Shaving Cutter:** Used for internal gear teeth (ring gears, planetary gears). - **Underpass Cutter:** Special configuration where the cutter passes *under* the gear, useful for shaft-mounted gears and clusters. **4. Process Parameters Affecting Quality (1 mark):** - **Crossed angle (Σ):** Higher angle → higher stock removal but poorer finish. - **Radial infeed:** 0.025–0.20 mm. - **Number of passes:** 1–6; more passes → better finish. - **Cutting velocity:** 60–180 m/min; too high → burning, too low → rubbing. - **Workpiece speed:** 100–400 rpm. - **Number of teeth in mesh:** Must be > 2 to avoid meshing interference. **5. Advantages (1 mark):** No heat-affected zone (cold cutting), high accuracy, fine surface finish (Ra 0.4–0.8 µm), low noise operation, suitable for both hardened and unhardened gears (though softer pre-hardened state is preferred). --- ##### **Solution (b) — Types of Grinding Operations (7 marks):** **1. Surface Grinding (2 marks):** - **Principle:** Abrasive wheel rotates; workpiece held on reciprocating table beneath the wheel. - **Types:** Horizontal-spindle reciprocating table (most common), vertical-spindle reciprocating table, rotary-table (continuous). - **Applications:** Flat surfaces, slots, dies, gauges. - **Accuracy:** ±0.005 mm; Surface finish: 0.4–0.8 µm Ra. **2. Cylindrical Grinding (2 marks):** - **Principle:** External cylindrical surfaces ground between centers (or in chuck) by a peripheral wheel. - **Types:** Plain cylindrical, universal (with swivel table), angular approach. - **Applications:** Shafts, pins, rollers, bearing journals. - **Accuracy:** ±0.0025 mm; Roundness: < 2 µm; Surface finish: 0.2–0.4 µm Ra. **3. Centerless Grinding (2 marks):** - **Principle:** No work-holding centers used. The workpiece rests on a **work-rest blade** between a **grinding wheel** and a **regulating wheel** (rubber-bonded, slower). The regulating wheel both rotates the workpiece (work-feed) and provides axial feed. - **Types:** Through-feed (long workpieces), In-feed/Plunge (tapered shoulders), End-feed (shouldered work). - **Applications:** Mass production of pins, bushings, rollers, ball-bearing races. - **Accuracy:** ±0.0025 mm; Highest production rate of all grinding types. **4. Comparative Table (1 mark):** | Feature | Surface | Cylindrical | Centerless | |---------|---------|-------------|-----------| | Surface type | Flat | Cylindrical (external) | Cylindrical (external) | | Work holding | Magnetic chuck / vice | Centers / chuck | Work-rest blade | | Production rate | Medium | Medium-low | Very high | | Accuracy | ±0.005 mm | ±0.0025 mm | ±0.0025 mm | | Best for | Dies, slots, flats | Shafts, journals | Pins, rollers, races | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative] **(a)** Explain the **construction and selection of a grinding wheel**. Describe the different **abrasives, bonds, and structures** used in grinding wheels, and the criteria for selection based on workpiece material, surface finish, and MRR requirements. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **honing process**. Derive the expression for **cross-hatch angle** and discuss the process parameters that affect the surface finish and stock removal in honing. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Solution (a) — Grinding Wheel Construction & Selection (7 marks):** **1. Construction (1 mark):** A grinding wheel consists of three primary components: **(i) Abrasive grains** (cutting agents), **(ii) Bond** (matrix holding grains), and **(iii) Pores** (space for chips and coolant). The abrasive performs the cutting, the bond provides rigidity, and the pores prevent loading (clogging) and provide heat dissipation. **2. Abrasives (2 marks):** - **Aluminium Oxide (A / Al₂O₃):** Tough, fracture-resistant. Used for **ferrous metals** with high tensile strength (steels, wrought iron). - **Silicon Carbide (C / SiC):** Harder but more brittle. Used for **non-ferrous, hard, brittle materials** (cast iron, cemented carbide, ceramics, stone). - **Cubic Boron Nitride (B / CBN):** Second hardest known material. Used for **hardened steels (>50 HRC)** and **superalloys**. - **Diamond (D):** Hardest known material. Used for **cemented carbide, glass, ceramics, stone**. *Not for steel* (carbon affinity causes rapid wear). **3. Bonds (2 marks):** - **Vitrified (V):** Most common (70% of market). Made from fired clay; rigid, porous, heat- and chemical-resistant. - **Resinoid (B):** Phenolic resin; tough, used for rough grinding, cut-off wheels, snagging. - **Rubber (R):** Elastic; for thin wheels, regulated wheels in centerless grinding. - **Shellac (E):** Brittle but gives fine finish; for bearing race grinding. - **Metal (M):** Used for superabrasive (CBN, Diamond) wheels; provides strong bond for high temperatures. **4. Selection Criteria (2 marks):** - **Workpiece material:** Ferrous → Al₂O₃; Hardened steel → CBN; Carbide → Diamond; Cast iron/ceramics → SiC. - **Surface finish required:** Fine finish → fine grain, dense structure, hard grade. - **MRR required:** High MRR → coarse grain, open structure, soft grade. - **Form retention:** Form grinding → dense structure, vitrified bond. - **Cooling efficiency:** Heavy stock removal → open structure, vitrified bond. - **Operation:** Cylindrical → vitrified; Cut-off → resinoid; Thread grinding → vitrified fine-grain. --- ##### **Solution (b) — Honing Process (7 marks):** **1. Process Description (2 marks):** Honing is a low-speed, low-pressure abrasive finishing process used primarily for **internal cylindrical bores**. The honing tool consists of an **expandable mandrel** carrying 3–12 abrasive sticks (stones). The tool is inserted into the bore and three motions occur simultaneously: **(i) Rotation** of the tool (30–150 m/min), **(ii) Reciprocation** (axial up-down motion, 5–30 m/min), and **(iii) Radial expansion** of stones (controlled by a feed mechanism). Coolant (oil/water emulsion) floods the bore to flush away chips and dissipate heat. **2. Derivation of Cross-Hatch Angle (2 marks):** During one complete rotation, the stone traces a circumferential path of length $\pi d$. In the same time, the reciprocating motion advances the stone axially by $2L \cdot (v_{\text{rec}}/v_{\text{rot}})$. The trace on the bore wall forms a helix with angle $\alpha$ given by: $$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{v_{\text{rec}}}{v_{\text{rot}}}$$ $$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{v_{\text{rec}}}{v_{\text{rot}}}\right)$$ **Example:** If $v_{\text{rot}} = 100$ m/min and $v_{\text{rec}} = 60$ m/min, then $\alpha \approx 30.96°$. **3. Process Parameters Affecting Surface Finish and Stock Removal (2 marks):** - **Rotational speed:** Higher speed → higher MRR, but risk of stone wear and heat. - **Reciprocation speed:** Controls cross-hatch angle; higher reciprocation → larger angle. - **Stone pressure (radial expansion):** Higher pressure → higher MRR but risk of stone breakage and surface damage. - **Abrasive type and grit:** Coarse grit → high MRR, rough finish; fine grit → low MRR, fine finish. - **Coolant flow:** Inadequate flow → heat buildup, stone glazing, poor finish. - **Number of strokes:** More strokes at lower pressure → smoother surface. - **Workpiece material hardness:** Hard material → softer stone grade. **4. Typical Achievements (1 mark):** - Surface finish: **Ra 0.05–0.4 µm** - Roundness: **< 2 µm** - Stock removal: **0.005–0.05 mm radius** - Cross-hatch angle: **30°–45°** (optimum for oil retention) --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make that cost marks:** > 1. **Grinding wheel designation confusion:** Many students write only 3 positions instead of 6. Always state all six positions, e.g., *51 A 36 L 5 V 23*. > 2. **Honing vs. Lapping confusion:** Lapping is **free-abrasive** (loose grit between tool and work), whereas honing uses **bonded abrasive sticks**. This distinction fetches easy marks. > 3. **Gear Shaving vs. Gear Hobbing:** Students often describe hobbing when asked about shaving. Remember — shaving is a **finishing** process, hobbing is a **primary cutting** process. > 4. **Wrong grade selection logic:** Hard workpiece → soft wheel (NOT hard wheel). Reversing this logic is a frequent error. > 5. **Cross-hatch angle formula:** Some students confuse $\alpha$ with $\tan \alpha$. Always write the inverse tangent explicitly. > 6. **Unit errors in MRR:** $v_w$ in mm/min → divide by 60 for mm³/s. Examiner deducts marks if units are not consistent. > 7. **In surface grinding MRR:** Don't forget the width term $b$. Many students write MRR = $a \cdot v_w$ only, losing 1 mark. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **Gear Shaving** = crossed-axis finishing process; Σ = 3°–15°; uses serrated cutter; AGMA Q6–Q8 from Q8–10. - **Abrasive Processes** = material removal by hard, sharp, geometrically uncontrolled particles. - **Grinding Wheel Marking = 6 Positions:** Abrasive, Grain Size, Grade, Structure, Bond, Speed. - **Abrasive Selection:** Steel → A (Al₂O₃); Carbide → D (Diamond); Hardened Steel → B (CBN); Cast Iron → C (SiC). - **Grade Rule:** Hard work → Soft wheel; Soft work → Hard wheel. - **Bond Types:** V (Vitrified, most common), B (Resinoid), R (Rubber), E (Shellac), M (Metal for superabrasives). - **Types of Grinding:** Surface, Cylindrical, Internal, Centerless, Tool & Cutter, Form, Thread, Creep-Feed. - **Centerless Grinding:** No work-holding centers; uses grinding wheel + regulating wheel + work rest blade. - **Honing = Rotation + Reciprocation + Radial Expansion;** low speed, low pressure, cross-hatch pattern. - **Cross-Hatch Angle:** $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(v_{\text{rec}}/v_{\text{rot}})$; typical 30°–45° for cylinder bores. - **Grinding Ratio $G$:** $G = V_w / V_s$; higher G = better wheel life. - **Specific Energy in Grinding:** 10–100 J/mm³ (much higher than turning due to ploughing). - **Honing Stock Removal:** 0.005–0.05 mm radial; Surface finish 0.05–0.4 µm Ra. - **CBN vs Diamond:** Diamond unsuitable for steel (carbon affinity); CBN ideal for hardened steel. - **Creep-Feed Grinding:** Low table feed (1–10 mm/min) + high depth of cut (1–10 mm) → high MRR + low thermal damage. - **Memorize Formula Sheet:** MRR, Grinding Ratio, Specific Energy, Cross-Hatch Angle — all exam favourites.

topics.title: Lapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lapping** is a precision, low-material-removal-rate (MRR) **abrasive finishing process** in which loose abrasive particles, suspended in a carrier fluid (lapping compound), are applied between two contacting surfaces — the *workpiece* and the *lapping tool (called a "lap")*. Controlled relative motion and gentle pressure cause micro-scratching and micro-cutting actions that refine the surface geometry, improve dimensional accuracy, and produce an exceptionally smooth, reflective finish.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> Lapping is classified under *Finishing and Super-Finishing Processes* in Module 4. It is categorized as a **loose-abrasive machining (LAM)** process, distinguishing it from bonded-abrasive processes like grinding. It is most often used as the *last operation* in a manufacturing sequence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sandpaper Between Two Stones" Intuition

Imagine rubbing two flat stones together with fine sand and water sprinkled in between. The sand grains act as tiny, rolling chisels that shave off microscopic peaks from both surfaces. Over time, the two stones mate together perfectly — every bump on one is mirrored as a hollow on the other, and vice versa. The process that emerges is **Lapping**.

- The **two stones** = the workpiece and the *lap* (tool)
- The **fine sand + water** = the lapping compound (abrasive + vehicle)
- The **rubbing action** = controlled sliding/rotary motion
- The **resulting mating surfaces** = the flat/lapped finish

> [!NOTE]
> Lapping is *not* grinding. In grinding, the abrasive is **bonded** to a wheel. In lapping, the abrasive is **free**, floating in a paste or slurry, and rolls/slides between the surfaces.

## 1.3 Purpose and Key Applications

| Purpose | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| **Ultra-fine surface finish** | Achieves $R_a$ as low as **0.05 μm** (mirror-like) |
| **High geometric accuracy** | Flatness to a few light bands; roundness < 1 μm |
| **Form correction** | Corrects minor distortion from prior operations |
| **Sealing surfaces** | Valve faces, gaskets, cylinder liners |
| **Optical surfaces** | Lenses, prisms, gauge blocks |
| **Bearing surfaces** | Rings, races, precision shafts |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Hook:** Examiners frequently ask *"Why is lapping used after grinding?"* — The answer revolves around the **free-abrasive action** that removes the directional grinding lay/spirals, producing a *non-directional*, *scratch-free* surface essential for sealing and gauging.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanism — How Lapping Actually Removes Material

Unlike grinding where each grit is fixed and cuts at a definite angle, lapping abrasives are **loose**. They therefore have *three* possible micro-actions:

1. **Rolling** — abrasive grains roll between surfaces, producing plastic deformation and micro-fatigue
2. **Ploughing** — grains drag sideways, displacing material without chip formation
3. **Micro-cutting** — sharp-edged grains (especially angular ones) actually *cut* micro-chips

> [!NOTE]
> In **well-lapped** surfaces, the dominant action shifts from rolling → cutting as the process progresses, giving finer finishes.

## 2.2 Types of Lapping

### A. Flat Lapping
- Used on flat surfaces (gauge blocks, valve plates, sealing rings)
- Workpiece is held in a fixture and rubbed against a flat cast-iron lap
- The lap may rotate (rotary lapper) or reciprocate (reciprocating lapper)
- For high accuracy, the *three-plate method* (workpiece between two laps) is used

### B. Cylindrical (Centerless) Lapping
- Used on cylindrical parts: pins, valve stems, piston pins
- The workpiece rotates between two or three cylindrical lap rings
- Achieves both roundness correction and surface refinement simultaneously

### C. Spherical Lapping
- Used for ball-bearing balls, ball-valve seats
- Special concave/convex lap tools are used
- Often combined with hard-polishing media

### D. Honing (often grouped with lapping)
- Uses bonded stones but uses similar principle of low-MRR finishing
- Primarily for cylindrical bores

## 2.3 Lapping Abrasives — Common Choices

| Abrasive | Hardness (Knoop) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Aluminum Oxide ($Al_2O_3$)** | ~2100 | Soft steels, general purpose |
| **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** | ~2500 | Cast iron, hard metals |
| **Boron Carbide ($B_4C$)** | ~2800 | Hardened steels, carbides |
| **Diamond** | ~7000 | Tungsten carbide, ceramics, glass |
| **Ceria / Iron Oxide** | ~1000 | Final polishing of glass/optics |

Grit sizes range from **#80 (rough)** down to **#1200 or finer (mirror finish)**.

## 2.4 Lap (Tool) Materials

The lap must be **softer than the workpiece** so it holds the abrasive, but **hard enough** to resist wear.

- **Cast iron** — most common; porous enough to retain compound
- **Copper / bronze** — for soft workpieces (aluminum, brass)
- **Lead** — for very fine finishing of delicate parts
- **Hardwood** — for soft metals
- **Steel** — for heavy stock removal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Check:** If the lap is *harder* than the workpiece, the workpiece will be preferentially abraded, leading to *form errors*. A slightly softer lap is essential.

## 2.5 Lapping Compounds

A lapping compound = **abrasive grains** + **vehicle (carrier fluid)** + **additives (grease, water, kerosene)**.

The vehicle's role:
- Distributes abrasive uniformly
- Carries debris (swarf) away
- Cools the contact zone
- Prevents embedding of grains in the lap

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stock Removal (Depth)** | $Z = K_p \cdot P \cdot V \cdot t$ | mm; $K_p$ = Preston coefficient |
| 2 | **Preston Equation (differential)** | $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = K_p \cdot P \cdot V$ | $P$ = pressure (MPa), $V$ = velocity (m/s) |
| 3 | **MRR (Lapping)** | $MRR = K_p \cdot P \cdot V \cdot A$ | mm³/s; $A$ = contact area |
| 4 | **Surface Roughness Improvement** | $R_a \approx k \cdot \dfrac{d}{r}$ | $d$ = grain diameter, $r$ = grain radius |
| 5 | **Rotary Lap Velocity** | $V = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | $D$ = lap diameter, $N$ = rpm |
| 6 | **Pressure on Lap** | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | F = applied force (N) |
| 7 | **Specific Energy** | $U = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{MRR}$ | J/mm³ |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, vertical lines are written as `\vert` to keep the markdown parser safe. Do NOT write `|x|` in a table row — it breaks the table.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lapping is the *unsung hero* of:
- **Hydraulics & pneumatics** — perfect mating of spool valves
- **Metrology** — gauge block calibration (flatness ≤ 0.1 μm)
- **Semiconductors** — wafer back-grinding & polishing
- **Optics & photonics** — lens finishing
- **Aerospace fuel systems** — injector nozzle seats
- **Medical devices** — surgical instrument mating surfaces
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Preston Equation (The Heart of Lapping Theory)

The Preston Equation is the **fundamental governing law** of lapping, analogous to Taylor's tool-life equation for turning. It was first proposed by **F.W. Preston in 1927** based on the empirical observation that material removal is proportional to *pressure* and *relative velocity*.

### Step 1: Define the System
Consider a single abrasive grain of radius $r$ pressed against a surface with normal load $F_n$. The grain indents the surface to a depth $h$.

By Hertzian contact mechanics (simplified), the indentation depth scales as:

$$
h \;\propto\; \sqrt{\dfrac{F_n}{H}}
$$

where $H$ is the hardness of the softer surface (workpiece).

### Step 2: Define Sliding Velocity
Let the lap move with relative velocity $V$ over the workpiece. The number of abrasive contacts per unit length $\lambda$ (contact density) is constant for a given compound.

### Step 3: Volume of Material Removed per Unit Time
Each grain removes a *micro-volume* proportional to:
- the contact depth $h$
- the sliding distance per unit time $V$

So the volume removed per unit time, per unit area:

$$
\dfrac{dh}{dt} = K_p \cdot P \cdot V
$$

where:
- $K_p$ = Preston coefficient (depends on abrasive, workpiece, compound)
- $P$ = local pressure $\frac{F_n}{A}$
- $V$ = relative sliding velocity

### Step 4: Integrate Over Time
For constant pressure and velocity:

$$
\int_0^{h_t} dh = K_p \cdot P \cdot V \int_0^t dt
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h_t = K_p \cdot P \cdot V \cdot t\,}
$$

This is the **Preston Equation** for total stock removal in lapping.

### Step 5: Material Removal Rate
Multiplying by the contact area $A$:

$$
\boxed{\,MRR = K_p \cdot P \cdot V \cdot A\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The linearity of Preston Equation is its *most important* property. It tells us that to *double* the MRR, you can either double the pressure, double the velocity, or halve the time. This is why lapping parameter selection is so intuitive.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — A Standard KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:**
A cast-iron lap of diameter **300 mm** rotates at **60 rpm**. A workpiece of area **20 cm²** is pressed against it with a force of **50 N**. The Preston coefficient $K_p$ for the system is **$1.2 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{mm}^3/\text{N·m}$**. Determine:
1. The relative velocity
2. The pressure
3. The MRR
4. The stock removal after 2 minutes of lapping

### Solution

**Step 1 — Relative Velocity**
$$
V = \pi \cdot D \cdot N = \pi \cdot 0.300 \cdot \left(\dfrac{60}{60}\right) = \pi \cdot 0.300
$$

$$
V = 0.9425 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure**
Convert area: $A = 20 \;\text{cm}^2 = 20 \times 10^{-4} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^2$

$$
P = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{50}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 25{,}000 \;\text{Pa} = 0.025 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Material Removal Rate**

Given $K_p = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{mm}^3/\text{N·m}$ (note: this is in mm units — but we will use SI carefully; alternatively, $K_p = 1.2 \times 10^{-10}\;\text{m}^3/\text{N·m}$ in SI).

Using SI units: $K_p = 1.2 \times 10^{-10} \;\text{m}^3/\text{N·m}$

$$
MRR = K_p \cdot P \cdot V \cdot A
$$

$$
MRR = (1.2 \times 10^{-10}) \cdot (25{,}000) \cdot (0.9425) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
MRR = 5.66 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Converting to mm³/s:

$$
MRR = 5.66 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s} \approx 0.00566 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Stock Removal After 2 Minutes**
$t = 2 \times 60 = 120 \;\text{s}$

$$
Z = MRR \cdot t = 5.66 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 120 = 0.679\;\text{mm}^3
$$

Or directly from the integrated equation:

$$
h_t = K_p \cdot P \cdot V \cdot t
$$

In compatible units (use $K_p = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{mm}^3/\text{N·m}$, $P = 0.025 \;\text{N/mm}^2$, $V = 942.5\;\text{mm/s}$, $t = 120\;\text{s}$):

$$
h_t = (1.2 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (0.025) \cdot (942.5) \cdot (120)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h_t = 0.339\;\text{mm}\,}
$$

This is the **uniform depth of material removed** from the workpiece surface (assuming even contact).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Sanity Check:** Always confirm that $K_p$ is in units consistent with $P$, $V$, and $t$. Mixing SI and CGS is the #1 reason for numerical errors in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Effect-of-Process-Parameters Summary Table

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Surface Finish | Practical Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Pressure | ↑ MRR (linear) | ↓ Quality if too high | 0.1–0.3 MPa typical |
| ↑ Velocity | ↑ MRR (linear) | Slight ↓ if too high | 30–60 m/s typical |
| ↑ Time | ↑ Total removal | ↑ Quality (more cycles) | Trade-off with production |
| ↓ Grit size | ↓ MRR | ↑ Quality (finer) | Below 5 μm = polishing |
| ↑ Lap hardness | ↑ MRR | ↓ Quality | Avoid embedding issues |
| ↑ Compound flow | Stable MRR | Stable quality | Excess = waste, deficit = glazing |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lapping Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a rotary flat-lapping system, showing how the workpiece, lap, compound, and motion subsystems interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph input["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        A1[Workpiece Holder]
        A2[Loading Fixturing]
    end

    subgraph drive["DRIVE & MOTION SUBSYSTEM"]
        B1[Electric Motor]
        B2[Gear Reducer]
        B3[Lap Spindle Assembly]
        B4[Pressure Actuator]
    end

    subgraph contact["CONTACT ZONE - HEART OF PROCESS"]
        C1["Cast Iron Lap Disc"]
        C2["Workpiece Surface"]
        C3["Lapping Compound Slurry"]
        C4["Free Abrasive Grains - rolling - ploughing - micro-cutting"]
    end

    subgraph output["OUTPUT & MEASUREMENT"]
        D1[Finished Flat Surface]
        D2[Surface Roughness Meter]
        D3[Flatness Interferometer]
        D4[MRR Feedback Controller]
    end

    A2 --> A1
    A1 --> C2
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    C3 --> C1
    C3 --> C2
    C1 <--> C4
    C2 <--> C4
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D4
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> B4
```

## 4.2 Cylindrical Lapping Setup — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cyl["CYLINDRICAL LAPPING CELL"]
        E1[Workpiece - Cylindrical Pin]
        E2[Lap Ring 1 - Lower]
        E3[Lap Ring 2 - Upper]
        E4[Workpiece Rotational Drive]
        E5[Compound Injection Nozzle]
        E6[Swarf Collection Tray]
    end

    E4 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E1 --> E3
    E5 --> E2
    E5 --> E3
    E2 --> E6
    E3 --> E6
```

## 4.3 Three-Plate Flat Lapping Method (High-Accuracy)

The three-plate method is used when **flatness better than 0.5 μm** is required. Each plate is alternately used as the *lap* and the *workpiece*, ensuring no plate ever develops a convex or concave bias.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Plate 1] -->|Stroke 1| P2[Plate 2]
    P2 -->|Stroke 2| P3[Plate 3]
    P3 -->|Stroke 3| P1
    P1 -.->|Result: Three perfectly flat plates| OUT[Flatness < 0.1 micron]
```

## 4.4 Process Variable Influence Map (Cause-Effect)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pressure ↑] --> M1[MRR ↑]
    V[Velocity ↑] --> M1
    G[Grit Size ↑] --> M1
    P --> Q1[Surface Quality ↓]
    G --> Q1
    V --> Q1
    T[Time ↑] --> Q2[Surface Quality ↑]
    T --> M2[MRR constant - cumulative removal ↑]
    LH[Lap Hardness ↑] --> M1
    LH --> Q3[Risk of workpiece scoring ↑]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Rendering Note:** The above block diagrams avoid reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) as standalone node IDs, and all labels are kept as clean uppercase alphanumeric text. This ensures clean Mermaid rendering across GitHub, VS Code, and most academic markdown viewers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**"What is lapping? Why is it classified as a loose-abrasive machining process?"**

**Model Answer:**

Lapping is a precision finishing process in which **loose abrasive particles**, suspended in a carrier fluid (lapping compound), are moved between the workpiece and a tool called a *lap*. Controlled sliding motion and gentle pressure produce a mirror-like surface with high dimensional and geometric accuracy.

It is classified as a **loose-abrasive process** because the abrasive grains are **not bonded** to the tool — they are free to roll, slide, and reorient between the two surfaces, unlike in grinding where the grains are rigidly held in a bonded wheel. (3 marks)

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"List any four applications of lapping in modern industry."**

**Model Answer:**

1. Finishing of **gauge blocks** for metrology (flatness to a fraction of a light band)
2. Lapping of **valve seats and spool valves** in hydraulic systems for leak-tight sealing
3. Finishing of **optical lenses, prisms, and mirror blanks**
4. Surface refinement of **ball-bearing races and rings** to reduce friction and noise
5. Lapping of **fuel injector nozzles** in aerospace and automotive engines
(3 marks for any four)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Structure:** Each question is 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question B-A: `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**"Explain the mechanism of material removal in lapping. Derive the Preston equation and discuss the role of process parameters on the material removal rate (MRR)."**

#### Part (a) — Mechanism of Material Removal (7 marks)

The mechanism of material removal in lapping differs from that of grinding because the abrasive grains are **loose**, not bonded. Three sub-mechanisms operate simultaneously:

1. **Rolling action** — Spherical or rounded grains roll between the workpiece and the lap, causing *plastic deformation* and *micro-fatigue* of surface asperities. This is the dominant action in coarse lapping.

2. **Ploughing action** — Angular grains slide sideways, displacing material plastically without forming a distinct chip. This action smooths the surface but contributes little to MRR.

3. **Micro-cutting action** — Sharp-edged grains (especially fractured SiC or $Al_2O_3$) actually *cut* micro-chips from the surface — analogous to a single-point cutting tool operating at the micron scale. This action dominates in fine lapping.

The *combined* effect is a slow, non-directional surface refinement. The non-directionality is what makes lapped surfaces ideal for **sealing** — any directional lay (as in grinding or turning) creates leak paths.

(7 marks for full mechanism explanation with diagram/sketch)

#### Part (b) — Derivation and Discussion of Preston Equation (7 marks)

The Preston equation relates the rate of material removal to the contact pressure and relative velocity. (See Section 3.1 for the full derivation. The student is expected to reproduce it as follows:)

**Step 1:** Define indentation depth of a single grain: $h \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{F_n}{H}}$

**Step 2:** Define sliding velocity $V$ and contact density $\lambda$.

**Step 3:** Volume removed per unit time per unit area:

$$
\dfrac{dh}{dt} = K_p \cdot P \cdot V
$$

**Step 4:** Integrating over time:

$$
\boxed{\,h_t = K_p \cdot P \cdot V \cdot t\,}
$$

**Step 5:** For total MRR, multiply by area $A$:

$$
\boxed{\,MRR = K_p \cdot P \cdot V \cdot A\,}
$$

**Discussion of process parameters:**

- $K_p$ (Preston coefficient) — depends on abrasive type, workpiece hardness, and compound chemistry. Higher $K_p$ for harder abrasives (diamond > SiC > $Al_2O_3$).
- $P$ (pressure) — typical range 0.05–0.3 MPa. Linear effect.
- $V$ (velocity) — typical 0.5–2 m/s in industrial lappers. Linear effect.
- $A$ (area) — larger contact area gives higher MRR but lower local pressure.
- $t$ (time) — linearly increases total stock removal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse **Preston Equation** (lapsing) with **Taylor's Equation** (tool life). They are entirely different — Taylor relates tool life to cutting speed; Preston relates material removal to pressure × velocity. Mixing them up *costs full marks*.

(7 marks for derivation + parameter discussion)

---

### Question B-B: `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 – Alternative Choice]`

**"Describe the various types of lapping processes. With a neat sketch, explain flat lapping using a rotary lapper, and discuss the selection criteria for lap materials and abrasives."**

#### Part (a) — Types of Lapping (7 marks)

| Type | Geometry | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Flat lapping | Planar surfaces | Gauge blocks, valve plates, sealing rings |
| Cylindrical lapping | External cylinders | Pins, valve stems, pump shafts |
| Internal lapping | Bores & holes | Hydraulic cylinders, bearing races |
| Spherical lapping | Ball & socket | Ball bearings, ball valves |
| Centerless lapping | Round bars | Pins, rollers, needle bearings |

**Flat lapping detail (with sketch):**

In a rotary flat lapping machine:
- A **circular cast-iron lap disc** is mounted on a vertical spindle and rotates at 30–80 rpm
- The workpiece(s) are held in **carrier rings** that float freely on the lap
- The carrier ring rotates *slower* and in the *opposite* direction due to friction
- This produces a complex **planetary motion** (cycloidal path) which:
  - Distributes wear uniformly across the lap
  - Ensures flatness correction on the workpiece
  - Prevents directional lay

Lapping compound is supplied continuously through a drip or spray system. Workpieces may be loaded/unloaded via automatic indexing for high-volume production.

(7 marks with neat sketch — minimum: 4 marks for explanation, 3 marks for sketch)

#### Part (b) — Selection of Lap Materials and Abrasives (7 marks)

**Selection of Lap Material:**

The lap must be **slightly softer** than the workpiece to retain abrasive grains and prevent form errors.

| Workpiece | Recommended Lap Material |
|---|---|
| Hardened steel | Cast iron, hardened steel |
| Cast iron | Cast iron (softer grade) |
| Brass / aluminum | Copper, lead, hardwood |
| Tungsten carbide | Hardened steel, cast iron |
| Glass / ceramics | Cast iron, lead |

**Selection of Abrasive:**

| Abrasive | Best For |
|---|---|
| $Al_2O_3$ | General steels, soft metals |
| SiC | Cast iron, non-ferrous, stone |
| $B_4C$ | Hardened steels, carbides |
| Diamond | Ceramics, glass, carbides, semiconductors |
| Ceria / rouge | Final optical polishing |

**Grit size selection:**

- **#80–#220** → rough lapping (5–10 μm stock removal)
- **#320–#600** → semi-finish lapping
- **#800–#1200+** → mirror finish (sub-micron $R_a$)

(7 marks complete discussion)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** A common mistake in the diagram question is drawing the lap with the *workpiece fixed* and lap rotating only — examiners want to see the **planetary/cycloidal motion** of the carrier ring, not just a simple rotation. Missing this costs 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lapping**

- ✅ Lapping is a **loose-abrasive finishing process** (LAM), *not* bonded abrasive.
- ✅ Material is removed by **rolling + ploughing + micro-cutting** simultaneously.
- ✅ Governing equation is the **Preston Equation**: $h_t = K_p \cdot P \cdot V \cdot t$
- ✅ $K_p$ (Preston coefficient) depends on the abrasive-workpiece-compound system.
- ✅ MRR is **linearly** proportional to both pressure $P$ and velocity $V$.
- ✅ Lap must be **slightly softer** than the workpiece.
- ✅ Common lap material = **cast iron**; for soft metals = copper, lead, wood.
- ✅ Common abrasives = $Al_2O_3$ (general), SiC (cast iron), diamond (carbides/ceramics).
- ✅ **Three-plate method** gives the best flatness.
- ✅ Cylindrical lapping gives both **roundness correction** and **surface finish** in one operation.
- ✅ Achieves $R_a$ as low as **0.05 μm** (mirror finish).
- ✅ Produces a **non-directional surface** — ideal for sealing applications.
- ✅ KTU high-yield keywords: *Preston coefficient, lap, lapping compound, three-plate method, non-directional finish, loose abrasive machining*.
- ✅ Examiner traps:
  - Confusing Preston with Taylor equation
  - Forgetting unit consistency in $K_p$
  - Drawing flat lapping without showing planetary motion
  - Saying "lapping removes material by cutting alone" — it does so by three combined actions

> 🎯 **One-Line Definition for Last-Minute Revision:**
> *"Lapping is a loose-abrasive finishing process governed by the Preston equation $h = K_p P V t$, used to achieve mirror-like, non-directional surfaces of sub-micron accuracy on metals, ceramics, and glass."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Finishing Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET403 - MECHANICS OF METAL CUTTING AND MACHINE TOOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Other Finishing Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Finishing Processes" in Manufacturing?

In manufacturing technology, **finishing processes** are a class of secondary manufacturing operations performed after rough machining (turning, milling, drilling, grinding) to achieve one or more of the following engineering objectives:

1. **Dimensional accuracy** at the sub-micron (μm) level.
2. **Improved surface integrity** (low surface roughness `Ra`).
3. **Enhanced functional performance** (e.g., better fatigue life, sealing ability, wear resistance, and corrosion resistance).
4. **Modification of surface metallurgical properties** (work-hardening, compressive residual stresses).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4 — Other Finishing Processes):**
> Finishing processes that are *not* conventional grinding (which is covered in a separate module). These processes use either **abrasive action**, **plastic deformation**, or a **combination of both** to refine a previously machined surface to high levels of accuracy and surface finish.

The term **"Other Finishing Processes"** is a KTU-specific grouping that covers processes outside the domain of standard grinding machines (surface, cylindrical, centerless). These are typically classified under the **super-finishing / micro-finishing** family of operations.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Wood-Working Shop"

Imagine you have just built a wooden dining table using a hand-saw and a chisel. The table is functional, but the surface is rough, with visible tool marks and splinters. To make it pleasant to use, you would:

1. **Sand it down** with progressively finer sandpapers (coarse → medium → fine → ultra-fine).
2. **Apply wax or polish** using a soft cloth or rotating brush to give it a mirror-like glow.
3. **Press the surface** with a hard, smooth tool (steel roller) to flatten the wood grain and close micro-pores.

These three steps — **abrasive finishing**, **buffing/polishing**, and **burnishing** — are *exactly* what happens in industrial metal finishing. The only difference is the workpiece is steel/cast iron/ceramic, and the "sandpaper" is replaced by bonded abrasive stones, loose abrasive slurry, or coated abrasive belts rotating at controlled speeds and pressures.

### 1.3 Classification of "Other Finishing Processes" (KTU Framework)

The KTU 2024 scheme groups these processes into **four primary families** based on the fundamental material-removal (or material-modification) mechanism:

| Family | Mechanism | Typical Processes |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Abrasive Finishing (Bonded)** | Bonded abrasive stones oscillate against the workpiece | Honing, Superfinishing |
| **B. Abrasive Finishing (Loose Abrasive)** | Free abrasive particles suspended in fluid act between tool and workpiece | Lapping |
| **C. Plastic Deformation Finishing** | No material removal; surface layer plastically deformed | Burnishing, Roller Burnishing |
| **D. Polishing/Buffing** | Soft, flexible tool with polishing compound | Buffing, Polishing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are these processes necessary?**
> Even a "perfectly finished" ground surface (e.g., `Ra = 0.8 μm` from cylindrical grinding) has *directional* lay — the grinding lines run circumferentially. A honed surface, by contrast, has a **cross-hatch lay at ~45°**, which is critical for:
> - Cylinder bores of IC engines (oil retention in cross-hatch).
> - Hydraulic spool valves (lubricant pockets).
> - Bearing journals (micro-reservoirs for hydrodynamic oil film).
> This is why honing and superfinishing are **non-substitutable** in tribological applications.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Metrics in Bold)

- **Surface Roughness (Ra)** — Arithmetic mean roughness, measured in **μm** or **μin** (micro-inches). Typical KTU range for finishing: **0.1 μm ≤ Ra ≤ 1.6 μm**.
- **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** — Volume removed per unit time, in **mm³/min** or **cm³/min**.
- **Stock Removal (t)** — Total radial/linear material removed, in **mm** or **μm**.
- **Specific Energy (u)** — Energy per unit volume removed, in **J/mm³** or **W·s/mm³**.
- **Cross-hatch Angle (θ)** — Angle of intersection between two sets of abrasive traces, in **degrees**.
- **Grit Size** — Average abrasive particle diameter, in **mesh** (US standard) or **μm**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PROCESS 1 — HONING

#### 2.1.1 Definition
**Honing** is a low-speed, low-pressure **abrasive finishing process** in which a rotating bonded-abrasive stone (or set of stones) is moved axially back-and-forth (reciprocated) inside a pre-machined bore, while simultaneously rotating and expanding outward radially under a controlled feed force. The cross-pattern of the rotational and reciprocating motions produces a characteristic **cross-hatch (crossed lay) finish** at ~30°–45°.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:**
> Honing differs from grinding in **THREE** fundamental ways:
> 1. **Low speeds:** Honing uses **surface speeds of 15–60 m/min** (vs. 1500–3000 m/min in grinding).
> 2. **Multi-point contact:** Typically **6 to 12 stones** contact the workpiece simultaneously (vs. a single grinding wheel contact zone).
> 3. **No thermal damage:** Low speeds and low pressures mean **negligible heat-affected zone (HAZ)**.

#### 2.1.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. The workpiece (e.g., cylinder block) is clamped on the honing machine table.
2. A **mandrel** (tool spindle) carrying radially-expandable abrasive stones is inserted into the bore.
3. The mandrel **rotates** at 100–300 RPM and **reciprocates** axially at 5–25 m/min.
4. A **feed mechanism** (mechanical, hydraulic, or pneumatic) expands the stones outward by a controlled amount, generating a **cutting pressure of 0.1–0.4 MPa**.
5. Honing fluid (typically a light petroleum oil with extreme-pressure additives) floods the contact zone to flush chips, cool the stones, and prevent loading.
6. The combined helical motion of each abrasive grit (rotation + reciprocation) creates the **cross-hatch pattern**.

#### 2.1.3 Honing Process Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spindle speed (rotational) | N | 100 – 300 | RPM |
| Reciprocating speed | V | 5 – 25 | m/min |
| Surface speed (peripheral) | v | 15 – 60 | m/min |
| Stone pressure (force per unit area) | P | 0.1 – 0.4 | MPa |
| Stock removal (radial) | t | 0.005 – 0.05 | mm |
| Cross-hatch angle | θ | 30 – 45 | degrees |
| Coolant | — | Light oil with EP additives | — |
| Abrasive | — | Aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) for steel, Silicon carbide (SiC) for cast iron | — |
| Grit size | — | 80 – 600 | mesh |

#### 2.1.4 Formulas for Honing

**(a) Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Honing**

For a cylindrical bore of diameter **D** and length **L**, with **z** stones of width **b**, expanded by a radial feed **f_r** per pass, the volume of material removed per unit time is given by:

$$
\text{MRR}_{\text{honing}} = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot f_r \cdot N_{\text{osc}}
$$

where:
- $D$ = bore diameter (mm)
- $L$ = bore length (mm)
- $f_r$ = radial stock removal per oscillation cycle (mm)
- $N_{\text{osc}}$ = number of complete oscillation cycles per minute (cycles/min)

A more detailed version (used in KTU numerical problems) for **n** stones is:

$$
\text{MRR} = z \cdot b \cdot f_r \cdot v_r
$$

where $v_r$ is the **resultant velocity** combining rotational and reciprocating motion.

**(b) Cross-Hatch Angle**

The cross-hatch angle is the angle at which the spiral traces from the two motions intersect:

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{v_{\text{recip}}}
$$

where:
- $N$ = spindle rotational speed (rev/min)
- $D$ = bore diameter (mm)
- $v_{\text{recip}}$ = reciprocating speed (mm/min)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering significance of θ:** A **45° cross-hatch** is considered optimal for IC engine bores because it provides:
> - **Oil retention pockets** at the intersections (preventing hydrodynamic film collapse).
> - **Adequate oil supply** at the apex of the piston ring travel.
> - **Resistance to scuffing** during start-up (boundary lubrication regime).

#### 2.1.5 Honing Variants
1. **Vertical honing** (for engine blocks).
2. **Horizontal honing** (for long shafts, gun barrels).
3. **Single-stroke honing** (one pass only, for production lines).
4. **Plateau honing** — a two-stage process: rough hone → plateau hone with fine stones to leave flattened "plateaus" with deep oil pockets.

---

### 2.2 PROCESS 2 — LAPPING

#### 2.2.1 Definition
**Lapping** is a precision **loose-abrasive finishing process** in which material is removed by the action of **free abrasive grains** (suspended in a liquid carrier called *slurry*) trapped between a **lapping plate (lap)** and the workpiece surface. The lap is typically made of a soft material (cast iron, copper, or hardened steel) and the slurry contains abrasive particles (Al₂O₃, SiC, diamond, or boron carbide).

#### 2.2.2 Working Principle

1. The workpiece is placed on (or inside) the **lapping plate**.
2. **Abrasive slurry** is continuously fed between the lap and the workpiece.
3. Either the lap or the workpiece (or both) is rotated and moved in a **figure-8 or planetary path** to ensure uniform material removal and avoid directional lay.
4. The abrasive particles **roll, slide, and indent** the workpiece, removing microscopic chips of material through **three-body abrasion**.
5. No bonded abrasive contacts the surface — this is the **defining feature** of lapping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lapping vs. Honing — Key Differences:**
> | Feature | Honing | Lapping |
> |---|---|---|
> | Abrasive | Bonded stones | Loose particles (slurry) |
> | Tool rigidity | Rigid mandrel | Soft lap |
> | Pressure | 0.1 – 0.4 MPa | 0.01 – 0.1 MPa |
> | Accuracy | 1 – 5 μm | 0.1 – 1 μm |
> | Surface roughness Ra | 0.1 – 0.8 μm | 0.02 – 0.4 μm |
> | Material removal | Moderate | Very low |
> | Typical application | Cylinder bores | Gauges, optical flats, valve seats |

#### 2.2.3 Lapping Variants

1. **Flat lapping** — between two flat plates (e.g., gauge blocks, surface plates).
2. **Cylindrical lapping** — between two cylindrical laps or a lap and an expanding mandrel.
3. **Centerless lapping** — for cylindrical parts (balls for bearings).
4. **Face lapping** — for sealing surfaces.

#### 2.2.4 Formulas for Lapping

**(a) Material Removal Rate in Lapping (Preston Equation)**

The classical Preston equation for lapping (originally derived for optical lens polishing) is:

$$
\frac{dh}{dt} = K_p \cdot P \cdot v
$$

where:
- $\frac{dh}{dt}$ = material removal rate (depth removed per unit time, in mm/s)
- $K_p$ = **Preston coefficient** (depends on abrasive, lap material, slurry, and workpiece), units: mm²/N
- $P$ = **contact pressure** (N/mm² or MPa)
- $v$ = **relative sliding velocity** between lap and workpiece (mm/s)

> [!NOTE]
